### [1] [RAMS: Resource-Adaptive and Detection-Conditioned Model Switching for Embedded Edge Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14716)
*Kushal Khemani,Evan Leri,George Xu,Amit Hod*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RAMS is a lightweight runtime controller that dynamically selects among three YOLOv8 tiers (NANO/SMALL/MEDIUM) to balance latency and detection quality under varying device pressure, introducing detection-conditioned switching policies and a VRU-Weighted Accuracy Score (SWAS) for offline evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Edge object detection on embedded hardware requires balancing inference latency and detection quality under changing resource pressure, but existing solutions lack lightweight runtime controllers that can dynamically adapt without model-reload latency.

Method: RAMS monitors device pressure, calibrates switching thresholds from idle behavior, and dynamically selects among three YOLOv8 tiers (NANO/SMALL/MEDIUM at 320/416/640 px) without model-reload latency. It defines five switching policies including detection-conditioned variants that prevent aggressive downgrades after VRU detections, and introduces SWAS metric for offline policy comparison.

Result: Across Raspberry Pi 5, x86 laptops, and Jetson Orin deployments, safety2 policy achieves 3.41 ms mean latency (5.6x faster than fixed-MEDIUM) while retaining 74% proxy accuracy. Detection-conditioned switching improves SWAS by 25.4% (oracle) and 47.3% (detector-derived) over threshold-only policies. Live KITTI evaluation reports VRU recall of 24.2%, 41.2%, and 59.0% per tier.

Conclusion: Detection-conditioned switching significantly improves accuracy retention under load, but reactive overrides are fundamentally limited by baseline detector recall, suggesting future work should improve recall of lightweight detectors.

Abstract: Edge object detection on embedded hardware requires balancing inference latency and detection quality under changing resource pressure. We present RAMS, a lightweight runtime controller that monitors device pressure, calibrates switching thresholds from idle behavior, and dynamically selects among three resident YOLOv8 tiers (NANO/SMALL/MEDIUM at 320/416/640 px) without model-reload latency. RAMS defines five switching policies, including two detection-conditioned variants that prevent aggressive downgrades after recent vulnerable-road-user (VRU) detections. We further introduce the VRU-Weighted Accuracy Score (SWAS), a scalar metric for offline policy comparison without ground-truth annotations, together with an oracle-bounded variant that separates detector circularity from genuine tier-retention benefit. Across Raspberry Pi 5, x86 laptops, and Jetson Orin ONNX/TensorRT deployments, the same controller equations operate over a 37x latency range. On Jetson Orin TensorRT under heavy load, the safety2 policy achieves 3.41 ms mean latency, 5.6x faster than fixed-MEDIUM inference, while retaining 74% of its proxy accuracy through near-NANO operation with selective SMALL and MEDIUM locks during VRU-positive windows. Detection-conditioned switching improves SWAS by 25.4% under oracle scoring and 47.3% under detector-derived scoring relative to threshold-only policies under heavy load. Live KITTI evaluation reports per-tier VRU recall of 24.2%, 41.2%, and 59.0%, showing that reactive overrides are fundamentally limited by baseline detector recall.

</details>


### [2] [AI for Maritime Security: Comparative Evaluation of CNN and Vision Transformer Architectures for Maritime Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14720)
*Ismet Gocer,Zakirul Bhuiayn,Shakeel Ahmad,Raza Hasan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This study evaluates six deep learning models (CNN, Xception, VGG16, MobileNetV2, EfficientNetV2L, and Vision Transformer) for ship detection in maritime environments using 6,468 images under various weather conditions, finding ViT achieves 100% accuracy with the fastest processing time, while lightweight models suit resource-limited devices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance maritime security by designing intelligent object detection systems capable of detecting ships on the sea surface under diverse real-time environmental conditions.

Method: A maritime image dataset of 6,468 images (cloudy, foggy, rainy, sunny) was used to evaluate six deep learning architectures: a base CNN, four transfer learning models (Xception, VGG16, MobileNetV2, EfficientNetV2L), and a Vision Transformer (ViT). Models were compared using accuracy, Type I/II errors, model size, and video processing time.

Result: ViT achieved the best overall performance with 100% accuracy, lowest error rates, and fastest video processing time. Lightweight architectures are suitable for resource-limited devices, while performance varies depending on computational constraints and deployment conditions.

Conclusion: AI-driven computer vision systems show strong potential for maritime surveillance, border protection, and autonomous navigation applications.

Abstract: This study aims to enhance maritime security by using advanced Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Computer Vision (CV) techniques. For this purpose, it was designed and assessed intelligent object detection systems that can detect the presence of ships on the sea surface under different real-time environments. To achieve this goal, a maritime image dataset with 6,468 images was used, covering different weather conditions like cloudy, foggy, rainy, and sunny environments. Six deep learning architectures were evaluated, including a base Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model, four transfer learning models (Xception, VGG16, MobileNetV2, and EfficientNetV2L), and a Vision Transformer (ViT) model. The models were compared using multiple performance indicators, including accuracy, Type I and Type II errors, model size, and video processing time. The results show that model performance varies depending on computational constraints and deployment conditions. While lightweight architectures are suitable for resource-limited devices, the ViT achieved the best overall performance, reaching 100% accuracy with the lowest error rates and the fastest video processing time. The findings highlight the potential of AI-driven computer vision systems for maritime surveillance, border protection, and autonomous navigation.

</details>


### [3] [Disagreement-Based Cross-Model Routing for Implicit Video Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14723)
*Durga Sandeep Saluru*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a training-free inference-time method called disagreement-based cross-model routing that improves video question answering on the ImplicitQA benchmark by routing only the ~20% of questions where three Gemini samples disagree to a second model (Claude), achieving +1.43 AvgAcc improvement on the validation set and +1.81 on the challenge test set.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard self-consistency (majority voting) fails for hard video QA because model errors are correlated; the paper seeks a better inference-time strategy to push beyond the accuracy ceiling of single frontier video LLMs without requiring labels or training.

Method: Triple-sample Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview at temperature zero, identify the ~20% of questions where samples disagree, and route only those to Claude Opus 4.8 (which uses uniformly sampled frames with adaptive thinking) for final answer prediction.

Result: On the 1001-question validation set: +1.43 AvgAcc improvement, with biggest gains in Motion & Trajectory (+5.49), Inferred Counting (+3.45), and Vertical Spatial Reasoning (+1.82). On the 172-question CVPR 2026 challenge test set: 82.03 AvgAcc / 79.71 MacroAvgAcc (+1.81).

Conclusion: Disagreement-based cross-model routing is an effective, training-free inference-time method that leverages model diversity to improve video QA, especially for categories requiring cross-shot reasoning.

Abstract: We study multiple-choice video question answering on the ImplicitQA benchmark, where the correct answer is never explicitly shown but must be inferred from off-screen events, line-of-sight cues, causal structure, and cross-shot spatial layout. On this benchmark a single frontier video LLM already operates near its accuracy ceiling, and we observe that conventional self-consistency strategies -- majority voting across repeated samples of the same model -- can hurt rather than help, because the model's errors on hard questions are correlated. We propose disagreement-based cross-model routing, a pure inference-time procedure that requires no labels and no training. We triple-sample a native-video model (Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview) at temperature zero, exploit the genuine sample-to-sample variance of its video-processing pipeline to identify the roughly 20% subset of questions where the three samples disagree, and route only that subset to a second model from a different family (Claude Opus 4.8) that consumes uniformly sampled frames with adaptive thinking. On the 1001-question validation set with public ground truth -- our main evaluation -- the method improves AvgAcc by +1.43 over the best single sample of the primary model, with per-category gains concentrated on Motion & Trajectory (+5.49), Inferred Counting (+3.45), and Vertical Spatial Reasoning (+1.82) -- the categories most dependent on cross-shot reference resolution. The same pipeline applied to the held-out 172-question CVPR 2026 ImplicitQA challenge test set achieves 82.03 AvgAcc / 79.71 MacroAvgAcc (+1.81 over the best single sample of the primary model), confirming the validation result on an independent split.

</details>


### [4] [VigilFormer: Deformable Attention for Video Anomaly Detection with Causal Risk Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14724)
*Xinze Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VigilFormer is a unified video anomaly detection framework that balances accuracy and speed by combining deformable spatio-temporal attention, causal temporal modeling, and adaptive frame skipping, outperforming weakly-supervised methods on three benchmarks at 41.5 FPS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video anomaly detection methods trade off between detection accuracy and real-time throughput—strong feature extractors are accurate but slow, while efficient architectures are fast but less accurate. No prior work effectively combines both.

Method: (1) Deformable Spatio-Temporal Encoder (DSTE) for sparse attention across frames; (2) Causal Anomaly Classifier (CAC) with dilated causal convolutions and contrastive MIL loss; (3) Adaptive Confidence Scheduler (ACS) to skip low-information frames at inference.

Result: AUC scores: 87.83% (UCF-Crime), 97.21% (ShanghaiTech), 89.74% (CUHK Avenue); runs at 41.5 FPS on a single GPU, outperforming recent weakly-supervised methods in both accuracy and speed.

Conclusion: VigilFormer demonstrates that combining deformable attention, causal modeling, and adaptive frame skipping yields a unified solution that excels in both detection accuracy and real-time throughput for video anomaly detection.

Abstract: Video anomaly detection in surveillance settings must balance detection accuracy against real-time throughput, a tension that existing methods address either through stronger feature extractors or more efficient architectures, but rarely both. We present VigilFormer, a unified framework that combines deformable spatio-temporal attention with causal temporal modeling to detect anomalies in untrimmed surveillance video. The proposed Deformable Spatio-Temporal Encoder (DSTE) attends to a sparse set of informative locations across frames, avoiding the quadratic cost of dense attention while retaining the ability to capture irregular motion patterns. A Causal Anomaly Classifier (CAC) applies dilated causal convolutions over snippet-level features and optimizes a contrastive multiple-instance learning objective that separates anomalous and normal representations without frame-level labels. To meet deployment constraints, an Adaptive Confidence Scheduler (ACS) dynamically skips low-information frames at inference time, reducing redundant computation in static scenes. Evaluated on UCF-Crime, ShanghaiTech, and CUHK Avenue, VigilFormer achieves AUC scores of 87.83%, 97.21%, and 89.74% respectively, at 41.5 FPS on a single GPU, outperforming recent weakly-supervised methods in both accuracy and speed.

</details>


### [5] [Interpolation between Convolution and Attention via K-Nearest Neighbors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14725)
*Mingi Kang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ConvNN unifies convolution and self-attention within a single k-nearest neighbor aggregation framework, showing they lie on a continuous spectrum rather than being fundamentally distinct operations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ConvNN and Transformers are viewed as fundamentally distinct architectures, but the paper argues they can be unified under a single k-nearest neighbor aggregation framework.

Method: ConvNN formalizes the connection by treating both convolution and self-attention as special cases of neighbor selection and weighted aggregation, with configurable similarity functions, neighbor selection strategies, positional encodings, and aggregation kernels.

Result: ConvNN exactly recovers standard/depthwise convolution (via spatial proximity) and self-attention/sparse variants (via scaled dot-product similarity), enabling systematic exploration of the intermediate spectrum.

Conclusion: Convolution and self-attention are not categorically different but lie on a continuous spectrum, and ConvNN serves as a drop-in replacement for both layer types.

Abstract: The shift from Convolutional Neural Networks to Transformers has reshaped computer vision, yet these two architectural families are typically viewed as fundamentally distinct. Convolutional Neural Networks are defined by spatially local convolution operations, while Transformers rely on global self-attention. We argue that convolution and self-attention, despite their apparent differences, can be unified within a single k-nearest neighbor aggregation framework. The critical insight is that both operations are special cases of neighbor selection and weighted aggregation. Convolution selects neighbors by spatial proximity while self-attention selects by feature similarity, revealing that they lie on a continuous spectrum rather than representing categorically different computations.
  We introduce Convolutional Nearest Neighbors (ConvNN), a unified framework that formalizes this connection. ConvNN exactly recovers standard and depthwise convolution by restricting neighbor selection to normalized spatial coordinates, and exactly recovers self-attention and its sparse variants, including KVT-attention, by replacing spatial proximity with scaled dot-product similarity. Beyond these special cases, ConvNN serves as a drop-in replacement for both convolution and attention layers, enabling systematic exploration of the intermediate spectrum between local and global aggregation through configurable similarity functions, neighbor selection strategies, positional encodings, and aggregation kernels.

</details>


### [6] [FairGen: Preference-Aligned Diffusion for Demographically Equitable Medical Image Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14727)
*Zhimin Li,Ruichen Zhang,Zhen Tan,Howard J Aizenstein,Jingtong Hu,Tianlong Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FairGen is a fairness-aware diffusion framework that generates demographically balanced medical images to mitigate diagnostic bias in AI models, achieving significant fairness improvements across dermatology, radiology, and neuroimaging tasks while maintaining diagnostic accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical imaging AI systems suffer from demographic imbalances in clinical data, causing biased diagnosis and perpetuating healthcare disparities. These imbalances arise from inequitable healthcare access, differential disease prevalence, and naturally rare phenotypic presentations across demographic groups.

Method: FairGen is a fairness-aware diffusion framework that synthesizes demographically balanced medical images while preserving pathology-relevant features. It embeds physician-aligned preferences into the generation process to improve subgroup coverage.

Result: FairGen achieves fairness improvements of 95.9% for skin images, 80.0% for chest radiography, and 35.2% for brain MRI, while maintaining competitive diagnostic accuracy compared to models trained on original clinical data.

Conclusion: FairGen effectively mitigates diagnostic bias across multiple medical imaging domains, with clinician review and external validation confirming that gains extend beyond standard fidelity metrics and generalize to independent datasets.

Abstract: Medical imaging is central to modern diagnostics, and artificial intelligence (AI) systems are increasingly used to support image-based analysis by improving efficiency, accuracy, and access to care. However, inequities in healthcare access and differential disease prevalence create severe demographic imbalances in clinical image data. Such imbalances are compounded by the fact that diseases can manifest with distinct features across demographic groups, rendering certain phenotypic presentations naturally rare. AI models trained on such imbalanced data risk perpetuating diagnostic bias and widening healthcare disparities. Here we introduce FairGen, a fairness-aware diffusion framework that synthesizes demographically balanced medical images while preserving pathology-relevant visual features. By embedding physician-aligned preferences into the generation process, FairGen improves subgroup coverage during synthesis and downstream classification. Applied to dermatology, radiology, and neuroimaging benchmark tasks, FairGen achieves fairness improvements of 95.9% for skin images, 80.0% for chest radiography, and 35.2% for brain MRI, while maintaining competitive diagnostic accuracy relative to models trained on original clinical data. Clinician-facing expert review and external validation on independent cohorts further support that these gains extend beyond standard fidelity metrics and are not confined to the original in-distribution datasets.

</details>


### [7] [FUSE: Quantifying Uncertainty in Vision-Language Models by Bayesian Fusing Epistemic and Aleatoric Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14728)
*Harry Zhang,Luca Carlone*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FUSE is a probabilistic framework that quantifies two complementary sources of uncertainty in VLMs (aleatoric embedding-level and epistemic model-level uncertainty) via Bayesian fusion, achieving SOTA calibration for predicting output correctness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-language models are increasingly used in critical applications (e.g., robotics) where quantifying output uncertainty is essential but underexplored.

Method: FUSE combines aleatoric uncertainty (from input data ambiguity) and epistemic uncertainty (from semantic response diversity) through a Bayesian fusion mechanism to produce a scalar uncertainty measure.

Result: The method outperforms baselines and achieves state-of-the-art uncertainty calibration for predicting model output correctness.

Conclusion: The proposed uncertainty quantification framework is reliable for downstream applications requiring confidence assessment in VLM outputs.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) are playing an increasingly important role across multiple domains. In many applications, such as robotics, it is crucial to quantify the uncertainty in the output of these models. } We develop FUSE, a probabilistic framework for capturing two complementary sources of uncertainty in vision-language modeling: (i) aleatoric embedding-level uncertainty derived from input data vision-language ambiguity, and (ii) epistemic model-level uncertainty estimated from the semantic response diversity of VLMs. Our approach formulates a Bayesian fusion mechanism that analytically combines these uncertainty sources to produce a scalar measure of uncertainty. This measure can be used to reliably predict the model's output correctness for downstream applications. We demonstrate that our method outperforms baselines and achieves SOTA uncertainty calibration.

</details>


### [8] [Hierarchical GRU with Input-Conditioned Slot Queries for Ball Action Anticipation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14730)
*Parthsarthi Rawat*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A hierarchical model with Transformer encoder, GRU aggregator, and Transformer decoder using frequency-reweighted Hungarian matching and Gaussian soft targets achieves 17.91% mAP for ball action anticipation in football broadcast video.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To anticipate future ball actions in football broadcasts (predicting actions in a 5-second window after observing 30 seconds), addressing the challenge of rare action classes.

Method: 1) Shared local Transformer encodes clip-level features per 5-second sub-window; 2) GRU aggregates temporal context across sub-windows; 3) Transformer decoder with K input-conditioned event slots decodes via three decoupled heads (objectness, class, temporal offset); 4) Frequency-reweighted Hungarian matching favors rare classes; 5) Gaussian soft targets for temporal bin supervision.

Result: 17.91% mAP on SoccerNet Ball Action Anticipation benchmark test server.

Conclusion: The proposed hierarchical architecture with class-balancing matching and soft temporal supervision effectively tackles ball action anticipation in football videos.

Abstract: We present a hierarchical model for ball action anticipation in football broadcast video. Given a 30-second observation window, the system predicts actions occurring in the subsequent 5-second window across 10 classes. A shared local Transformer encodes clip-level features within each 5-second sub-window; a GRU then aggregates temporal context across all sub-windows; finally, a Transformer decoder with K input-conditioned event slots decodes the anticipation target via three decoupled heads (objectness, class, temporal offset). We introduce frequency-reweighted Hungarian matching that systematically favours rare action classes, and Gaussian soft targets for temporal bin supervision. On the SoccerNet Ball Action Anticipation benchmark, our method achieves 17.91% mAP on the test server.

</details>


### [9] [SP$^3$: Spherical Priors for Plug-and-Play Restoration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16396)
*Sean Man,Ron Raphaeli,Matan Kleiner,Or Ronai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SP$^3$ is a fast Plug-and-Play image restoration algorithm that uses Spherical Encoders as generative priors, achieving comparable quality to diffusion/flow methods while being 3-630x faster.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accelerate maximum a posteriori image restoration by replacing computationally expensive denoisers with Spherical Encoders as generative priors, avoiding gradient computation during inference.

Method: Uses Spherical Encoders (SE) with tightly structured latent space as a robust projection onto the natural image manifold, alternating with closed-form data-consistency via Half-Quadratic Splitting, requiring no gradient computation.

Result: Achieves perceptual quality comparable to state-of-the-art zero-shot diffusion and flow methods while being 3-630x faster, with "anytime" restoration capability producing sharp images from the first iteration.

Conclusion: SP$^3$ offers a practical trade-off between speed and quality, significantly accelerating image restoration while maintaining competitive perceptual quality.

Abstract: In this paper, we introduce SP$^3$, a novel Plug-and-Play algorithm that accelerates maximum a posteriori image restoration by replacing denoisers with Spherical Encoders (SE) as generative priors. SP$^3$ approximates the intractable proximal prior step by utilizing the SE tightly structured latent space as a robust projection onto the natural image manifold. Alternating this projection with a closed-form data-consistency step, via Half-Quadratic Splitting, achieves stable convergence without requiring gradient computation during inference. This unique formulation unlocks "anytime" restoration capabilities, producing sharp, plausible images from the first iteration. Evaluations across a variety of image restoration tasks demonstrate that SP$^3$ achieves perceptual quality comparable to state-of-the-art zero-shot diffusion and flow methods while being $3$-$630\times$ faster.

</details>


### [10] [BBR-Net: Boundary-Balanced Replay for Continual Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14731)
*Zahid Ullah,Sieun Choi,Jihie Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BBR-Net uses boundary-aware replay for continual cardiac ultrasound segmentation, finding that structural consistency (not memory capacity) determines knowledge retention, and replay fails when source data has noisy/inconsistent structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Replay-based continual learning methods preserve appearance information but fail to explicitly model anatomical structure, leaving a gap in understanding whether structural consistency governs knowledge retention under domain shift in medical image segmentation.

Method: Proposes Boundary-Balanced Replay Network (BBR-Net) with boundary-aware priority and class-balanced replay sampling; evaluated on CAMUS and CardiacNet under forward and reverse task orders; includes controlled structural perturbation analysis.

Result: Forward: BBR-Net retains source-task performance near joint-training reference with reduced forgetting. Reverse: structure-aware replay fails when source data is noisy/inconsistent. Forgetting increases as structural reliability decreases.

Conclusion: Anatomical structure preservation is central to continual medical image segmentation; replay mechanisms must account for structural reliability, not just memory capacity.

Abstract: Continual learning for medical image segmentation remains challenging under domain shift because replay-based methods often preserve appearance information without explicitly modeling anatomical structure. This study investigates whether structural consistency governs knowledge retention in continual cardiac ultrasound segmentation. We propose the Boundary-Balanced Replay Network (BBR-Net), which selects replay samples using boundary-aware priority and class balance to preserve anatomically informative regions. The method is evaluated on CAMUS and CardiacNet under forward (CAMUS to CardiacNet) and reverse (CardiacNet to CAMUS) task orders. In the forward setting, BBR-Net retains source-task performance close to an offline joint-training reference, while markedly reducing catastrophic forgetting and preserving competitive target-task adaptation. Ablation results show that boundary-aware prioritization contributes to retention and improves the balance between source-task preservation and target-task adaptation when combined with class-aware sampling. In contrast, the reverse setting reveals that structure-aware replay fails when initial representations are learned from noisy and structurally inconsistent data. To isolate this effect, we conduct a controlled structural perturbation analysis by progressively corrupting source-task boundaries while keeping the dataset, architecture, and training protocol fixed. Forgetting increases consistently as structural reliability decreases, suggesting that replay effectiveness is strongly influenced by the quality of stored structural information, rather than by memory capacity alone. These findings indicate that preserving anatomical structure under domain shift is a central factor in continual medical image segmentation, and that replay mechanisms should account for structural reliability to support robust knowledge retention.

</details>


### [11] [Simulation-Based Multi-Fillet Evaluation of Woody Breast Poultry Fillets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16951)
*Chirantan Sen Mukherjee,Seung-Chul Yoon,William J. Beksi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a top-down multi-fillet imaging system for detecting Woody Breast myopathy in broiler chickens, using a digital twin and physics-based simulations to extract shape deformation scores, offering a scalable alternative to side-view single-fillet systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: State-of-the-art automated WB detection relies on side-view imaging with a single-fillet field of view, creating throughput bottlenecks on commercial processing lines. This paper aims to address this limitation.

Method: The authors develop a high-fidelity digital twin of an industrial conveyor, synthesize a diverse dataset of 3D fillet meshes, model viscoelastic bending dynamics via physics-based simulation, and extract a continuous 2D shape deformation score from a top-down camera perspective.

Result: The top-down shape score effectively captures contour changes of fillets as they bend, providing a robust and scalable alternative to side-view imaging for simultaneous multi-fillet WB evaluation.

Conclusion: The proposed top-down multi-fillet architecture overcomes the throughput bottleneck of single-fillet side-view systems, enabling scalable, simultaneous Woody Breast detection on commercial processing lines.

Abstract: Woody breast (WB) is a myopathy in modern broiler chickens that causes the breast muscle to become unusually stiff and fibrous, leading to decreased meat quality and significant economic losses. State-of-the-art automated WB detection relies on a side-view imaging system to analyze the bending behavior of a single fillet as it falls off a conveyor belt. While highly accurate, this approach is constrained by its single-fillet field of view, creating throughput bottlenecks on commercial processing lines. In this paper, we address this limitation via a novel multi-fillet detection architecture utilizing a top-down camera configuration. To validate our approach, we first develop a high-fidelity digital twin of an industrial conveyor system. Next, we synthesize a diverse dataset of 3D fillet meshes and model their viscoelastic bending dynamics using a physics-based simulation engine. Lastly, a continuous 2D shape deformation score is extracted from the top-down perspective as the simulated fillets traverse the roller precipice. Experimental results demonstrate that the top-down shape score effectively captures the contour changes of the fillets as it bends, providing a robust and scalable alternative to a side-view imaging system for simultaneous multi-fillet WB evaluation.

</details>


### [12] [Steady-Forcing: Balancing Spatial Persistence and Motion Continuity in Long-Horizon Nature Video Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14732)
*Matiur Rahman Minar,Seunghun Oh,GangHyeon Jeong,Unsang Park*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Steady-Forcing is a framework that improves long-horizon autoregressive video generation by balancing background stability with natural motion (e.g., water, fire, smoke) through visual anchors, motion memory, cache purification, and teacher distillation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive video diffusion models degrade over long rollouts—static scene layouts drift, and mechanisms that improve stability suppress motion, causing natural flows to stagnate. The authors aim to resolve this stability-motion trade-off in fixed-camera nature video generation.

Method: Steady-Forcing combines: (1) a persistent visual anchor (V-Sink), (2) exponential moving-average motion memory (EMA-Sink), (3) block-relative temporal encoding, (4) periodic cache purification, and (5) distillation from a Wan2.1-14B teacher with motion-rewarded priors under task-focused configurations.

Result: Improves long-horizon background consistency and imaging quality over seven baselines. A blind user study indicates stronger perceived stability and motion continuity. Also reveals that generic VBench metrics under-penalize fixed-camera artifacts and misclassify drift as motion while ignoring texture hardening or flow stagnation.

Conclusion: The proposed components effectively preserve background identity while sustaining fluid dynamics over multi-minute autoregressive rollouts. The benchmarks highlight the need for task-specific evaluation for static-camera nature-flow generation.

Abstract: Autoregressive video diffusion models enable streaming generation but often degrade over long rollouts: static scene layouts drift, while mechanisms that improve spatial stability tend to suppress motion, causing natural flows such as water, fire, or smoke to stagnate. We study this stability-motion trade-off in fixed-camera long-horizon nature video generation, where the two failure modes can be more clearly separated than in moving-camera settings. We propose Steady-Forcing, a memory and training framework combining a persistent visual anchor (V-Sink), an exponential moving-average motion memory (EMA-Sink), block-relative temporal encoding, periodic cache purification, and distillation from a Wan2.1-14B teacher with motion-rewarded priors under task-focused configurations. Together, these components are designed to preserve background identity while sustaining visually plausible fluid dynamics over multi-minute autoregressive rollouts. Evaluations across seven baselines show that Steady-Forcing improves long horizon background consistency and imaging quality, while a blind user study indicates stronger perceived stability and motion continuity. The benchmark evaluation further suggest that generic VBench aggregate scores under-penalize fixed-camera artifacts as well as rewarding drift-induced optical flow as Dynamic Degree while not directly penalizing texture hardening or flow stagnation - motivating future task-specific benchmarks for static-camera nature-flow evaluation. Project page: https://minar09.github.io/steadyforcing/

</details>


### [13] [UtVAA: Ultra-tiny Vision Transformer with Affix Attention for Mobile Image Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14735)
*Romiyal George,Sathiyamohan Nishankar,Selvarajah Thuseethan,Roshan G. Ragel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces UtVAA, an ultra-tiny Vision Transformer architecture with novel Affix Attention and Dilated Bottleneck blocks, achieving competitive accuracy with as few as 204.67K parameters (53.95M FLOPs) for deployment on mobile and edge devices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision Transformers have strong representation capability but suffer from quadratic self-attention complexity and large parameter counts, making them unsuitable for resource-constrained mobile and edge devices.

Method: UtVAA introduces: (1) Affix Attention block combining depthwise-pointwise local feature extraction, linear self-attention, coordinate attention, and ternary fusion; (2) Dilated Bottleneck blocks using dilated depthwise separable convolutions with residual connections to expand receptive fields efficiently; (3) Scalable Tiny/Medium/Large variants.

Result: The smallest UtVAA model (Tiny) has 204.67K parameters and 53.95M FLOPs. On CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, PlantVillage-Tomato, and SLIF-Tomato datasets, UtVAA achieves competitive accuracy within the sub-million-parameter regime.

Conclusion: Transformer-based vision models can be redesigned into ultra-tiny architectures without significant loss in discriminative performance, making UtVAA suitable for mobile and edge deployment.

Abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) have demonstrated strong representation capability in image classification. However, their quadratic self-attention complexity and large parameter counts limit deployment on resource-constrained mobile and edge devices. This paper introduces UtVAA, an ultra-tiny Vision Transformer architecture designed for efficient visual recognition under strict computational budgets. It incorporates a novel Affix Attention block that combines depthwise-pointwise local feature extraction, linear self-attention, coordinate attention for spatial dependency modelling, and a lightweight ternary fusion strategy to integrate local and global representations. In addition, Dilated Bottleneck blocks expand the receptive field using dilated depthwise separable convolutions while maintaining low FLOPs and stable optimisation through residual connections. UtVAA is implemented in scalable Tiny, Medium, and Large variants, with the smallest model containing 204.67K parameters and 53.95M FLOPs. Experimental results on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, PlantVillage-Tomato and SLIF-Tomato datasets show that UtVAA achieves competitive accuracy within a sub-million-parameter regime. Overall, the results demonstrate that transformer-based vision models can be redesigned into ultra-tiny architectures without significant loss in discriminative performance, making UtVAA suitable for mobile and edge deployment. Code is available at https://github.com/romiyal/UtVAA

</details>


### [14] [GridVQA-X: A Framework for Evaluating Multimodal Explainability Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14740)
*Sujay Belsare,Sudarshan Nikhil,Sushant Kumar,Ponnurangam Kumaraguru,Chirag Agarwal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces GridVQA-X, a diagnostic framework to evaluate whether multimodal explainability methods can distinguish between genuine cross-modal reasoning and shallow shortcuts, finding that current methods fail this test.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal explainability evaluation lacks ground truth to differentiate between true cross-modal reasoning (e.g., spatial composition) and shallow shortcuts (e.g., Bag-of-Words matching), making it unclear if explanations faithfully capture model reasoning.

Method: GridVQA-X uses a closed-world synthesis logic to generate unique, mathematically guaranteed explanations, training paired models—$M_{\text{pure}}$ (genuine spatial reasoning) and $M_{\text{spur}}$ (cross-modal shortcut exploitation)—on identical architectures to create a rigorous testbed for explainers.

Result: Widely used explainability methods fail to distinguish between the two models, unable to differentiate genuine spatial-relational reasoning from cross-modal shortcut exploitation.

Conclusion: Current MxAI methods exhibit a critical gap in capturing true cross-modal synergy, misrepresenting how multimodal models actually make decisions.

Abstract: With the increasing development of Vision-Language Models, it becomes imperative that their predictions are readily explainable to relevant stakeholders. However, the field of explainability has not kept pace with the multimodal surge. While recent Multimodal Explainable AI (MxAI) methods generate explanations to attribute the interaction between different modalities, current evaluation protocols lack the ground truth required to distinguish between true cross-modal reasoning (e.g., spatial composition) and shallow cross-modal shortcuts (e.g., Bag-of-Words attribute matching). It remains unknown whether MxAI methods faithfully capture synergistic interactions or merely hallucinate reasoning on models acting as simple feature detectors. In this paper, we introduce GridVQA-X, the first diagnostic framework specifically designed to evaluate cross-modal explainability. Unlike natural datasets, GridVQA-X leverages a closed-world synthesis logic to generate unique, mathematically guaranteed explanations. We utilize this controlled environment to train paired ground-truth models on identical architectures: $M_{\text{pure}}$, which learns robust spatial-relational reasoning and $M_{\text{spur}}$, which is structurally forced to rely on cross-modal shortcuts. This behavioral divergence creates a rigorous testbed: a faithful explainer must report distinct reasoning pathways for each model. Our findings reveal that widely used methods fail to distinguish between models relying on genuine spatial-relational reasoning and those exploiting cross-modal shortcuts, highlighting a critical gap in capturing true cross-modal synergy and misrepresenting how multimodal models actually make decisions.

</details>


### [15] [HorusEye: Language as Dynamic Attention for Emergency Visual Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14741)
*Armel Yara*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HorusEye proposes language as dynamic attention for emergency visual analysis, introducing the RefCOCO-Degraded benchmark with 15,244 images across four conditions. Evaluating multiple VLMs, key findings include model-dependent language feedback effectiveness (Gemini +47.3% vs Qwen2-VL -5.1% in thermal), the 'Thermal Paradox' where cropping fails in thermal imagery, and BLIP-2's increased hallucination under degradation making it unsuitable for emergency use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate Vision-Language Models (VLMs) under systematically degraded visual conditions (fog, smoke, thermal) for emergency applications, and to explore whether language feedback can serve as dynamic attention to compensate for poor visual quality.

Method: Created RefCOCO-Degraded benchmark (3,811 base images × 4 conditions = 15,244 images). Evaluated five VLMs (Gemini, Qwen2-VL, BLIP-2, LLaVA, Kosmos-2) across four tasks: visual grounding, language feedback recovery, health VQA, and hallucination analysis.

Result: Language feedback effectiveness is highly model-dependent: Gemini improved by +47.3% in thermal conditions, while Qwen2-VL degraded by -5.1%. Identified 'Thermal Paradox' where cropping strategies effective for RGB fail catastrophically in thermal imagery. BLIP-2 shows increased hallucinations under degradation.

Conclusion: Language as dynamic attention can significantly improve VLM performance in degraded visual conditions, but only for certain models. BLIP-2 is unsuitable for emergency deployment due to increased hallucination under degradation.

Abstract: We introduce HorusEye, Language as Dynamic Attention for Emergency Visual Analysis. Our investigation followed five stages. The first one is benchmarking RefCOCO-Degraded, a dataset of 15,244 images (3,811 base images x 4 conditions: Clean, Fog, Smoke and Thermal) with systematic visual degradation. Through four research questions, we evaluate multiple VLMs (Gemini, Qwen2-VL, BLIP-2, LLaVA, Kosmos-2) across visual grounding the second stage, language feedback recovery the third one, health VQA tasks the fourth, and hallucination analysis the final stage. Our key finding is that language feedback effectiveness is model-dependent: Gemini achieves +47.3% improvement in thermal conditions through iterative language feedback, while Qwen2-VL shows -5.1% degradation under the same protocol. We also identify the 'Thermal Paradox' where cropping strategies that improve RGB performance catastrophically fail in thermal imagery. Furthermore, BLIP-2 uniquely hallucinates more under degradation, making it unsuitable for emergency deployment

</details>


### [16] [Style-CCL: Content-Preserving Style Transfer via Curriculum Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14746)
*Shiwen Zhang,Haoyuan Wang,Xianghao Zang,Haibin Huang,Chi Zhang,Xuelong Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multi-stage curriculum continual learning framework (Style-CCL) for training Style-Content Diffusion Transformers to achieve better content-preserving style transfer by learning from semantic to texture styles and clean to synthetic data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Content-preserving style transfer with Diffusion Transformers is challenging because entangled content and style features cause one-stage training on mixed style categories to let semantic styles dominate, which hinders texture style learning and harms content preservation.

Method: Style-CCL uses a Multi-Stage Curriculum Continual Learning framework that trains SC-DiT progressively: from semantic (easy) to texture (hard) styles, and from clean to synthetic data, with Random Memory Rehearsal across stages to prevent catastrophic forgetting. SC-DiT itself decouples style and content via separate ROPE embeddings and causal masking, trained on a reverse triplet synthesis pipeline dataset.

Result: The proposed Style-CCL achieves state-of-the-art performance in three core metrics: style similarity, content consistency, and aesthetic quality.

Conclusion: By addressing the semantic style dominance problem through curriculum learning from easy to hard styles, Style-CCL effectively enables content-preserving style transfer with Diffusion Transformers.

Abstract: Content-Preserving Style transfer, given content and style references, remains challenging for Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) due to entangled content and style features. With a reverse triplet synthesis pipeline to build a million-scale training set and a dual-branch Style-Content DiT (SC-DiT) that decouples style and content via separate ROPE embeddings and causal masking, we observe that such a one-stage training paradigm on mixed style categories causes semantic styles to dominate, hindering texture style learning, and harming content preservation. To address these issues, we propose Style-CCL, a Multi-Stage Curriculum Continual Learning framework that trains SC-DiT from semantic (easy) to texture (hard) styles, and from clean to synthetic data, with Random Memory Rehearsal across stages to avoid catastrophic forgetting. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our Style-CCL achieves state-of-the-art performance in three core metrics: style similarity, content consistency, and aesthetic quality.

</details>


### [17] [MMLongEmbed: Benchmarking Multimodal Embedding Models in Long-Context Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14747)
*Haitian Wang,Ruoxi Sun,Quantong Qiu,Juntao Li,Junhui Li,Hua Chen,Jinxiong Chang,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces MMLongEmbed, the first comprehensive benchmark for evaluating Multimodal Embedding Models (MEMs) in long-context scenarios, finding that current models struggle with deep semantic understanding and exhibit varying robustness across modalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MEMs have expanded theoretical context windows, but there is no systematic evaluation of how well they actually comprehend and represent long-context multimodal inputs in practice, hindering real-world deployment.

Method: The authors introduce MMLongEmbed, a comprehensive benchmark with four retrieval tasks spanning multiple context-length ranges across text, document, and video modalities, and evaluate state-of-the-art MEMs on it.

Result: Current MEMs rely heavily on superficial feature matching and fail to capture deep semantic and structural dependencies; performance degradation varies systematically with context length and key information placement; models show different robustness to redundant contextual information across modalities.

Conclusion: Larger context windows do not guarantee effective long-context multimodal understanding, and there is a critical need for models that can better handle deep semantic dependencies in long multimodal inputs.

Abstract: Recent advancements have significantly expanded the theoretical context windows of Multimodal Embedding Models (MEMs). However, larger context windows do not necessarily translate into effective comprehension and representation of long-context multimodal inputs, which remains a critical bottleneck for real-world deployment. To address the lack of systematic evaluation in this setting, we introduce MMLongEmbed, the first comprehensive benchmark for evaluating MEMs in long-context scenarios. MMLongEmbed comprises four retrieval tasks spanning multiple context-length ranges, covering text, document, and video modalities. Through extensive evaluation of state-of-the-art models, we find that current architectures rely heavily on superficial feature matching and struggle to capture deep semantic and structural dependencies. We further observe that performance degradation varies systematically with context length and key information placement. Moreover, models exhibit substantially different robustness to redundant contextual information across modalities. For reproducibility, the benchmark and code are publicly available.

</details>


### [18] [Is My Vision-Language Data in Your AI? Membership Inference Test (MINT) Demo 2](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14748)
*Daniel DeAlcala,Gonzalo Mancera,Julian Fierrez,Aythami Morales,Ruben Tolosana,Ruben Vera-Rodriguez*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MINT Demo 2 is a framework for auditing whether specific data was used in ML model training, achieving up to 90% detection accuracy across image and text modalities, with a web platform for AI transparency and regulatory compliance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve transparency in machine learning training processes and experimentally determine if specific data was used during model training, addressing the need for AI accountability and regulatory compliance.

Method: Proposes multiple architectures (MINT, aMINT, gMINT) for membership inference depending on knowledge about the audited models, integrated into a comprehensive web platform supporting both image and text modalities.

Result: Achieves up to 90% accuracy in detecting training data across a face recognition model, 4 state-of-the-art LLMs, and multiple large-scale public image/text databases.

Conclusion: MINT provides a practical tool for promoting AI transparency and fostering compliance with emerging AI regulations.

Abstract: We present the Membership Inference Test (MINT) Demo 2, a framework designed to improve transparency in machine learning training processes. MINT is a technique for experimentally determining whether specific data were used during machine learning model training. We establish the theoretical framework and propose multiple architectures for MINT depending on the amount of information known about the models that are being audited. Experimental results using a popular face recognition model, 4 state-of-the-art LLMs, and multiple, diverse, and large-scale public image and text databases achieve promising accuracy levels in the detection of training data of up to 90%. Building on these results, we introduce a comprehensive web platform1 that expands these capabilities to image and text modalities. The platform integrates a diverse technological stack, including MINT, aMINT, and gMINT, allowing users to audit a wide range of models. This demonstrator aims to promote AI transparency and provides a practical tool to foster compliance with emerging AI regulations.

</details>


### [19] [Automated 3D Kinematic Monitoring for Circadian Activity and Anomaly Detection in Juvenile Fish](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14749)
*Chih-Wei Huang,Chang-Wen Huang,Chung-Ping Chiang,Tsung-Wei Pan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A high-throughput 3D behavioral phenotyping framework integrating deep learning and binocular stereo vision enables real-time monitoring, body length estimation, and 3D trajectory reconstruction of juvenile tilapia in high-density environments, establishing circadian locomotor baselines for early stress detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Precision aquaculture faces a "phenotyping bottleneck" in tracking high-resolution behavioral traits, as conventional methods cannot quantify instantaneous 3D physical exertion.

Method: The framework integrates deep learning object detection with binocular stereo vision for real-time monitoring, automates non-contact body length estimation, and reconstructs 3D swimming trajectories from absolute spatial coordinates.

Result: The system eliminates 2D perspective distortions, precisely quantifies 3D velocity and acceleration, and marks the first estimation of true physical swimming speeds in free-roaming juveniles. It successfully establishes circadian locomotor baselines.

Conclusion: The framework serves as an early warning system for physiological stress and provides an objective metric for fish vitality in precision aquaculture.

Abstract: Precision aquaculture faces a "phenotyping bottleneck" in tracking high-resolution behavioral traits, as conventional methods cannot quantify instantaneous three-dimensional (3D) physical exertion. To address this, we present a high-throughput 3D behavioral phenotyping framework integrating deep learning object detection with binocular stereo vision for real-time monitoring of juvenile tilapia in high-density environments. The system automates non-contact body length estimation and reconstructs 3D swimming trajectories from absolute spatial coordinates. By eliminating 2D perspective distortions, this approach precisely quantifies 3D velocity and acceleration, marking the first estimation of true physical swimming speeds in free-roaming juveniles. Results show the framework successfully establishes circadian locomotor baselines, serving as an early warning system for physiological stress and providing an objective metric for fish vitality.

</details>


### [20] [X-Tokenizer: A Multimodal Action Tokenizer for Vision-Language-Action Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14752)
*Xirui Kang,Yanpei Shi,Lucy Liang,Roy Gan,Dongxiu Liu,Pushi Zhang,Danpeng Chen,Xiaoyi Qin,Yinan Zheng,Jinliang Zheng,Hao Wang,Xianyuan Zhan,Hang Su*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: X-Tokenizer introduces Semantic Residual Quantization (SRQ) with Masked Action Modeling to transform action tokenization from mere compression into a semantic interface for VLA models, achieving significant improvements in multimodal grounding and long-horizon tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing action tokenizers discretize actions primarily for reconstruction, producing codes that preserve motion geometry but provide only weak semantic supervision, thus failing to bridge pretrained vision-language reasoning and precise robot control effectively.

Method: X-Tokenizer uses an encoder-SRQ-decoder architecture where SRQ imposes asymmetric residual quantization: the first level uses Masked Action Modeling (MAM) for coarse motion intent, deeper levels preserve fine-grained details. It also employs contrastive alignment with a foundation model and next-frame vision-language feature prediction during pretraining on 2.4M trajectories.

Result: X-Tokenizer achieves top real-world aggregate results and strong RoboTwin 2.0 simulation results, outperforming FAST by +13.5% in multimodal grounding and +8.25% in long-horizon tasks.

Conclusion: Action tokenizers should serve as semantic interfaces for VLA pretraining rather than just action compression tools, as demonstrated by X-Tokenizer's superior performance.

Abstract: Modern Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models must bridge pretrained vision-language reasoning and precise continuous robot control. Existing action tokenizers discretize actions primarily for reconstruction, producing codes that preserve motion geometry but provide only weak semantic supervision to the backbone. We therefore formulate action tokenization not as mere compression, but as semantic interface learning between multimodal reasoning and executable control. To this end, we introduce X-Tokenizer, a lightweight encoder-Semantic Residual Quantization (SRQ)-decoder architecture that provides a shared action interface across diverse robotic arm embodiments. Its key component, SRQ, imposes an asymmetric structure on residual vector quantization: the first level is trained with Masked Action Modeling (MAM) to form a discrete action language that captures coarse motion intent, while deeper levels remain reconstruction-oriented residuals that preserve fine-grained details. To further align action tokens with multimodal semantics, X-Tokenizer is pretrained with contrastive alignment to the representation space of a pretrained foundation model and with next-frame vision-language feature prediction. Pretrained on 2.4M trajectories (2.0B action frames), a single frozen X-Tokenizer plugs into a mixed discrete-continuous VLA as a representation-shaping supervision signal. X-Tokenizer achieves top real-world aggregate and strong RoboTwin 2.0 simulation results. Outperforming FAST in multimodal grounding (+13.5%) and long-horizon tasks (+8.25), it shows that action tokenizers serve as semantic interfaces for VLA pretraining beyond mere action compression.

</details>


### [21] [Beyond Self-Attention: Sub-Quadratic Vision Transformers for Fast Image Captioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14753)
*Chiradeep Ghosh,Dakshina Ranjan Kisku*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes an efficient image captioning model that replaces the standard quadratic self-attention in Vision Transformers with a Gaussian Mixture Model-based probabilistic clustering approach, reducing computational complexity from O(n²) to O(nK), and uses a GPT-based decoder for caption generation, showing competitive results on Flickr30K.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing transformer-based image captioning methods suffer from (1) lack of rich local feature representations and (2) high computational cost due to quadratic self-attention complexity O(n²).

Method: The standard self-attention in Vision Transformers is replaced with a GMM-based probabilistic transformer using EM algorithm for soft-clustering. Similar patches are grouped into K clusters, reducing attention complexity from O(n²) to O(nK). An autoregressive GPT-based decoder generates captions.

Result: Evaluated on the Flickr30K dataset, the model demonstrates competitive and significant improvement over existing works.

Conclusion: The proposed GMM-based clustering approach effectively improves computational efficiency while maintaining or improving captioning quality, offering a promising direction for efficient vision-language models.

Abstract: Image captioning is a challenging and significant task that aims to generate coherent and semantically meaningful textual descriptions for given images. To accomplish this task, it requires a deep understanding of visual content along with the ability to express that understanding in natural language. Despite remarkable progress with transformer-based architectures, existing approaches often suffer from limitations, such as a lack of rich local feature representations and the high computational cost of quadratic self-attention. The proposed model focuses on improving computational efficiency by restructuring the vision transformer architecture. In designing this approach, the standard self-attention mechanism in Vision Transformers is replaced with a probabilistic transformer approach based on a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM), a soft-clustering technique. Instead of computing pairwise attention among all image patches, the model groups similar patches into a fixed number of clusters using an Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm. This clustering-based mechanism reduces the computational complexity from quadratic O(n^2) to linear O(nK), where K << n. The autoregressive GPT-based decoder is used for caption generation. The model is evaluated on the Flickr 30K dataset, demonstrating competitive and significant improvement over existing works.

</details>


### [22] [Sub-Semantic Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14754)
*Aviad Cohen Zada,Nadav Orenstein,Shai Avidan,Gal Oren*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces DETECTURE, a method for sub-semantic image segmentation that uses language to describe stable appearance patterns (not objects), and presents the new TextureADE dataset for this task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing segmentation approaches fall into two categories: texture segmentation (based on visual cues) and semantic segmentation (based on object identity). The authors propose a new "sub-semantic" category that bridges the gap, where language describes appearance patterns rather than naming entire objects.

Method: DETECTURE couples a vision-language model with SAM 3, a promptable segmentation backbone. It addresses three failure modes: (1) language leakage between texture regions, (2) prompt competition inside the segmentation backbone, and (3) semantic distortion at the language-to-mask interface. A new dataset TextureADE is also derived from ADE20K.

Result: DETECTURE achieves the strongest performance on several datasets across different metrics compared to multiple baselines.

Conclusion: Sub-semantic segmentation is a viable new category, and DETECTURE effectively handles the identified failure modes, outperforming existing methods.

Abstract: Images can be segmented based on visual cues (i.e., texture segmentation) or into objects (i.e., semantic segmentation). We propose a new category of sub-semantic image segmentation that blurs the line between the two. In sub-semantic image segmentation, language is not used to name whole objects. Instead, it is used to partition an image into stable appearance patterns that can be described by language. To do that, we couple a general-purpose vision-language model to SAM 3, a promptable segmentation backbone whose native text pathway can ground rich descriptions into masks. Simple coupling fails for a number of reasons that we identify in the paper, and we overcome them by introducing DETECTURE that resolves three concrete failure modes -- language leakage between texture regions, prompt competition inside the segmentation backbone, and semantic distortion at the language-to-mask interface. Since there is no dataset of sub-semantic image segmentation, we introduce one, termed TextureADE. The new dataset is derived from the ADE20K dataset using a system we designed. We compare DETECTURE to a number of baselines and find that it achieves the strongest performance on several datasets using different metrics. Code is available at https://github.com/Scientific-Computing-Lab/TextureDetecture.

</details>


### [23] [Where Does Texture Evidence Live in SAM? Features, Proposal Masks, and Texture Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14755)
*Nadav Orenstein,Aviad Cohen Zada,Shai Avidan,Gal Oren*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Frozen SAM preserves texture evidence in multiscale features and proposal banks, but fails due to readout mismatch and commitment issues rather than texture blindness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Texture segmentation is challenging for foundation segmentation models like SAM, which default to object-centric partitions. This paper aims to diagnose whether SAM's failure on texture-defined regions stems from absent evidence, missing proposals, or incorrect readout.

Method: The authors study two frozen evidence spaces: (1) multiscale features with minimal clustering readout, and (2) automatic proposal bank with supervised consolidation readout. SAM is frozen throughout—no fine-tuning or retraining. Evaluated on RWTD, STLD, ADE20K refined-crop, and ControlNet-stitched PTD datasets.

Result: Frozen SAM is not a texture segmenter by default, but failures are not due to simple texture blindness. Coarse features preserve texture organization; proposal banks contain texture-aligned masks. Natural scenes need assembly over fragments, while synthetic cases reduce to selecting coherent proposals.

Conclusion: Default mask failure should be decomposed into representation evidence, proposal-bank support, readout mismatch, and commitment failure.

Abstract: Texture segmentation stresses foundation segmentation because meaningful regions are defined by material or repeated appearance rather than object identity. Segment Anything Models (SAMs) often fail by default on such texture-defined partitions, but this failure is ambiguous: the texture evidence may be absent, missing from the proposal bank, or present but selected or assembled incorrectly by an object-centric readout. We ask what texture-relevant evidence is already preserved in frozen SAM before adaptation. We study two frozen evidence spaces: multiscale features, probed with a minimal clustering readout, and the automatic proposal bank, treated as evidence for a supervised consolidation readout. SAM is frozen throughout; we do not fine-tune the backbone or retrain the proposal generator. Across RWTD, STLD, an ADE20K-selected refined-crop complement, and a ControlNet-stitched PTD bridge archive, frozen SAM is not a texture segmenter by default, but its failures are not simple texture blindness. Coarse frozen features preserve texture organization, and proposal banks often contain texture-aligned masks or fragments. Natural scenes more often require assembly and commitment over fragments, while cleaner synthetic cases more often reduce to selecting an already coherent proposal. Default mask failure should therefore be decomposed into representation evidence, proposal-bank support, readout mismatch, and commitment failure.

</details>


### [24] [Divide-and-Denoise: A Game-Theoretic Method for Fairly Composing Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14756)
*Abhi Gupta,Polina Barabanshchikova,Vikas Garg,Samuel Kaski,Tommi Jaakkola*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Divide-and-Denoise coordinates multiple pre-trained diffusion models by using a fair division game to allocate regions of the noisy sample to each model, preventing domination or disagreement during sampling, and outperforms baselines on conditional image generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Combining multiple pre-trained diffusion models risks one model dominating or models disagreeing with each other, and existing methods lack a principled way to coordinate them fairly.

Method: At each timestep, (i) update an allocation by solving a fair division game that divides the sample into regions maximizing total utility under fairness constraints, and (ii) guide each model to denoise within its assigned region.

Result: Outperforms baselines on conditional image generation across quality metrics including GenEval, resolving failures like missing objects and mismatched attributes.

Conclusion: Divide-and-Denoise effectively utilizes each model's expertise without neglecting any model, enabling fair and efficient composition of diffusion models.

Abstract: The abundance of pre-trained diffusion models provides an opportunity for composition. Combining several models, however, runs the risk of one model dominating or models disagreeing with each other. Here, we propose Divide-and-Denoise, a method for coordinating multiple pre-trained diffusion models during sampling. Much like managing a specialized workforce, our method creates a fair but efficient division of labor across models. Central to our method is the notion of an allocation which defines the responsibility of each model to every region of the noisy sample. At every timestep, we then denoise by (i) updating the allocation by solving a fair division game, where we divide the sample into regions that maximize total utility under fairness constraints, and (ii) aligning the models with this allocation, where we guide each model to denoise within its assigned region. This leads to a new composite denoising process that evolves in tandem with a division process. We evaluate Divide-and-Denoise on conditional image generation. Across several quality metrics, including the GenEval benchmark, our method outperforms baselines and resolves common failures including missing objects and mismatched attributes. Experiments show that Divide-and-Denoise utilizes each model's expertise without neglecting any other model.

</details>


### [25] [Spatial Priors via Space Filling Curves for Small and Limited Data Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14757)
*Leyla Naz Candogan,Arshia Afzal,Pol Puigdemont,Volkan Cevher*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VIOLIN introduces a lightweight masked attention mechanism using Space Filling Curves to inject spatial inductive bias into Vision Transformers, improving performance with negligible extra parameters (less than 0.0015%) and computational cost, especially in small models and limited data settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision Transformers lack explicit spatial inductive biases due to permutation equivariance, which becomes problematic when model capacity is small or training data is limited. Existing solutions are either computationally expensive or insufficient.

Method: VIOLIN scans images using multiple Space Filling Curves (SFCs) to construct curve-specific decay masks, which are combined and multiplied with the attention matrix, encoding spatial structure with less than 0.0015% extra parameters.

Result: Boosts accuracy by up to 8.7% on VTAB-1K fine-tuning tasks requiring spatial information, up to 7.2% on CIFAR-100 pixel-level training, and consistently improves DeiT/DINO ViT architectures on ImageNet-1K pretraining.

Conclusion: VIOLIN provides a computationally efficient yet effective method to inject spatial inductive bias into ViTs, particularly benefiting small models and limited data settings, and can be combined with PEFT methods like LoRA.

Abstract: Though Vision Transformers (ViTs) have become the dominant backbone in many computer vision tasks, due to permutation equivariance, their attention mechanism lacks explicit spatial inductive biases. This become particularly important in two settings: when model capacity is small or training data is limited. Inspired by the attention masking strategies in Linear Transformers and the scanning patterns of Vision SSMs, we introduce VIOLIN, a lightweight masked attention mechanism that encodes spatial structure within attention via Space Filling Curves (SFCs) with less than 0.0015% extra parameters and negligible computational overhead. VIOLIN scans the image using multiple SFCs to construct curve-specific decay masks, which are then combined and multiplied with the attention matrix. Across a wide range of evaluations, VIOLIN consistently improves performance. In limited data regimes such as fine-tuning on VTAB-1K, it boosts accuracy across all task groups and by up to 8.7% on the tasks where spatial information is essential. It can be combined with parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods such as LoRA to further increase the performance. Beyond fine-tuning, VIOLIN improves various small scale ViT architectures (e.g., DeiT, DINO) during pretraining on ImageNet-1K. Additionally, on pixel-level CIFAR-100 training, a task that is highly dependent on location information, VIOLIN increases accuracy by up to 7.2%. Overall, VIOLIN provides a computationally efficient yet effective way to inject spatial inductive bias into ViTs, especially benefiting small models and limited data settings.

</details>


### [26] [Disentangling Hallucinations: Orthogonal Semantic Projection for Robust Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14758)
*Emirhan Bilgiç,Baptiste Caramiaux,Zhi Yan,Gianni Franchi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper identifies "semantic hallucination" in XAI methods for Vision-Language Models, where attribution maps highlight wrong regions based on incorrect text prompts. The authors propose a unified theoretical framework (LSA) proving this is due to Linear Semantic Leakage in high-dimensional embedding spaces. They introduce OSP (Orthogonal Semantic Projection), a geometric intervention that orthogonalizes query vectors against distractor concepts to minimize hallucination while preserving fidelity for correct prompts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: XAI methods for VLMs suffer from semantic hallucination (e.g., highlighting a dog when prompted "cat"), yet a formal mathematical analysis of this problem is missing in the literature.

Method: The authors propose Linear Semantic Attribution (LSA), a unified theoretical framework generalizing across discriminative methods. They introduce OSP (Orthogonal Semantic Projection), a geometric intervention using the residual property of OMP to disentangle unique semantic signals from shared concepts.

Result: Theoretically proven and empirically demonstrated that OSP minimizes hallucination by orthogonalizing the query vector against distractor concepts, rendering the attribution model blind to shared features while preserving fidelity for correct prompts.

Conclusion: Semantic hallucination is a fundamental consequence of Linear Semantic Leakage in high-dimensional embedding spaces, not specific to a single architecture. OSP effectively addresses this issue.

Abstract: As Vision-Language Models are increasingly deployed in safety-critical applications, the trustworthiness of their explanations becomes crucial. Explainable AI (XAI) methods for Vision-Language Models often suffer from semantic hallucination, where attribution maps highlight prominent image regions even when prompted with incorrect text descriptions (e.g., highlighting a dog when prompted ``cat''). Although this problem is widespread, a formal mathematical analysis of XAI methods and CLIP embeddings is largely missing in the literature. We demonstrate that this phenomenon is not specific to a single architecture but is a fundamental consequence of Linear Semantic Leakage in high-dimensional embedding spaces. We propose a unified theoretical framework, Linear Semantic Attribution (LSA), which generalizes across discriminative methods. We introduce OSP, a geometric intervention that utilizes the residual property of OMP to disentangle unique semantic signals from shared concepts. We prove theoretically and demonstrate empirically that OSP minimizes hallucination by orthogonalizing the query vector against distractor concepts, rendering the attribution model blind to shared features while preserving fidelity for correct prompts. Our code is available at: https://github.com/emirhanbilgic/Orthogonal-Semantic-Projection

</details>


### [27] [Temporally Consistent and Controllable Video Generation of 2D Cine CMR via Latent Space Motion Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14759)
*Yiheng Cao,Gustavo Andrade-Miranda,Jiatian Zhang,Guillaume Sallé,Xin Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a text-to-video generative method for synthesizing temporally coherent and anatomically consistent cardiac cine MRI sequences, using a fine-tuned diffusion model for initial frame generation and a latent flow model for cardiac motion, achieving strong fidelity (FID 31.68, CLIP 31.04).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Public cardiac MRI datasets are scarce, limiting the development of advanced data-driven models for cardiac function assessment.

Method: A text-to-video framework that decouples spatial structure (fine-tuned diffusion model generates initial frame from text prompt) from temporal motion (latent flow model conditioned on cardiac phase embedding generates complete cardiac motion).

Result: Achieves FID of 31.68 for image realism and CLIP score of 31.04 for text-image alignment, with high temporal coherence and anatomical/pathological diversity.

Conclusion: The method shows potential for producing high-fidelity, on-demand medical data, offering a scalable solution to data scarcity in cardiac imaging.

Abstract: Cine cardiac magnetic resonance is the gold standard for assessing cardiac function, but the scarcity of public datasets limits the development of advanced data-driven models. To address this limitation, we propose a generative method for synthesizing temporally coherent and anatomically consistent cardiac sequences. Our text-to-video framework decouples cardiac spatial structure from temporal motion. First, a fine-tuned diffusion model synthesizes an initial frame from a clinical text prompt, controlling anatomical features. Then, a latent flow model conditioned on a cardiac phase embedding generates the complete cardiac motion, ensuring spatial consistency and temporal control. Our model generates anatomically and pathologically diverse sequences with high temporal coherence and strong fidelity to input prompts, achieving a FID of 31.68 for image realism and a CLIP score of 31.04 for text-image alignment. These experimental results highlight its potential to produce high-fidelity, on-demand medical data, offering a scalable solution to data scarcity.

</details>


### [28] [GeoRoPE: Ground-Aware Rotary Adaptation for Remote Sensing Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14760)
*Yu Luo,Kun Hu,Mengwei He,Xiaogang Zhu,Shan Zeng,Allen Benter,Wei Xiang,Patrick Filippi,Thomas Francis Bishop,Zhiyong Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeoRoPE introduces ground-aware positional calibration (GCC for cross-GSD scaling, GFC for scene-dependent frequency adjustment) to adapt remote-sensing foundation models across varying sensor resolutions and spatial granularities, improving cross-resolution robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Remote-sensing foundation models face scale mismatch during downstream adaptation because (1) fixed token-grid offsets represent different ground distances across sensors with different GSDs, making positional priors physically inconsistent, and (2) heterogeneous spatial granularity means different regions (urban vs. homogeneous landscapes) require different positional sensitivities even under the same GSD.

Method: GeoRoPE has two components: (1) Geo-Coordinate Calibration (GCC) rescales raw token-grid offsets by ground distance per token step to produce geo-calibrated relative coordinates across GSDs; (2) Geo-Frequency Calibration (GFC) adjusts native RoPE frequency with a relation-specific factor for scene-dependent spatial granularity. It is injected via a lightweight adapter, preserving frozen spatial priors while adding geo-aware corrections.

Result: Experiments across multiple RSFMs, sensors, resolutions, and downstream tasks show that GeoRoPE improves cross-resolution robustness and scale-sensitive representation learning.

Conclusion: GeoRoPE is an effective, parameter-efficient, and RoPE-compatible method for spatial adaptation in remote-sensing foundation models, addressing cross-resolution and scale-sensitive challenges without full fine-tuning.

Abstract: Remote-sensing foundation models (RSFMs) benefit from pretraining on imagery from multiple sensors and ground sampling distances (GSDs), but such exposure alone does not resolve scale mismatch during downstream adaptation. A fixed token-grid offset can correspond to different ground distances across sensors, making grid-based positional priors physically inconsistent. Meanwhile, heterogeneous spatial granularity means that compact urban regions and homogeneous landscapes may require different positional sensitivities even under the same GSD. Therefore, we propose {GeoRoPE}, a ground-aware, RoPE-compatible, and parameter-efficient spatial adaptation method for RSFMs. GeoRoPE recalibrates token-level positional interactions from two complementary aspects. First, \textit{Geo-Coordinate Calibration (GCC)} rescales raw token-grid offsets according to the ground distance represented by one token-grid step, producing geo-calibrated relative coordinates across GSDs. Second, \textit{Geo-Frequency Calibration (GFC)} adjusts the native RoPE frequency with a relation-specific factor, enabling position sensitive adaptation to scene-dependent spatial granularity. GeoRoPE is injected into pretrained RSFMs through a lightweight adapter, preserving the frozen spatial prior while adding geo-aware positional corrections.
