Experiments across multiple RSFMs, sensors, resolutions, and downstream tasks demonstrate that GeoRoPE improves cross-resolution robustness and scale-sensitive representation learning.

</details>


### [29] [Scribby: A Multi-Level LLM Framework for Semantic Video Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14762)
*Julian Abelarde,Hugo Garrido-Lestache Belinchon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes an LLM-based video summarization framework that combines macro-level understanding (full transcript analysis) with micro-level semantic analysis (sentence-level evaluation and grouping by semantic similarity) to produce more structured and detailed video analyses than existing high-level summary approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI-based video summarization tools provide only coarse, high-level overviews from transcripts and lack detailed analysis of video structure, thematic progression, and semantic relationships, which are needed for comprehensive understanding of long-form educational and entertainment content.

Method: The framework operates in stages: (1) full transcript analysis for macro-level comprehension, (2) individual sentence-level analysis, and (3) grouping sentences by semantic similarity using an LLM as a judge. Context is preserved by feeding global transcript analysis and adjacent sentence information into each evaluation prompt.

Result: The framework enables visualization of semantic chunking and relevance-based heatmaps for video analysis tools. Limitations and future expansions are discussed, though quantitative results are not detailed in the abstract.

Conclusion: The proposed LLM-based framework offers a foundation for more detailed, structured video analysis that bridges the gap between coarse summaries and fine-grained semantic understanding, with potential applications in educational and entertainment video domains.

Abstract: As video content continues to expand across educational platforms, recorded lectures, and live-streamed entertainment, the need for efficient and structured analysis of long-form footage has increased \cite{1}. Although many existing AI programs provide high-level video summaries based on AI-generated transcripts \cite{2,3,4,5}, these approaches are often limited to coarse overviews and lack detailed analysis of a video's structure, thematic progression, and semantic relationships, all of which are required for comprehensive video analysis.
  This paper proposes an LLM-based video summarization framework that balances macro-level comprehension with micro-level semantic analysis \cite{6,12,13}. The first stage of the process indexes the video at a micro level by (1) analyzing the full transcript, (2) analyzing individual transcript sentences, and (3) grouping these sentences by semantic similarity using an LLM as a judge \cite{6,13}. Contextual continuity is retained during sentence-level processing by incorporating both the global transcript analysis and adjacent sentence information into each evaluation prompt.
  This framework establishes a foundation for video analysis tools that visualize semantic chunking and semantic matching through relevance-based heatmaps. Limitations and future expansions of the framework are also discussed.

</details>


### [30] [Avoiding Exponential Blow-Up in Distributive Lattice Submodular Minimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14764)
*Ishant Shanu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new framework for minimizing submodular functions on distributive lattices that works directly within the lattice space, avoiding the costly transformation to boolean lattice which exponentially expands the working space, resulting in significant runtime improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for submodular function minimization on distributive lattices require transforming the function to a boolean lattice, which exponentially expands the working space (by an exponential number of elements in set size), making the optimization system too inefficient.

Method: A generic framework that works directly within the distributive lattice, allowing existing submodular function minimization algorithms designed for boolean lattices to be applied without the inefficient space-expanding transformation.

Result: Experiments show huge improvement in running time compared to traditional methods for handling distributive lattice.

Conclusion: The proposed framework efficiently handles submodular function minimization on distributive lattices by avoiding the boolean lattice transformation, enabling the use of established algorithms while achieving substantial runtime improvements.

Abstract: Submodular function minimization has gained a lot of interest in recent years. They are highly applicable in the area of Computer Vision and Machine Learning. Often such applications require to work with submodular functions defined on distributive lattice. Current best way of dealing with it is using a transformation which extrapolates the submodular function for the respective boolean lattice. It makes optimization system too inefficient due to enlargement of the working space. Quantitatively, the expanded space has additional exponential (in set size) number of elements. We propose a generic framework for dealing with distributive lattice which only works within distributive lattice. Our framework allows one to use already established submodular function minimization algorithms for boolean lattice. In our experiment, we show the huge improvement in terms of running time over tranditional methods for handling distributive lattice.

</details>


### [31] [Momentum-Guided Semantic Forecasting (MoFore) for Self-Supervised Video Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14765)
*Qinwu Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MoForMoFo proposes Momentum-Guided Semantic Forecasting (MoFo), a self-supervised video representation learning method that predicts future latent embeddings from temporally distant context clips, combining predictive forecasting with contrastive regularization to learn temporally consistent and semantically meaningful representations without reconstruction objectives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing self-supervised video representation learning methods rely on pixel-level reconstruction (MAE, VideoMAE) or contrastive alignment (CLIP), which either recover masked content or align embedding spaces. These approaches may not explicitly learn temporally predictive representations that capture long-range motion dynamics, motivating a forecasting-based framework.

Method: MoFo framework introduces: (1) predictive latent forecasting from temporally distant context clips to future embeddings, (2) randomized temporal-gap forecasting during training for robustness across temporal scales, and (3) combination of latent forecasting with contrastive regularization to ensure temporal consistency and prevent representation collapse.

Result: Experiments on UCF101 demonstrate: (1) temporally consistent and semantically meaningful video representations without action labels, (2) strong temporal stability and emergent category-level structure in the embedding space, (3) motion-aware organization in qualitative retrieval experiments.

Conclusion: Long-range latent forecasting is an effective and computationally efficient self-supervised video representation learning approach, eliminating the need for reconstruction-based objectives while achieving strong temporal and semantic properties.

Abstract: Self-supervised video representation learning has recently advanced through contrastive learning, masked reconstruction, and predictive representation learning. Reconstruction-based approaches such as MAE and VideoMAE learn representations by recovering masked visual content \cite{he2022mae,tong2022videomae}, while contrastive methods such as CLIP learn semantically meaningful embedding spaces through representation alignment \cite{radford2021clip}.
  In this work, we introduce a Momentum-Guided Semantic Forecasting framework (MoFore) for self-supervised video representation learning. Instead of optimizing for pixel-level reconstruction or task-specific semantic alignment, the proposed method learns temporally predictive video representations by forecasting future latent embeddings from temporally distant context clips. To improve robustness across temporal scales, we further introduce randomized temporal-gap forecasting during training. The framework combines predictive latent forecasting with contrastive regularization to encourage temporal consistency while preventing representation collapse.
  Experiments on the UCF101 dataset demonstrate that the proposed framework learns temporally consistent and semantically meaningful video representations without using action labels during training. Quantitative analysis shows strong temporal stability and emergent category-level structure in the learned embedding space, while qualitative retrieval experiments reveal motion-aware organization across related activities. Overall, the results suggest that long-range latent forecasting provides an effective and computationally efficient approach for self-supervised video representation learning without relying on reconstruction-based objectives.

</details>


### [32] [XMedFusion: A Knowledge-Guided Multimodal Perception and Reasoning Framework for Autonomous Medical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14766)
*Hamza Riaz,Arham Haroon,Maha Baig,Muhammad Dawood Rizwan,Muhammad Naseer Bajwa,Muhammad Moazam Fraz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: XMedFusion is a modular AI framework that improves radiology report generation by decomposing visual information into coordinated agents (visual perception, knowledge graph construction, retrieval-guided drafting, and synthesis) to achieve stronger visual grounding, interpretability, and diagnostic accuracy, outperforming baseline vision-language models on chest radiograph benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing end-to-end multimodal models for radiology report generation suffer from weak visual grounding, leading to unreliable interpretations and omission of subtle clinical findings, which limits their reliability for autonomous medical and robotic systems.

Method: XMedFusion is a modular AI framework with four components: (1) a visual perception agent extracting image-grounded evidence, (2) a knowledge graph construction agent structuring clinically relevant findings, (3) a retrieval-guided drafting agent ensuring consistent reporting structure, and (4) a synthesis agent that iteratively integrates visual and structured evidence via reasoning-driven verification.

Result: On a public chest radiograph dataset, XMedFusion achieved significant improvements over baseline vision-language models: BLEU-1 (+0.0493 to 0.3359), ROUGE-L (+0.0863 to 0.2440), METEOR (+0.0829 to 0.1708), Consistency (2.38 to 7.80), and Accuracy (2.34 to 6.93).

Conclusion: The structured multi-agent perception and reasoning approach effectively enhances robustness, transparency, and automation in intelligent medical imaging systems, enabling integration into autonomous healthcare and robotic diagnostic workflows.

Abstract: Autonomous medical and robotic systems increasingly rely on intelligent perception and reasoning capabilities to interpret visual data and support clinical decision making. Radiology report generation represents a critical component of such automated diagnostic workflows, yet existing end-to-end multimodal models often suffer from weak visual grounding, resulting in unreliable interpretations and omission of subtle clinical findings. This paper presents XMedFusion, a modular AI framework designed as an intelligent perception and reasoning module for autonomous medical systems. The proposed framework decomposes visual information into coordinated functional components that emulate expert-driven analysis, including a visual perception agent that extracts image-grounded evidence, a knowledge graph construction agent that structures clinically relevant findings, and a retrieval-guided drafting process that ensures a consistent reporting structure. A synthesis agent iteratively integrates visual and structured evidence through reasoning-driven verification to produce reliable and interpretable diagnostic outputs. Experimental evaluation on a public chest radiograph dataset demonstrates significant improvements over baseline vision-language models, achieving gains from 0.0493 to 0.3359 in BLEU-1, 0.0863 to 0.2440 in ROUGE-L, and 0.0829 to 0.1708 in METEOR, along with substantial improvements in semantic evaluation metrics such as Consistency (2.38 to 7.80) and Accuracy (2.34 to 6.93). The results highlight the effectiveness of structured multi-agent perception and reasoning for enhancing robustness, transparency, and automation in intelligent medical imaging systems, enabling integration into autonomous healthcare and robotic diagnostic workflows.

</details>


### [33] [An Empirical Analysis of Optimization Dynamics and Sparsity Boundaries in Large-Scale Pedestrian Attribute Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14770)
*Houssam El Mir*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper addresses extreme class imbalance in Pedestrian Attribute Recognition (PAR) by applying Multi-Label Focal Loss on a ResNet-18 backbone. The proposed configuration (alpha=0.50, gamma=2.0) achieves 62.32% Macro F1-score, matching BCE baseline while offering superior hard-example mining. The paper identifies the "Sparsity Wall" where positive samples below 0.1% require instance-level intervention beyond global loss reweighting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extreme class imbalance in PAR datasets (e.g., PETA and PA-100K) causes minority attributes with <1% positive samples to be suppressed during standard BCE optimization, a problem termed the "majority negative class cheating trap."

Method: Systematic ablation of Multi-Label Focal Loss hyperparameters (alpha and gamma) on a ResNet-18 backbone, using pure loss-function engineering with zero computational overhead.

Result: Calibrated configuration (alpha=0.50, gamma=2.0) achieves Macro F1-score of 62.32%, matching BCE baseline while preserving superior hard-example mining and convergence dynamics.

Conclusion: Identifies the "Sparsity Wall" where positive sample fractions below 0.1% make global loss reweighting ineffective, requiring instance-level intervention for further improvement.

Abstract: Pedestrian Attribute Recognition (PAR) is critical for video surveillance, enabling forensic search and re-identification systems. Extreme class imbalance remains a fundamental obstacle when merging PETA and PA-100K into a 109,000-image composite corpus, where minority attributes have positive sample fractions below 1%. This causes standard BCE optimization to suppress rare traits, a phenomenon we term the majority negative class cheating trap. We present a systematic ablation of Multi-Label Focal Loss hyperparameters (alpha and gamma) on a ResNet-18 backbone. A calibrated configuration (alpha=0.50, gamma=2.0) achieves a Macro F1-score of 62.32%, matching BCE baseline while preserving superior hard-example mining and convergence dynamics. Our approach uses pure loss-function engineering with zero computational overhead for edge deployment. We identify the Sparsity Wall, a hard boundary where positive sample fractions below 0.1% make global loss reweighting ineffective, requiring instance-level intervention.

</details>


### [34] [ScoutVLA: UAV-Centric Active Perception via a Dual-Expert VLA Model for Open-World Embodied Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14772)
*Wenhao Lu,Zhengqiu Zhu,Xiaofeng Wang,Xiaoran Zhang,Yatai Ji,Yong Zhao,Yue Hu,Yingzhen Nie,Jinlong Zhu,Zheng Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FG-EQA is a fine-grained active perception benchmark for aerial EQA with 40K+ simulated and 1K real trajectories. The authors propose ScoutVLA, a Vision-Language-Action model inspired by bee waggle dance, featuring a decoupled dual-expert architecture for evidence-driven viewpoint refinement. It achieves 10.48× higher strict success rate and 7.72× higher QA correctness over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing outdoor EQA systems stop once the target enters the UAV's field of view, failing to perform fine-grained viewpoint adjustments needed for evidence-seeking questions. This gap leaves reasoning about fine-grained details unresolved.

Method: ScoutVLA uses a decoupled dual-expert architecture: (1) a vision-language expert infers semantic intent to identify missing evidence, and (2) an independent action expert uses high-DoF flow matching to generate continuous viewpoint-refinement trajectories. A decoupled training strategy with knowledge insulation prevents action gradients from erasing multimodal reasoning ability.

Result: 10.48× higher average strict success rate and 7.72× higher average QA correctness compared to state-of-the-art baselines in simulation, with qualitative validation in real-world field study.

Conclusion: The bio-inspired (bee waggle dance) decoupled dual-expert approach effectively addresses fine-grained viewpoint adjustment for evidence-seeking questions in outdoor aerial EQA, significantly outperforming prior methods.

Abstract: Aerial Embodied Question Answering (EQA) requires Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) to actively perceive the environment and answer natural language questions. Existing outdoor EQA systems usually stop once the target enters the UAV's field of view, leaving the fine-grained viewpoint adjustment needed for evidence-seeking questions largely unresolved. To address this issue, we introduce FG-EQA, a fine-grained active perception EQA benchmark with more than 40K simulated trajectories and 1K real-world trajectories. Drawing inspiration from the ``waggle dance'' of scout bees, which iteratively adjust their flight paths to verify target information, we propose ScoutVLA, an evidence-driven Vision-Language-Action model for outdoor EQA. To emulate this active exploration behavior, ScoutVLA features a decoupled dual-expert architecture: a vision-language expert infers the semantic intent to identify missing evidence, while an independent action expert employs high-DoF flow matching to generate continuous viewpoint-refinement trajectories. To balance the competing demands of continuous control and semantic reasoning, we devise a decoupled training strategy with a knowledge insulation mechanism that prevents the action gradients from erasing the model's multimodal reasoning ability. Extensive simulated experiments and a qualitative real-world field study both verify the superiority of ScoutVLA over the state-of-the-art baselines, demonstrating a 10.48$\boldsymbol{\times}$ higher average strict success rate and a 7.72$\boldsymbol{\times}$ higher average QA correctness.

</details>


### [35] [Double-Helix Vision (DH-V2): A Geometry-Based Visual Sampler for Bandwidth-Constrained Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14773)
*Jinwen Wen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Double-Helix Vision is a geometry-based visual sampler that compresses images into 1D signals using golden-ratio spiral trajectories, achieving 1,433x compression at 4K and running perception pipelines in 0.52 ms on CPU without neural networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable ultra-efficient visual perception that avoids heavy neural network computation, using biologically-inspired foveated sampling to compress images while preserving geometric structure for real-time robotics applications.

Method: Two phase-shifted golden-ratio spirals (Alpha and Beta, offset by 180°) sample images with center-biased density. The pipeline includes spatial mapping, temporal collision detection, and intra-frame structural disparity estimation, all CPU-based.

Result: 1,433x compression at 4K (99.93% reduction), 0.52 ms pipeline at 1080p on CPU, +6.03% accuracy gain over uniform random sampling on CIFAR-10 at K=128, 2.7 KB serialized JSON reports.

Conclusion: DH provides an extremely lightweight, geometry-driven alternative to neural dense perception, suitable for resource-constrained robotics with sub-millisecond latency and minimal data overhead.

Abstract: We present Double-Helix Vision (DH), a geometry-based visual sampler that compresses 2D images into compact 1D signals using paired golden-ratio-inspired spiral trajectories. Rather than processing every pixel uniformly, DH employs two phase-shifted helices (Alpha and Beta, offset by 180 degrees) to sample the image with biologically-inspired foveation: high density at the center, sparse coverage at the periphery. At 4K resolution, DH achieves a 1,433x compression ratio (99.93% reduction) while preserving the geometric structure of the scene. The full perception pipeline -- including spatial mapping, temporal collision detection, and intra-frame structural disparity estimation -- runs in 0.52 ms at 1080p on CPU-only hardware, with no neural network dependencies. On CIFAR-10 at extreme sampling budgets (K=128 points per helix), DH achieves a +6.03% accuracy gain over uniform random sampling. A JSON-serializable Robotics API is provided, delivering sub-millisecond spatial perception reports in 2.7 KB packets. Code and benchmarks are available under the MIT License.

</details>


### [36] [JoyAI-VL-Interaction: Real-Time Vision-Language Interaction Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14777)
*Dingyu Yao,Junhao Zhou,Chenxu Yang,Chuanyu Qin,Haowen Hou,Zheming Liang,Congcong Wang,Yuhang Cao,Shenglong Ye,Shuai Xie,Shuhuan Gu,Haoyang Huang,Qingyi Si,Nan Duan,Jiaqi Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces JoyAI-VL-Interaction, an 8B-scale open-source vision-first interaction model that continuously watches real-time video, decides autonomously when to speak or stay silent, and delegates difficult tasks to a background model—outperforming Doubao and Gemini across six real-world scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current large models are turn-based (question-answer) even when deployed in seemingly interactive settings like video calls. The authors argue for a paradigm shift: models should be "present in the world," continuously observing, autonomously deciding when to speak, and interacting in real time without waiting for user prompts.

Method: The authors build an 8B-scale vision-first VL-interaction model (JoyAI-VL-Interaction) that internally decides each second to stay silent, respond, or delegate to a background model. They release the full training recipe, data, and a deployable system with pluggable ASR/TTS, memory, UI, and a background brain that connects to any API or agent.

Result: Across six real-world scenarios, human raters preferred JoyAI-VL-Interaction over Doubao and Gemini's in-app video-call assistants by a wide margin. Emergent capabilities appeared without explicit training, such as guiding a shopper through app screens or improvising a lecture from slides.

Conclusion: This is the first fully open-source vision-driven interaction model released with its training recipe, data, and complete deployable system, advancing a new paradigm of always-on, autonomously interactive AI.

Abstract: Many moments in the real world do not wait for a user to ask. A fire starts on a security monitor, an expression flickers across a video call, or a product a viewer wants flashes by in a livestream. Yet today's large models remain mostly turn-based by design: they answer only when addressed, and even video-call apps that appear interactive still operate as question-answer systems, reacting only when polled or prompted. We argue for a different paradigm: a model that is present in the world like a person. It continuously watches what is happening now, decides on its own whether to speak or stay silent, interacts in real time, and delegates to a background model when the problem is hard. To advance interaction models and their adoption across domains, we make two fully open-sourced contributions. First, we release JoyAI-VL-Interaction, an 8B-scale, vision-first VL-interaction model. The model makes the response decision internally, choosing each second to stay silent, respond, or delegate to a background model, and it excels at vision-triggered responsiveness and time awareness. We pair it with a transferable training recipe, from which capabilities we never trained for emerge, such as guiding a shopper through changing app screens or improvising a lecture from a slide deck. Second, we release a complete, deployable system built around that model. The system streams any ongoing video into the model, making it genuinely present in the world. All other components are pluggable, including ASR/TTS modules, memory, visualization UI, and a background brain that can connect to any API or agent. Across six real-world scenarios, human raters prefer JoyAI-VL-Interaction over the in-app video-call assistants of Doubao and Gemini by a wide margin. To our knowledge, this is the first open, vision-driven interaction model released together with its training recipe, data, and complete deployable system.

</details>


### [37] [FactCheck: Feasibility-aware Long-term Action Anticipation with Multi-agent Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14778)
*Rui Cao,Jiannong Cao,Bo Yuan,Zhiyuan Wen,Mingjin Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FactCheck is a multi-agent framework that improves long-term action anticipation by using a closed-loop "Observe-Plan-Verify" mechanism, where specialized agents (Observer, Planner, Verifier) check action feasibility against physical environment constraints, outperforming prior methods on EPIC-Kitchens-55 and EGTEA Gaze+.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing open-loop methods for long-term action anticipation hallucinate non-existent objects, violate object affordances, or disregard object states because they lack explicit verification mechanisms against the physical environment.

Method: FactCheck decomposes LTA into three specialized roles: Observer (recognizes history, builds dual-form memory with History Action Abstract and History Action Graph), Planner (generates draft future actions), and Verifier (validates drafts against the History Action Graph and refines infeasible actions), forming a closed-loop Observe-Plan-Verify cycle.

Result: FactCheck consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on EPIC-Kitchens-55 and EGTEA Gaze+ benchmarks.

Conclusion: This work establishes a new paradigm for feasibility-aware long-term action anticipation, effectively closing the loop of action recognition, action prediction, and action verification.

Abstract: Long-term action anticipation (LTA) aims to predict an ordered sequence of future verb-noun actions from a partially observed video. While this task serves as the foundation for embodied intelligence, anticipating physically feasible long-term actions remains a critical challenge. Existing methods, which operate in an open-loop manner, often hallucinate non-existent objects, violate object affordances, or disregard object states, as they lack explicit mechanisms to verify action feasibility against the physical environment. To address this, we propose FactCheck, a novel multi-agent collaboration framework that improves feasibility through a closed-loop "Observe-Plan-Verify" mechanism. FactCheck decomposes the complex LTA task into specialized roles: an Observer that recognizes historical actions from video observations and constructs a dual-form structured memory, comprising a History Action Abstract that captures high-level human intentions and environmental status, and a History Action Graph that encodes object states and temporal dependencies; a Planner that generates draft future actions conditioned on both low-level historical actions and high-level History Action Abstract; and a Verifier that rigorously validates the draft against the History Action Graph and refines infeasible actions. Extensive experiments on the EPIC-Kitchens-55 and EGTEA Gaze+ benchmarks demonstrate that FactCheck consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Our work establishes a new paradigm for feasibility-aware long-term action anticipation, effectively closing the loop of action recognition, action prediction and action verification.

</details>


### [38] [YTClickbait21K: Human-Annotated Multimodal Dataset for YouTube Clickbait Detection Across Diverse Channels and Content Categories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14780)
*Md. Minhazul Islam,Md. Tanbeer Jubaer,Amith Khandakar,Shovon Sarker,Sumaiya Rahman,Md. Masum Mia,Mohamed Arselene Ayari,Hamed Noori*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces YTClickbait21K, a large-scale multimodal YouTube clickbait dataset with 21,238 videos, rigorous human annotation (k=0.65 agreement), and diverse content categories, aimed at advancing automated clickbait detection and cross-modal understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clickbait on video platforms threatens information reliability, but progress in automated detection is limited by the lack of large-scale, high-quality multimodal datasets.

Method: The authors built YTClickbait21K with 21,238 YouTube videos from 40 channels across 29 countries, covering news, entertainment, education, and gaming. Each sample includes metadata, engagement stats, and thumbnails. Three annotators labeled each video using a standardized decision framework (textual, visual, cross-modal cues), with final labels via majority voting.

Result: Substantial inter-annotator agreement (Cohen's kappa = 0.65), confirming reliable labeling despite clickbait detection subjectivity.

Conclusion: YTClickbait21K provides a robust benchmark for multimodal clickbait detection, cross-modal semantic understanding, and automated content moderation systems.

Abstract: Clickbait content on video-sharing platforms poses a significant challenge to information reliability, yet progress in automated detection has been constrained by the lack of large-scale, high-quality multimodal datasets. We present YTClickbait21K, a human-annotated YouTube clickbait dataset comprising 21,238 videos collected from 40 channels across 29 countries, covering diverse content categories such as news, entertainment, education, and gaming. Each sample includes structured metadata (title, description, engagement statistics) along with associated thumbnail images, enabling comprehensive multimodal analysis. To ensure annotation quality, every video was independently labeled by three annotators using a standardized decision framework that incorporates textual, visual, and cross-modal consistency cues, with final labels determined through majority voting. The dataset exhibits substantial inter-annotator agreement (k=0.65), confirming reliable labeling despite the inherent subjectivity of clickbait detection. By combining scale, annotation rigor, and multimodal richness, this dataset provides a robust benchmark for developing and evaluating machine learning models, facilitating research in cross-modal semantic understanding, and advancing automated content moderation systems.

</details>


### [39] [Variational Deep Unfolding with Mamba-Based Nonlocal Modeling for Underwater Image Enhancement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14781)
*Daniel Torres,Julia Navarro,Catalina Sbert,Joan Duran*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A model-based deep unfolding network integrating variational modeling, Mamba layers, and a proximal trajectory loss for underwater image enhancement, achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Underwater images suffer from poor visibility and color distortion. Existing methods need better integration of physical priors and learnable architectures to address these degradation issues effectively.

Method: 1) Variational deep unfolding framework with dehazing decomposition; 2) Multiplicative residual component for artifact absorption; 3) Nonlocal gradient-type constraint for structure preservation and edge sharpness; 4) Mamba layers for capturing self-similarities; 5) Proximal trajectory loss for enforcing consistency between unfolding stages and ideal restoration iterations.

Result: Improved visual quality and competitive quantitative performance compared to recent state-of-the-art underwater image enhancement methods. Theoretical existence of solution for the minimization problem is also established.

Conclusion: The proposed variational unfolding approach with Mamba layers and proximal trajectory loss effectively enhances underwater images, outperforming existing methods. Source code is publicly available.

Abstract: Underwater imaging plays a crucial role in ocean engineering, although captured data often suffer from poor visibility and color distortion. To address these challenges, we propose a model-based deep unfolding network for underwater image enhancement that integrates variational modeling into a learnable architecture. The framework is guided by a variational formulation based on a dehazing decomposition, incorporating a multiplicative residual component to absorb remaining artifacts and a nonlocal gradient-type constraint to preserve structural details and enhance edge sharpness. We provide a theoretical analysis establishing the existence of solution for the associated minimization problem. The proposed unfolding method incorporates Mamba layers to efficiently capture self-similarities in the scene. In addition, we introduce a proximal trajectory loss that enforces consistency between the unfolding stages and the iterations of an ideal restoration regularizer. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed unfolding approach achieves improved visual quality and competitive quantitative performance compared with recent state-of-the-art methods. The source code will be available at https://github.com/MIA-UIB/Variational-Unfolding-Mamba-Underwater-Enhancement .

</details>


### [40] [Last But Not Least: Boundary Attention CalibratiON for Multimodal KV Cache Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14782)
*Tianhao Chen,Yuheng Wu,Kelu Yao,Xiaogang Xu,Xiaobin Hu,Dongman Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BACON improves KV cache compression for MLLMs by calibrating observation window attention with last-query evidence and suppressing noise, achieving 7.5% average improvement (up to 30.9%) under aggressive budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing compression methods relying on observation window attention can dilute sparse visual evidence and discard answer-critical tokens, while last-query attention contains answer-irrelevant noise. BACON aims to recover critical tokens by combining both sources.

Method: BACON calibrates observation window attention using last-query evidence and suppresses isolated noise through intra-layer coherence and inter-layer persistence. It is a plug-and-play method compatible with various models and compression methods.

Result: BACON improves multimodal KV compression by 7.5% on average under the most aggressive budget, with gains up to 30.9%, across diverse benchmarks, models, budgets, and compression methods.

Conclusion: Last-query attention is a complementary source for recovering answer-critical tokens missed by observation window attention, and BACON effectively calibrates both sources while suppressing noise for better KV cache compression in MLLMs.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) achieve strong vision-language reasoning, but long visual contexts enlarge the KV cache and increase decoding latency. Existing compression methods rely on observation window attention for stable token-importance estimation, yet this aggregation can dilute sparse visual evidence and discard answer-critical tokens under aggressive compression. Therefore, we identify last-query attention as a complementary source for recovering such evidence, but its answer-irrelevant signals can mislead retention. We propose BACON, a plug-and-play method that calibrates observation window attention with last-query evidence and suppresses isolated noise via intra-layer coherence and inter-layer persistence. Across diverse benchmarks, models, budgets, and compression methods, BACON improves multimodal KV compression by 7.5% on average under the most aggressive budget, with gains up to 30.9%.

</details>


### [41] [The Vision Encoder as a Privacy Boundary: Visual-Token Side Channels in Encoder-Free Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14783)
*Chenyu Zhou,Qiliang Jiang,Shuning Wu,Xu Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Encoder-free VLMs expose a privacy side channel through visual tokens that can be inverted to recover readable text and images, unlike encoder-based models, with the key factor being spatial sampling density.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify and demonstrate that encoder-free VLMs, which route image patches directly into the language token stream, create a new architectural privacy attack surface through intermediate visual tokens acting as a side channel.

Method: Token-access adversaries decode visual-token streams from encoder-free VLMs (Gemma4, Fuyu) to recover images and text; compared against encoder-based controls; within-model ablations vary spatial sampling fidelity; evaluated on KV cache inversion, realistic document degradation, and zero-shot transfer.

Result: Encoder-free VLMs leak recognizable image structure and readable held-out access codes; encoder-based models only localize regions without string recovery. The key factor is spatial sampling density, not token count. KV cache tensors are also invertible. Attack survives clutter, degradation, zero-shot transfer, and resists noise/quantization defenses.

Conclusion: Removing the vision encoder is a first-class privacy decision in VLM deployment; effective mitigation requires reducing spatial sampling density rather than post-hoc defenses.

Abstract: A vision encoder compresses image pixels into semantic embeddings, implicitly acting as a privacy boundary by preserving semantic content while attenuating pixel-local detail required for exact text recovery. Encoder-free vision-language models (VLMs) remove this boundary by routing image patches directly into the language-model token stream, thereby exposing an architectural privacy attack surface: intermediate visual tokens become a pre-output side channel. Under a token-access adversary, decoders invert visual-token streams from two encoder-free VLMs, Gemma4 and Fuyu, recovering recognizable image structure and readable held-out access codes, whereas matched encoder-based controls localize target regions but recover no exact strings. Within-model ablations show that the operative factor is spatial sampling fidelity of the visual-token grid, especially character-direction sampling density, rather than token or value count. The leakage is not limited to exported tokens: Gemma4 layer-0 key-value cache tensors are directly invertible, placing the side channel within KV caches commonly persisted by production serving stacks for decoding efficiency. The attack survives clutter, realistic document degradation, and zero-shot transfer to public document images, and it resists value-level defenses such as additive noise and quantization. Effective mitigation must therefore reduce spatial sampling, making removal of the vision encoder a first-class privacy decision in VLM deployment.

</details>


### [42] [Vision-Encoder Behavioral Fingerprints of Image-to-Image Generative Models: A Training-Paradigm-Driven Taxonomy of Six Commercial APIs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14787)
*Hunter Hill*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Six production image-to-image AI systems are tested under adversarial perturbation, revealing two distinct behavioral clusters based on edit-trained vs. T2I-base models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically evaluate and compare the robustness and behavioral consistency of production image-to-image AI systems under adversarial perturbations.

Method: Six image-to-image AI systems are tested using a content-adaptive sub-JND adversarial perturbation pipeline, with outputs scored by frozen DINOv2 ViT-B/14 token distances against clean references across a 3,588-call corpus.

Result: The six systems partition into two image-invariant behavioral bands on a 2D (patch_mean, ssim_clean) plane: edit-trained models cluster in a tight band, while T2I-base models adapted at sampling time cluster in a drift band.

Conclusion: Image-to-image AI systems exhibit distinct behavioral clusters depending on whether they are edit-trained or T2I-base models, suggesting fundamental differences in how perturbation robustness manifests across architectures.

Abstract: We study six production image-to-image AI systems (gpt-image-1, Gemini 2.5 Flash Image, Flux Kontext, SDXL img2img, SD3 img2img, and Qwen Image Edit) under a content-adaptive sub-JND adversarial perturbation pipeline, scoring all outputs by frozen DINOv2 ViT-B/14 token distances against clean references. Across a 3,588-call corpus spanning COCO photographs, CelebA-HQ portraits, and AI-generated inputs, the six systems partition into two image-invariant behavioral bands on a 2D (patch_mean, ssim_clean) plane: edit-trained models (Flux Kontext, Qwen Edit, Gemini) cluster in a tight band, while T2I-base models adapted at sampling time (SDXL, SD3, gpt-image-1) cluster in a drift band.

</details>


### [43] [Efficient Reinforcement for Visual-Textual Thinking with Discrete Diffusion Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14792)
*Yoonjeon Kim,Yuhta Takida,Chieh-Hsin Lai,Eunho Yang,Yuki Mitsufuji*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper shows that multimodal discrete diffusion models can replace autoregressive models for RL-based post-training in interleaved visual-textual reasoning, achieving 26.9% less rollout computation. They also propose factorized reward assignment to address cross-modal interference, yielding 38.04% improvement over the base model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL-based post-training for interleaved multimodal reasoning relies on autoregressive unified models, which require full image regeneration during visual reasoning—a computationally expensive process. The authors aim to find a more efficient alternative.

Method: The authors (1) apply multimodal discrete diffusion models as an alternative to AR models for RL-based interleaved reasoning, leveraging localized visual editing for efficient rollouts; (2) identify cross-modal interference in joint reward assignment; (3) propose factorized reward assignment that assigns rewards independently to text and vision segments.

Result: The diffusion-based approach reduces rollout computation during GRPO by 26.9% vs AR baselines with minimal performance drop. Factorized reward assignment achieves 11.2% improvement over joint reward assignment and 38.04% improvement over the base model.

Conclusion: Multimodal discrete diffusion models are effective and efficient alternatives to AR models for RL-based interleaved visual-textual reasoning, and factorized reward assignment effectively mitigates cross-modal interference.

Abstract: RL-based post-training has been widely adopted to enable interleaved visual and textual reasoning in unified multimodal models capable of both text and image generation. However, most existing approaches are built upon autoregressive (AR) unified models, which require full image regeneration during visual reasoning. In this work, we demonstrate that multimodal discrete diffusion models are effective alternatives to AR models for reinforcement learning in interleaved reasoning, owing to their ability to perform efficient visual rollouts via localized visual editing rather than full image-token regeneration. This reduces rollout computation during GRPO by 26.9\% compared to AR baselines, with minimal performance drop. Despite the improved efficiency, we find that joint reward assignment, which employs a shared reward signal across modalities, introduces cross-modal interference between unrelated image and text token sequences during RL updates. To address this issue, we propose factorized reward assignment, a strategy that assigns rewards independently to text and vision segments. With factorized reward assignment, our RL approach achieves an 11.2% improvement over joint reward assignment and a 38.04% improvement over the base model.

</details>


### [44] [Position: The Systemic Lack of Agency in Visual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14795)
*Yizhao Huang,Haoyang Chen,Shiqin Wang,Pohsun Huang,Jiayuan Li,Haoyuan Du,Yandong Shi,Zheng Wang,Zhixiang Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper identifies that VLMs lack Agency in implicit reasoning—they perform passive semantic retrieval well but fail at active, self-directed visual exploration. The authors introduce V-IRD, a benchmark to measure this gap, and show that strong recognition doesn't imply active reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLMs treat visual reasoning as passive semantic retrieval, lacking the autonomous visual exploration needed for implicit reasoning. Existing benchmarks fail to measure this capacity.

Method: The authors propose V-IRD (Visual Implicit Reasoning Diagnosing Benchmark), requiring models to derive answers strictly through autonomous visual analysis rather than relying on explicitly specified targets.

Result: Prominent VLMs exhibit strong retrieval abilities but struggle to utilize reference objects and attend to visual evidence requiring self-directed inquiry. Strong semantic recognition does not equate to active visual exploration.

Conclusion: There is a critical gap in current VLMs: they lack the Agency needed for active, situated visual reasoning. This dimension remains largely unmeasured and unaddressed.

Abstract: This paper argues that a systemic lack of Agency constrains the implicit reasoning capabilities of current Vision-Language Models (VLMs). Implicit reasoning refers to the ability to autonomously discover and utilize hidden visual evidence to bridge information gaps, rather than merely relying on explicitly specified targets. This capacity underlies human visual understanding and everyday reasoning. We argue that this limitation arises from a tendency to approach visual reasoning primarily as passive semantic retrieval, rather than as active, situated reasoning that depends on autonomous visual exploration. As a result, most existing benchmarks primarily assess Passive Capacity, leaving this aspect of reasoning largely unmeasured. To address this gap, we introduce the Visual Implicit Reasoning Diagnosing Benchmark (V-IRD), which targets this missing quadrant by requiring models to derive answers strictly through autonomous visual analysis. Our results show that, despite strong retrieval abilities, prominent VLMs struggle to utilize reference objects and to attend to visual evidence that requires self-directed inquiry. Simply put, strong semantic recognition does not equate to active visual exploration, revealing a critical gap in current VLMs. More information can be found at https://haoychen.github.io/Implicit-Reasoning/

</details>


### [45] [HSQ-VLM: A Novel Spatially-Constrained Quadrant Segmentation VLM Model for Explainability in Diabetic Retinopathy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14803)
*Shivum Telang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HSQ-VLM is a novel quadrant segmentation pipeline for fundus images that uses Landmark-Anchored Cartesian Cross-Attention and 4-quadrant Topological Latent Partitioning to enable explainable, anatomically-precise DR diagnosis with high lesion detection sensitivity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current DR diagnostic AI models are black-box systems that lack explainability—they cannot detail the exact anatomical landmarks and lesion distributions leading to clinical decisions, hindering clinical adoption.

Method: HSQ-VLM uses Landmark-Anchored Cartesian Cross-Attention with 4-quadrant Topological Latent Partitioning (TLP) aligned to a fovea-centered coordinate system, enabling Vision-Language Models to generate structured reports with anatomical precision.

Result: On 3,500 fundus images: 99.6% sensitivity for hemorrhages, 96.4% for microaneurysms, and significant reduction in boundary-ambiguity errors vs. baselines.

Conclusion: HSQ-VLM achieves high diagnostic accuracy while providing clinically explainable outputs with precise anatomical localization, addressing the black-box limitation in DR diagnostic AI.

Abstract: Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) is an aggressive retinal disease and a leading cause of global blindness, yet its clinical management is currently hindered by the black-box nature of diagnostic AI. While deep learning models achieve high classification accuracy, there is a critical lack of explainability methods capable of detailing the exact anatomical landmarks and lesion distributions that lead to a clinical decision for DR. Therefore, we propose HSQ-VLM, a novel quadrant segmentation pipeline on fundus images that utilizes a Landmark-Anchored Cartesian Cross-Attention mechanism to unify visual feature extraction with structured clinical reasoning. Unlike traditional methods that rely on arbitrary image partitioning, our pipeline implements 4-quadrant Topological Latent Partitioning (TLP) to dynamically align retinal features with a fovea-centered coordinate system. This allows the Vision-Language Model to generate natural language reports that quantify pathology with anatomical precision. On a dataset of 3,500 high-resolution fundus images, this innovative methodology achieved a lesion detection sensitivity of 99.6% for hemorrhages and 96.4% for microaneurysms, while demonstrating a significant reduction in boundary-ambiguity errors compared to standard segmentation baselines.

</details>


### [46] [S23DR 2026: End-to-End 3D Wireframe Prediction via DETR-Style Set Prediction with Contrastive Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14811)
*Nitiz Khanal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WireframeDETR directly predicts 3D building wireframes as sets of edge coordinate pairs from multi-view point clouds using DETR-style set prediction with three key contributions: contrastive denoising training, multi-scale encoder aggregation, and progressive auxiliary loss weighting, achieving 0.575 HSS on the S23DR 2026 Challenge test set.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the Structured Semantic 3D Reconstruction (S23DR) 2026 Challenge, which requires predicting 3D building wireframes from multi-view COLMAP point clouds, without relying on intermediate vertex detection stages.

Method: WireframeDETR applies DETR-style set prediction directly to 3D point clouds, producing wireframes as sets of edge coordinate pairs. Three contributions: (1) contrastive denoising training to stabilize early Hungarian matching; (2) multi-scale encoder aggregating last encoder layer outputs via learned scalar weights; (3) progressive auxiliary loss weighting to focus gradient signals on decoder layers that benefit most.

Result: Public test HSS of 0.575 (F1=0.664, IoU=0.516), best validation HSS of 0.534 on the cleaned val split.

Conclusion: WireframeDETR demonstrates that direct set prediction for 3D wireframe reconstruction is effective, with contrastive denoising and progressive weighting being key to training stability and performance.

Abstract: We present WireframeDETR, our submission to the Structured Semantic 3D Reconstruction (S23DR) 2026 Challenge, which requires predicting a 3D building wireframe from multi-view COLMAP point clouds. Our method applies DETR-style set prediction directly to 3D point clouds, producing wireframes as sets of edge coordinate pairs without any intermediate vertex detection stage. We introduce three technical contributions: (1) contrastive denoising training that stabilises noisy Hungarian matching in early epochs; (2) a multi-scale encoder that aggregates the last encoder layer outputs via learned scalar weights; and (3) progressive auxiliary loss weighting that concentrates gradient signal on the decoder layers that most benefit from it. Our model achieves a public test HSS of 0.575 (F1~=~0.664, IoU~=~0.516) and a best validation HSS of 0.534 on the cleaned val split.

</details>


### [47] [Multi-HMR 2: Multi-Person Camera-Centric Human Detection, Mesh Recovery and Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14841)
*Guénolé Fiche,Philippe Weinzaepfel,Romain Brégier,Fabien Baradel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Multi-HMR 2 is a DETR-based framework that achieves state-of-the-art human mesh recovery with improved metric 3D localization and detection accuracy, without requiring ground-truth cameras or video supervision, by predicting scene-consistent cameras and distilling SAM2 features for tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing HMR methods focus on pelvis-centered recovery and ignore metric 3D localization and detection accuracy in camera coordinate systems, limiting real-world applications like human-robot interaction.

Method: A DETR-based framework that predicts scene-consistent cameras alongside human meshes and distills SAM2 image-based memory features for tracking without video supervision.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art pelvis-centered performance while substantially improving detection accuracy and metric 3D localization.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates that camera-space perception with metric 3D localization can be achieved without handcrafted components, fixed camera assumptions, or ground-truth intrinsics.

Abstract: Most advances in human mesh recovery (HMR) have focused on pelvis-centered recovery, overlooking metric 3D localization and detection accuracy in the camera coordinate system - two key factors for real-world applications such as human-robot interaction and social scene understanding. Current evaluation protocols often ignore these aspects, emphasizing per-person, root-centered recovery rather than camera-space perception. As a result, existing approaches rely on fixed camera assumptions or handcrafted post-processing, limiting their robustness and practical deployment. We introduce Multi-HMR 2, a simple yet robust DETR-based framework for Multi-person Camera-centric Human detection, mesh Recovery, and tracking. Multi-HMR 2 predicts a scene-consistent camera together with human meshes, enabling metric 3D localization without ground-truth intrinsics. Moreover, by distilling image-based memory features from SAM2, Multi-HMR 2 extends to tracking, achieving consistent identity association without video supervision. Despite its conceptual simplicity - no handcrafted components, no video input, and no ground-truth cameras - Multi-HMR 2 achieves state-of-the-art pelvis-centered performance while substantially improving detection accuracy and metric 3D localization.

</details>


### [48] [An Ensemble Deep Learning Approach for Reliable and Scalable Lemon Leaf Disease Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14871)
*Shayan Abrar,Sudeepta Mandal,Abdul Awal Yasir,Sonjoy Bhattacharjee,Sadman Haque Bhuiyan,Samanta Ghosh,Rafi Ahamed*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper uses an ensemble of InceptionV3 and MobileNetV2 with adversarial training to detect lemon leaf diseases, achieving 99.27% accuracy on a 9-class dataset (1354 images), with Grad-CAM used for interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early detection of plant diseases is critical to prevent reduced yield, quality loss, and increased susceptibility to other stresses for plants and farmers.

Method: Two pretrained models (InceptionV3 and MobileNetV2) were applied and combined via ensemble learning on a preprocessed lemon leaf dataset (1354 images, 9 classes, 70/15/15 split). Adversarial training was used to improve robustness, and Grad-CAM provided visual explanations.

Result: The ensemble model achieved 99.27% accuracy.

Conclusion: The ensemble approach with adversarial training and Grad-CAM visualization provides robust and interpretable plant disease detection, reliable even under noisy data conditions.

Abstract: Early detection of plant diseases is crucial to plants and for the farmers. Plant diseases reduce fruit yield and quality, and plants are more susceptible to other stresses when they are infected. The lemon leaf disease dataset contains 1354 images. The dataset has 9 classes. Among the 9 classes only one class is for healthy leaf, and the other 8 classes are leaf diseases. The dataset was split into training (70%), testing (15%) and validation (15%) sets after comprehensive preprocessing. Two pretrained models (InceptionV3 and MobileNetV2) were applied and then combined these models using an ensemble technique to boost robustness. Ensemble models showed a promising performance of 99.27% accuracy. Adversarial Training is applied to improve models' ability and ensure reliable predictions under noisy data. Grad-CAM visualization highlights the important regions of leaf images that validate the model prediction with confidence level.

</details>


### [49] [Understanding Cross-Modal Contributions in Continual Vision-Language Models: A Theoretical Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14883)
*Salimeh Sekeh,Mary Wisell*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a theoretical framework for understanding cross-modal (vision-language) contributions in continual learning settings, showing how modality-specific roles shift across task sequences and offering empirical validation on large VLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current continual learning approaches for VLMs focus on mitigating catastrophic forgetting but lack theoretical understanding of how vision and language modalities individually contribute across sequential tasks/environments.

Method: The authors propose a new theoretical perspective to analyze cross-modal contributions across consecutive environments, then empirically validate their framework on large vision-language models.

Result: The framework effectively captures environment-level cross-modal contributions, demonstrates robustness to varying task orders and inter-task similarities, and improves generalization performance.

Conclusion: Understanding modality-specific roles throughout a task sequence provides deeper insights into continual VLMs and leads to more robust and generalizable models.

Abstract: Continual vision-language models are commonly addressed through sequential fine-tuning; however, although this paradigm enables adaptation to new environments (tasks), it inherently emphasizes the contribution of previously learned environments (tasks) at the expense of the stability required to preserve previously acquired knowledge. While existing approaches have adequately studied continual learning and catastrophic forgetting in vision-language models (VLMs), the theoretical understanding of modality-specific contributions across a sequence of environments remains largely unexplored. In this paper, we present a new theoretical perspective to understand the cross-modal (vision-language) contributions to consecutive environments. We empirically evaluate our theoretical findings on large VLMs and demonstrate their effectiveness in capturing environment-level cross-modal contributions. Our analysis provides deeper insights into continual VLMs, highlighting their contribution robustness to varying task orders and inter-task similarities, and their improved generalization performance.

</details>


### [50] [Improved Knowledge Distillation for Land-Use Image Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14886)
*Arundhuti Sur,Abhiroop Chatterjee,Susmita Ghosh,Emmett Ientilucci*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes an improved Knowledge Distillation framework using VGG16 as a teacher and MobileNetV2 as a student, combining KL divergence and cosine similarity losses for land-use classification, achieving 99.04% accuracy with efficient compression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve competitive classification accuracy while reducing computational complexity for land-use image classification tasks by compressing deep CNNs without significant performance loss.

Method: A teacher-student KD framework where VGG16 transfers knowledge to lightweight MobileNetV2, integrating hard supervision (ground truth labels) with soft supervision combining KL divergence and Cosine Similarity losses.

Result: Achieves 99.04% accuracy on three land-use datasets, outperforming baseline student training and single-loss distillation approaches while maintaining substantial model compression.

Conclusion: The proposed multi-loss KD framework effectively balances accuracy and compression, demonstrating superior performance for land-use classification compared to existing methods.

Abstract: In the present article, an improved Knowledge Distillation (KD) framework has been proposed for efficient compression of deep convolutional neural networks for land-use image classification task. Motivated by the need to achieve competitive classification accuracy while reducing computational complexity, a teacher-student learning paradigm is adopted in which a VGG16 network transfers knowledge to a lightweight MobileNetV2 model. The proposed framework integrates hard supervision from ground truth labels with a soft supervision strategy that combines Kullback-Leibler divergence and Cosine Similarity losses. Experiments conducted on three land-use datasets show that the proposed KD-based method yields improved performance, and achieves an accuracy of 99.04%, outperforming both baseline student training and single-loss distillation approaches, while retaining substantial model compression.

</details>


### [51] [Deep Learning in Seismic Interpretation: Federated Advances in Salt Dome Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14905)
*Muhammad Zain Mehdi,Muhammad Zaid,Owais Aleem*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FedSaltNet is a federated learning framework for privacy-preserving salt-dome segmentation that uses a lightweight U-Net backbone and a novel Foreground-Weighted aggregation strategy, achieving 4%IoU improvement over conventional FL methods and demonstrating that architectural simplicity outperforms high-capacity models in data-constrained federated environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated salt segmentation is critical for hydrocarbon exploration and drilling safety, but existing deep learning approaches face severe limitations due to data sovereignty concerns, dataset bias, and scarcity of labeled seismic volumes.

Method: The framework combines a lightweight Small U-Net backbone with a novel Foreground-Weighted (FG-WEIGHTED) aggregation strategy to handle domain-specific class imbalance, evaluated under non-IID conditions across four seismic datasets (TGS, SEAM, F3, GBS).

Result: FG-WEIGHTED achieved 4.0% relative improvement in IoU over the best conventional FL method; the simple U-Net outperformed ResNet-18 U-Net by 166% in average IoU under federated settings.

Conclusion: FedSaltNet establishes the viability of federated deep learning for collaborative subsurface interpretation by providing a validated, high-performance solution that preserves privacy while overcoming data heterogeneity challenges.

Abstract: Salt-dome delineation is a critical, high-impact task in subsurface geological interpretation, driving decisions in hydrocarbon exploration, reservoir modeling, and drilling safety. While convolutional encoder-decoder architectures have delivered significant improvements in automated salt segmentation, their widespread application is severely limited by data sovereignty concerns, dataset bias, and the scarcity of labeled seismic volumes. This paper introduces FedSaltNet, a Federated Learning (FL) framework explicitly engineered for robust, generalizable, and privacy preserving salt-dome segmentation. We couple a lightweight Small U-Net backbone, chosen for its efficiency and regularization properties with a novel Foreground-Weighted (FG-WEIGHTED) aggregation strategy designed to tackle domain-specific class imbalance. Through an extensive comparative study emulating non-IID conditions across four diverse seismic datasets (TGS, SEAM, F3, GBS), we demonstrate two critical findings: The FG-WEIGHTED algorithm effectively mitigates data heterogeneity, yielding a 4.0% relative improvement in Intersection over Union (IoU) over the best conventional FL method. The simple U-Net architecture proved essential, outperforming the higher capacity ResNet-18 U-Net variant by 166% in average IoU, underscoring the necessity of architectural simplicity in data-constrained federated environments. FedSaltNet provides a validated, high-performance solution that establishes the viability of federated deep learning for collaborative, next-generation subsurface interpretation.

</details>


### [52] [Mask Proposal Voting Based on Geodesic Framework for Robust Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14912)
*Li Liu,Mingzhu Wang,Zhenjiang Li,Da Chen,Laurent D. Cohen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a mask proposal voting framework for minimal path-based segmentation that overcomes initialization sensitivity. It introduces adaptive domain cuts for generating diverse mask proposals and a novel voting scheme with priors to produce accurate, robust segmentations in complex scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Minimal path-based segmentation methods are highly sensitive to model initialization, limiting their practical application in cluttered backgrounds, complex intensity variations, and challenging topology appearances.

Method: (1) Efficient adaptive domain cuts for initializing region-based min-cut evolution to generate diverse mask proposals. (2) A mask voting scheme that incorporates priors to assign different importance weights to individual masks, building a voting score map for final segmentation.

Result: The proposed method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art minimal path-based approaches in both accuracy and robustness, with insensitivity to initialization.

Conclusion: The mask proposal voting framework effectively addresses initialization sensitivity in minimal path segmentation, enabling robust and accurate segmentation in complex scenarios.

Abstract: Despite great advances, finding accurate segmentation remains a challenging task, especially in scenarios with cluttered backgrounds, complex intensity variations and topology appearance. Minimal path models have exhibited their strong ability in addressing image segmentation tasks. However, the performance of minimal paths-based segmentation approaches is heavily influenced by model initialization, hence limiting their application scope in practice. In this work, we propose a novel mask proposal voting framework that overcomes the major drawback of classical approaches, allowing robust segmentation even in complicated scenarios. Firstly, we introduce an efficient method for constructing adaptive domain cuts as a constraint for initializing the region-based min-cut evolution, by which diverse and reliable mask proposal candidates can be generated, substantially increasing the possibility of accurately covering the objective region by these proposals. Secondly, we propose a new mask voting scheme to build a voting score map encoding the final segmentation information. In contrast to classical path voting methods, our model allows incorporating priors to assign different importance to each individual mask. As a consequence, the proposed segmentation model is capable of accurately delineating object boundaries under complex scenarios, and is insensitive to initialization. Experiments demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art minimal path-based approaches in both accuracy and robustness.

</details>


### [53] [FlexPooling with Simple Auxiliary Classifiers in Deep Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14926)
*Muhammad Ali,Omar Alsuwaidi,Salman Khan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes FlexPooling, an adaptive pooling method that learns weighted averages over activations, combined with Simple Auxiliary Classifiers (SAC), achieving 1-3% accuracy improvement over standard pooling methods in image classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard pooling methods (max, average, strided convolution) are lossy and may discard important information. The authors aim to preserve more prominent information from activations to improve network discriminability.

Method: FlexPooling: an adaptive pooling method that generalizes average pooling by learning a weighted average over activations jointly with the rest of the network. Additionally, Simple Auxiliary Classifiers (SAC) are attached to the CNN to further boost performance.

Result: Experiments on multiple popular image classification datasets show FlexPooling consistently outperforms baseline networks, achieving approximately 1-3% improvement in accuracy.

Conclusion: FlexPooling with SAC is a simple yet effective alternative to standard pooling methods, demonstrating consistent accuracy gains across different datasets.

Abstract: In computer vision, the basic pipeline of most convolutional neural networks consists of multiple feature extraction layers, where the input signal is downsampled to a lower resolution in each subsequent layer. This downsampling process is commonly referred to as pooling, which is an essential operation in CNNs. Pooling improves robustness against transformations, reduces the number of trainable parameters, increases the receptive field, and lowers computation time. Since pooling is a lossy process but remains important for extracting high-level information from low-level representations, it is important to preserve the most prominent information from previous activations to improve network discriminability. Standard pooling is usually performed using dense pooling methods, such as max pooling or average pooling, or through strided convolutional kernels. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective adaptive pooling method, called FlexPooling, which generalizes average pooling by learning a weighted average over activations jointly with the rest of the network. We further show that attaching Simple Auxiliary Classifiers (SAC) to the CNN improves performance and demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed method compared with standard pooling methods. Experiments on multiple popular image classification datasets show that FlexPooling consistently outperforms baseline networks, achieving approximately 1 to 3 percent improvement in accuracy.

</details>


### [54] [Learning Sparse Latent Predictive Foundation Model for Multimodal Neuroimaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14957)
*Haoxu Huang,Long Chen,Jingyun Chen,Jinu Hyun,James Ryan Loftus,Kara Melmed,Daniel Orringer,Jennifer Frontera,Seena Dehkharghani,Arjun Masurkar,Narges Razavian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Neuro-JEPA is a sparse multimodal neuroimaging foundation model using latent predictive objectives and Mixture-of-Experts to learn unified representations across T1w, T2w, and FLAIR MRI sequences. Pretrained on 1.55M+ scans, it outperforms existing models across 47 tasks from 3 health systems and 12 public datasets, demonstrating consistent gains over simple CNN baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods lack the ability to learn unified representations across multiple MRI contrast mechanisms at health-system scale, and current neuroimaging foundation models show inconsistent improvements over simple baselines.

Method: Introduced Neuro-JEPA, which combines a latent predictive objective with a Mixture-of-Experts architecture, pretrained on 1,551,862 scans across T1w, T2w, and FLAIR sequences. Includes systematic study of architectural, masking, objective, and sparsity design choices.

Result: Evaluated on 25 tasks from 3 health systems and 22 tasks from 12 public datasets. Neuro-JEPA achieved stronger and more consistent performance than existing neuroimaging foundation models across all settings, while competitors showed inconsistent gains over a simple CNN baseline.

Conclusion: Established a scalable methodological framework for multimodal neuroimaging representation learning. Highlights the need for evaluation protocols that include simple baselines, clinically heterogeneous cohorts, and controlled multimodal comparisons.

Abstract: Brain MRIs are routinely acquired as multiple complementary sequences with unique contrast weighting, including T1-weighed imaging (T1w) anatomic and fluid-sensitive T2-weighted (T2w) contrasts. However, methods for learning unified representations across the multitude of MRI contrast mechanisms at health-system scale are lacking. In this study, we introduce Neuro-JEPA, a sparse multimodal neuroimaging foundation model that combines a latent predictive objective with a Mixture-of-Experts architecture to encode brain MRI across core T1w, T2w, and fluid-suppressed FLAIR imaging (FLAIR). We further provide a systematic methodological study of architectural, masking, objective, and sparsity design choices beneficial for robust neuroimaging multimodal representation learning. Neuro-JEPA was pretrained on 1,551,862 scans from 428,647 studies after modality-specific preprocessing with data curation across three core structural brain MRI sequences. We evaluated the learned representations across clinical and research settings, including 25 tasks from three health systems: NYU Langone, NYU Long Island, and Massachusetts General Hospital, and 22 tasks from 12 public datasets, covering unimodal, multimodal and cross-domain evaluation configurations. Across these benchmarks, existing neuroimaging foundation models showed inconsistent gains over a simple convolutional neural network (CNN) baseline, whereas Neuro-JEPA achieved stronger and more consistent performance across all evaluated settings. These results establish a scalable methodological framework for multimodal neuroimaging representation learning and highlight the need for foundation model evaluation protocols that include simple baselines, clinically heterogeneous cohorts and controlled multimodal comparisons.

</details>


### [55] [MVEB: Massive Video Embedding Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14958)
*Adnan El Assadi,Roman Solomatin,Isaac Chung,Chenghao Xiao,Deep Shah,Manan Dey,Shriya Sudhakar,Zacharie Bugaud,Wissam Siblini,Ayush Sunil Munot,Yashwanth Devavarapu,Rakshitha Ireddi,Michelle Yang,Márton Kardos,Niklas Muennighoff,Kenneth Enevoldsen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces MVEB, a 23-task benchmark for video embeddings, evaluates 33 models finding no single dominant model, and reveals that audio's contribution depends on dataset annotation provenance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a comprehensive, standardized benchmark for evaluating video embeddings across diverse tasks (classification, retrieval, QA, etc.) and to systematically understand model capabilities and the role of audio modality.

Method: MVEB is a 23-task benchmark distilled from a 184-task pool (MVEB+). It spans classification, zero-shot classification, clustering, pair classification, retrieval, and video QA. 33 models were evaluated, including MLLM-based embeddings, multimodal binding models, and generative MLLMs.

Result: No single model dominates; MLLM-based embeddings excel in classification, clustering, pair classification, and QA; multimodal binding leads in retrieval and zero-shot classification; generative MLLMs without contrastive adaptation fail on cross-modal tasks. Audio's contribution depends on annotation provenance: helpful when labels come from both modalities, harmful (6-point gap) when labels come from visuals alone.

Conclusion: MVEB provides a unified, task-diverse benchmark integrated into the MTEB ecosystem, enabling fair comparison across video embedding models and revealing important insights about modality interaction in multimodal embeddings.

Abstract: We introduce the Massive Video Embedding Benchmark (MVEB), a 23-task benchmark for video embeddings spanning classification, zero-shot classification, clustering, pair classification, retrieval, and video-centric question answering. We evaluate 33 models and find that no single model dominates: MLLM-based embeddings lead on classification, clustering, pair classification, and QA; multimodal binding leads on retrieval and zero-shot classification; generative MLLMs without contrastive adaptation collapse on cross-modal tasks. Paired video-only vs. audio+video evaluations show that audio's contribution depends on dataset annotation provenance: audio helps when labels were produced from both modalities and hurts when they were produced from visuals alone, a six-point gap consistent across model families. MVEB is derived from MVEB+, a 184-task pool, and is designed to maintain task diversity while reducing evaluation cost. It integrates into the MTEB ecosystem for unified evaluation across text, image, audio, and video. We release MVEB and all 184 tasks along with code and a leaderboard at https://github.com/embeddings-benchmark/mteb.

</details>


### [56] [Multi-Modal Attention for Automated Disaster Damage Assessment Using Remote Sensing Imagery and Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14963)
*Tewodros Syum Gebre,Jagrati Talreja,Leila Hashemi-Beni*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a deep learning framework using pre- and post-disaster satellite imagery with a multi-modal attention mechanism and lightweight ConvNeXT-Tiny backbone to automate building damage classification into four levels, achieving 94.90% accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional disaster damage assessment methods relying on manual inspections or sparse data are slow and error-prone, necessitating an automated, accurate, and scalable solution for timely emergency response and resource allocation.

Method: The framework uses pre- and post-disaster satellite imagery, a lightweight ConvNeXT-Tiny backbone, and a novel multi-modal cross-attention mechanism to fuse bi-temporal features. It includes an optimized preprocessing pipeline and robust data augmentation techniques for large-scale datasets.

Result: Experiments on a large-scale disaster dataset show an overall classification accuracy of 94.90%, with the model effectively discriminating between four damage categories (no damage, minor damage, major damage, destroyed) and remaining resilient to incomplete data.

Conclusion: The system significantly improves assessment speed and accuracy for emergency responders, advancing automated disaster damage detection through multi-temporal imagery and deep learning, and offering a scalable solution for real-time response.

Abstract: Timely and accurate disaster damage assessment is crucial for effective emergency response, resource allocation, and recovery. Traditional methods, which often rely on manual inspections or sparse data, are typically slow and error-prone. This paper introduces a novel framework leveraging remote sensing imagery and deep learning to automate building damage classification. Using pre- and post-disaster satellite imagery, our model categorizes buildings into four damage levels: no damage, minor damage, major damage, and destroyed.
