Gemini Pro demonstrated the strongest spatial localisation according to three of the four metrics, whereas GPT-4o produced the closest distributional match to human attention as measured by KL divergence.
  These findings suggest that large vision-language models can identify regions that broadly correspond to where humans direct visual attention in safety-relevant scenes without requiring eye-tracking training data. The results highlight the potential of vision-language models as a scalable tool for approximating human attentional patterns.

</details>


### [82] [Show the Signal, Hide the Noise: Spectral Forcing for Pixel-Space Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15236)
*Weichen Fan,Haiwen Diao,Penghao Wu,Ziwei Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Spectral Forcing introduces a time-conditional DCT low-pass filter for pixel-space diffusion models, improving efficiency and performance by explicitly separating signal from noise in the frequency domain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard pixel-space diffusion models waste capacity because the denoiser must internally discover a moving frequency boundary between signal and noise, and expends computation on frequency-time regions where the optimal prediction is trivial rather than data modeling.

Method: Spectral Forcing: a parameter-free, time-conditional 2D-DCT low-pass operator applied to noisy inputs before the patch embedder, with a cutoff that expands monotonically with diffusion time.

Result: On ImageNet-256 (JiT-700M/32), Spectral Forcing consistently improves both FID and Inception Score. In SenseNova-U1 (text-to-image), it improves DPG-Bench and GenEval. Benefits are most pronounced with coarse patch tokenization and noise-dominated high-frequency data.

Conclusion: Spectral Forcing offers a simple, transferable route to capacity-efficient pixel-space diffusion by explicitly showing the signal and hiding the noise, suggesting that input-side spectral priors are beneficial beyond class-conditional generation.

Abstract: Pixel-space diffusion models are trained on full-bandwidth noisy images, yet the useful signal available to the denoiser is strongly frequency dependent. Under rectified-flow diffusion and natural-image power-law spectra, the per-band data-to-noise contour $k^{*}(t) = (1-t)^{-2/α}$ separates a signal-bearing low-frequency region from a noise-dominated high-frequency region at each time $t$. We show that this implicit coarse-to-fine structure is not merely descriptive: it induces a capacity-allocation problem. A standard pixel-space denoiser must discover the moving bandwidth boundary internally and can spend computation on frequency-time regions where the optimal prediction collapses to deterministic baselines rather than data-distribution modeling. To make this boundary explicit, we introduce Spectral Forcing, a parameter-free, time-conditional 2D-DCT low-pass operator applied to the noisy input before the patch embedder. Its cutoff expands monotonically with the diffusion time and becomes the identity at the data endpoint. Through controlled synthetic experiments, we identify the regime in which the operator is beneficial: coarse patch tokenization and data whose high-frequency content is predominantly noise rather than essential signal. On ImageNet-256 with JiT-700M/32, Spectral Forcing consistently improves both FID and Inception Score across different training epochs, demonstrating robust gains throughout training; at finer tokenization, the spectral forcing is still competitive. We further insert the unchanged operator into SenseNova-U1, a unified text-to-image model, where it improves DPG-Bench and GenEval, showing that the input-side spectral prior transfers beyond class-conditional generation. These results suggest a route to capacity-efficient pixel-space diffusion by showing the signal and hiding the noise.

</details>


### [83] [SPARK: Spatial Policy-driven Adaptive Reinforcement learning for Knowledge distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15243)
*Mohamed Jismy Aashik Rasool,Shabir Ahmad,Gisong Oh,Teag Kuen Whangbo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SPARK is an RL-based framework that adaptively allocates knowledge distillation effort across spatial regions during quantization-aware training, improving image restoration network performance under low-bit quantization without adding inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-bit quantization introduces rounding noise that disproportionately degrades high-frequency image regions (edges, fine textures), and existing KD methods apply uniform distillation signals across all spatial locations, overlooking varying reconstruction difficulty across different image regions.

Method: A lightweight RL policy network processes four difficulty features (Laplacian variance, pixel variance, student reconstruction error, teacher-student knowledge gap) to produce a stochastic spatial weight map that modulates KD loss during QAT.

Result: Consistently outperforms PTQ, QAT, and SOTA KD approaches across multiple student architectures, achieving reconstruction quality closest to full-precision teacher under significant computational constraints.

Conclusion: SPARK is IR task-agnostic, adds no inference cost, integrates into any QAT pipeline without architectural changes, and effectively addresses spatial variation in reconstruction difficulty under quantization.

Abstract: Low-bit quantization enables deployment of image restoration (IR) networks on resource-constrained devices, but introduces rounding noise that disproportionately degrades high-frequency regions such as edges and fine textures. Existing knowledge distillation (KD) methods apply distillation signals uniformly across all spatial locations, overlooking the varying reconstruction difficulty across image regions. To address this, we propose SPARK (Spatial Policy-driven Adaptive Reinforcement Learning for Knowledge Distillation), a framework that adaptively allocates distillation effort using a lightweight reinforcement learning (RL) policy network. At each training step, a difficulty feature extractor computes four signals, namely Laplacian variance, pixel variance, student reconstruction error, and teacher-student knowledge gap, which are fed into a compact policy CNN that produces a stochastic spatial weight map to modulate the KD loss during quantization-aware training (QAT). SPARK is IR task-agnostic, adds no inference cost, and integrates into any existing QAT pipeline without architectural changes. Experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that SPARK consistently outperforms PTQ, QAT, and state-of-the-art (SOTA) KD approaches across multiple student architectures, achieving reconstruction quality closest to the full-precision teacher under significant computational constraints.

</details>


### [84] [Landmark-free Assessment of Lower-limb Alignment with Implicit Neural Shape Functions from Knee Radiographs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15250)
*Zhisen Hu,Antti Kemppainen,David Johnson,Egor Panfilov,Huy Hoang Nguyen,Timothy Cootes,Claudia Lindner,Aleksei Tiulpin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes INSF, an automated workflow for lower-limb alignment assessment that uses implicit neural shape functions to directly regress clinical measurements from latent anatomical codes, avoiding reliance on fixed landmarks. Evaluated on 566 knee radiographs, it achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art methods while offering greater flexibility for extension to new tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional manual measurement of LLA is time-consuming, and existing ML methods depend on fixed anatomical landmarks, which limits flexibility and requires re-annotation when clinical definitions change.

Method: The method encodes anatomy into a compact latent space using Implicit Neural Shape Functions (INSF), then directly regresses clinical alignment measurements from these latent codes, bypassing explicit landmark localization.

Result: Performance was comparable to state-of-the-art landmark-based methods and manual agreement on both internal (50 patients) and external (402 MRKR cases) test sets.

Conclusion: INSF offers a flexible shape representation that achieves competitive accuracy while enabling rapid extendability to new measurement tasks without altering the backbone model.

Abstract: Radiographic assessment of lower-limb alignment (LLA) is important for predicting joint health and surgical outcomes in total knee arthroplasty. Traditional measurement methods are manual and time-consuming, while recent machine learning approaches typically rely on locating a fixed set of anatomical landmarks. This dependence limits flexibility and may require re-annotation when clinical definitions change. To address this, we propose an automated workflow using Implicit Neural Shape Functions (INSF). Rather than relying on explicit landmark coordinates, we encode the anatomy into a compact latent space and regress clinical alignment measurements directly from these latent codes. This architecture allows for rapid extendability to new tasks without altering the backbone representation. We trained our method on an internal dataset of 566 knee radiographs, each annotated with the outline of the femur and tibia. We evaluated it on both an internal test dataset of 50 patients and a separate external set of 402 preoperative cases from the MRKR dataset. Manual clinical measurements are available for these data, and the MRKR measurements will be made publicly accessible. Performance was comparable to state-of-the-art landmark-based methods and manual agreement, while offering a flexible shape representation that can be extended to additional measurement tasks.

</details>


### [85] [Focus, Align, and Sustain: Counteracting Gradient Dilution in Incremental Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15253)
*Aoting Zhang,Dongbao Yang,Chang Liu,Xiaopeng Hong,Yu Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper identifies Gradient Dilution as the root cause of performance degradation in Incremental Object Detection (IOD), caused by Signal Dispersion, Assignment Drift, and Support Attrition. The authors propose FAS—a unified framework that Focuses, Aligns, and Sustains gradient flow via prior-injected queries, deterministic anchor distillation, and manifold-support replay—achieving over 5.0 AP improvement in challenging settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the systemic challenge of adapting Detection Transformers to Incremental Object Detection (IOD), where set-based optimization becomes destabilized by sequential learning, and the root cause is identified as Gradient Dilution—a progressive weakening of optimization signals needed to preserve old knowledge.

Method: FAS framework with three components: (1) Prior-injected queries to focus discriminative signals by filtering background interference; (2) Deterministic anchor distillation to align query-target assignments and enforce semantic consistency across stages; (3) Manifold-support replay to sustain distributional support of old classes and counteract representational erosion.

Result: FAS restores robust optimization dynamics and outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving over 5.0 AP improvement in the challenging 40+10x4 incremental setting.

Conclusion: By systematically counteracting Gradient Dilution through focused, aligned, and sustained gradient flow, the proposed FAS framework effectively stabilizes incremental learning in Detection Transformers and sets a new state-of-the-art for IOD.

Abstract: Adapting Detection Transformers to Incremental Object Detection (IOD) poses a systemic challenge, as set-based optimization is inherently destabilized by sequential learning. In this work, we identify Gradient Dilution as the root cause of performance degradation, wherein optimization signals required to preserve old knowledge are progressively weakened. This phenomenon manifests as a cascading erosion of preservation gradients in magnitude, direction, and support coverage, driven by three tightly coupled factors: Signal Dispersion, where foreground gradients are overwhelmed by background noise; Assignment Drift, where stochastic query-target matching induces inconsistent gradient trajectories; and Support Attrition, where gradients from retained samples insufficiently cover the old-class feature space, weakening decision boundaries under interference from new classes. To counteract this, we propose FAS, a unified framework that Focuses, Aligns, and Sustains gradient flow throughout incremental learning. Specifically, we introduce prior-injected queries to focus discriminative signals by filtering background interference at the source. We further propose deterministic anchor distillation to align query-target assignments and enforce semantic consistency across stages under unstable matching. Finally, we devise manifold-support replay to sustain distributional support of old classes, counteracting representational erosion induced by continual updates. Extensive experiments show that FAS restores robust optimization dynamics and outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving over 5.0 AP improvement in the challenging 40+10x4 incremental setting.

</details>


### [86] [Trusted Multi-View Deep Learning Classification of Fetal Congenital Heart Disease with Feature-level and Decision-level Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15265)
*Tan Zhou,Shifa Yao,Suncheng Xiang,Dahong Qian,Baoying Ye*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a multi-view deep learning framework for CHD classification using echocardiographic images, integrating attention mechanisms and uncertainty-based decision-making to handle low-quality images, achieving top-tier performance on a large-scale dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional diagnostics for CHD often lack accuracy and efficiency due to the complexity of cardiac anatomy, motivating the need for an automated, multi-view deep learning approach.

Method: A multi-view deep learning framework using five echocardiographic views, advanced feature extraction, attention mechanisms, and uncertainty-based decision-making for binary CHD classification.

Result: The method achieves top-tier performance on a large-scale CHD dataset, demonstrating robust early detection capabilities.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a robust tool for early CHD detection with strong clinical potential; dataset and code will be released upon acceptance.

Abstract: Congenital heart disease (CHD) refers to the abnormal anatomical structure caused by the abnormal development of the heart and great vessels during embryonic development. Traditional diagnostics often fail to achieve high accuracy and efficiency, especially given the complexity of cardiac anatomy. This study presents a specialized multi-view deep learning framework for CHD binary classification using echocardiographic images. A large-scale CHD dataset, including five views, was used to train the model, enabling it to integrate multi-angle image data. The framework utilizes advanced feature extraction and attention mechanisms to improve diagnostic precision and reliability. An uncertainty-based decision-making component is also integrated to handle low-quality images, enhancing diagnostic outcomes. Experimental results show that this method achieves top-tier performance on our dataset and provides a robust tool for early CHD detection, underscoring its potential for clinical use. The dataset and source code will be released upon paper acceptance.

</details>


### [87] [MamBOA: State-Space Architecture for Video Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15275)
*Mustafa Bora Çelik*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MamBOA is a backbone-agnostic temporal framework for fine-grained action recognition that uses interleaved state-space recurrence (S6) to encode motion directly within hidden state dynamics, achieving competitive accuracy on Diving48 with low computational overhead (~2.1 GFLOPs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing fine-grained action recognition methods face tradeoffs: 3D dense operators couple computation to input volume, while difference-based methods approximate motion via rigid, hand-crafted subtraction of uncontextualized features—both have limited expressiveness or flexibility.

Method: MamBOA introduces an interleaved scan structure that feeds consecutive features from a pretrained backbone into a single alternating sequence, driving the S6 recurrence to encode inter-frame transitions as intrinsic state dynamics. A cascade of alignment and decoding operations extracts explicit motion representations, aggregated via a dual-path pooling mechanism balancing attention and uniform coverage.

Result: Achieves 85.02% (image-pretrained) and 86.24% (video-pretrained) Top-1 accuracy on Diving48 with only ~2.1 GFLOPs added per feature pair, processing the entire video in a single forward pass.

Conclusion: Structurally induced state-space dynamics constitute a principled and general foundation for motion modeling, compatible with CNN, Transformer, and Mamba backbone families.

Abstract: Fine-grained action recognition demands temporal reasoning that general-purpose architectures address through different cost-accuracy tradeoffs: 3D dense operators couple computation to the input volume, while difference-based methods approximate motion through rigid, hand-crafted subtraction of uncontextualized features - each reflecting a deliberate design choice with corresponding limitations in expressiveness or flexibility. We present MamBOA, a backbone-agnostic temporal framework built upon a novel interleaved scan structure that recasts the selective state-space recurrence (S6) as a native motion synthesizer. By interleaving consecutive feature representations extracted from a pretrained backbone into a single alternating sequence, the proposed scan structurally drives the recurrence to encode both temporal observations of each position within a shared hidden state, separated by only a single decay step - rendering the inter-frame transition an intrinsic component of the state dynamics rather than an externally computed quantity. A cascade of dedicated alignment and decoding operations then distills this joint encoding into an explicit motion representation, which a dual-path pooling mechanism adaptively aggregates by balancing attention-driven selection with uniform temporal coverage. The framework interfaces seamlessly with CNN, Transformer, and Mamba backbone families, adding only ~2.1 GFLOPs per feature pair. On Diving48, MamBOA achieves 85.02% Top-1 accuracy with an image-pretrained backbone and 86.24% with a video-pretrained backbone processing the entire video in a single forward pass - demonstrating that structurally induced state-space dynamics constitute a principled and general foundation for motion modeling.

</details>


### [88] [Enhancing Precision Agriculture with a Hybrid Deep Learning Framework for Multi-Class Plant Disease Classification and Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15282)
*Hasibul Islam Sufi,Ridam Roy,Shayla Alam Setu,Mahimul Islam Nadim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes deep learning architectures (ResNet-50, hybrid ResNet+ViT) for multi-class plant disease classification from leaf images, achieving 98.74% accuracy with ResNet-50 and 98.58% with the hybrid model, along with interpretability via Grad-CAM and saliency maps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop accurate, interpretable, and computationally efficient deep learning systems for multi-class multi-disease plant disease classification to aid cultivation management and precision farming.

Method: Used a dataset of 15,200 training and 3,800 validation images across 38 classes. Compared ResNet-50, MobileNetV2, EfficientNet-B0, and a hybrid ResNet+ViT model. All models fine-tuned with AdamW optimizer and cross-entropy loss, with early stopping. Applied Grad-CAM and saliency maps for interpretability and segmentation for affected leaf part identification.

Result: ResNet-50 achieved 98.74% accuracy; the hybrid ResNet+ViT model achieved 98.58% accuracy, demonstrating competitive performance and effective capture of both local and global information.

Conclusion: Transformer-based hybrid models show promise for achieving highly accurate, interpretable plant disease classification systems, offering practical assistance for precision agriculture.

Abstract: This study proposes an overall deep learning architecture for multi-class classification of plant diseases from high-resolution leaf imagery, with a particular interest in investigating the behavior of ResNet-50 and a hybrid ResNet + Vision Transformer (ViT) design. A specially gathered image database with 15,200 training images and 3,800 validation images spanning 38 classes across multiple crops, including tomato, apple, grape etc. were subjected to preprocessing steps such as resizing, normalization, and data augmentation to enhance model robustness. Multiple architectures, including ResNet-50, MobileNetV2, and EfficientNet-B0, were trained and compared with the hybrid ResNet + ViT model. All models were fine-tuned using the AdamW optimizer and cross-entropy loss, with early stopping applied to prevent overfitting and ensure generalization. Furthermore, interpretability techniques such as Grad-CAM and saliency maps were implemented to indicate disease-relevant regions, while segmentation-based analysis was performed to identify the affected parts of a leaf. For every one of the considered architectures, ResNet-50 led to the highest accuracy of 98.74%, whereas the hybrid ResNet + ViT model achieved a competitive accuracy of 98.58%, showing that the hybrid architectures were effective in capturing both local and overall information. The experimental results showcase the promise of transformer-based models to achieve highly accurate, interpretable, and computationally efficient computer-based multi-class multi-disease classification systems, providing helpful assistance for cultivation management practices as well as for precision farming.

</details>


### [89] [Decoupled Motion Representation Learning for Moving Infrared Small Target Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15286)
*Guoyi Zhang,Peiwen Wu,Han Wang,Xiangpeng Xu,Xiaohu Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a decoupled motion representation learning framework for infrared small target detection, which separates globally coherent background motion (explicit branch using optical flow) from sparse local target anomalies (implicit branch with deformable alignment), and introduces a coherent-motion-guided module to suppress false alarms, achieving state-of-the-art performance on dynamic scene benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-frame methods perform implicit temporal modeling where coherent background dynamics dominate motion learning, causing an inherent trade-off between detection accuracy and false alarms. The authors observe that false alarms mainly originate from coherent background dynamics rather than genuine target motions.

Method: 1) An explicit motion branch using pretrained optical flow priors with structure-preserving self-supervised adaptation for globally coherent motion modeling. 2) An implicit motion branch with deformable feature alignment for target-sensitive local anomaly capture under coherent motion guidance. 3) A coherent-motion-guided local anomaly reasoning module to identify and suppress false responses from coherent background dynamics.

Result: Consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on two challenging infrared small target detection benchmarks, especially in dynamic scenes with complex motions, while maintaining favorable inference efficiency.

Conclusion: Decoupling globally coherent background motions from sparse local target anomalies effectively resolves the detection-false alarm trade-off, and coherent-motion-guided false alarm suppression is critical for dynamic scene infrared small target detection.

Abstract: Infrared small target detection in dynamic scenes remains challenging due to the highly coupled motions among targets, imaging platforms, and dynamic backgrounds. Existing multi-frame methods usually perform implicit temporal modeling, where coherent background dynamics dominate motion correspondence learning, leading to an inherent trade-off between detection and false alarms. In this work, we observe that background motions exhibit strong global coherence, whereas small targets mainly correspond to sparse local motion anomalies. Moreover, many false-alarm responses maintain high consistency with globally coherent motion patterns, indicating that they mainly originate from coherent background dynamics rather than genuine target motions. Based on these observations, we propose a decoupled motion representation learning framework for moving infrared small target detection. Specifically, an explicit motion branch is introduced to model globally coherent motion dynamics using pretrained optical flow priors, together with a structure-preserving self-supervised adaptation strategy for infrared motion correspondence learning. Meanwhile, an implicit motion branch based on deformable feature alignment is designed to capture target-sensitive local motion anomalies under coherent motion guidance. Furthermore, a coherent-motion-guided local anomaly reasoning module is proposed to identify and suppress coherent-motion-induced false responses during localized motion modeling. Extensive experiments on two challenging infrared small target detection benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches, particularly in dynamic scenes with complex motions, while maintaining favorable inference efficiency.

</details>


### [90] [G2IA: Geometry-Guided Instance-Aware Retrieval and Refinement for Cross-Modal Place Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15287)
*Xianyun Jiao,Jingyi Xu,Zhongmiao Yan,Xieyuanli Chen,Lin Pei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: G2IA is a geometry-guided instance-aware framework for cross-modal place recognition that integrates visual geometry priors and instance features for retrieval, then re-ranks candidates via cross-modal consistency verification of local instance shapes and spatial layouts, outperforming baselines on image-to-point-cloud localization benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-modal place recognition (CMPR) for image-to-point-cloud localization suffers from two coupled ambiguities: the modality gap between RGB images and sparse LiDAR geometry, and perceptual aliasing among urban environments with similar structures. The paper argues that reliable retrieval needs both geometry-aware representation alignment and fine-grained candidate verification, rather than just global descriptor matching.

Method: G2IA operates in two stages: (1) Retrieval stage — integrates visual geometry priors from VGGT and instance features to construct place descriptors compatible with LiDAR-derived map representations; (2) Refinement stage — re-ranks retrieved candidates by verifying cross-modal consistency of local instance shapes and their relative spatial layouts.

Result: The method consistently improves image-to-point-cloud place recognition under different localization thresholds and exhibits strong cross-dataset generalization on public benchmarks.

Conclusion: Treating CMPR as a two-stage process with geometry-aware retrieval and instance-level verification effectively addresses modality gap and perceptual aliasing challenges, outperforming single-descriptor matching approaches.

Abstract: Cross-modal place recognition (CMPR) enables camera-only robots to localize against pre-built LiDAR maps in autonomous navigation scenarios. This image-to-point-cloud setting is challenged by two coupled ambiguities: the modality gap between perspective RGB appearance and sparse metric geometry, and perceptual aliasing among urban places with similar roads, facades, intersections, and object arrangements. Instead of treating CMPR as a single global descriptor matching problem, we argue that reliable retrieval requires both geometry-aware representation alignment and fine-grained candidate verification. In this paper, we propose G2IA, a geometry-guided instance-aware framework for image-to-point-cloud place recognition. In the retrieval stage, visual geometry priors from VGGT and instance features are integrated to construct place descriptors that are more compatible with LiDAR-derived map representations. In the refinement stage, the retrieved candidates are re-ranked by explicitly verifying whether local instance shapes and their relative spatial layouts are consistent across modalities. Experiments on public benchmarks demonstrate that G2IA consistently improves image-to-point-cloud place recognition under different localization thresholds, and exhibits strong cross-dataset generalization.

</details>


### [91] [HemExp: Clinically-Guided Latent Diffusion for Modeling Hematoma Expansion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15304)
*Orhun Utku Aydin,Satoru Tanioka,Tzu I Chuang,Alexander Koch,Dimitrios Rallios,Marie Gultom,Begum Tahhan,Fujimaro Ishida,Dietmar Frey,Adam Hilbert*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HemExp is a latent diffusion model that generates patient-specific follow-up CT images and hematoma segmentations after intracerebral hemorrhage, conditioned on baseline scans, clinical variables, and expansion indicators, enabling uncertainty-aware estimation of hematoma progression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hematoma expansion (HE) is critical for neurosurgical triage, but existing methods only provide binary risk or single volume estimates without capturing uncertainty. Clinicians need probabilistic, patient-specific predictions for better decision-making.

Method: HemExp uses a hemorrhage-aware multi-head VAE with a conditional latent diffusion model trained on paired baseline-follow-up CT scans from 450 patients. It generates multiple synthetic follow-up images per patient to produce spatial probability maps of HE, and can perturb clinical variables (e.g., onset-to-imaging time) to shift predicted distributions.

Result: Evaluated on 107 held-out patients, HemExp produces robust spatial HE probability maps and distributions of plausible follow-up volumes. Perturbing clinical inputs (e.g., anticoagulant status) shifts volume distributions as expected. It outperforms binary predictors and captures intraventricular involvement and mass effects.

Conclusion: Controllable latent diffusion is a promising direction for uncertainty-aware modeling of early ICH progression, supporting clinically relevant outcome estimation beyond simple binary classification.

Abstract: Hematoma expansion (HE) after spontaneous intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) is a major determinant of acute triage and treatment decisions in neurosurgical care. However, most existing methods provide either a binary expansion risk or a single follow-up volume, limiting uncertainty-aware decisions. We introduce HemExp, a clinically-guided latent diffusion model that generates patient-specific follow-up non-contrast CT images, along with segmentations of intraparenchymal and intraventricular hemorrhage. Generation is conditioned on baseline imaging, clinical variables, and an explicit expansion indicator, enabling controllable simulation of realistic clinical scenarios. HemExp uses a hemorrhage-aware multi-head variational autoencoder and models progression as the difference between baseline and follow-up latent representations with a conditional diffusion model. The model is trained on paired scans from 450 patients across multiple centers and evaluated on 107 patients from a held-out institution. HemExp produces spatial HE probability maps by generating multiple synthetic follow-up images per patient to estimate distributions of plausible follow-up hematoma volumes. Perturbing clinical inputs such as symptom-onset-to-imaging time or anticoagulant status shifts the predicted follow-up volume distribution. HemExp extends binary predictors and demonstrates robust estimation of clinically relevant outcomes in the imaging space, such as hematoma volume, intraventricular involvement, and mass effects. Overall, our results support controllable latent diffusion as a promising direction for uncertainty-aware modeling of early ICH progression.

</details>


### [92] [CoMNeT: A MedNeXt-CorrDiff Framework for Volumetric Brain Tumor Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15305)
*Michael L. Evans,MD Fayaz Bin Hossen,MD Shibly Sadique,Walia Farzana,Khan M. Iftekharuddin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CoMNeT combines a MedNeXt 3D convolutional segmentation model with corrective diffusion-based refinement (CorrDiff) and ensembling to improve brain tumor segmentation, achieving superior Dice scores on the UTSW-Glioma dataset compared to baseline models like SegResNet and standalone MedNeXt.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Brain tumor segmentation from multiparametric MRI is critical for treatment planning and research, but remains difficult due to variation in tumor appearance, MRI protocols, and the small size of clinically important regions (enhancing tumor and tumor core) relative to the full brain volume.

Method: CoMNeT uses MedNeXt as the primary 3D convolutional segmentation model with Global Response Normalization, followed by CorrDiff (a corrective diffusion-based residual refinement method) as postprocessing to fix errors in probability maps before final thresholding. Four MRI modalities are used as input, and fold-level probability ensembling is applied.

Result: Using five-fold cross-validation, CoMNeT achieved Dice scores of 0.7543 (ET), 0.6806 (TC), 0.9049 (WT), and 0.7798 (average), outperforming SegResNet (0.7555 average Dice) and standalone MedNeXt (0.7697 average Dice).

Conclusion: Corrective diffusion-based refinement and fold-level probability ensembling are practical additions to existing 3D convolutional models for improved automated glioma segmentation.

Abstract: Accurate brain tumor segmentation from multiparametric magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is critical for treatment planning, response assessment, and quantitative neuro-oncology research. However, automated segmentation remains a difficult task in computer vision because of variation in tumor appearance and MRI protocols across patient scans. Moreover, clinically important regions such as enhancing tumor (ET) and tumor core (TC) are often small relative to the full brain volume, furthering increasing the difficulty of achieving high voxel-level precision. In this paper, we show that combining a modern 3D convolutional segmentation model with corrective diffusion-based refinement and ensembling improves volumetric glioma segmentation on the UTSW-Glioma dataset. We propose CoMNeT, a MedNeXt-CorrDiff framework that uses four MRI modalities as input and predicts ET, TC, and whole tumor (WT) regions for automated brain tumor segmentation. MedNeXt is used as the primary segmentation model with Global Response Normalization for feature learning, while CorrDiff is trained as a postprocessing residual refinement method to correct errors in the probability maps before final thresholding. Using five-fold cross-validation, CoMNeT achieved the highest Dice score for most tumor regions, with ET, TC, WT, and average Dice scores of 0.7543 +/- 0.0261, 0.6806 +/- 0.0166, 0.9049 +/- 0.0128, and 0.7798 +/- 0.0184, respectively. CoMNeT outperformed two selected baseline models: SegResNet (0.7555 +/- 0.0190 average Dice) and standalone MedNeXt (0.7697 +/- 0.0154 average Dice). Our findings support the use of corrective diffusion and fold-level probability ensembling as practical additions to existing state-of-the-art 3D convolutional models for automated glioma segmentation.

</details>


### [93] [Conditional Multi-Event Temporal Grounding in Long-Form Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15320)
*Yuanhao Zou,Arthad Kulkarni,Lucas Tonanez,Lincoln Spencer,Guangyu Sun,Tianxingjian Ding,Andong Deng,Yi Li,Shuangjun Liu,Yuan Li,Dashan Gao,Ning Bi,Taotao Jing,Shuai Zhang,Chen Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces CoMET-Bench, a benchmark for conditional multi-event temporal grounding in long-form videos, and CoMET-Agent, a training-free agentic framework that improves grounding performance through structured search-and-aggregate reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world video understanding requires localizing every event satisfying compositional temporal and spatial conditions, but existing benchmarks only handle single moment queries, count without temporal conditions, or treat grounding and counting as separate tasks.

Method: The authors construct CoMET-Bench (2789 queries, 600 videos, ~34 min average) with compositional temporal/spatial conditions and a negative-query subset. They propose a unified evaluation protocol including Rejection-F1 metric. They also develop CoMET-Agent, a training-free agentic framework reformulating the task as structured search-and-aggregate.

Result: Existing MLLMs, agent-based, and grounding-specialized methods perform poorly on this task. CoMET-Agent improves F1@0.5 by 6.1% over GPT-5 purely through structural reasoning without training.

Conclusion: Three open directions remain: fine-grained entity tracking, position-uniform retrieval, and causal event pairing.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models have made rapid progress in video temporal grounding, yet real-world applications routinely require localizing every event that satisfies compositional temporal and spatial conditions. Existing benchmarks fall short: they localize only a single moment per query, count without temporal conditions, or treat grounding and counting as disjoint tasks. We introduce CoMET-Bench for Conditional Multi-Event Temporal Grounding in long-form video, comprising 2789 queries over 600 videos averaging 33.8 minutes across five real-world domains, with each query composed from 4 temporal conditions, 3 spatial conditions, and a dedicated negative-query subset. We further propose a unified evaluation protocol jointly measuring counting, grounding, and negative-query recognition, including a new Rejection-F1 metric that prevents trivial gaming by lazy "always-empty" models. Benchmarking a broad suite of MLLMs, agent-based, and grounding-specialized methods reveals that existing approaches remain far from solving this task. Building on these findings, we propose CoMET-Agent, a training-free agentic framework that reformulates the task as structured search-and-aggregate, improving F1@0.5 by 6.1% over GPT-5 purely through structural reasoning. Failure analysis further surfaces three open directions: fine-grained entity tracking, position-uniform retrieval, and causal event pairing.

</details>


### [94] [PPDM: Pixel Puzzling Diffusion Model for Speed and Memory Efficient Volumetric Medical Image Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15323)
*Tianqi Chen,Jun Hou,Yinchi Zhou,James S. Duncan,Chi Liu,Bo Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PPDM is a memory-efficient 3D medical image translation framework using a reversible pixel puzzle-unpuzzle operator, direct bridge diffusion, and puzzle-gradient loss, achieving up to 10x GPU memory reduction while matching or outperforming full 3D diffusion models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models for 3D medical image translation suffer from prohibitive GPU memory and computational costs. Existing memory-efficient methods compromise either global volumetric consistency or fine anatomical detail.

Method: (1) Reversible pixel puzzle-unpuzzle operator that trades spatial resolution for channel dimension to reduce activation memory. (2) Direct bridge diffusion starting from conditional input instead of pure noise. (3) Puzzle-gradient loss to enforce spatial coherence and suppress grid artifacts.

Result: PPDM matches or outperforms full 3D diffusion models while reducing training GPU memory by up to an order of magnitude and significantly accelerating inference. It outperforms existing memory-efficient approaches based on latent compression or frequency decomposition across multiple 3D translation tasks.

Conclusion: PPDM provides a practical and scalable solution for high-fidelity 3D diffusion-based medical image translation under limited computational resources.

Abstract: Diffusion models have demonstrated superior fidelity for medical image-to-image translation, but their extension to high-resolution 3D volumes is severely constrained by prohibitive computational cost and GPU memory requirements. Existing memory-efficient strategies often compromise global volumetric consistency or fine anatomical detail. In this work, we propose the Pixel Puzzling Diffusion Model (PPDM), a simple and effective framework for memory- and speed-efficient 3D medical image translation. PPDM introduces a reversible pixel puzzle-unpuzzle operator that trades spatial resolution for channel dimensionality, substantially reducing activation memory while preserving global context. To further improve efficiency and stability, we adopt a direct bridge diffusion formulation that starts from the conditional input rather than pure noise, enabling the model to focus on task-relevant residuals. In addition, a puzzle-gradient loss is incorporated to enforce spatial coherence and suppress grid-like artifacts introduced by spatial rearrangement. We evaluate PPDM on multiple challenging 3D medical image translation tasks, including low-count PET denoising, joint PET denoising and attenuation correction, and cross-modal MRI translation. Across all tasks, PPDM consistently matches or outperforms full 3D diffusion models while reducing training GPU memory usage by up to an order of magnitude and significantly accelerating inference, and it outperforms existing memory-efficient diffusion approaches based on latent compression or frequency decomposition. These results demonstrate that PPDM provides a practical and scalable solution for high-fidelity 3D diffusion-based medical image translation under limited computational resources.

</details>


### [95] [SGFormer++: Semantic Graph Transformer for Incremental 3D Scene Graph Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15328)
*Mengshi Qi,Changsheng Lv,Zijian Fu,Xianlin Zhang,Huadong Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SGFormer++ is a Transformer-based framework for 3D scene graph generation that outperforms GCN-based methods, featuring a Graph Embedding Layer++ for efficient global context, a Semantic Injection Layer++ for LLM/VLM-enhanced semantics, and a Spatial-guided Feature Adapter with Cascaded Binary Prediction Head for incremental learning, achieving SOTA with +4.49% Predicate A@1 on 3DSSG.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GCN-based 3D scene graph generation methods suffer from over-smoothing and limited receptive fields, struggling to model complex global scene structure. Additionally, incremental SGG where new relationship categories arrive sequentially poses a practical challenge of catastrophic forgetting.

Method: SGFormer++ uses Transformer layers as backbone with two key components: (1) Graph Embedding Layer++ for edge-aware global context with linear complexity, and (2) Semantic Injection Layer++ to enrich visual features with LLM/VLM linguistic priors without extra parameters. For incremental SGG, it introduces Spatial-guided Feature Adapter (calibrates predicate features using subject-object geometry) and Cascaded Binary Prediction Head (task-incremental classifier expansion + logit distillation to mitigate forgetting).

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art on 3DSSG benchmark in both standard and incremental settings, with a 4.49% absolute improvement in Predicate A@1 under the incremental setting.

Conclusion: The proposed Transformer-based approach effectively addresses the limitations of GCN-based methods for 3D scene graph generation, and the incremental learning components successfully handle the practical challenge of sequentially arriving new relationship categories.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose SGFormer++, a novel Semantic Graph Transformer for 3D scene graph generation (SGG), which aims to parse point cloud scenes into semantic structural graphs, where nodes denote detected object instances and edges encode their pairwise relationships, with the core challenge lying in modeling complex global scene structure. While existing graph convolutional network (GCN)-based methods suffer from over-smoothing and limited receptive fields, SGFormer++ leverages Transformer layers as its backbone to enable global message passing. Specifically, we introduce two key components tailored for 3D SGG: (1) a Graph Embedding Layer++ that efficiently integrates edge-aware global context with linear computational complexity, and (2) a Semantic Injection Layer++ that enriches visual features with linguistic priors from large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs), boosting semantic representation without introducing extra trainable parameters. To further address the practical challenge of incremental SGG (I-SGG), where new relationship categories arrive sequentially, we equip SGFormer++ with a novel Spatial-guided Feature Adapter, which calibrates predicate features using subject-object spatial geometry to counter scale variation, and a Cascaded Binary Prediction Head that mitigates catastrophic forgetting via task-incremental classifier expansion and logit distillation. Extensive experiments on the 3DSSG benchmark demonstrate that SGFormer++ achieves state-of-the-art performance in both standard and incremental settings: it yields a significant 4.49% absolute improvement in Predicate A@1 under the incremental setting. Code and data are available at: https://github.com/Andy20178/SGFormer.

</details>


### [96] [CausalDrive: Real-time Causal World Models for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15341)
*Tianyi Yan,Huan Zheng,Dubing Chen,Meizhi Qu,Yingying Shen,Lijun Zhou,Mingfei Tu,Bing Wang,Guang Chen,Hangjun Ye,Haiyang Sun,Cheng-zhong Xu,Jianbing Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CausalDrive is a real-time, controllable driving world renderer that predicts causal interactions from only the initial frame, ego trajectory, and text prompt—without requiring future NPC layouts. It uses a Context-Forced DMD architecture for 12 FPS interactive simulation, enabling closed-loop evaluation, RL post-training, and human-in-the-loop simulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video generative models for autonomous driving either rely on oracle future trajectories (non-reactive) or lack semantic control and are too slow for closed-loop policy learning. There is a need for a controllable, reactive, real-time simulator.

Method: CausalDrive uses only the initial front-view frame, ego trajectory, and text prompt to predict future frames without future NPC layouts. It introduces Context-Forced DMD, combining continuous flow-matching with a self-correcting distillation objective, achieving 12 FPS interactive speeds.

Result: Achieves 12 FPS interactive simulation; significantly mitigates collision artifacts in closed-loop evaluation; enables large-scale RL post-training via Video2Reward; trained policies show superior real-world interaction capabilities.

Conclusion: CausalDrive transforms passive video generation into a playable neural simulator, enabling reactive, controllable, and real-time driving simulation for downstream tasks like evaluation, RL training, and human-in-the-loop simulation.

Abstract: World models have emerged as a promising paradigm for scaling autonomous driving (AD) data, yet existing video generative models fall short as interactive simulators. Layout-conditioned renderers rely on "oracle" future trajectories of all background agents, rendering them strictly non-reactive. Conversely, pure action-conditioned predictors lack semantic control over complex interactions and suffer from prohibitive diffusion latencies, hindering closed-loop policy learning. To bridge this gap, we present CausalDrive, a controllable, real-time foundation driving world renderer. CausalDrive operates solely on the initial front-view frame, the ego-vehicle's trajectory, and a macroscopic text prompt. By excluding future NPC layouts, we compel the model to intrinsically predict causal interactions, enabling text-driven control over Driving Sociology, allowing users to dynamically orchestrate diverse counterfactual reactions to identical ego-actions. To overcome the efficiency bottleneck and address the covariate shift in autoregressive generation, we propose a novel Context-Forced DMD architecture. This combines continuous flow-matching with a self-correcting distillation objective, achieving interactive speeds of 12 FPS. This breakthrough transforms the passive video generator into a playable neural simulator. We demonstrate its versatility across three downstream applications: (1) generative closed-loop evaluation with significantly mitigated collision artifacts, (2) large-scale Reinforcement Learning (RL) post-training driven by a Video2Reward module, and (3) real-time human-in-the-loop simulation. Extensive experiments validate that policies trained within CausalDrive's reactive scenarios exhibit superior interaction capabilities in the real world.

</details>


### [97] [DYNA-PRUNER: Input-Adaptive Data-Model Co-Pruning for Efficient and Scalable Spatio-Temporal Media Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15346)
*Fuyan Zhang,Yuqi Li,Yingli Tian,Edmond S. L. Ho*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Dyna-Pruner is an end-to-end framework for input-dependent co-pruning of data and model structure in spatio-temporal prediction, achieving up to 70% FLOPs reduction and 2.5× speedup with negligible accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spatio-temporal prediction models are often too expensive for real-time deployment due to a mismatch between dense computation and input-dependent redundancy (e.g., calm seas or clear skies).

Method: Dyna-Pruner uses a shared-importance synchronization mechanism that generates coupled masks to prune both redundant regions and their corresponding computational units (e.g., convolutional filters), producing per-sample sparse sub-networks at inference time.

Result: Experiments on WeatherBench, SEVIR, and TaxiBJ show seamless integration with CNN, RNN, and Transformer backbones, reducing FLOPs by up to 70% and achieving 2.5× speedup on NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin with negligible accuracy loss (<1%).

Conclusion: Dyna-Pruner enables automated, resource-aware architecture optimization for scalable media analysis by dynamically pruning both data and model structure based on input redundancy.

Abstract: Spatio-temporal prediction supports radar/satellite nowcasting and city-scale traffic monitoring, but modern models are often too expensive for real-time deployment. This stems from a mismatch between dense computation and strong input-dependent redundancy (e.g., calm seas or clear skies). To enable automated, resource-aware architecture optimization in scalable media analysis, we propose Dyna-Pruner, an end-to-end framework for input-dependent co-pruning of data and model structure. A shared-importance synchronization mechanism generates coupled masks that prune redundant regions and their corresponding computational units (e.g., convolutional filters), yielding per-sample sparse sub-networks at inference time. Experiments on WeatherBench, SEVIR, and TaxiBJ show seamless integration with CNN, RNN, and Transformer backbones, reducing FLOPs by up to $70\%$ and achieving a $2.5\times$ speedup on NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin with negligible accuracy loss ($<1\%$).

</details>


### [98] [Facial Affect Analysis for Service-Oriented Systems: Advances, Challenges, and Future Visions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15351)
*Spyridon Georgiou,Aggelos Psiris,Thomas Lagkas,Vasileios Argyriou,Panagiotis Sarigiannidis,Iraklis Varlamis,Georgios Th. Papadopoulos*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper reframes Facial Affect Analysis (FAA) as a reusable service component for Service-Oriented Software Ecosystems, evaluating architectures (CNN, Transformer, graph, hybrid) through systems-engineering lenses like deployability, contracts, governance, and lifecycle management, concluding that benchmark performance alone is insufficient for operational readiness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: FAA is shifting from a standalone recognition task to a reusable capability within Service-Oriented Software Ecosystems (SoSE). The paper aims to bridge the gap between FAA methodological advances and real-world systems-engineering requirements for composable, dependable services.

Method: The paper preserves FAA's methodological core while reframing recent advances through systems-engineering requirements. It reviews progress in static/dynamic expression analysis, AU/micro-expression modeling, and architectures (CNN, Transformer, graph, hybrid), then interprets them by operational fit in edge, cloud, and hybrid pipelines.

Result: Benchmark gains alone are insufficient for SoSE readiness. System-level concerns—robustness under shift, intervention stability, fairness, privacy, runtime guarantees—are equally critical for deployability.

Conclusion: The paper provides a roadmap for treating FAA as an operational service component with explicit interfaces, measurable quality attributes, and accountable lifecycle management, emphasizing SoSE-driven evaluation criteria over pure recognition performance.

Abstract: Facial Affect Analysis (FAA) is evolving from a stand-alone recognition task into a reusable perception capability for Service-Oriented Software Ecosystems (SoSE). This paper preserves the FAA methodological core while reframing recent advances through systems-engineering requirements for composable and dependable services. We review representative progress in static and dynamic expression analysis, action-unit and micro-expression modeling, and modern CNN, Transformer, graph, and hybrid architectures, then interpret these advances by their operational fit in edge, cloud, and hybrid service pipelines. The synthesis emphasizes SoSE concerns that determine deployability: service contracts for uncertainty-aware outputs, latency and availability envelopes, lifecycle monitoring and recalibration, governance-aware integration, and interoperability across independently evolving components. Our analysis shows that benchmark gains alone are insufficient for SoSE readiness; robustness under shift, intervention stability, fairness, privacy posture, and runtime guarantees are equally critical. We conclude with a roadmap for treating FAA as an operational service component with explicit interfaces, measurable quality attributes, and accountable lifecycle management.

</details>


### [99] [Sustainable Face Recognition on Low-Power Devices with VQ-VAE Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15355)
*Christos Chronis,Georgios Th. Papadopoulos,Iraklis Varlamis*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a sustainable, edge-deployable face recognition framework using VQ-VAE to generate compact latent representations, combined with knowledge distillation from pre-trained face embeddings, achieving comparable accuracy to state-of-the-art models while significantly reducing memory, computation, and network overhead for low-power edge devices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional face recognition models are computationally intensive and cloud-dependent, causing high network traffic, energy consumption, and carbon footprint. The authors aim to create a more sustainable and efficient solution suitable for edge deployment.

Method: The framework uses Vector-Quantized Variational Autoencoders (VQ-VAE) to generate compact, semantically rich latent representations of facial images. It combines VQ-VAE embeddings on the edge with pre-trained face embeddings via knowledge distillation to maintain accuracy while reducing resource requirements.

Result: The system achieves accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art face embedding models while significantly reducing memory footprint, computational demands, and network overhead. The VQ-VAE compression preserves key identity characteristics in reconstructed images, enhancing robustness.

Conclusion: The integration of VQ-VAE compression with knowledge distillation enables efficient, low-power face recognition on edge devices, offering a sustainable alternative to cloud-dependent approaches without sacrificing matching accuracy.

Abstract: Face recognition has become a cornerstone of modern AI applications, yet conventional approaches often rely on computationally intensive models deployed in cloud environments, leading to increased network traffic, high energy consumption, and a heavy carbon footprint. This work introduces a sustainable, edge-deployable face recognition framework based on Vector-Quantized Variational Autoencoders (VQ-VAE), which generates compact and semantically rich latent representations of facial images. By leveraging the compression capacity and reconstruction quality of VQ-VAE embeddings on the edge and combining them with the power of pre-trained face embeddings in a knowledge distillation setup, our system achieves comparable accuracy to state-of-the-art face embedding models while significantly reducing memory and computation requirements on the edge, making it suitable for low-power edge devices. The integration of VQ-VAE compression minimizes network overhead while keeping the matching accuracy high by retaining only the most informative facial features in the latent space. As a result, the reconstructed images preserve the key identity characteristics, improving the robustness and overall performance of the face embeddings.

</details>


### [100] [MNet++: Extended 2D/3D Networks for Anisotropic Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15370)
*Kirsten Odendaal,Rade Bajic*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper reproduces MNet (a hybrid 2D/3D CNN for anisotropic medical image segmentation) within nnU-Net, confirming its performance on PROMISE (89.0% DSC) and LiTS (94.3%/54.6% DSC). Two extensions are introduced: Fusion Gating (+0.8% DSC, <3% overhead) and VMamba state-space module (reduced variance, 95.8% liver Dice), both preserving robustness to anisotropy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To verify the reproducibility of MNet's reported performance and robustness to anisotropic (variable voxel spacing) medical image segmentation, and to explore lightweight architectural improvements for adaptive 2D-3D feature fusion and long-range depth modeling.

Method: Re-implemented MNet within the nnU-Net framework; introduced two extensions: (1) a learned Fusion Gating mechanism for adaptive 2D-3D feature blending, and (2) a VMamba state-space module for efficient long-range depth modeling. Experiments conducted on PROMISE prostate MRI and LiTS liver CT under matched preprocessing and compute constraints.

Result: Reproduced MNet: PROMISE DSC 89.0±0.9% (within 0.8% of published), LiTS liver 94.3±1.9% / tumor 54.6±3.1%. Fusion Gating improved DSC by +0.8% with <3% overhead. VMamba reduced PROMISE variance to ±0.7% and achieved best LiTS liver DSC of 95.8%. Both extensions maintained robustness with delta DSC=1.5% across 1-4mm voxel spacing.

Conclusion: The study confirms MNet's reproducibility and demonstrates that adaptive fusion (Fusion Gating) and state-space modeling (VMamba) can further strengthen segmentation reliability under anisotropic conditions, though further tests are needed for definitive conclusions.

Abstract: This work demonstrates a full reproduction and extension of MNet, a hybrid 2D/3D convolutional network designed for anisotropic medical image segmentation. The original architecture was re-implemented within the nnU-Net framework to verify its reported performance and robustness to variable voxel spacing, known as anisotropy. Experiments were conducted on PROMISE prostate MRI and a controlled subset of LiTS liver CT under matched preprocessing and compute constraints. The reproduced MNet achieved a Dice similarity coefficient (DSC) of 89.0 +/- 0.9% on PROMISE, within 0.8% of the published result, and 94.3 +/- 1.9% / 54.6 +/- 3.1% for liver and tumor segmentation on LiTS, respectively. Two lightweight extensions were further introduced: (1) a learned Fusion Gating mechanism enabling adaptive 2D-3D feature blending, and (2) a VMamba state-space module for efficient long-range depth modelling. The Spatial Gating variant improved DSC by +0.8% with less than 3% inference overhead, while VMamba improved performance consistency, reducing PROMISE Dice variation to +/- 0.7% and achieving the strongest LiTS liver performance at 95.8% Dice. Both extensions preserved MNet robustness to anisotropy, with delta Dice = 1.5% across 1-4 mm voxel spacing. Overall, the study confirms MNet reproducibility and demonstrates that adaptive fusion and state-space modelling have the potential to further strengthen segmentation reliability under anisotropic conditions. However, further tests are required to provide definitive conclusions.

</details>


### [101] [Timestep Rescheduling in Diffusion Inversion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15389)
*Shangquan Sun,Ting Gong,Zhirui Liu,Jiamin Wu,Runkai Zhao,Mianxin Liu,Wenqi Ren,Xiaochun Cao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a nonuniform timestep scheduler for diffusion inversion that reduces inversion errors by strategically allocating computational effort based on the observation that errors follow a parabolic trend across timesteps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion inversion suffers from accumulated deviations during the inversion process. Existing methods overlook how timestep selection in the noise scheduler influences inversion fidelity.

Method: The authors propose a nonuniform timestep scheduler combining global rescaling with local dynamic programming-based rescheduling to minimize overall inversion error.

Result: The method consistently boosts performance of existing inversion techniques in image reconstruction and editing tasks, with no extra parameters or computational overhead.

Conclusion: The proposed scheduler is an effective off-the-shelf enhancement for existing inversion methods, reducing errors by leveraging the discovered parabolic error trend.

Abstract: Diffusion inversion, which maps images back to the Gaussian latent space of a diffusion model, is a critical task for image reconstruction and editing. While DDIM enables fast deterministic inversion, it inherently introduces deviations that accumulate into noticeable inversion errors. Existing methods often address this by solving a fixed-point problem but largely overlook how the selection of the diffusion timestep in the noise scheduler influences inversion fidelity. In this work, we reveal that the deviation scale in diffusion inversion is strongly dependent on the timestep size, and exhibits a parabolic trend, with larger errors concentrated at both small and large timesteps. Based on this finding, we propose a simple yet effective nonuniform timestep scheduler that integrates a global rescaling with a local dynamic programming based rescheduling, enabling a strategic allocation of computational effort that minimizes the overall inversion error and preserves higher inversion accuracy. Our method serves as an off-the-shelf enhancement for existing inversion techniques and requires no extra parameters or computational overhead. Through extensive experiments, we verify that integrating our scheduler consistently boosts the performance of existing inversion methods, achieving superior results in image reconstruction and editing.

</details>


### [102] [Segmentation-based Detection for Efficient Multi-Task Spacecraft Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15409)
*Sivaperuman Muniyasamy,Surendar Devasundaram*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A compact MobileNetV3 + U-Net architecture achieves 2nd place in SPARK 2026 Challenge (score 0.9482) for combined spacecraft classification, detection, and segmentation, with detection derived analytically from segmentation masks rather than a separate regression head.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Progress in space vision is limited by scarce annotated data and challenging visual conditions (illumination changes, low SNR, high contrast). The authors aim to address Stream 1 of the SPARK 2026 Challenge requiring a single model for three tasks.

Method: A lightweight architecture combining MobileNetV3 encoder with U-Net-style decoder. Detection is derived analytically from the union of predicted component segmentation masks, eliminating the need for a separate bounding-box regression head in the single-spacecraft setting.

Result: Overall leaderboard score of 0.9482 (2nd place). Task-specific scores: 1.0000 in classification, 0.9788 in detection, 0.8917 in segmentation.

Conclusion: Lightweight encoder-decoder architectures can deliver strong multi-task performance suitable for practical onboard space vision systems.

Abstract: Vision-based perception is fundamental to Space Situational Awareness and autonomous on-orbit operations such as rendezvous, docking, servicing, and navigation. However, progress in this area is limited by the scarcity of annotated space imagery and by challenging visual-domain characteristics including severe illumination changes, low signal-to-noise ratio, and high contrast. We address Stream 1 of the SPARK 2026 Challenge, which requires a single model for spacecraft classification, detection, and fine-grained component segmentation across multiple target types. We propose a compact architecture that integrates a MobileNetV3 encoder with a U-Net-style decoder, combining computational efficiency with accurate dense prediction. Detection is derived analytically from the union of predicted component masks, avoiding a separate bounding-box regression head in the single-spacecraft setting. Our method achieved an overall leaderboard score of 0.9482, with task-specific scores of 1.0000 in classification, 0.9788 in detection, and 0.8917 in segmentation. The proposed approach ranked second overall in the SPARK 2026 Challenge, demonstrating that lightweight encoder-decoder architectures can deliver strong multi-task performance for practical onboard space vision systems.

</details>


### [103] [From Frames to Temporal Graphs: In-Context Egocentric Action Recognition with Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15417)
*Bessie Dominguez-Dager,Francisco Gomez-Donoso,Miguel Cazorla,Marc Pollefeys,Daniel Barath,Zuria Bauer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes converting egocentric videos into Temporal Action Graphs (symbolic representations) to decouple visual perception from reasoning, showing that VLMs perform better as symbolic reasoners than direct visual observers across 11 models on EGTEA and Epic-Kitchens-100 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: General-purpose VLMs struggle with fine-grained action reasoning in egocentric video when operating directly on raw pixels, motivating a decoupled approach that separates visual perception from symbolic reasoning.

Method: A multi-stage prompting pipeline: (1) dense natural language narratives over short temporal windows as a semantic bottleneck, (2) formalizing narratives into structured, open-vocabulary Temporal Action Graphs, (3) using graph representations for in-context learning or zero-shot reasoning with VLMs.

Result: On EGTEA and Epic-Kitchens-100 datasets, graph-based few-shot learning outperforms zero-shot frame/graph inference. Zero-shot graph reasoning remains competitive with pixel-based inference. Consistent across 11 open-weight VLMs (2B-235B parameters, 6 model families).

Conclusion: Current VLMs are more effective as symbolic reasoners than direct visual observers. Projecting video into the language domain offers a scalable, fine-tuning-free alternative that better leverages VLMs' latent reasoning strengths.

Abstract: Action reasoning in egocentric video requires capturing fine-grained transitions of hand-object interactions, a task where general-purpose Vision-Language Models (VLMs) often struggle when operating directly on raw pixels. We propose to decouple visual perception from symbolic reasoning by converting videos into Temporal Action Graphs. In a multi-stage prompting pipeline, we first generate dense natural language narratives over short temporal windows as a semantic bottleneck, then formalize them into structured, open-vocabulary graph representations. On the EGTEA and Epic-Kitchens-100 datasets, the symbolic representation unlocks efficient in-context learning: few-shot graph demonstrations yield substantial accuracy gains over zero-shot frame and graph-based inference alike. Even in the zero-shot setting, graph-based reasoning remains competitive with pixel-based inference despite potential pretraining contamination favoring the latter. Across 11 open-weight VLMs from 6 model families ranging from 2B to 235B parameters, our findings indicate that current VLMs are more effective as symbolic reasoners than as direct visual observers. By projecting video into the language domain, we provide a scalable, fine-tuning-free alternative to end-to-end approaches that better leverages these models' latent reasoning strengths. The code will be made public.

</details>


### [104] [Lesion-DDPM: Lesion-Enhanced 3D Diffusion for MS MRI Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15457)
*Weidong Zhang,Yongchan Jung,Shafayat Mowla Anik,Furen Xiao,Vasudevan Janarthanan,Enkhzaya Chuluunbaatar,Byeong Kil Lee,Jeeho Ryoo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Lesion-DDPM is a 3D conditional diffusion model for generating FLAIR MRI with multiple sclerosis lesions, using anatomical mask injection and lesion-weighted loss to improve synthesis. It outperforms GAN/diffusion baselines in reconstruction error and boosts downstream lesion segmentation Dice from 0.569 to 0.616 (synthetic-only) and 0.685 (when combined with real data).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Public MS datasets are small and heterogeneous, limiting robust ML model development, especially for generative models that must preserve small, sparse lesions in FLAIR MRI synthesis.

Method: Lesion-DDPM uses a 3D conditional diffusion framework with multi-level anatomical mask injection and a lesion-weighted reconstruction loss to emphasize lesion voxels while maintaining global brain structure.

Result: Lesion-DDPM achieved the lowest lesion-region reconstruction error. In downstream segmentation (3D U-Net), synthetic-only training achieved Dice 0.616 (vs. 0.569 for best competing method); adding Lesion-DDPM images to real training raised Dice to 0.685.

Conclusion: Lesion-DDPM is effective for lesion-aware FLAIR synthesis, improving both image quality and downstream segmentation performance, and augmenting real datasets with synthetic scans further boosts model robustness.

Abstract: 3D FLAIR MRI is widely recommended as one of the standard MRI sequences for brain imaging in multiple sclerosis (MS), but publicly available MS datasets remain relatively small and vary across scanners, acquisition protocols, and lesion patterns. This scarcity and variability hinder the development of robust neuroimaging machine learning models and are particularly challenging for generative models that aim to synthesize images while preserving small, sparse lesions. We propose Lesion-DDPM, a 3D conditional diffusion framework for lesion-aware FLAIR synthesis that incorporates multi-level anatomical mask injection together with a lesion-weighted reconstruction loss to emphasize lesion voxels while maintaining global brain structure. Using a curated subset of the MSLesSeg dataset, we compare Lesion-DDPM with representative state-of-the-art GAN- and diffusion-based models, assessing both image-generation metrics and downstream 3D U-Net segmentation. In our experiments, Lesion-DDPM achieved the lowest lesion-region reconstruction error among all methods. In a downstream 3D U-Net lesion segmentation task, a model trained only on Lesion-DDPM-generated scans and evaluated on real MRIs reached a Dice score of 0.616 compared with 0.569 for the best competing synthetic dataset. When Lesion-DDPM images were added to the real training set, the Dice score further increased to 0.685.

</details>


### [105] [Analyzing Visual Aircraft Representations with Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15468)
*Deepshik Sharma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper applies sparse autoencoders to decompose ConvNeXt representations into interpretable features for aircraft classification, finding partially interpretable patterns with limitations like polysemanticity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision models achieve strong classification performance but their internal representations are difficult to interpret, motivating the need for methods to decompose and understand these representations.

Method: Train a ConvNeXt classifier on FGVC-Aircraft, extract spatial activations from the final feature stage, train a sparse autoencoder, then analyze features via top-activating patches, activation strength, and class selectivity, followed by input-space and feature-space ablations.

Result: Sparse features correspond to recognizable aircraft structures; ablations show they affect class logits, margins, and prediction confidence, but limitations include polysemanticity and coarse spatial localization.

Conclusion: Sparse autoencoders can reveal partially interpretable, class-relevant visual features for aircraft recognition, though polysemanticity and coarse localization remain challenges.

Abstract: Vision models can achieve strong performance on classification tasks, but the internal representations supporting their predictions are often difficult to interpret. This work investigates whether sparse autoencoders can decompose intermediate representations of a vision model into interpretable features. We train a ConvNeXt classifier on the FGVC-Aircraft dataset, extract spatial activations from its final feature stage, and train a sparse autoencoder on these activations. The learned sparse features are analyzed using top-activating image patches, activation strength, and class selectivity. Qualitative visual inspection reveals that several features correspond to recognizable aircraft structures and visual patterns. We evaluate a subset of selected features using input-space and feature-space ablations, measuring how blurring image patches and suppressing sparse features affect class logits, classification margins, and prediction confidence. The results suggest that sparse autoencoders can reveal partially interpretable, class-relevant visual features associated with aircraft recognition, while also exposing limitations such as polysemanticity and coarse spatial localization.

</details>


### [106] [ST-DiffEye: Diffusion-based Continuous Gaze Generation via Joint Scanpath-Trajectory Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15486)
*Brian Nlong Zhao,Ozgur Kara,Junho Kim,James M. Rehg*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ST-DiffEye is a joint trajectory-scanpath diffusion framework for human gaze modeling that treats gaze variability as intrinsic, concatenating both eye-tracking trajectories and discrete scanpaths as input channels, and introduces CRPS-based evaluation for distribution-aware assessment, achieving SOTA on visual search and free-viewing benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generative gaze models supervise on only one of two gaze representations (continuous trajectories or discrete scanpaths) in isolation, but these modalities describe gaze at complementary scales and are jointly informative during training.

Method: ST-DiffEye is a diffusion framework that couples trajectories and scanpaths by concatenating them as additional raw input channels, requiring no architectural overhead beyond input/output channel expansion. Evaluation uses CRPS (Continuous Ranked Probability Score) to generalize any sequence similarity metric into a proper scoring rule for assessing both accuracy and diversity.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on task-driven visual search (both target-present and target-absent) and free-viewing benchmarks. Detailed ablations confirm the benefit of joint modeling and distribution-aware evaluation.

Conclusion: Treating gaze variability as a defining property and jointly modeling trajectories and scanpaths improves generative gaze modeling. The CRPS-based evaluation framework effectively captures the intrinsic variability of human gaze.

Abstract: We study the problem of human gaze modeling, which aims to generate the gaze patterns a viewer produces while observing a visual stimulus. Gaze is primarily captured through two modalities: continuous eye-tracking trajectories, which describe fine-grained motion dynamics, and discrete scanpaths, which describe high-level fixation structure. Because gaze varies substantially across viewers and trials, we treat this variability as a defining property rather than noise and model gaze as a stochastic generative process. Existing generative gaze models supervise on only one of these two representations in isolation. We hypothesize that trajectories and scanpaths describe gaze at complementary scales and are jointly informative during training, and test this hypothesis through ST-DiffEye, a joint trajectory-scanpath diffusion framework that couples both modalities by concatenating them as an additional raw input channel, requiring no architectural overhead beyond an input and output channel expansion. We further introduce a principled evaluation framework based on the Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS), which generalizes any existing sequence similarity metric into a proper scoring rule that jointly assesses the accuracy and diversity of generated gaze. Experiments on task-driven visual search, covering both target-present and target-absent scenarios, and on free-viewing benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance. These results, along with detailed ablations, confirm the benefit of joint modeling and the value of distribution-aware evaluation in capturing the intrinsic variability of human gaze. Project webpage: https://st-diffeye.github.io/

</details>


### [107] [Selective Synergistic Learning for Video Object-Centric Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15527)
*WonJun Moon,Jae-Pil Heo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SSync proposes Selective Synergistic Learning for video object-centric learning, selectively distilling reliable cues from encoder (boundary refinement) and decoder (interior denoising) with linear complexity, instead of costly dense alignment, while also introducing transitive pseudo-label merging to reduce slot redundancy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dense alignment strategies in VOCL propagate weaknesses (noisy encoder predictions, blurred decoder boundaries) and incur quadratic computational cost from exhaustive patch-to-patch comparison, limiting scalability.
