Many targets are near-convex, but enforcing shape priors uniformly on shape-heterogeneous data washes out concavities and fine details.

Method: Derives Hadwiger Shape Priors from Hadwiger's theorem using three 2D measures (area, perimeter, Euler characteristic) as a global regularizer. Proposes Conflict-Aware Objective Balancing (CAOB) to integrate these priors with segmentation by removing conflicting gradient components and adaptively regulating objective influences.

Result: Enables stable use of shape priors on shape-heterogeneous data without erasing genuine concavities or fine structural details, improving segmentation accuracy across organs and modalities.

Conclusion: HadBalance provides a unified, plug-and-play geometric regularization framework that generalizes across diverse medical segmentation targets while preserving structural details through conflict-aware gradient balancing.

Abstract: Precise biomedical image segmentation is crucial for clinical diagnosis. Geometric cues (e.g., boundary, shape, and topology) can improve structural consistency, yet most are task-specific and lack a unified geometric foundation that generalizes across organs and modalities. We are motivated by the observation that several medical segmentation targets can be approximated as globally near-convex shapes. A convex region is one in which any two interior points can be connected by a line segment entirely contained within the region. In practice, medical targets may exhibit small local concavities or boundary irregularities; we refer to such globally convex-like shapes as near-convex. Motivated by this, we derive Hadwiger Shape Priors from Hadwiger's theorem as an interpretable global regularizer using three 2D measures: area A, perimeter P, and Euler characteristic chi, enabling transfer across organs and modalities. However, because medical datasets are shape-heterogeneous, enforcing near-convex priors uniformly can over-regularize non-convex anatomy with significant concavities, washing out concavities and fine details and degrading segmentation accuracy. To address this challenge, we propose Conflict-Aware Objective Balancing (CAOB), which integrates shape priors with segmentation in a gradient-aware manner. For each prior, CAOB removes only the gradient component that conflicts with segmentation while preserving the remaining aligned component, and adaptively regulates objective influences to prevent prior dominance. This enables stable use of shape priors on shape-heterogeneous data without erasing genuine concavities or fine structural details. We call this plug-and-play framework HadBalance.

</details>


### [160] [Mind the Gap: Diagnosing Constraint Discovery Failures in Text-in-Image Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15982)
*Rui Gui*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Multimodal LLMs struggle to discover unstated visual constraints (46% recall) unless explicitly given; causal explanations help most (0.782 recall), but naive self-discovery can hurt task performance due to false positives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether multimodal reasoning failures stem from inability to recognize visual content vs. inability to identify which visual dependencies are relevant under a specific task.

Method: Constructed 461 diagnostic edit-induced constraint cases (19 subtypes) across 4 MLLMs; compared unguided prompting vs. explicit constraint provision; used oracle-field decomposition to compare cue types (causal explanations, region names, type labels).

Result: Unguided recall 46% vs. 94% with explicit constraints; causal explanations yield 0.782 recall vs. 0.610 (region names) and 0.646 (type labels); higher self-discovery recall does not guarantee better downstream task performance due to false positives.

Conclusion: A major failure mode is deciding which unstated dependencies to surface, not just recognizing content; precision-aware constraint elicitation (not just recall) is needed for effective multimodal reasoning.

Abstract: A key challenge in multimodal reasoning is determining which visual dependencies become relevant under a specific task, rather than merely recognizing visible content. We study this through edit-induced constraint discovery in text-in-image editing, a controlled diagnostic setting where a local text change can activate secondary consistency constraints: given a valid editing instruction and an image, can a model identify the secondary regions that must also change? Across 461 diagnostic cases, four MLLMs, and 19 constraint subtypes, models recover only 46% case-level macro recall under unguided prompting versus 94% when constraints are explicitly provided, suggesting that a substantial portion of the failure arises when models must decide which unstated dependencies to surface. Oracle-field decomposition shows that case-specific causal explanations are the most effective partial guidance (0.782 recall), above region names (0.610) or type labels (0.646), suggesting that edit-specific causal cues account for much of the oracle gain. A downstream experiment further shows that higher self-discovery recall does not necessarily improve task performance: unverified self-discovery introduces false positives that offset recall gains, motivating precision-aware constraint elicitation.

</details>


### [161] [A Text Recognition Dataset from Sahidic Coptic Ancient Manuscripts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15987)
*Fabio Quattrini,Carmine Zaccagnino,Costanza Bianchi,Silvia Cascianelli,Rita Cucchiara*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces SCAM, a challenging line-level handwritten text recognition dataset from Sahidic Coptic ancient manuscripts, highlighting the performance gap between modern well-resourced scripts and historically low-resource scenarios through benchmarking various state-of-the-art approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address Handwritten Text Recognition (HTR) in low-resource scenarios caused by underrepresented languages, rare scripts, and degraded historical documents, and to provide a benchmark for this challenging setting.

Method: Built a new line-level dataset (SCAM) from digitized Sahidic Coptic manuscripts with heterogeneous acquisition conditions and typical degradations; benchmarked several state-of-the-art HTR approaches based on different paradigms.

Result: Current HTR approaches show significantly lower performance on this historically grounded, low-resource dataset compared to well-resourced modern scripts, highlighting a substantial performance gap.

Conclusion: SCAM provides a reference point for future HTR development in low-resource and historically degraded scenarios, underlining the need for further advancements to close the performance gap with modern-script HTR.

Abstract: In this work, we target Handwritten Text Recognition (HTR) in low-resource scenarios, which arise from underrepresented languages, rare scripts, and degraded visual conditions typical of historical documents. We introduce SCAM (Sahidic Coptic Ancient Manuscripts), a new line-level dataset built from digitized ancient manuscripts written in the extinct Sahidic Coptic dialect. The dataset reflects a realistic and challenging setting, as it combines heterogeneous acquisition conditions across libraries with typical manuscript degradations such as ink fading, bleed-through, and material deterioration. In addition to visual complexity, SCAM poses significant linguistic challenges due to the scarcity of resources for Sahidic Coptic, its uncommon alphabet, and dialect-specific diacritics. To support research in low-resource HTR, we benchmark several state-of-the-art approaches based on different paradigms, highlighting their limitations and strengths in this setting. Our results underline the gap between current HTR performance on well-resourced modern scripts and historically grounded, low-resource scenarios, thus providing a reference point for future developments.

</details>


### [162] [Multi-Task Tennis Stroke Biomechanics Analysis Using MediaPipe Pose](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15992)
*Jigyashman Hazarika*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multi-task RGB-video pipeline predicts tennis stroke type, shot direction, and posture quality, then generates coaching tips. Using a compact transformer trained on 1,281 pro/amateur strokes, it achieves 83.7% stroke accuracy but fails to transfer direction prediction cross-player.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To automate tennis biomechanics analysis from plain video, enabling AI coaching without specialized motion-capture hardware or manual stroke annotation.

Method: MediaPipe Pose → weighted joint velocity score for automatic stroke detection → 30-frame×39-feature sequences → TennisTransformerGPU (4 layers, 4 heads, d=128, 564K params) with 3 output heads for stroke type, shot direction, and posture grade → rule-based feedback layer.

Result: 83.7% stroke-type, 61.9% direction, 62.6% posture accuracy (random split). Cross-player: stroke type 82.9% (robust), direction collapses to majority-class baseline (no transfer). World coordinates are critical: image-space coords drop cross-player accuracy from 83%→47% (stroke) and 68%→21% (direction).

Conclusion: World-coordinate pose representation enables robust cross-player stroke recognition, but direction prediction fails to generalize across different skill levels, requiring future work on diversity or domain adaptation.

Abstract: We built a multi-task pipeline for tennis stroke biomechanics from plain RGB video. On top of pose-based stroke recognition, it adds two new tasks, predicting shot direction and grading posture quality, plus a rule-based feedback layer that suggests coaching tips. Strokes are found automatically using a weighted joint velocity score, s(t) = 0.5 v_wrist + 0.3 m_elbow + 0.2 m_shoulder, removing the need for manual annotation. Pose comes from MediaPipe Pose Landmarker (33 landmarks, metric world coordinates), with each stroke turned into a 30-frame by 39-feature sequence for TennisTransformerGPU, a compact 564,103-parameter transformer (4 layers, 4 heads, d=128) with three parallel output heads. Trained on 1,281 labeled strokes from 7 pros and 1 amateur across 11 videos, it hits 83.7% stroke-type accuracy, 61.9% on direction, and 62.6% on posture under a random 80/20 split. The interesting test is cross-player: train on pros, evaluate on the amateur. Stroke type barely budges, 82.9%, a 0.8% drop. Direction prediction does not transfer; it just falls back to the majority class. An ablation shows why world coordinates matter so much here: switching to image-space landmarks tanks cross-player stroke-type accuracy from 83% to 47% and direction from 68% to 21%. Everything runs on Kaggle's free T4 GPU tier and is fully reproducible.

</details>


### [163] [Stringalign: Moving beyond summary statistics with a transparent Unicode-aware tool for evaluating automatic transcription models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16015)
*Yngve Mardal Moe,Marie Roald*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Stringalign is a Python library for transparent, flexible, and reproducible evaluation of text transcription tasks (HTR, OCR, ASR), addressing ambiguity in CER/WER metrics by ensuring preprocessing transparency and providing error analysis tools, while following FAIR principles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current string comparison metrics like CER and WER have ambiguous definitions due to inconsistent preprocessing (normalization/tokenization), leading to opaque and confusing evaluation results. There is a need for tools that enable flexible, reproducible, and transparent evaluation across diverse transcription models.

Method: Stringalign is a Python library that enforces transparent and replicable preprocessing (normalization and tokenization), provides tools to analyze both error rates and error types, offers visualization capabilities, and adheres to FAIR principles while remaining lightweight and easy to integrate.

Result: Through examples, the authors demonstrate that existing tools produce opaque and sometimes confusing results, whereas Stringalign provides an easy-to-use and unambiguous alternative for string comparison in transcription evaluation.

Conclusion: Stringalign addresses key limitations of existing evaluation tools by ensuring preprocessing transparency, enabling detailed error analysis beyond summary statistics, and providing a FAIR-compliant, lightweight solution for researchers working on HTR, OCR, and ASR tasks.

Abstract: Comparing text strings is crucial when evaluating and understanding the performance of various text processing tasks such as document recognition and audio transcription. With an increasingly complex landscape of AI-based handwritten text recognition (HTR), optical character recognition (OCR) and automatic speech recognition (ASR) models, there is a need for tools that facilitate evaluation in a flexible and reproducible way. This paper presents Stringalign, a Python library designed to simplify the evaluation process for automatic transcription projects and facilitate transparent evaluation. Stringalign's tools to examine and visualise both the rate of errors and the types of errors a model makes, give insights into possible improvements and help inform model selection for a particular task. Widely used string comparison metrics, such as the character and word error rates (CER and WER), although useful, can be ambiguous due to varying definitions of what constitutes a character and a word. Stringalign addresses this challenge by ensuring all preprocessing (i.e. normalisation and tokenisation) is transparent and easily replicable, and by providing tools to move beyond summary statistics and analyse common model errors. Moreover, Stringalign adheres to FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable) principles for research software while staying lightweight and easy to adapt into researchers existing workflows. In this paper, we discuss challenges with character and word level string comparisons and show through examples that where existing tools can yield opaque and sometimes confusing results, Stringalign provides an easy-to-use and unambiguous alternative.

</details>


### [164] [The Third Challenge on Image Denoising at NTIRE 2026: Methods and Results](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16031)
*Lei Sun,Hang Guo,Bin Ren,Shaolin Su,Xian Wang,Danda Pani Paudel,Luc Van Gool,Radu Timofte,Yawei Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NTIRE 2026 Challenge on high-noise image denoising (σ=50) benchmarks unconstrained neural architectures from 20 finalist teams (out of 116 registrants), focusing purely on PSNR performance without limitations on model size or compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To push the boundaries of image denoising under high noise levels by removing constraints on parameter count and computational cost, enabling exploration of maximal restoration quality.

Method: Competition-based benchmarking: 116 teams registered, 20 finalists submitted advanced neural architectures trained on AWGN-corrupted images (σ=50), evaluated solely by PSNR.

Result: Synthesized contributions from 20 finalist teams, establishing a comprehensive snapshot of state-of-the-art unconstrained image denoising performance.

Conclusion: The challenge reveals the latest technical innovations in high-noise image restoration and sets new benchmarks for the field, demonstrating the value of unconstrained competition tracks.

Abstract: This paper reports on the NTIRE 2026 Challenge on Image Denoising, specifically focusing on the high-noise regime ($σ= 50$). The competition investigates advanced neural architectures designed to restore high-fidelity details from images corrupted by additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN). Unlike constrained benchmarks, this track emphasizes peak quantitative performance, measured by Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR), without limitations on parameter count or computational overhead. By synthesizing contributions from 20 finalist teams out of 116 registrants, this report benchmarks the latest technical innovations and provides a comprehensive snapshot of the current state-of-the-art in unconstrained image restoration.

</details>


### [165] [Trusting Right Predictions for Wrong Reasons: A LIME Based Analysis of Deep Learning Interpretability in Lung Cancer Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16036)
*Samarpan Poudel,Vladislav D Veksler*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Three deep learning models (CNN, ResNet50, ViT) achieve high accuracy (93-99%) and near-perfect prediction agreement on lung cancer classification, but LIME explanation analysis reveals dramatically different reasoning patterns (correlation <0.26), showing that prediction agreement does not imply reasoning consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lung cancer diagnosis is critical, but prior deep learning evaluations focused solely on predictive accuracy without examining model decision-making processes, creating a gap in understanding how models actually reason.

Method: Three architecturally distinct models (CNN, pretrained ResNet50, Vision Transformer) were trained on the IQ-OTH/NCCD lung cancer CT dataset. LIME was applied for interpretability, and a dual-correlation framework measured both prediction and explanation agreement across model pairs.

Result: ResNet50 achieved 98.61% accuracy, CNN 97.91%, ViT 93.75%, all with 0.99 ROC-AUC. Prediction correlations >0.99 across pairs, but LIME explanation correlations <0.26. Misclassified samples showed attention outside lung parenchyma vs. correct predictions focused within lung regions.

Conclusion: Prediction agreement is a poor proxy for reasoning consistency; interpretability evaluation must be treated as an independent validation criterion alongside predictive performance in clinical AI systems.

Abstract: Lung cancer is the leading cause of cancer-related mortality, with approximately 2.5 million new cases and 1.8 million deaths annually, making reliable diagnosis a clinical priority. Although deep learning models have achieved strong performance in lung cancer classification, evaluation has largely focused on predictive accuracy, leaving their decision-making processes insufficiently examined. This study compares three architecturally distinct models: a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), a pretrained ResNet50, and a Vision Transformer (ViT), trained on the IQ-OTH/NCCD lung cancer CT dataset. Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations (LIME) were applied to investigate model reasoning. In addition to standard performance metrics, a dual-correlation framework was introduced to measure both prediction agreement and explanation agreement across model pairs. All three models achieved strong classification performance, with ResNet50 attaining 98.61% accuracy, CNN 97.91%, and ViT 93.75%, while all achieved ROC-AUC scores of 0.99. Prediction correlations exceeded 0.99 across all model pairs, indicating highly consistent outputs. However, LIME explanation correlations remained below 0.26, revealing substantial differences in the image regions used to reach those predictions. Analysis of misclassified samples further identified a consistent spatial pattern: incorrect predictions were associated with attention outside the lung parenchyma, whereas correct predictions focused primarily within lung regions. These findings demonstrate that prediction agreement is a poor proxy for reasoning consistency, and that interpretability evaluation must be treated as an independent validation criterion alongside predictive performance in clinical AI systems.

</details>


### [166] [PointDiffusion: Diffusion-Based Scene Completion in the Point Cloud Domain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16048)
*Chidera Agbasiere,Mikhail Sannikov,Faith Ogunwoye,Erik Shaikhiev,Alex Kozinov,Ilya Mikhalchuk,Iana Zhura,Dzmitry Tsetserukou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a multi-token Gaussian VAE with cross-attention pooling for stable scene-scale LiDAR compression and an anchor-based ICP ground truth refinement pipeline, enabling a scaffold-free single-step diffusion completion model that achieves 16x reduction in Chamfer distance, outperforms prior methods (LiDiff, ScoreLiDAR) by 10-19%, and operates at 25-143x lower latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reconstructing dense 3D scenes from sparse LiDAR point clouds is challenging due to (1) unstable global representations in existing object-level autoencoders at outdoor scale, (2) corrupted ground truth data from odometry drift degrading supervision quality, and (3) prohibitive multi-step diffusion inference latency for real-time deployment.

Method: (1) Multi-token Gaussian VAE with cross-attention pooling for stable scene-scale LiDAR compression; (2) Anchor-based ICP ground truth refinement pipeline to eliminate drift-induced noise; (3) Scaffold-free single-step diffusion completion model.

Result: ~16x reduction in squared Chamfer distance on SemanticKITTI seq. 08 (0.396 m² → 0.024 m²); surpasses LiDiff by 17-19% and ScoreLiDAR by 10-11%; operates at 25-143x lower inference latency.

Conclusion: Data quality dominates model design in this regime; multi-token latent spaces provide a stable first stage for latent diffusion-based scene completion.

Abstract: Reconstructing dense 3D scenes from sparse LiDAR point clouds is a fundamental challenge in autonomous driving, where latent diffusion models offer a promising solution. However, existing approaches rely on object-level autoencoders that collapse into unstable global representations at outdoor scale and suffer from ground truth data corrupted by odometry drift that systematically degrades supervision quality. Furthermore, multi-step diffusion inference incurs prohibitive latency for real-time deployment. We propose a novel multi-token Gaussian VAE with cross-attention pooling for stable scene-scale LiDAR compression, combined with an anchor-based ICP ground truth refinement pipeline that eliminates drift-induced noise from training supervision. Together, these components enable a scaffold-free single-step diffusion completion model that achieves an approximately 16x reduction in squared Chamfer distance on SemanticKITTI seq. 08 (0.396 m^2 to 0.024 m^2), surpasses LiDiff and ScoreLiDAR by 17-19% and 10-11%, respectively, and operates at 25-143x lower inference latency. Our results demonstrate that data quality dominates model design in this regime and that multi-token latent spaces provide a stable first stage for latent diffusion-based scene completion.

</details>


### [167] [Stepwise Token Selection for Efficient Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16067)
*Landi He,Shawn Young,Lijian Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a sequential pointer-style selection mechanism for visual token pruning in MLLMs, which dynamically determines which tokens to keep and when to stop, outperforming fixed-ratio baselines and achieving 1.88x speed-up while preserving 94.6% accuracy under 88.9% token removal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In MLLMs, inference cost is dominated by the visual token prefix rather than the language backbone, motivating token reduction for efficiency. Existing methods assign independent importance scores and retain a fixed number of top tokens, assuming token independence and uniform compression across inputs.

Method: Reformulates visual token pruning as sequential decision-making with a pointer-style selection mechanism that iteratively chooses informative tokens conditioned on previous selections, and dynamically determines when to stop via a learned termination action. Uses a differentiable relaxation with variance-preserving noise interpolation for end-to-end training.

Result: On LLaVA-v1.5-7B and Qwen2.5-VL-7B, the method consistently outperforms fixed-ratio baselines across compression levels. Under 88.9% token removal, it preserves 94.6% accuracy with 1.88x speed-up in prefill latency.

Conclusion: The sequential decision-making approach with learned termination is more effective than fixed-ratio pruning for visual token reduction in MLLMs, enabling joint optimization of both subset selection and compression size.

Abstract: In multimodal large language models (MLLMs), inference cost is largely dominated by the visual token prefix rather than the language backbone, making token reduction a key factor for improving efficiency. Existing approaches typically assign independent importance scores to visual tokens and retain a fixed number of top-ranked tokens, implicitly assuming token independence and a uniform compression ratio across inputs. In this work, we reformulate visual token pruning as a sequential decision-making process. Specifically, we introduce a pointer-style selection mechanism that iteratively chooses informative tokens, conditioning each decision on previously selected ones, and dynamically determines when to stop via a learned termination action. This enables joint optimization of both the selected subset and its size. To enable end-to-end training under standard language modeling objectives, we design a differentiable relaxation based on a variance-preserving noise interpolation scheme, allowing gradients to propagate through the discrete selection process. Extensive experiments on LLaVA-v1.5-7B and Qwen2.5-VL-7B demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms fixed-ratio baselines across different compression levels. Under aggressive pruning that removes 88.9% of visual tokens, our method preserves 94.6% of the original accuracy while achieving a 1.88x speed-up in prefill latency.

</details>


### [168] [Tool-IQA: Augmenting Image Quality Assessment with Simple Tools](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16082)
*Guanyi Qin,Junjie Zhang,Chunming He,Yibing Fu,Jie Liang,Tianhe Wu,Lei Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Tool-IQA proposes a tool-augmented workflow for VLM-based Image Quality Assessment, using Magnifier and Gamma Corrector tools with a structured pipeline (initial observation, tool-augmented inspection, final quantification) and a batch-aware training strategy, achieving SOTA results (e.g., 0.854 PLCC on CLIVE).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static one-shot scoring in current VLM-based IQA methods limits assessment due to (1) insufficient perception of local details at global scale, and (2) original intensity distribution overwhelming visibility, unlike human dynamic visual inspection.

Method: Tool-IQA equips VLMs with two view tools: Magnifier (local detail inspection) and Gamma Corrector (uncover hidden artifacts). It follows a three-stage pipeline: initial observation with rubric notes, tool-augmented in-depth inspection, and final calibrated quantification. A batch-aware training strategy rewards beneficial tool interactions.

Result: Outperforms existing SOTA models across multiple IQA benchmarks, achieving 0.854 PLCC on the CLIVE dataset.

Conclusion: Shifting from passive static scoring to tool-augmented dynamic assessment with effective tool calling and calibrated scoring significantly improves VLM-based IQA performance.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have been increasingly adopted for Image Quality Assessment (IQA). However, current methods typically employ a static one-shot scoring paradigm, despite the fact that humans assess image quality through dynamic visual inspection, e.g., selectively adjusting views to verify details and subtle artifacts. Specifically, relying solely on a single-pass observation introduces two primary limitations: first, perceiving the image only at a global scale restricts the assessment of finer local details; second, the original intensity distribution of the image may overwhelm the visibility, leading to insufficient inspection of image quality. To address these issues, we propose Tool-IQA, shifting the assessment mechanism from passive scoring to a tool-augmented workflow. In particular, we equip VLMs with simple yet effective view tools: a Magnifier to inspect local details, and a Gamma Corrector to uncover visibility and hidden artifacts. The assessment follows a structured pipeline that consists of an initial observation with rubric notes, a tool-augmented in-depth inspection, and a final quantification for calibrated quality score. Furthermore, to ensure efficient and purposeful tool callings, we introduce a batch-aware training strategy to reward tool interactions that can yield positive contributions rather than simply encouraging usage. Experiments on a variety of IQA benchmarks demonstrate that, with effective tool calling and calibrated assessment, our proposed Tool-IQA significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art models, e.g., it achieves a PLCC of 0.854 on the challenging CLIVE dataset.

</details>


### [169] [VinQA: Visual Elements Interleaved Long-form Answer Generation for Real-World Multimodal Document QA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16092)
*Young Rok Jang,Hyesoo Kong,Kyunghwan An,Jae Sub Huh,Gyeonghun Kim,Stanley Jungkyu Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VinQA is a dataset for multimodal document QA requiring long-form answers with interleaved text and visual element citations. The paper compares two encoding methods (Page Encoding vs. Modality Encoding), proposes evaluation frameworks (M-GroSE, Visual Source F1), and shows that fine-tuning open Qwen2.5-VL models on VinQA narrows the gap with proprietary frontier models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MLLMs for document QA produce text-only responses, underutilizing visual elements (tables, charts, diagrams) present in real-world documents. The paper aims to enable long-form answers where cited visual elements are explicitly interleaved with supporting text and grounded in document pages.

Method: The paper introduces (1) VinQA dataset for long-form multimodal answer generation; (2) two encoding methods: Page Encoding (full-page images with bounding boxes) and Modality Encoding (separately parsed text and cropped visual elements); (3) M-GroSE evaluation framework for completeness, answer relevancy, faithfulness, and unanswerability; and (4) Visual Source F1 metric for citation accuracy. Fine-tuning Qwen2.5-VL models is also explored.

Result: Proprietary frontier models achieve best overall scores on VinQA, but fine-tuning open Qwen2.5-VL models substantially improves performance and narrows the gap. Modality Encoding is more robust initially for complex documents; Page Encoding reaches comparable levels after training. Visual G-Eval confirms semantically appropriate insertion of visual elements with faithful text.

Conclusion: Fine-tuning open-source MLLMs on the VinQA dataset significantly improves multimodal document QA performance, and Page Encoding can compete with Modality Encoding after training, suggesting potential for simpler end-to-end approaches without explicit parsing.

Abstract: Real-world documents combine text with tables, charts, photographs, and diagrams arranged in diverse layouts, yet existing research on multimodal large language models (MLLMs) for document QA predominantly produces text-only responses, underutilizing these visual elements. We introduce VinQA, a dataset for long-form answer generation where cited visual elements are explicitly interleaved with their supporting text and grounded in relevant document pages. To support this task, we study two encoding methods for feeding raw document page images into an MLLM, along with their visual-element citation mechanisms: (1) Page Encoding, which directly encodes full-page images with bounding boxes of visual elements and treats these boxed regions as citable units; and (2) Modality Encoding, which parses each page to extract text and crop visual elements, encodes them separately, and uses these cropped elements as citable units. In our experiments, we propose M-GroSE, a multimodal evaluation framework extending GroUSE to assess answers along four dimensions: completeness, answer relevancy, faithfulness, and unanswerability. We additionally report Visual Source F1 to directly measure visual citation accuracy. Although proprietary frontier models still achieve the best overall scores on the VinQA test split, fine-tuning open Qwen2.5-VL models on the training split substantially improves their performance and narrows this gap. Modality Encoding is initially more robust for complex documents with long text, many visual elements, and diverse citation requirements. After training on VinQA, however, Page Encoding reaches a comparable level, competing effectively even without the explicit parsing used in Modality Encoding. Finally, Visual G-Eval, an MLLM-based judge, confirms that fine-tuned models insert visual elements at semantically appropriate positions with faithful supporting text.

</details>


### [170] [SceneCraft: Interactive System for Image Editing via Scene Graph](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16103)
*Duc-Manh Phan,Ngoc-Dai Tran,Duy-Khang Do,Tam V. Nguyen,Minh-Triet Tran,Trung-Nghia Le*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SceneCraft is an interactive framework that uses editable scene graphs to enable intuitive, structured control for natural language-driven image editing in complex scenes, reducing reliance on precise text prompts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generative AI systems for image editing struggle with complex scenes containing multiple interacting objects because they depend on users crafting precise text prompts, which is cumbersome and prone to ambiguity.

Method: SceneCraft represents images as editable scene graphs that users can directly manipulate for spatial/relational operations. Graph modifications are automatically translated into context-aware editing prompts, which are then dispatched to multiple state-of-the-art generative models.

Result: SceneCraft provides more intuitive control, significantly reduces cognitive burden of manual prompt engineering, and generates outputs rated higher in quality and fidelity by users.

Conclusion: SceneCraft effectively bridges user intent and model execution through structured scene graph representation, offering a superior alternative to text-prompt-dependent editing in complex scenes.

Abstract: Recent advances in generative AI have enabled natural language-driven image editing, yet existing systems often fail in complex scenes with multiple interacting objects because they rely heavily on users crafting precise text prompts. To address the absence of structured control, we propose SceneCraft, a novel interactive framework that bridges user intent and model execution by representing images as editable scene graphs. Instead of guessing text prompts through trial and error, users interact directly with a visual graph to perform complex spatial and relational operations. These graph modifications are automatically translated into precise, context-aware editing prompts, effectively eliminating linguistic ambiguity. To ensure robust and diverse results, structured prompts are dispatched to multiple state-of-the-art generative models. Evaluations across diverse editing scenarios show that SceneCraft provides a more intuitive control mechanism, significantly reducing the cognitive burden of manual prompt engineering while generating outputs that users consistently rate as higher in quality and fidelity.

</details>


### [171] [EdgeZSAD: Practical Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection on Edge Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16119)
*Taewan Cho,Andrew Jaeyong Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EdgeZSAD is a compact zero-shot anomaly detection system using a TinyViT-21M-512 backbone (<1/10 the parameters of ViT-L), asymmetric global-local readout (EdgeGLR), and source-side training on Real-IAD-DR, achieving competitive performance (91.6 AUROC on MVTec-AD, 88.2 on VisA) while being directly deployable on edge devices like Jetson Orin Nano and RB5 Gen2 with negligible deployment drift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zero-shot anomaly detection models for industrial inspection typically rely on massive ViT-L foundation backbones (~300M parameters) that exceed the memory and operator constraints of embedded edge hardware, creating a gap between research capability and practical deployment.

Method: The system uses a TinyViT-21M-512 backbone with an asymmetric global-local readout (EdgeGLR) and a source-side training recipe on Real-IAD-DR, following a source-trained, target-unseen protocol with a single checkpoint evaluated across six benchmarks.

Result: Achieves 91.6 AUROC on MVTec-AD and 88.2 on VisA across three runs, remains deployable on Jetson Orin Nano Super and RB5 Gen2, and exhibits <0.2 point image-AUROC drift across six device-rescored benchmarks.

Conclusion: Compact ZSAD systems can achieve competitive performance while meeting edge deployment constraints, with minimal performance drift when exported to target hardware.

Abstract: Industrial inspection needs zero-shot anomaly detection (ZSAD) that remains useful under edge deployment constraints. Recent methods often rely on ViT-L foundation backbones (~300M parameters), which exceed the memory and operator budget of typical embedded hardware. We study this regime through EdgeZSAD, a compact reference system built around a TinyViT-21M-512 backbone, an asymmetric global-local readout (EdgeGLR), and a reproducible source-side training recipe (Real-IAD-DR). We train a single checkpoint in a source-trained, target-unseen protocol and evaluate it across six industrial benchmarks. Across three independent runs, the resulting model reaches an average image AUROC of 91.6 on MVTec-AD and 88.2 on VisA, while remaining directly deployable on Jetson Orin Nano Super (TensorRT FP16) and RB5 Gen2 (QNN GPU FP16). Across the six device-rescored benchmarks, image-AUROC drift stays below 0.2 points, indicating that the exported graph preserves host-side ranking behavior in the evaluated deployment setting.

</details>


### [172] [Training-Free Open-Vocabulary Visual Grounding for Remote Sensing Images and Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16124)
*Ke Li,Di Wang,Yongshan Zhu,Ting Wang,Weiping Ni,Tao Lei,Quan Wang,Xinbo Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RSVG-ZeroOV is a training-free, zero-shot framework for open-vocabulary remote sensing visual grounding that uses frozen foundation models (VLMs and DMs) via an Overview-Focus-Evolve paradigm, achieving competitive results against supervised methods across image and video benchmarks without task-specific annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RSVG methods rely on costly task-specific manual annotations, limiting their ability to generalize to open-vocabulary queries involving novel objects, fine-grained attributes, complex spatial relationships, and functional semantics in diverse real-world geospatial scenarios.

Method: The framework uses an Overview-Focus-Evolve paradigm: (1) Overview uses a VLM to extract cross-attention maps capturing semantic correlations; (2) Focus leverages a diffusion model's fine-grained priors to compensate for object structure/shape; (3) Evolve introduces an attention evolution module to suppress irrelevant activations. For video, a query-relevant key-frame selector and temporal propagator extend image grounding to spatio-temporal grounding.

Result: Extensive experiments on six image and video grounding benchmarks show RSVG-ZeroOV consistently outperforms existing zero-shot baselines and achieves competitive/superior performance compared with weakly- and fully-supervised methods.

Conclusion: RSVG-ZeroOV demonstrates that frozen generic foundation models can effectively perform zero-shot open-vocabulary RSVG without training or task-specific annotations, and successfully extends image-level grounding to video via key-frame selection and temporal propagation.

Abstract: Remote sensing visual grounding (RSVG) aims to localize a referred target in a remote sensing image or video according to a natural language expression. Existing RSVG methods usually rely on task-specific manual annotations, which are costly to collect and inevitably limited in covering the diversity of real-world geospatial scenarios. As a result, they often struggle to generalize to open-vocabulary queries involving novel objects, fine-grained attributes, complex spatial relationships, and functional semantics. In this paper, we propose RSVG-ZeroOV, a training-free framework that leverages frozen generic foundation models for zero-shot open-vocabulary RSVG. RSVG-ZeroOV follows an Overview-Focus-Evolve paradigm, which exploits the distinct yet complementary attention patterns of vision-language models (VLMs) and diffusion models (DMs) to progressively generate precise grounding results. Specifically, (i) Overview utilizes a VLM to extract cross-attention maps that capture semantic correlations between the referring expression and visual regions; (ii) Focus leverages the fine-grained modeling priors of a DM to compensate for object structure and shape information often overlooked by VLM attention; and (iii) Evolve introduces a simple yet effective attention evolution module to suppress irrelevant activations, yielding purified object masks. To handle video inputs, we further present Video RSVG-ZeroOV, which extends image-level grounding to spatio-temporal grounding through a query-relevant key-frame selector and a temporal propagator, enabling efficient and temporally coherent video grounding without video annotations or fine-tuning. Extensive experiments on six image and video grounding benchmarks show that RSVG-ZeroOV consistently outperforms existing zero-shot baselines and achieves competitive or superior performance compared with weakly- and fully-supervised methods.

</details>


### [173] [Shift-and-Sum Quantization for Visual Autoregressive Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16131)
*Jaehyeon Moon,Bumsub Ham*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a post-training quantization (PTQ) framework for visual autoregressive models (VAR), addressing two key challenges: large reconstruction errors in attention-value products and frequency-probability mismatch in codebook sampling. It introduces shift-and-sum quantization and a resampling strategy, achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-training quantization for visual autoregressive models (VAR) remains unexplored, and existing PTQ methods fail due to two key challenges: large reconstruction errors in attention-value products (especially at coarse scales with high attention scores) and a discrepancy between codebook entry sampling frequencies and their predicted probabilities caused by limited calibration data.

Method: (1) Shift-and-sum quantization: reduces reconstruction errors by aggregating quantized results from symmetrically shifted duplicates of value tokens. (2) Resampling strategy for calibration data: aligns sampling frequencies of codebook entries with their predicted probabilities.

Result: Consistent improvements across various VAR architectures on class-conditional image generation, inpainting, outpainting, and class-conditional editing tasks, achieving a new state of the art in PTQ for VAR.

Conclusion: The proposed PTQ framework effectively addresses the unique challenges of quantizing visual autoregressive models, enabling efficient deployment while maintaining high generation quality across multiple tasks.

Abstract: Post-training quantization (PTQ) enables efficient deployment of deep networks using a small set of data. Its application to visual autoregressive models (VAR), however, remains relatively unexplored. We identify two key challenges for applying PTQ to VAR: (i) large reconstruction errors in attention-value products, especially at coarse scales where high attention scores occur more frequently; and (ii) a discrepancy between the sampling frequencies of codebook entries and their predicted probabilities due to limited calibration data. To address these challenges, we propose a PTQ framework tailored for VAR. First, we introduce a shift-and-sum quantization method that reduces reconstruction errors by aggregating quantized results from symmetrically shifted duplicates of value tokens. Second, we present a resampling strategy for calibration data that aligns sampling frequencies of codebook entries with their predicted probabilities. Experiments on class-conditional image generation, inpainting, outpainting, and class-conditional editing show consistent improvements across VAR architectures, establishing a new state of the art in PTQ for VAR.

</details>


### [174] [A Comprehensive Survey of Medical Image Segmentation: Challenges, Benchmarks, and Beyond](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16153)
*Pengyu Zhu,Xiaojing Zhang,Kunbo Zhang,Chunyan Zhang,Zhenyu Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A comprehensive review of medical image segmentation covering U-Net, Transformer, and SAM-based methods, public datasets, evaluation metrics, and challenges, with a unified analytical framework and public GitHub resources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a systematic review that organizes U-Net, Transformer, and SAM methods within a unified framework, unlike prior surveys focused on single model families or specific clinical applications, to guide future research and clinical translation.

Method: A comprehensive literature review covering widely used public datasets, representative methods based on U-Net, Transformer, and SAM architectures, evaluation metrics, and analysis of major challenges from multiple perspectives.

Result: A unified analytical framework organizing three major model families with focus on segmentation accuracy and efficiency improvement, plus a public GitHub repository with related resources.

Conclusion: This review aims to guide future research and support clinical translation of medical image segmentation.

Abstract: Medical image segmentation plays a critical role in clinical diagnostics, treatment planning, disease monitoring, and neurological disorder identification. This article presents a comprehensive review of its systematic development, covering widely used public datasets, representative methods built on the U-Net, Transformer, and SAM architectures, and key evaluation metrics with their differences, followed by an analysis of major challenges from multiple perspectives. Unlike surveys that focus on a single model family or a specific clinical application, this review organizes U-Net-, Transformer-, and SAM-based methods within a unified analytical framework, with a particular focus on their effectiveness in improving segmentation accuracy and efficiency. This work aims to guide future research and support clinical translation of medical image segmentation, with all related resources publicly available in our GitHub repository: https://github.com/andrew-pengyu/Awsome_MedSeg/tree/main.

</details>


### [175] [Focus When Necessary: Adaptive Routing and Collaborative Grounding for Training-Free Visual Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16158)
*Yifan Wang,Peiming Li,Shiyu Li,Zhiyuan Hu,Xiaochen Yang,Wenming Yang,Yang Tang,Zheng Wei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LazyMCoT is a training-free, dynamic framework that adaptively allocates visual grounding effort based on sample difficulty, using an Adaptive Routing mechanism and Collaborative Grounding module to improve reasoning accuracy while reducing inference latency for MLLMs on complex high-resolution images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MLLMs struggle with fine-grained details in complex high-resolution images, and current training-free methods (image scaling, localized cropping) introduce computational redundancy for simple queries and can degrade accuracy by truncating global context or adding irrelevant noise.

Method: LazyMCoT consists of two key components: (1) Adaptive Routing mechanism that evaluates predictive uncertainty via first-token statistics from a single forward pass, using conformal calibration to bypass confident cases while recalling difficult ones; (2) Collaborative Grounding module that integrates cross-modal attention with an external visual expert through a two-stage refinement process to generate precise localized displays for small/occluded targets.

Result: Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks show LazyMCoT rivals training-based approaches, simultaneously improving reasoning accuracy and reducing average inference latency.

Conclusion: LazyMCoT effectively balances efficiency and accuracy by dynamically allocating computational resources based on sample difficulty, offering a training-free solution that competes with training-based methods.

Abstract: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel in cross-modal reasoning, they often struggle to perceive fine-grained details in complex high-resolution images. Recent training-free methods address this through image scaling and localized cropping. However, applying these manipulations indiscriminately introduces computational redundancy for simple queries and can degrade accuracy by truncating essential global context or introducing irrelevant background noise. To this end, we propose LazyMCoT, a dynamic and training-free framework that adaptively allocates visual grounding efforts based on sample difficulty. The framework features an Adaptive Routing mechanism that evaluates predictive uncertainty using first-token statistics from a single forward pass. This efficiently bypasses confident cases while ensuring the recall of difficult samples via conformal calibration. For these challenging cases, a Collaborative Grounding module integrates the inherent cross-modal attention of the model with an external visual expert through a two-stage refinement process. This refinement process generates a precise localized display to recover small or occluded targets. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that LazyMCoT rivals training-based approaches by simultaneously improving reasoning accuracy and reducing average inference latency. Our code is availble at https://github.com/TencentBAC/LazyMCoT.

</details>


### [176] [Continuous Splatting meets Retinex: Continuous Gaussian Splatting and Implicit Reflectance Modeling for Low-Light Image Enhancement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16159)
*Yuhan Chen,Yicui Shi,Guofa Li,Wenxuan Yu,Ying Fang,Guangrui Bai,Wenbo Chu,Keqiang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CGS-Retinex proposes the first low-light image enhancement framework using explicit-implicit joint modeling, integrating continuous Gaussian splatting with Retinex theory to eliminate artifacts and improve color/structure fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing low-light enhancement methods suffer from color distortion and structural artifacts when balancing global illumination adjustment and local detail recovery, due to limitations of discrete Gaussian sampling and inadequate illumination-texture decoupling.

Method: The framework jointly models explicit illumination via a continuous Gaussian renderer (spatially continuous global illumination distribution) and implicit reflectance via an implicit neural representation guided by shallow high-frequency features, with physics-inspired brightness consistency and illumination smoothness constraints within the Retinex framework.

Result: Significant suppression of dark-region noise and overexposure, exceptional high-frequency structural fidelity, and accurate color restoration through precise decoupling of illumination and texture.

Conclusion: CGS-Retinex establishes a novel continuous physical representation paradigm that effectively addresses key limitations of prior low-light enhancement methods, enabling high-fidelity recovery.

Abstract: Low-light image enhancement aims to recover clear images from low-illumination observations and is crucial for high-level downstream vision tasks. However, existing methods frequently encounter color distortion and structural artifacts when balancing global smooth illumination adjustment and local high-frequency detail recovery. To address these issues, we propose CGS-Retinex as the first low-light image enhancement framework based on explicit-implicit joint modeling. Our framework deeply integrates continuous Gaussian splatting with Retinex theory. Specifically, we represent the image grid as a continuous parameter field and propose a continuous Gaussian renderer to estimate the spatially continuous global illumination distribution. This approach fundamentally eliminates grid artifacts caused by discrete Gaussian sampling. Furthermore, we introduce an implicit neural representation to model reflectance independently. We leverage shallow high-frequency features to guide the network in accurately reconstructing degraded texture details. Within the Retinex framework, we incorporate physics-inspired brightness consistency constraints and illumination smoothness regularization to enable explicit illumination and implicit reflectance to maintain proper exposure and achieve high-fidelity recovery of high-frequency structures and colors. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CGS-Retinex significantly suppresses dark-region noise and overexposure while achieving exceptional high-frequency structural fidelity and color restoration by precisely decoupling illumination and texture. This work establishes a novel continuous physical representation paradigm for low-light image enhancement.

</details>


### [177] [Multimodal LLM-Empowered Re-Ranking for Generalizable Person Re-Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16161)
*Jiachen Li,Xiaojin Gong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes MUSE, an MLLM-empowered re-ranking method for Domain Generalizable person Re-ID that improves inference-stage performance by computing a robust μ-distance metric, rather than focusing on training better encoders like prior work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DG Re-ID methods focus on training domain-generalizable encoders but neglect inference-stage refinements. Conventional re-ranking methods fail on unseen target domains because the encoder lacks sufficient generalizability to produce reliable feature distances.

Method: (1) Adapt an MLLM to Re-ID data via supervised fine-tuning with a domain-agnostic prompt and query-candidate hard mining scheme. (2) Use the adapted MLLM to compute a μ-distance during inference that is robust to domain gaps. (3) Integrate this μ-distance into existing re-ranking frameworks in a model-agnostic manner.

Result: Consistent and substantial performance improvements across multiple DG Re-ID benchmarks.

Conclusion: Leveraging MLLMs for re-ranking is a promising direction for DG Re-ID, offering model-agnostic integration and robust domain generalization at inference time.

Abstract: Domain Generalizable (DG) person re-identification (Re-ID) has attracted growing research interest due to its potential for deployment in unseen real-world scenarios. Most existing approaches address DG Re-ID by focusing on training domain-generalizable encoders but ignore the possible refinements in inference stage. In contrast, this work explores an alternative direction which improves inference re-ranking to enhance DG Re-ID. Conventional re-ranking methods typically rely on neighborhood-based distances to refine the initial ranking list, inherently depending on features produced by the Re-ID encoder. However, they deteriorate on target domains since the encoder lacks sufficient generalizability to produce reliable feature distances on unseen scenarios. Inspired by the remarkable generalization capabilities of recent Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), we propose an MLLM-empowered distance metric to improve re-ranking in DG Re-ID. Specifically, we first adapt an MLLM to Re-ID data through supervised fine-tuning, which incorporates a domain-agnostic prompt and a query-candidate hard mining scheme. Then, the adapted MLLM is employed to compute a $μ$-distance during inference, which is robust to domain gap and significantly enhances subsequent re-ranking performance. Our approach is model-agnostic and can be seamlessly integrated into previous re-ranking frameworks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach consistently yields substantial performance improvements across multiple DG Re-ID benchmarks. The code of this work will be released at https://github.com/RikoLi/MUSE soon.

</details>


### [178] [Dehaze-GaussianImage: Zero-Shot Dehazing via Efficient 2D Gaussian Splatting Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16163)
*Yuhan Chen,Wenxuan Yu,Guofa Li,Kunyang Huang,Ying Fang,Yicui Shi,Wenbo Chu,Keqiang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Dehaze-GaussianImage introduces 2D Gaussian Splatting for zero-shot image dehazing, modeling hazy images as continuous anisotropic Gaussian fields with a reconstruction-decoupling learning strategy that embeds the atmospheric scattering model into Gaussian parameters, achieving SOTA performance unsupervised with minimal parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dehazing methods suffer from computational redundancy in pixel-level optimization and lack physical interpretability in implicit neural networks, hindering the balance between representation efficiency and reconstruction fidelity.

Method: Proposes a zero-shot framework using 2D Gaussian Splatting (2DGS) to model hazy images as continuous anisotropic Gaussian fields, with a reconstruction-decoupling strategy embedding the atmospheric scattering model into Gaussian parameter space, enabling adaptive split/clone/prune operations for geometric-level decoupling of transmission medium and textures, plus explicit structure-preserving constraints.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in a fully unsupervised manner with minimal parameters.

Conclusion: Highlights the potential of explicit Gaussian representation for low-level vision tasks, demonstrating that breaking the pixel-grid paradigm can improve dehazing quality without supervision.

Abstract: Existing single image dehazing methods are often constrained by computational redundancy in pixel-level optimization and the lack of physical interpretability in implicit neural networks. These limitations hinder the balance between representation efficiency and reconstruction fidelity. To address these issues, we propose Dehaze-GaussianImage, the first zero-shot framework that introduces 2D Gaussian Splatting (2DGS) into the image dehazing domain to break the traditional pixel-grid processing paradigm. Distinct from static convolutional neural networks (CNNs) or Transformers, our approach models hazy images as continuous and dynamically evolvable anisotropic Gaussian fields. Specifically, we propose a novel reconstruction-decoupling zero-shot learning strategy that embeds the atmospheric scattering model into the Gaussian parameter space. This strategy drives Gaussian primitives to adaptively split, clone, and prune during optimization, achieving geometric-level decoupling of the transmission medium and clear textures. Furthermore, explicit structure-preserving constraints are introduced to suppress artifacts commonly caused by traditional physical priors. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in a fully unsupervised manner with minimal parameters, highlighting the potential of explicit Gaussian representation for low-level vision tasks.

</details>


### [179] [Fi-Gaussian: Frequency-Aware Implicit Gaussian Splatting for Single Image Dehazing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16168)
*Yuhan Chen,Ying Fang,Guofa Li,Wenxuan Yu,Yicui Shi,Kunyang Huang,Wenbo Chu,Keqiang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Fi-Gaussian is a frequency-aware implicit Gaussian splatting network for single image dehazing that models clear images as continuous 2D feature representations, decouples low/high-frequency information for detail recovery, and uses physics-driven scattering renormalization to achieve state-of-the-art dehazing performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single image dehazing suffers from loss of high-frequency details and difficulty in accurate physical scattering modeling.

Method: Proposes a frequency-aware implicit Gaussian splatting network (Fi-Gaussian) that: (1) uses implicit Gaussian splatting to model clear images as continuous 2D feature representations, (2) decouples low-frequency structural and high-frequency texture information with complex-valued weights, and (3) introduces a physics-driven scattering renormalization mechanism to estimate transmission map and atmospheric light using implicit Gaussian priors.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art quantitative performance and visually superior dehazed results on multiple benchmark datasets.

Conclusion: Validates the effectiveness of implicit Gaussian splatting for low-level vision tasks, particularly single image dehazing.

Abstract: Single image dehazing continues to be hindered by the loss of high-frequency details and the difficulty of accurate physical scattering modeling. To address these issues, we propose Fi-Gaussian, a frequency-aware implicit Gaussian splatting network for single image dehazing. Unlike explicit rendering methods that rely on 3D point clouds, our method employs implicit Gaussian splatting to adaptively model the underlying distribution of clear images as a continuous representation in 2D feature space. The core of the network is a frequency-aware implicit Gaussian splatting module, which decouples low-frequency structural information and high-frequency texture information in the frequency domain and then performs adaptive Gaussian aggregation with complex-valued weights to recover fine details. In addition, a physics-driven scattering renormalization mechanism is introduced to estimate the transmission map and atmospheric light under the guidance of implicit Gaussian priors. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that Fi-Gaussian achieves state-of-the-art quantitative performance and produces visually superior dehazed results, validating the effectiveness of implicit Gaussian splatting for low-level vision tasks.

</details>


### [180] [To forget is to preserve: Machine Unlearning for 3D medical image segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16180)
*Nitesh Kumar Singh,Akhilesh Singh,Arjun Arora*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper explores approximate unlearning strategies for 3D medical image segmentation under GDPR compliance, using a 3D ResNet-50 on the MRBrainS18 dataset. The Noisy Label strategy achieved the best trade-off: 93% forgetting with 84% retained accuracy after 50 epochs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: New data privacy laws like GDPR allow individuals to request erasure of their personal data from trained ML models, creating a need for effective machine unlearning techniques.

Method: Four approximate unlearning strategies (including Noisy Label) are applied to a 3D ResNet-50 pre-trained via Med3D, fine-tuned on MRBrainS18. Evaluation uses Dice similarity coefficient and MAE at 20 and 50 epochs.

Result: Noisy Label achieves the best trade-off: 93% reduction in forget set accuracy while maintaining 84% retained set accuracy after 50 epochs. Other strategies suffered catastrophic degradation in retain set performance.

Conclusion: The study provides a strict baseline for subject-specific unlearning performance metrics, offering clear criteria for practitioners to select appropriate unlearning strategies under privacy regulations.

Abstract: With new data privacy laws such as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) [1] that allow individuals to ask that any of their personal information be erased from trained machine learning models, there has been a push to investigate the unlearning of data from models as a way to comply with these laws. In this regard, based on four mechanics, we consider several approximate unlearning strategies applied to the MRBrainS18 dataset [2]. We use a 3D ResNet-50 [3] as a backbone architecture for segmentation that has been pre-trained with the Med3D framework [4]. Considering the pre-trained model as a baseline, we evaluate respective retention accuracy on 2 types of subjects, i.e., retain and forget. We assess these approaches through their Dice similarity coefficient and mean absolute error (MAE) values using two separate training horizons 20 and 50 epochs. The results show that the Noisy Label strategy had the best overall trade-off with a decrease of 93% in the forget set while maintaining 84% accuracy for the retained set after 50 epochs. All other strategies showed extreme levels of forgetting at higher epoch numbers while also demonstrating catastrophic degradation of their retain set performance. The results of this study provide a strict baseline of performance metrics for unlearning on a subject-specific level and provide practitioners with clear criteria for selecting the proper strategies.

</details>


### [181] [Closed-Loop Triplet Synergistic Generation for Long-Form Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16184)
*Xinlei Yin,Xiulian Peng,Xiao Li,Zhiwei Xiong,Yan Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CoTriSyGen is an agentic framework for multi-shot long-form video generation that uses a closed-loop visual-text-memory (triplet) synergy process with intra-shot and inter-shot refinement pathways, guided by a VLM-based analyzer and entity-centric persistent memory, achieving improved cross-shot consistency and cinematic continuity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing storyboard-driven multi-shot video generation pipelines execute feed-forward without mechanisms to incorporate generated visual evidence back into conditioning, leading to identity drift and compounding inconsistencies across shots.

Method: The framework operates as a closed loop with three core components: (1) an entity-centric mutable memory storing evolving visual states, (2) a VLM-based analyzer reasoning over planned intent, memory, and generated visuals, (3) two refinement pathways—intra-shot (targeted regeneration for semantic/compositional violations) and inter-shot (rewriting subsequent prompts to propagate new entities/attributes).

Result: Experiments on a curated StoryBench benchmark demonstrate substantial improvements in cross-shot consistency, prompt adherence, and cinematic continuity compared to representative methods.

Conclusion: Closing the loop via visual-text-memory synergy enables long-range coherence in multi-shot video generation, with persistent memory and iterative correction being key to mitigating identity drift.

Abstract: Multi-shot long-form video generation remains challenging due to identity drift and compounding inconsistencies across shots. While storyboard-driven pipelines improve controllability, they are often executed in a feed-forward manner, with limited mechanisms to incorporate generated visual evidence back into subsequent conditioning. We propose CoTriSyGen, an agentic framework that formulates multi-shot long video generation as a closed-loop visual-text-memory synergy process, where planned intent, persistent memory, and generated visuals are jointly leveraged for iterative correction and long-range coherence. A vision-language-model-based analyzer reasons over this triplet and produces updates to both prompts and memory along two pathways: (i) intra-shot refinement, which triggers targeted regeneration when semantic or compositional violations are detected and refines image-to-video prompt for coherent motions; and (ii) inter-shot refinement, which rewrites subsequent-shot prompts to propagate newly manifested entities or attributes and improve prompt quality (e.g., compositional grounding and cinematic fluency) based on generated evidence. The loop is grounded in an entity-centric memory modeled as a mutable visual state that evolves as the story progresses, which is continuously updated by both the generator and the analyzer by adding new and evolved entities to reflect appearance changes, accumulated multi-view evidence, and multi-entity compositions. Experiments on our curated StoryBench benchmark demonstrate substantial improvements in cross-shot consistency, prompt adherence, and cinematic continuity over representative methods.

</details>


### [182] [Learned JPEG Compression for DNN Vision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16185)
*Kaixiang Zheng,Ahmed H. Salamah,Siyu Chen,En-Hui Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: J4D is a training framework that optimizes JPEG compression parameters for DNN vision tasks by using a differentiable soft quantizer and entropy-based rate estimation, achieving up to 11.60% higher accuracy at the same rate or 80.05% lower compression at the same accuracy compared to default JPEG.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In the AI era, much of the compressed image data is consumed by DNNs rather than humans, yet JPEG is designed for human perception. This mismatch motivates optimizing JPEG for DNN inference performance rather than visual quality.

Method: J4D introduces a differentiable soft quantizer with probabilistic quantization to make the JPEG codec differentiable, and computes entropy analytically as a rate estimate. This enables end-to-end optimization of JPEG encoding parameters via backpropagation to balance compression rate and DNN accuracy.

Result: Across multiple datasets and DNN architectures, J4D consistently beats default JPEG and other optimized JPEG codecs. It achieves up to 11.60% accuracy improvement at the same rate, or up to 80.05% rate reduction at the same accuracy. It also enables universal JPEG parameters for various DNNs.

Conclusion: J4D effectively bridges the gap between JPEG compression and DNN vision tasks, demonstrating that learned encoding parameters tailored for neural networks can drastically improve efficiency while maintaining performance.

Abstract: JPEG, a lossy image compression technique designed for human viewers, has maintained its dominance for decades. However, in the era of artificial intelligence (AI), a substantial portion of image data, often compressed by JPEG, is and will continue to be consumed by deep neural networks (DNNs) instead of humans, thus creating a need to optimize JPEG for DNN inference performance. To this end, we propose learned JPEG compression for DNN vision (J4D), a novel training framework for determining JPEG encoding parameters to minimize compression rate while maximizing DNN inference performance. The major challenge of solving this optimization problem lies in representing the JPEG codec and compression rate in closed form. By incorporating a differentiable soft quantizer based on a probabilistic quantization scheme, we not only obtain a differentiable proxy for the JPEG codec, but are also able to compute the entropy of the coded source analytically, which is a close estimate of the actual compression rate. Equipped with both the differentiable JPEG codec and the information-theoretic rate estimator, we are then able to solve the aforementioned optimization problem with backpropagation. After training, the learned encoding parameters will be subsequently used in actual JPEG encoding based on probabilistic quantization. Extensive experimental results across multiple datasets and DNN architectures demonstrate that J4D consistently and significantly outperforms the default JPEG and other competitive JPEG codecs optimized for DNNs. Notably, compared to the default JPEG, J4D achieves an increase in accuracy by as much as 11.60% at the same rate, or a reduction of compression rate up to 80.05% at the same accuracy. Additionally, with the help of J4D, we show the potential to design universal JPEG encoding parameters for various DNN architectures for the first time.

</details>


### [183] [teasr: training-efficient any-step diffusion transformer for real-world image super-resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16188)
*Xiang Gao,Chenxin Zhu,Yushun Fang,Qiang Hu,Xiaoyun Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TEASR is a training-efficient any-step diffusion framework for Real-ISR that enables both one-step and multi-step restoration in a unified model via self-adversarial distillation and timestep-aware rectification, eliminating auxiliary teachers and achieving superior performance across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing one-step distillation methods for Real-ISR require auxiliary teacher models that inflate training memory and restrict scalability to large-scale architectures. Also, fixed-step models lack flexibility to trade off speed for quality.

Method: Proposes TEASR: (1) Self-adversarial distillation within a single diffusion model, eliminating auxiliary teachers/discriminators. (2) Timestep-aware rectification strategy for stable one-step generation. (3) Dual-branch diffusion transformer with decoupled timestep condition separating noise state and denoising target. Enables distilling 20B-parameter models on a single GPU.

Result: Supports seamless any-step sampling (one-step to multi-step), consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across multiple datasets, and dramatically improves training efficiency (20B model distillation on single GPU).

Conclusion: TEASR provides a flexible, efficient, and scalable solution for Real-ISR that unifies fast and high-quality restoration without auxiliary models, advancing practical deployment of diffusion-based super-resolution.

Abstract: Diffusion models excel in Real-World Image Super-Resolution (Real-ISR) due to their powerful generative priors but suffer from slow iterative sampling. Although existing one-step distillation methods accelerate inference, they typically require auxiliary teacher models that inflate training memory and restrict scalability to large-scale architectures. Furthermore, these fixed-step models lack the flexibility to trade off speed for quality. In this paper, we propose TEASR, a training-efficient any-step diffusion framework for Real-ISR that enables both one-step and multi-step restoration within a unified model. Our key idea is to perform self-adversarial distillation within a single diffusion model, eliminating the need for auxiliary teachers or discriminators. Specifically, we propose a timestep-aware rectification strategy that stabilizes one-step generation across noise levels. These two designs further enables the distillation of 20B-parameter diffusion models on a single GPU, significantly improving training efficiency. Moreover, we introduce a dual-branch diffusion transformer with decoupled timestep condition to separate the current noise state and the denoising target to enhance sampling quality. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TEASR supports seamless any-step sampling and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across multiple datasets.

</details>


### [184] [Cascaded Sparse Autoencoders Learn Multi-Level Visual Concepts in Multimodal LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16193)
*Yusong Zhao,Hengyi Wang,Tanuja Ganu,Akshay Nambi,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces Cascaded Sparse Autoencoders (CSAEs) that learn hierarchical visual concepts in MLLMs by training a second-level SAE on the first-level SAE's decoder weights, enabling "concepts of concepts" and improving interpretability and concept steering over existing SAE methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Sparse Autoencoders recover flat feature dictionaries and lack the ability to organize visual concepts hierarchically, making interpretability of MLLM internal representations difficult.

Method: CSAEs train a second-level SAE directly on the decoder weights of the first-level SAE, using learned low-level feature directions as inputs for higher-level abstraction, avoiding issues of nesting or stacked SAEs.

Result: Experiments on Qwen3-VL, Gemma-3, and LLaVA across multiple visual datasets show CSAEs achieve improved hierarchical concept coherence over state-of-the-art SAE baselines, and support effective group-level interventions in MLLM outputs.

Conclusion: CSAEs provide a scalable and effective approach for learning interpretable hierarchical visual concepts in MLLMs, enabling meaningful concept steering and better understanding of model internals.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated strong performance on vision-language tasks, yet their internal visual representations remain difficult to interpret. Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) provide a scalable way to decompose dense model activations into sparse, interpretable features. However, existing SAE architectures primarily recover flat feature dictionaries and are less suited for explicit multi-level concept organization. In this paper, we introduce cascaded sparse autoencoders (CSAEs) for learning hierarchical visual concepts in MLLMs. Rather than nesting or stacking SAE sparse activation codes, CSAEs train a second-level SAE directly on the decoder weights of the first-level SAE, treating learned low-level feature directions as inputs for higher-level abstraction. This design enables CSAEs to learn "concepts of concepts" while avoiding drawbacks from the shared-prefix coupling of nesting, Matryoshka-style hierarchies and the bottlenecks of naively stacked SAEs. Experiments across Qwen3-VL, Gemma-3, and LLaVA on multiple visual datasets show that CSAEs improve interpretability in terms of hierarchical concept coherence over state-of-the-art SAE baselines. Results on concept steering further demonstrate that the learned concept groups support effective group-level interventions in MLLM outputs.

</details>


### [185] [GRACE: Boosting Video MLLMs with Grounded Action-Centric Evidence for Viewer Sentiment Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16198)
*Ruoxuan Yang,Tieyuan Chen,Xiaofeng Huang,Haibing Yin,Jun Wang,Xiping Chen,Jun Yin,Xuesong Gao,Weiyao Lin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a grounded action-centric evidence augmentation framework that enhances video MLLMs for viewer sentiment prediction by extracting structured SVO triplets and localized visual entity crops from video descriptions, improving fine-grained emotional reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard MLLMs rely on holistic frame representations, leaving fine-grained, affect-relevant events implicit and complicating precise emotional reasoning. The gap between what is shown and what is felt in ads needs bridging.

Method: Extracts temporally ordered SVO triplets and visible textual cues from action-centric video descriptions; grounds subject and object entities as visual crops; feeds structured clues to MLLM for clue-enhanced emotional reasoning.

Result: Consistent improvements over Qwen2.5-VL and Qwen3-VL baselines on Pitts dataset; ablation studies, cross-dataset evaluation on AdsQA, and transfer experiments on TVQA subset confirm effectiveness and generalization.

Conclusion: Introducing explicit event structure (action triplets) and localized visual evidence (grounded entity crops) significantly enhances MLLMs' ability to reason about viewer emotions in video advertisements.

Abstract: Viewer sentiment prediction in video advertisements aims to infer the latent affective response evoked in the audience.
