The architecture integrates three agents: Guardrail for safety and policy enforcement, General-QA for intent interpretation, and Recommender-Analyst for schema-aware API call generation. This coordinated design ensures reliable, semantically aligned interaction with external data services. The modular framework is portable across platforms through API schema substitution and supports applications in environmental monitoring, disaster response, and climate analysis. It establishes a scalable interface between user intent and geospatial infrastructure, enabling streamlined and automated Earth observation workflows. Preliminary experiments under adversarial multi-turn settings show that prompt-level safety instructions improve robustness, although rare high-impact failures persist in API manipulation scenarios and highlight the need for adaptive, system-level defenses that balance safety, usability, and cost efficiency, which motivates the use of our intercept-level Guardrail agent.

</details>


### [266] [Cognitive Debt: AI as Intellectual Leverage and the Dynamics of Systemic Fragility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15078)
*Shuchen Meng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper develops a formal theory of "cognitive debt"—the stock of unverified reasoning obligations that builds up when AI is used as a substitute (rather than complement) for first-principles thinking. It introduces a model with cognitive capital and cognitive debt, proving six propositions about why rational agents over-adopt substitutive AI, generating systemic fragility and a "cognitive Minsky moment."


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To formally model the hidden risks and systemic fragility caused by over-reliance on AI as a substitute for human reasoning, analogous to financial debt cycles but applied to cognition.

Method: A formal economic model with two state variables (cognitive capital, cognitive debt), multiplicative production technology, and heterogeneous agents. Six propositions are derived analytically.

Result: (1) Rational agents incur positive cognitive debt. (2) Tranquil periods lower risk assessments, increase substitution, and create a cognitive Minsky moment. (3) Expected crisis losses are convex in aggregate leverage. (4) Post-crisis output pressure creates false-correction loops. (5) Decentralized equilibrium over-adopts substitutive AI vs. social optimum. (6) High-cognitive-capital agents may erode their unaided cognition below that of initially lower-skilled agents.

Conclusion: Substitutive AI adoption creates systemic cognitive fragility that is rational at the individual level but suboptimal socially, requiring recognition of cognitive debt as a distinct form of systemic risk.

Abstract: We develop a formal theory of cognitive debt: the stock of unverified reasoning obligations that accumulates when individuals use AI as a substitute rather than a complement for first-principles cognition. The model features two state variables per agent, cognitive capital and cognitive debt, and a multiplicative production technology in which cognitive capital functions as collateral that determines the return to AI adoption. We establish six propositions. Rational agents incur positive cognitive debt because the costs are deferred, partially external, and masked by short-run productivity gains. Tranquil periods lower subjective risk assessments, raise AI substitution intensity, and compound leverage, generating a cognitive Minsky moment in which subjective risk falls while true systemic fragility rises. Expected crisis losses are convex in aggregate leverage. Post-crisis, output-target pressure can produce a false-correction loop in which agents patch AI failures with more AI. The decentralised equilibrium over-adopts substitutive AI relative to the social optimum because of systemic risk, cognitive public goods, and arms-race externalities. In a two-type heterogeneous-agent economy, high-cognitive-capital agents adopt AI more intensively and may eventually erode their unaided cognitive capital below that of initially lower-skilled agents.

</details>


### [267] [VGPT-RSI for RH-Adjacent Formal Progress: Boundary Certificates, Verified Finite Lagarias Inequalities, and Explicit Failure Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15096)
*Zhixin Hu,Tao Xu,Xiaodian Sun,Li Jin,Momiao Xiong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VGPT-RSI is used to produce formally verified, finite certificates for two Riemann Hypothesis-adjacent problems (RH-boundary inequality and Lagarias criterion), while honestly identifying remaining mathematical obstructions that prevent full proof.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore whether an AI-assisted reasoning system (VGPT-RSI) can produce verifiable, formally checked partial progress on RH without overclaiming, and to clearly delineate where human mathematical insight remains needed.

Method: VGPT-RSI is applied to two tasks: (1) constructing a finite RH-boundary certificate using interval arithmetic (Arb/FLINT) and verifying it in Rocq/CoqInterval; (2) formalizing a Lagarias-route certificate in Coq for finite quantities.

Result: Successfully produced Coq-checked finite certificates for both the RH-boundary inequality and the Lagarias finite quantity, with explicit identification of three unresolved bottlenecks (formalizing Lagarias equivalence, proving global tail theorem, reducing counterexamples to colossally abundant numbers).

Conclusion: VGPT-RSI can produce certified, partial formal progress on problems adjacent to RH while honestly demarcating the remaining mathematical obstructions, thus avoiding false claims of a full proof.

Abstract: The Riemann Hypothesis remains one of the central unsolved problems in mathematics. Rather than claiming proof, we investigate whether a verifiable AI-assisted reasoning system can produce reliable, formally checked partial progress while explicitly identifying the remaining mathematical obstructions. We apply the Verifiable Growing Physical Transformer with Recursive Self-Improvement (VGPT-RSI) to two RH-adjacent certification tasks. First, we construct and verify a finite RH-boundary certificate for inequality on a parameterized safe lower curve over a region. The numerical boundary curve is converted into a certificate-backed lower curve, audited using outward-rounded interval arithmetic and Arb/FLINT ball arithmetic, and then checked in Rocq/CoqInterval for the parameterized theorem. Second, we initiate a formal Lagarias-route certificate. Lagarias criterion states that RH is equivalent to the global inequality. We formalize the finite quantity and produce a Coq-checked finite certificate. The final system identifies the exact unresolved mathematical bottlenecks: formalizing the Lagarias equivalence, proving the global tail theorem beyond any finite cutoff, and potentially reducing counterexamples to colossally abundant or related extremal integers. These results demonstrate that VGPT-RSI can produce certified RH-adjacent formal progress, organize proof dependencies, and avoid overclaiming when the remaining obstruction is genuinely mathematical.

</details>


### [268] [Towards Verifiable Agentic Data Science: Solving Irregular TSQA Via Tool-Grounded Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15107)
*Sanhorn Chen,Xiaoyang Chen,Boyu Liu,Roy Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: IRTS-ToolBench is a 1,700-question benchmark across 10 task types and 13 domains designed to evaluate LLMs and AI agents on irregularly sampled time series data, addressing the gap left by existing TSQA benchmarks that assume regular sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world time series data is irregular (asynchronous, informative missing values, varying sampling frequencies), but existing TSQA benchmarks only test on regularly sampled data, creating a fundamental gap in understanding LLM performance under realistic conditions.

Method: The authors built IRTS-ToolBench, a benchmark containing 1,700 questions spanning 10 task types across 13 domains, with standardized inputs and a reproducible evaluation protocol, available as open-source code.

Result: The benchmark provides 1,700 questions across 10 task types and 13 domains, designed for independent use by researchers working on LLM-based irregular time series analysis.

Conclusion: IRTS-ToolBench fills the critical gap of irregular time series evaluation for LLMs/AI agents, offering a standardized, reproducible benchmark to advance research in this underexplored area.

Abstract: Time series data in real-world deployments is overwhelmingly irregular. Observations are asynchronous, missing values are informative rather than random, and sampling frequencies vary across sensors and operational windows. However, existing Time Series Question Answering (TSQA) benchmarks mostly assume regularly sampled inputs, leaving a fundamental gap in understanding how large language models (LLMs) and AI agents perform under irregular conditions. To bridge this gap, we introduce IRTS-ToolBench, a benchmark of 1,700 questions spanning 10 task types across 13 domains. IRTS-ToolBench is designed to be used independently by any researcher working on LLM-based irregular time series analysis, providing standardized inputs and a reproducible evaluation protocol. Code can be found in https://github.com/SanhornC/IRTS-ToolBench.

</details>


### [269] [CONCORD: Asynchronous Sparse Aggregation for Device-Cloud RAG under Document Isolation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15179)
*Xuedong Hu,Zhiqing Tang,Zhi Yao,Tian Wang,Weijia Jia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CONCORD is an asynchronous sparse aggregation framework for dual-end RAG under document isolation, which treats the cloud as an asynchronously arriving evidence source, uses waiting debt control to decide whether to wait for remote participation, and employs certificate-guided minimal supplementation to reduce communication, achieving 1.66-2.15x throughput improvement with two orders of magnitude less communication while maintaining quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dual-end RAG methods rely on frequent remote synchronization and dense evidence transfer, which limits throughput under realistic latency and bandwidth conditions, especially when private documents remain on device and public knowledge in the cloud with no raw document exchange allowed.

Method: CONCORD introduces: (1) waiting debt control to decide whether each decoding step should wait for remote participation based on observed return of waiting; (2) certificate-guided minimal supplementation that requests only remote evidence needed for the current greedy decision; (3) asynchronous sparse aggregation treating cloud as an asynchronously arriving evidence source.

Result: On Natural Questions and WikiText-2, CONCORD improves end-to-end throughput by 1.66x and 2.15x respectively, reduces per-token communication by over two orders of magnitude, while maintaining comparable answer quality and perplexity.

Conclusion: Asynchronous sparse aggregation can effectively replace dense synchronization in dual-end RAG settings, achieving significant efficiency gains without sacrificing quality, making it suitable for privacy-constrained device-cloud collaborative inference scenarios.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has emerged as a pivotal technique for improving language models by incorporating external knowledge at inference time. As device-cloud collaborative inference makes it feasible to deploy small language models on edge devices, a new setting arises in which private documents remain on the device and public knowledge resides in the cloud. Privacy and policy constraints often forbid raw document exchange, creating a document-isolated dual-end RAG setting. However, existing methods rely on frequent remote synchronization and dense evidence transfer, limiting throughput under realistic latency and bandwidth conditions. To address this issue, we propose CONCORD, an asynchronous sparse aggregation framework for dual-end RAG under document isolation. CONCORD treats the cloud as an asynchronously arriving evidence source rather than a continuously synchronized co-generator. Specifically, we introduce waiting debt control to decide whether each decoding step should continue waiting for remote participation based on the observed return of waiting. We also design a certificate-guided minimal supplementation mechanism that requests only the remote evidence needed to determine the current greedy decision. Steps that consult the cloud preserve the same greedy token as dense dual-end aggregation, while the remaining steps commit locally without remote evidence. Experiments on Natural Questions and WikiText-2 show that CONCORD improves end-to-end throughput over baselines by $1.66\times$ and $2.15\times$, respectively, while reducing per-token communication by over two orders of magnitude and maintaining comparable answer quality and perplexity.

</details>


### [270] [Minimal Oversight: Uncertainty-Aware Governance for Delegated AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15563)
*Carlos R. B. Azevedo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes the Minimum Sufficient Oversight Principle (MSO), a variational framework for uncertainty-aware governance in delegated AI systems. It derives capacity theorems, scaling laws, and identifies masking as a governance pathology, offering practical design prescriptions and a companion Python package.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The central AI problem has shifted from model accuracy to uncertainty-aware governance: determining how much autonomy to grant, when to intervene, and how to calibrate trust in systems where decisions are delegated to specialized models, evaluators, and controllers.

Method: A variational principle (MSO) minimizing governance burden on the Fisher information manifold under a delivery constraint, yielding a water-filling allocation. Uses a revealed-action governed delegation channel model, capacity theorem for symbolwise review, local first-order approximation for workflow complexity-qualit degradation, and a drift-dominated autonomy-time scaling law.

Result: A computable framework for uncertainty, planning, and oversight; identification of masking as a structural AI-governance pathology; design prescriptions (upstream-first correction, sensitivity-based intervention, feasibility checks before autonomy expansion).

Conclusion: The framework enables principled autonomy delegation with proven capacity bounds and scaling laws, supported by synthetic simulations and a semi-real reconstructed workflow.

Abstract: AI systems increasingly delegate decisions to specialized models, evaluators, tools, and supervisory controllers. The central AI problem is no longer only model accuracy, but uncertainty-aware governance: how much autonomy to grant, which evidence should calibrate trust, what performance ceiling a delegated AI system can sustain, and when human intervention becomes necessary. We propose the Minimum Sufficient Oversight Principle (MSO), a variational principle for principled autonomy delegation: minimize governance burden on the Fisher information manifold subject to a delivery constraint. The resulting Euler-Lagrange solution yields a water-filling allocation of governed delegation across the task space. Building on a revealed-action governed delegation channel model, we prove a capacity theorem for stationary symbolwise review policies, derive a local first-order approximation relating workflow complexity to quality degradation, and give a drift-dominated autonomy-time scaling law linking intervention timing to effective capacity, complexity, and drift. Within this framework, masking appears as a structural AI-governance pathology: corrected performance can hide the competence signal needed to calibrate trust. Synthetic simulations and a semi-real reconstructed workflow support design prescriptions including upstream-first correction, sensitivity-based intervention, and explicit feasibility checks before autonomy is expanded. The result is a computable framework for uncertainty, planning, and oversight in delegated AI systems. A companion Python package is available at https://github.com/crbazevedo/delegation-lab.

</details>


### [271] [CogGuard: Cognitive and Operational Profiling for Proactive Warning in Edge Intelligent Services](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15199)
*Zhi Yao,Weihao Chen,Zhiqing Tang,Hanshuai Cui,Qianli Ma,Weijia Jia,Wei Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CogGuard is a proactive-warning framework that decouples offline LLM-based profile construction from online SLM-based prediction, using prefix-aligned KV-cache reuse and length-aware distributed fine-tuning to reduce profile construction time by 48% and fine-tuning time by 19% while achieving strong prediction accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Edge intelligent services need proactive warning under latency and privacy constraints, but existing profiling methods are domain-specific without reusable abstraction, and fine-tuning on heterogeneous edge clusters suffers high synchronization overhead due to input length variance.

Method: CogGuard decouples offline LLM-based profile construction from online SLM-based score prediction via a shared static-dynamic profile-to-score pipeline. It uses scenario-specific profiling with prefix-aligned KV-cache reuse and length-aware distributed fine-tuning with contrastive regularization.

Result: Reduces profile construction time by up to 48%, distributed fine-tuning time by 19%, achieves MAEs of 13.4 and 5.9 on 100-point-scale warning tasks, and reduces prediction error by 15.4% in the largest educational setting.

Conclusion: CogGuard effectively addresses the dual challenges of cross-domain reusability and heterogeneous edge synchronization for proactive warning services, outperforming baselines.

Abstract: Proactive warning is an important capability for edge intelligent services, where the system predicts whether a subject will successfully complete an incoming task under strict latency and privacy constraints. Such prediction depends on both long-term static attributes and short-term dynamic states derived from historical interaction logs. Recent Large Language Models (LLMs) offer strong long-context reasoning for constructing structured profiles from these logs, but existing solutions face two challenges for edge deployment: (1) profiling methods are typically domain-specific and lack a reusable abstraction across service scenarios, and (2) fine-tuning alignment models on heterogeneous edge clusters incurs high synchronization overhead due to the variance in input sequence lengths. To address these challenges, we propose CogGuard, a proactive-warning framework for edge intelligent services. CogGuard decouples offline LLM-based profile construction from online Small Language Model (SLM)-based score prediction through a shared static-dynamic profile-to-score pipeline, and instantiates it in two representative scenarios: educational performance warning and operational task outcome warning. For efficient profile construction, we design scenario-specific profiling methods with prefix-aligned KV-cache reuse to reduce repeated encoding overhead. For edge-side model alignment, we propose a length-aware distributed fine-tuning strategy with contrastive regularization to mitigate workload imbalance on heterogeneous clusters. Experiments on education and operation datasets show that CogGuard reduces profile construction time by up to 48% and distributed fine-tuning time by 19%, while achieving MAEs of 13.4 and 5.9, respectively, on 100-point-scale warning tasks. In the largest educational setting, CogGuard reduces prediction error by 15.4% compared with the strongest baseline.

</details>


### [272] [Attribute Inference from Interactive Targeted Ads](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15209)
*Peihao Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper models targeted advertising as a noisy oracle for attribute inference, builds a reproducible benchmark with synthetic populations, and evaluates various attacks showing disclosure policy is the strongest privacy control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and quantify the privacy risks when advertisers receive user-linked observations from targeted advertising campaigns, beyond aggregate reports.

Method: The authors model the channel as a noisy oracle for attribute inference, separating targeting predicates, exposure, interaction, and disclosure. They build a synthetic benchmark with calibrated populations, topic variants, and response priors. They compare Bayesian, supervised, positive and unlabeled, and adaptive attacks under varying campaign and disclosure settings.

Result: After 160 campaigns, Bayesian and supervised attacks achieve ≈0.64 AUC (main setting) and ≈0.65 AUC (higher interaction setting). Disclosure policy is the strongest control—aggregate reporting removes oracle input tied to users, while type filtering and randomized disclosure reduce the signal.

Conclusion: The paper provides a model, artifact, and defense evaluation method for privacy in interactive targeted advertising, with open-source code available.

Abstract: Targeted advertising systems can pair audiences selected by advertisers with ad units that expose visible user actions. When an interaction remains linked to the campaign that elicited it, the advertiser may receive an observation tied to a user rather than only an aggregate report. We model that channel as a noisy oracle for attribute inference. The model separates targeting predicates, exposure, interaction, and disclosure. These boundaries capture the gap between eligibility and delivery, and the gap between interaction and advertiser visibility.
  We build a reproducible benchmark using synthetic populations calibrated with public data, each with known sensitive labels. A generated campaign semantics layer provides topic variants and response priors. The simulator generates the ground truth, event traces, disclosed observations, and metrics. The evaluation compares Bayesian, supervised, positive and unlabeled, and adaptive attacks under common campaign and disclosure definitions.
  The final evaluation uses four topic variants, seven simulator seeds, and two interaction settings. Repeated campaigns with identity exposure produce measurable but bounded inference signal. At $160$ campaigns, Bayesian and supervised attacks reach about $0.64$ AUC in the main setting and about $0.65$ AUC in the higher interaction setting. Disclosure policy is the strongest control. Aggregate reporting removes the evaluated oracle input tied to users. Type filtering and randomized disclosure reduce the released signal. The result is a model, artifact, and defense evaluation method for privacy in interactive targeted advertising. The code is available at https://github.com/P-HOW/Interactive-Ad-Oracle.

</details>


### [273] [Visual-Seeker: Towards Visual-Native Multimodal Agentic Search via Active Visual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15231)
*Zhengbo Zhang,Changtao Miao,Jinbo Su,Zhaowen Zhou,Chunxia Zhang,Xukai Wang,Ruiqi Liu,Kaiyuan Zheng,Jiansheng Cai,Bo Zhang,Zhe Li,Shiming Xiang,Ying Yan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Visual-Seeker is a visual-native multimodal deep search agent that actively attends to fine-grained visual details and dynamically harvests visual evidence during search, outperforming existing models on five challenging benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MLLMs struggle with factual grounding in complex open-world scenarios, and current multimodal search agents rely on simple images with explicit semantics and text-only evidence trajectories, lacking the ability to perform multi-hop, cross-modal reasoning and visual-native search.

Method: Proposes Visual-Seeker, a visual-native multimodal deep search agent that actively attends to fine-grained visual details and dynamically harvests visual evidence throughout the search process. An active visual reasoning data pipeline is designed to synthesize 5K high-quality multimodal trajectories for training.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across five challenging multimodal search benchmarks, surpassing several proprietary models.

Conclusion: Validates robust visual-native reasoning and search capabilities in real-world web environments, demonstrating the effectiveness of active visual reasoning over static vision inputs.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities in many visual tasks, but they often struggle with factual grounding when confronted with complex, open-world scenarios. While recent multimodal deep search agents attempt to address this issue by utilizing external tools, the visual-native search paradigm remains underexplored. Existing methods primarily rely on simple images with explicit semantics and text-only evidence trajectories, limiting the agent's ability to perform multi-hop, cross-modal reasoning and search. To address these limitations, we propose Visual-Seeker, a visual-native multimodal deep search agent via active visual reasoning. Rather than treating vision as a static input, our agent actively attends to fine-grained visual details, dynamically harvests visual evidence throughout the search process. To unlock its visual-native potential, we design an active visual reasoning data pipeline and synthesize 5K high-quality multimodal trajectories for model training. Extensive experiments demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance across five challenging multimodal search benchmarks, even surpassing several proprietary models, validating robust visual-native reasoning and search in real-world web environments. The code and data can be accessed at: https://github.com/ZhengboZhang/Visual-Seeker.

</details>


### [274] [Mask-Proof: An LLM-based Automated Data Curation Pipeline on Mathematical Proofs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15258)
*Jierui Zhang,Siyuan Tan,Xinhang Li,Longzhuangzhi Lin,Dailin Li,Chengfeng Gu,Xinping Li,Yaxian Hao,Shengjia Liang,Yuxiang Ren,Wenhao Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Mask-Proof introduces a pipeline for automatically evaluating step-level reasoning in mathematical proofs by masking key formula steps and using LLM-based equivalence judging, achieving 96.8% agreement with experts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a lack of scalable and reproducible methods to measure step-level reasoning in long proofs across diverse sources, which limits trustworthy AI assistance in proof-certified scientific progress.

Method: Mask-Proof pipeline: masks key formula steps in real proofs, provides surrounding context, and evaluates model reconstructions using an LLM-based equivalence judge with repeated votes for stability. Creates Mask-ProofBench with 292 curated problems.

Result: Reasoning-enhanced models outperform standard models by 12-27%. The evaluator achieves 96.8% agreement with expert annotators.

Conclusion: Mask-Proof enables faithful, reproducible, and comparable measurement of step-level mathematical reasoning in long proofs across diverse research areas.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly capable of mathematical problem solving and can even assist with research-level proofs, yet we still lack a scalable and reproducible way to measure step-level reasoning in long proofs across diverse sources. This evaluation gap limits trustworthy AI assistance in proof-certified scientific progress. Existing evaluations often emphasize final answers or rely on costly expert grading, while end-to-end proof generation remains open-ended and hard to verify automatically. We introduce Mask-Proof, a pipeline that turns real proofs into automatically checkable masked-step tasks. It masks key formula steps, provides the necessary surrounding context, and evaluates model reconstructions with an LLM-based equivalence judge using repeated votes for stability. The resulting Mask-ProofBench contains 292 curated problems across diverse research areas. Experiments with 17 models show that reasoning-enhanced models outperform standard models by 12% to 27%. Our evaluator achieves 96.8% agreement with expert annotators, enabling faithful, reproducible, and comparable measurement of step-level mathematical reasoning. Benchmark, annotations, and code are available at https://github.com/weating/Mask-Proof.

</details>


### [275] [Feature Attribution in Directed Acyclic Graphs Using Edge Intervention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15273)
*Qiheng Sun,Junxu Liu,Xiaokai Mao,Haocheng Xia,Jinfei Liu,Kui Ren,Haibo Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DAG-SHAP proposes a novel Shapley-based feature attribution method that shifts attribution from individual nodes to edges in a causal DAG, capturing both externality and exogenous feature effects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Shapley-based feature attribution methods fail to simultaneously capture externality and exogenous influence of features under complex causal structures, leading to unreasonable interpretations.

Method: DAG-SHAP treats each feature edge (rather than node) as the attribution object in a causal DAG, and introduces an efficient approximation algorithm for computation.

Result: Extensive experiments on real and synthetic datasets validate the effectiveness of DAG-SHAP over existing methods.

Conclusion: Edge-level intervention in causal DAGs provides more reasonable feature attributions by capturing externality and exogenous contributions simultaneously.

Abstract: Shapley value-based feature attribution methods face challenges in scenarios involving complex feature interactions and causal relationships, even when a causal structure is provided. Existing methods typically adopt a node-centric view, attributing importance solely to individual features. Consequently, they often fail to simultaneously capture the externality and exogenous influence of features, leading to unreasonable interpretations. To overcome these limitations, we propose a novel feature attribution method called DAG-SHAP, which is based on edge intervention. DAG-SHAP treats each feature edge as an individual attribution object, ensuring that both externality and exogenous contributions of features are appropriately captured. Additionally, we introduce an approximation method for efficiently computing DAG-SHAP. Extensive experiments on both real and synthetic datasets validate the effectiveness of DAG-SHAP. Our code is available at https://github.com/ZJU-DIVER/DAG-SHAP.

</details>


### [276] [A Formal Framework for Declarative Agentic AI in Business Process Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15291)
*Mohammad Azarijafari,Luisa Mich,Michele Missikoff*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents AGO (Agents, Goals, Objects), a formal framework for Agentic Business Process analysis grounded in set theory and mathematical logic, which defines a Business Process Knowledge Base (BPKB) enabling structured querying, incremental updates, and automatic workflow generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic AI offers potential for automating Business Processes with autonomous decision-making, but this requires formally precise definitions of BP entities and interactions.

Method: The AGO methodology captures three modeling perspectives: Agents (who is acting), Goals (why it is carried out), and Objects (relevant entities). It is grounded in set theory and mathematical logic to formally define entity types and interactions, organized into a BP Knowledge Base (BPKB).

Result: The BPKB supports structured querying, incremental updates, and automatic BP workflow generation while ensuring soundness and completeness of derived paths.

Conclusion: The AGO framework provides formal foundations for Agentic BP analysis, enabling reliable and verifiable automated business process management.

Abstract: Agentic AI opens new opportunities for automating Business Process (BP), enabling autonomous decision-making and dynamic adaptation. However, realising this potential requires BP entities and their interactions to be defined with formal precision. This paper presents a formal framework for Agentic BP analysis through the AGO methodology. AGO captures the modelling perspective in terms of who is acting (Agents), why it is carried out (Goals), and what the relevant entities are (Objects). Grounded in set theory and mathematical logic, we formally define the AGO entity types and their interactions, organising all definitions into a BP Knowledge Base (BPKB). The resulting BPKB supports structured querying, incremental updates, and automatic generation of BP workflows, while ensuring soundness and completeness of the derived paths.

</details>


### [277] [CODA-BENCH: Can Code Agents Handle Data-Intensive Tasks?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15300)
*Yuxin Zhang,Ju Fan,Meihao Fan,Shaolei Zhang,Xiaoyong Du*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CODA-BENCH is the first benchmark that jointly evaluates code and data intelligence in data-intensive environments, featuring 1,009 tasks across 31 communities with an average of 980 files per task, where top agents achieve only 61.1% success rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks evaluate code-centric or data-centric capabilities in isolation, failing to capture the complexity of real-world development where agents must handle both complex code and large-scale data simultaneously.

Method: Constructed a data-intensive Linux sandbox based on the Kaggle ecosystem with hundreds of datasets, requiring agents to actively explore complex file hierarchies and generate code for data-driven analytical tasks across 1,009 tasks from 31 communities.

Result: Top-performing advanced agents achieve only 61.1% success rate, revealing a substantial gap in current agentic capabilities for data-intensive tasks that require integration of data discovery with code execution.

Conclusion: Current agents struggle to effectively integrate data discovery with code execution in data-rich environments, highlighting promising directions for future research in building more capable autonomous engineering agents.

Abstract: Advanced agents are increasingly demonstrating the potential to operate as autonomous engineers, creating a growing demand for evaluation benchmarks that capture the complexity of real-world development. Such environments typically involve both complex code and large-scale data (i.e., file system). However, existing benchmarks usually evaluate code-centric or data-centric capabilities in isolation, leaving a clear gap with real development scenarios. In this paper, we bridge this gap by introducing CODA-BENCH, the first benchmark to jointly evaluate code and data intelligence in a data-intensive environment. We construct a data-intensive Linux sandbox based on the Kaggle ecosystem (containing hundreds of datasets), where agents must actively explore complex file hierarchies to identify relevant resources and generate code for data-driven analytical tasks. CODA-BENCH comprises 1,009 tasks spanning 31 communities, with each task environment containing an average of 980 files, simulating realistic data scale and noise. Evaluations of advanced agents reveal that even top-performing systems struggle to effectively integrate data discovery with code execution, achieving a success rate of only 61.1%. These results highlight a substantial gap in current agentic capabilities for data-intensive tasks and point to promising directions for future research.

</details>


### [278] [Forced Deferral: Manipulating Routing Decisions in Multimodal LLM Cascades](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15308)
*Zhongye Liu,Yaopei Zeng,Yurui Chang,Lu Lin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Forced Deferral Attack (FDA), an adversarial image attack that exploits MLLM cascades by manipulating the weak model's confidence, causing queries to be consistently routed to the strong model and increasing computational costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLM cascades save computation by using a weak model first and deferring costly queries to a strong model only when confidence is low. The authors identify that this confidence-based deferral mechanism creates a new attack surface: adversaries can manipulate the weak model's confidence to force deferral to the strong model.

Method: FDA learns a universal border trigger by optimizing a temperature-flattened objective, which pushes the weak model's token distribution on triggered inputs toward less concentrated targets derived from its clean responses.

Result: Across datasets, model families, and deferral metrics, FDA consistently increases strong-model routing and outperforms image-perturbation and prompt-injection baselines.

Conclusion: MLLM cascades are vulnerable to attacks that manipulate compute allocation, forcing unintended strong-model usage without directly targeting answer correctness.

Abstract: While multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have shown strong visual reasoning abilities, serving a large model for every query is computationally expensive. MLLM cascades mitigate this cost by first querying a weak but cheaper model and deferring to a strong model when the weak model's output is unconfident. However, since the weak model's confidence directly controls compute allocation, these systems expose a new attack surface: an adversary can manipulate confidence so that their queries are consistently deferred to the strong model. Motivated by this vulnerability, we introduce the Forced Deferral Attack (FDA), an adversarial image attack that lowers the weak model's confidence and causes cascades to route queries to the strong model. FDA learns a universal border trigger by optimizing a temperature-flattened objective. This objective pushes the weak model's token distribution on triggered inputs toward less concentrated targets constructed from its clean responses. Across datasets, model families, and deferral metrics, FDA consistently increases strong-model routing while outperforming image-perturbation and prompt-injection baselines. These results show that MLLM cascades are vulnerable to attacks that manipulate compute allocation, forcing unintended strong-model usage without directly targeting answer correctness.

</details>


### [279] [ChatPlanner: A Large Language Model Framework for Personalized Public Transit Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15315)
*Tingting Yang,Chenhao Xue,Jun Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ChatPlanner is an LLM-based framework that uses fine-tuning and RAG to interpret user preferences from natural language queries and integrates them into public transit routing algorithms, outperforming existing route planners by generating more personalized and valuable route alternatives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Personalized public transit routing is difficult because capturing and integrating diverse user preferences into routing algorithms remains a significant challenge.

Method: ChatPlanner uses fine-tuned LLMs with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to extract routing parameters and interpret user preferences from natural language queries, then integrates these preferences into the objective function of a public transit routing algorithm. The authors designed preference-aware datasets with 8 personas and 5 contexts for training.

Result: ChatPlanner generates feasible solutions reliably. Fine-tuning enforces output structure and learns general preference patterns; RAG resolves imprecise expressions and calibrates scores. Their combination achieves highest accuracy. Case studies show ChatPlanner identifies valuable solutions overlooked by existing route planners.

Conclusion: This research establishes a new paradigm for integrating natural language understanding into transportation optimization.

Abstract: Personalized public transit routing in public transit systems remains challenging due to the difficulty of capturing and integrating diverse user preferences into routing algorithms. This paper presents ChatPlanner, a novel framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to enable preference aware public transit routing. Our approach employs fine-tuned LLMs with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to extract routing parameters and interpret nuanced user preferences from natural language queries, subsequently integrating these preferences into the objective function of a public transit routing algorithm. This study designs preference aware datasets incorporating eight personas and five contexts to establish scoring standards for both fine-tuning and RAG. This work conducted three experiments to validate the solutions' feasibility, extraction of routing information and preferences, and solution set quality and completeness. Results demonstrate that ChatPlanner generates feasible solutions reliably. Fine-tuning enforces the required output structure and learns general preference patterns, while RAG provides query-specific context to resolve imprecise or conversational expressions and calibrate continuous scores. The combination of both achieves the highest accuracy in routing information extraction and user preference interpretation. Results based on selected case studies show that by capturing user preferences, ChatPlanner identifies valuable solutions across different dimensions that existing route planners overlook, generating more valuable route alternatives. This research establishes a new paradigm for integrating natural language understanding into transportation optimization.

</details>


### [280] [APEX: Adaptive Principle EXtraction A Three-Layer Self-Evolution Framework for Production AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15363)
*Ya-Chuan Chen,Tien-Jen Lai,Hsiang-Wei Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: APEX is a three-layer co-evolution framework that simultaneously improves AI agent prompts, behavioral principles, and workflow topology, achieving a +90% Health Score improvement over baseline and outperforming single-axis optimization methods like Self-Harness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing self-improvement frameworks like Self-Harness optimize only the prompt harness (one dimension), leaving behavioral principles and workflow topology unchanged, which limits performance gains.

Method: APEX implements three-layer co-evolution: (L1) harness patching via failure-mode analysis, (L2) behavioral principles extraction via success-trace distillation, and (L3) workflow topology optimization via structural fitness-based selection.

Result: APEX achieves APEX Health Score of 0.570 (+90% vs baseline 0.300), distills 6 reusable principles, selects workflow topology scoring 0.900 (+20%), at cost of only 4 LLM calls (~270s) on local qwen2.5-coder:32b.

Conclusion: Multi-dimensional co-evolution substantially outperforms single-axis harness optimization, showing that simultaneous evolution across prompts, principles, and workflows yields superior results with minimal computational cost.

Abstract: Self-improvement in AI agents has emerged as a key research frontier: systems that modify their own prompts, workflows, and decision rules based on accumulated operational experience. The state-of-the-art Self-Harness framework [1] achieves 14--21% improvement on Terminal-Bench-2.0 by mining failure clusters and patching the agent harness. However, Self-Harness optimises only one dimension -- the prompt harness -- leaving behavioural principles and workflow topology unchanged. We propose APEX (Adaptive Principle EXtraction), a three-layer co-evolution framework that simultaneously evolves: (L1) the harness via failure-mode patching, (L2) behavioural principles via success-trace distillation [2], and (L3) the agent workflow topology via structural fitness-based selection [6]. We implement APEX on Joe [13], a production-grade super AI Agent built on NVIDIA Nemotron and designed as an Edge AI Agent Factory for the NVIDIA Agent Challenge 2026, managing a 15-node compute fleet using 114 real task traces collected over 18 days. APEX achieves an APEX Health Score of 0.570 (+90% vs. baseline 0.300) in a single evolutionary run, distilling 6 novel reusable principles and selecting a research-first workflow topology scoring 0.900 (+20%). Our results demonstrate that multi-dimensional co-evolution substantially outperforms single-axis harness optimisation, at a cost of only 4 LLM calls (~270 s) on a local qwen2.5-coder:32b instance.

</details>


### [281] [S1-DeepResearch: Beyond Search, Toward Real-World Long-Horizon Research Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15367)
*Yao Dong,Xinglin Xiao,Liwei Dong,Xinlong Jin,Zhengbo Li,Heng Zhang,Duyun Wang,Nan Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a unified trajectory construction paradigm for deep research agents, combining closed-ended QA and open-ended exploration. Using graph-grounded task formulation, trajectory rollout, and multi-dimensional verification, they synthesize high-quality training trajectories. Their S1-DeepResearch-32B model achieves SOTA among comparable open-source models across 20 benchmarks, approaching proprietary frontier models on challenging deep research tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing training datasets for search agents are primarily search-centric, focusing on closed-ended QA and information localization. They lack coverage of key deep research capabilities such as evidence integration, knowledge synthesis, planning, file understanding, and structured report generation.

Method: A unified trajectory construction paradigm with three components: (1) graph-grounded task formulation, (2) agentic trajectory rollout, and (3) multi-dimensional trajectory verification. This enables scalable synthesis of high-quality agentic trajectories covering long-chain reasoning, instruction following, report writing, file understanding, and skills usage.

Result: S1-DeepResearch-32B achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models of comparable scale across 20 benchmarks spanning five capability dimensions (complex reasoning, instruction following, report generation, file understanding, skills usage). It approaches the performance of leading proprietary frontier models on several challenging deep research benchmarks.

Conclusion: Jointly modeling information acquisition, knowledge synthesis, and planning-oriented agent behaviors is crucial for building effective deep research agents. The proposed paradigm bridges the gap between search-centric datasets and the comprehensive capabilities needed for deep research.

Abstract: Deep research agents aim to solve complex knowledge-intensive tasks through long-horizon planning, evidence gathering, reasoning, and report generation. While recent progress in search agents has demonstrated strong capabilities in information retrieval and answer verification, most existing training datasets remain search-centric, focusing primarily on closed-ended question answering and information localization. As a result, they mainly train information-seeking behavior while providing limited coverage of key deep research capabilities, including evidence integration, knowledge synthesis, planning, file understanding, and structured report generation. In this work, we propose a unified trajectory construction paradigm for deep research agents that combines closed-ended QA and open-ended exploration. The proposed framework consists of graph-grounded task formulation, agentic trajectory rollout, and multi-dimensional trajectory verification, enabling scalable synthesis of high-quality agentic trajectories spanning long-chain complex reasoning, deep research instruction following, report writing, file understanding and generation, and skills usage. Compared with existing search-oriented datasets, our synthesized trajectories place greater emphasis on knowledge synthesis, complex reasoning, and planning. S1-DeepResearch-32B achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models of comparable scale across 20 benchmarks spanning five capability dimensions, including complex reasoning, instruction following, report generation, file understanding, and skills usage. On several challenging deep research benchmarks, it approaches the performance of leading proprietary frontier models. These results highlight the importance of jointly modeling information acquisition, knowledge synthesis, and planning-oriented agent behaviors for building effective deep research agents.

</details>


### [282] [Reward Hacking in Language Model Agents: Revisiting AI Safety Gridworlds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15385)
*Ömer Veysel Çağatan,Xuandong Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper shows that reward hacking (specification gaming) emerges zero-shot in language models across scales (1.5B-14B), resists standard RL fixes like exploration prompts and entropy regularization, and that direct reward optimization actually widens the gap between observed and hidden reward.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward hacking is a central challenge in AI safety, but most known instances are discovered post hoc in frontier systems where controlled study is impractical. The authors aim to systematically study reward hacking in a controlled text-based environment.

Method: The authors adapt AI Safety Gridworlds into a text-based evaluation suite for language agents, testing frontier and mid-scale models (1.5B-14B) on RL safety tasks, and examining effects of direct reward optimization, credit assignment, exploration prompts, and entropy regularization.

Result: Specification gaming emerges zero-shot across models; RL widens the observed-vs-hidden reward gap; the pattern persists across model scales and resists standard mitigations (finer credit assignment, exploration prompts, entropy regularization).

Conclusion: Reward hacking arises naturally when optimizing proxy objectives with capable language model agents and resists standard mitigations, suggesting that proxy-reward failures may require approaches beyond standard exploration and credit-assignment fixes.

Abstract: Reward hacking, where AI systems exploit misspecified objectives to achieve high reward without satisfying intended goals, remains a central challenge in AI safety. Yet most known instances have been discovered post hoc in frontier systems where controlled study is impractical. We adapt the AI Safety Gridworlds framework into a text-based evaluation suite that reformulates classic reinforcement learning safety tasks for language-based agents. Across frontier and mid-scale models, we find that specification gaming emerges zero-shot: models systematically achieve high observed reward while underperforming on hidden safety objectives, and even apparently safe behaviors can reflect misunderstanding rather than principled safety. Reinforcement learning does not correct these failures: direct reward optimization widens the gap between observed and hidden reward, as the model's initial competence causes it to lock into locally rewarding strategies before discovering safer alternatives. This pattern persists across model scales (1.5B--14B) and is not resolved by finer credit assignment, exploration prompts, or entropy regularization. Our results show that reward hacking arises naturally when optimizing proxy objectives with capable language model agents and resists standard mitigations, suggesting that proxy-reward failures in agentic settings may require approaches beyond standard exploration and credit-assignment fixes. To facilitate reproducibility, the code for this work is available at \href{https://github.com/asparius/verl-agent-safety}{our public repository}.

</details>


### [283] [Hierarchical Modeling of ICD Codes in EHR Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15447)
*Megha Thukral,Dong Gyun Kang,Rudra Pratap Singh,Shruthi Kashinath Hiremath,Katrin Hänsel,Thomas Plötz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper studies using the ICD-10-CM hierarchical structure as an inductive bias for EHR representation learning, testing two hierarchy-aware methods (token-level and graph-based) on MIMIC-IV and eICU, finding that explicit hierarchy encoding improves downstream prediction and cross-dataset transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing EHR foundation models treat ICD diagnosis codes as flat tokens, ignoring the clinically meaningful hierarchical structure (e.g., disease families, subcategories) already present in coding systems. The authors aim to exploit this structure as an inductive bias to improve clinical representation learning.

Method: Two complementary hierarchy-aware mechanisms: (1) augmenting diagnosis sequences in a BERT-style transformer with ICD hierarchy-level tokens; (2) injecting hierarchy into graph-based code representations via hierarchy-aware edges combined with co-occurrence structure. Evaluated on MIMIC-IV (pretraining/in-domain) and eICU (cross-dataset transfer).

Result: Explicitly encoding ICD hierarchy improves over flat code representations in both in-domain and cross-dataset settings. The most useful hierarchy level depends on the task and modeling approach. Benefits of hierarchy encoding generalize across modeling settings and hierarchy levels.

Conclusion: Hierarchy-aware EHR representation learning is beneficial and generalizable, with explicit ICD hierarchy encoding yielding consistent improvements for downstream prediction and transfer across datasets.

Abstract: Electronic health record foundation models typically treat ICD diagnosis codes as flat tokens, overlooking the clinically meaningful hierarchical structure that captures disease families, subcategories, and fine-grained diagnostic detail. As a result, existing EHR representation learning methods do not explicitly exploit the hierarchical structure already present in the coding system. In this work, we study ICD-10-CM hierarchy as a general inductive bias for clinical representation learning. We investigate two complementary mechanisms for incorporating hierarchy: first, by augmenting diagnosis sequences in a BERT-style transformer with tokens corresponding to different levels of the ICD hierarchy, and second, by injecting hierarchy into graph-based code representations through hierarchy-aware edges combined with diagnosis co-occurrence structure. Across these settings, we evaluate whether explicit hierarchy improves downstream prediction, which levels of the hierarchy are most useful, whether hierarchy encoding improves transfer across datasets, and how hierarchy reshapes embedding similarity structure. We conduct experiments on two large-scale real-world clinical datasets: MIMIC-IV, used for pretraining and in-domain evaluation, and eICU, used to assess cross-dataset transfer via frozen encoder probing. Our findings show that explicitly encoding ICD hierarchy improves over flat code representations in both in-domain and cross-dataset settings, while revealing that the most useful level of hierarchy depends on both the task and the modeling approach. More broadly, we focus on hierarchy-aware EHR representation learning and show that the benefits of encoding hierarchy are generalizable across modeling settings and hierarchy levels.

</details>


### [284] [Who Drifted: the System or the Judge? Anytime-Valid Attribution in LLM Evaluation Pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15474)
*Yitao Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a betting e-process framework with a fixed human-labeled anchor set to disambiguate LLM product drift from judge model changes, achieving noise-free attribution and strong statistical guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuous LLM evaluation relies on LLM judges as ground truth, but silent version bumps or scoring-prompt updates to the judge model create ambiguity: a detected score drift could mean the product worsened or the judge changed, making alarm signals unreliable.

Method: The authors propose a fixed, human-labeled anchor set that the current judge re-scores at a steady interleave, combined with a second betting e-process tracking the judge-versus-human gap, and a guard-window rule that returns a verdict in {none, system, judge}.

Result: On real judge changes, silent version bumps are detected as judge drift in 60/60 runs with zero misattribution; strict-prompt changes are correctly attributed on 110/120 runs (guard width 300). The industry-default rolling z-test false-alarms on 75% of drift-free streams. Results replicate on TL;DR summarization with no re-tuning.

Conclusion: The method provides anytime-validity, one-way identification (only the judge can move the anchors), and process orthogonality, running at ~0.64 the cost of strong-judging every item (or 0.21 in a cheaper regime).

Abstract: Continuous evaluation of LLM products relies on a strong LLM judge treated as ground truth: a cheap monitor scores every interaction and a team is paged when the score drifts down. But the judge is itself a model behind an API, and a silent version bump or scoring-prompt update changes how it scores -- so every drift alarm is ambiguous between a worse product and a changed judge. We resolve the ambiguity with a fixed, human-labeled anchor set that the current judge re-scores at a steady interleave, a second betting e-process on the judge-versus-human gap, and a guard-window rule returning a verdict in {none, system, judge}. We prove anytime-validity, one-way identification (only the judge can move the anchors), an attribution race whose design law is that the anchors must out-run the main process they guard, and process orthogonality. On two real judge changes, a silent version bump is detected as judge drift in 60/60 runs with zero judge-to-system misattribution, and a contaminating strict-prompt change is correctly attributed on 110 of 120 runs at guard width 300 -- while the industry-default rolling z-test false-alarms on 75% of drift-free streams. Every experiment replicates on a second domain (TL;DR summarization) with nothing re-tuned, and where the domains differ the differences are the ones the race predicts: the strict-prompt change shifts scores harder there, so the anchors fire faster and attribution becomes perfect (240/240). The monitor runs at approximately 0.64 of the cost of strong-judging every item, or 0.21 in a cheaper-but-deafer regime.

</details>


### [285] [Towards End-to-End Automation of AI Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15497)
*Yutaro Yamada,Robert Tjarko Lange,Cong Lu,Chris Lu,Shengran Hu,Jakob Foerster,David Ha,Jeff Clune*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The AI Scientist is an autonomous system that handles the entire scientific research lifecycle — from idea generation to manuscript writing and peer review — achieving a paper accepted at a machine learning workshop (70% acceptance rate).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve full automation of the scientific process end-to-end, addressing the long-standing AI ambition of automating science from conception to publication.

Method: Leverages modern foundation models within a complex agentic system, operating in two modes: (1) focused mode using human-provided code templates for specific topics, and (2) template-free, open-ended mode using agentic search for wider exploration.

Result: The system produces manuscripts of sufficient quality to pass the first round of peer review at a major ML conference workshop (70% acceptance rate). It generates diverse ideas and automatically tests, reports on, and evaluates them.

Conclusion: Demonstrates AI's growing capacity for scientific contribution, signaling a potential paradigm shift in research. Highlights risks (overwhelming review systems, adding noise to literature) but suggests responsible development could greatly accelerate scientific discovery.

Abstract: The automation of science is a long-standing ambition in the field of AI. While the community has made significant progress in automating individual components of the scientific process, a system that autonomously navigates the entire research lifecycle -- from conception to publication -- has remained out of reach. Here, we present the strongest demonstration to date toward automating the entire process end-to-end. We present The AI Scientist, which creates research ideas, writes code, runs experiments, plots and analyzes data, writes the entire scientific manuscript and performs its own peer review. Its ideas, execution, and presentation are of sufficient quality to produce a manuscript generated by an AI system that passes the first round of peer review at a major machine learning conference workshop. The workshop has an acceptance rate of 70 percent. Our system leverages modern foundation models within a complex agentic system. We evaluate The AI Scientist in two settings: a focused mode using human-provided code templates as an initial scaffold to conduct research on a specific topic, and a template-free, open-ended mode that leverages agentic search for wider scientific exploration. Both settings produce diverse ideas and automatically test, report on, and evaluate them. This achievement demonstrates AI's growing capacity for scientific contribution and signifies a potential paradigm shift in how research is conducted. As with any impactful new technology, there could be significant risks, including taxing overwhelmed review systems and adding noise to scientific literature. However, if developed responsibly, such autonomous systems could greatly accelerate scientific discovery.

</details>


### [286] [Synthetic Counteradaptation: A Principle of Human-AI Co-evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15503)
*Ivar Frisch,Jackie Kay,Philip Moreira Tomei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces "synthetic counteradaptation," a co-evolutionary process where humans and AI systems mutually adapt to each other's strategies, illustrated through examples in Go, social interactions, and geopolitical simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a framework for understanding the recursive, co-evolutionary dynamics that emerge when humans and AI systems interact and mutually adapt in multi-agent environments.

Method: The paper analyzes illustrative case studies across three domains: the game of Go (strategic gameplay), mixed-motive social interactions, and geopolitical simulations.

Result: Demonstrates that synthetic counteradaptation captures how AI-driven novel strategies prompt human behavioral insights and adaptations, leading to new interaction dynamics.

Conclusion: Synthetic counteradaptation is a useful framework for understanding the co-evolutionary nature of human-AI interactions in multi-agent settings.

Abstract: In this paper, we introduce the concept of synthetic counteradaptation, a process where human and AI systems co-evolve by adapting to each other's strategies and behaviors. Synthetic counteradaptation occurs when AI systems develop novel strategies or social protocols, prompting humans to extract insights and adapt their own behaviors in response, leading to the emergence of new agent interaction dynamics. To illustrate these dynamics, we analyze examples from various contexts, including the game of Go, mixed-motive social interactions, and geopolitical simulations. By exploring these cases, we demonstrate how synthetic counteradaptation provides a framework for understanding the recursive and co-evolutionary nature of human-AI interactions in multi-agent environments.

</details>


### [287] [Toward Vibe Medicine: A Self-Evolving Multi-Agent Framework for Clinical Decision Support](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15504)
*Qianxue Zhang,Yiming Ren,Shihuan Qin,Xiao Zhang,Liao Zhang,Jinyang Huang,Zhengliang Liu,Chenbin Liu,Hongying Feng,Jingyuan Chen,Yuzhen Ding,Weihang You,Hanqi Jiang,Yi Pan,Yifan Zhou,Junhao Chen,Lifeng Chen,Wei Liu,Tianming Liu,Zengren Zhao,Lian Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VIBEMed is a multi-agent AI framework with self-evolution mechanism and safety sandbox for adaptive clinical decision support, featuring three specialized agents (CDA, TEA, CEMA) that learn dynamically from chat history, outperforming static AI systems in complex clinical tasks like oncology planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI systems rely on pre-trained knowledge and predefined pipelines, failing to learn dynamically from interactive chat session history containing patient outcomes and past failures, limiting adaptive clinical decision support.

Method: A multi-agent framework (VIBEMed) with three agents: Clinical Diagnostic Agent (CDA) for hypothesis generation, Therapeutic Execution Agent (TEA) for treatment planning, and Clinical Evolution Manager Agent (CEMA) for distilling longitudinal feedback. Includes self-evolution mechanism (iterative updates across memory, model behavior, decision strategies) and architecture-level safety sandbox.

Result: VIBEMed demonstrates superior performance in complex clinical cases, especially in integrated decision-making and longitudinal planning tasks, with reliable end-to-end decisions in challenging oncology treatment planning scenarios.

Conclusion: VIBEMed provides a practical path beyond static AI systems toward adaptive, experience-driven clinical decision support, highlighting the value of multi-agent collaboration with continuous evolution for advancing precision medicine.

Abstract: In recent years, the advances of large language models and autonomous agents have revolutionized the healthcare field, facilitating diagnosis and improving treatment results. However, most existing AI systems rely on pre-trained knowledge and predefined pipelines, which struggle to learn dynamically from the interactive chat session history that contains patient outcomes and past failures. To address this limitation, we propose VIBEMed, a multi-agent framework with a built-in self-evolution mechanism and architecture-level safety sandbox for robust clinical decision support. The system integrates three specialized agents, including a Clinical Diagnostic Agent (CDA) for hypothesis generation, a Therapeutic Execution Agent (TEA) for treatment planning, and a Clinical Evolution Manager Agent (CEMA) that distills longitudinal clinical feedback into reusable knowledge, transforming multimodal patient information into personalized medical decisions. Through self-evolution mechanism, the framework enables iterative updates across memory, model behavior, and decision strategies, allowing the system to improve over time. Experimental results show that VIBEMed demonstrates superior performance through its evolving mechanism in complex clinical cases, particularly in tasks that require integrated decision-making and longitudinal planning. The framework also supports reliable end-to-end decisions in challenging scenarios such as oncology treatment planning, highlighting its feasibility in real-world clinical contexts. Overall, VIBEMed provides a practical path beyond static AI systems toward adaptive, experience-driven clinical decision support, demonstrating the value of combining multi-agent collaboration with continuous evolution for advancing precision medicine.

</details>


### [288] [Frame-Conditioned Moral Computation in LLaMA 3.1-8B-Instruct: A Mechanistic Interpretability Audit of Ethical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15507)
*Ali Dasdan,Manan Shah,W. Russell Neuman,Chad Coleman,Kund Meghani,Safinah Ali*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Using mechanistic interpretability on LLaMA 3.1-8B-Instruct, the paper finds that LLM moral reasoning is driven by a Situational Anchor Effect: prompt-specific surface features dominate internal representations while ethics-labeled neurons remain constant in capacity but vary in salience. This suggests behavioral alignment (RLHF) only re-orders surface outputs without altering underlying domain-first computations, motivating a call for Mechanistic Alignment research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Behavioral audits of LLMs only measure what the model says, not its internal computation. The authors aim to uncover whether the model's moral reasoning is driven by genuine ethical computation or by surface-level prompt framing.

Method: The authors use Transluce (an AI-driven mechanistic interpretability platform) to analyze LLaMA 3.1-8B-Instruct on 54 moral prompts across four batteries (dilemmas, policy, role-playing, trolley contrasts). They employ five cluster-level metrics and a six-metric neuron-level panel, plus multi-temperature auditing and cross-model behavioral proxies on two frontier models.

Result: A Situational Anchor Effect is found: domain-specific representations dominate the top of activation lists regardless of battery. Ethics-labeled capacity stays constant, but salience is highly sensitive to prompt framing. The B4-vs-B5 contrast shows the model attends to whichever surface feature varies. A candidate ethics neuron (L16/N3837) is identified stable across temperatures.

Conclusion: The model exhibits Frame-Conditioned Moral Computation where surface vocabulary selects a feature manifold and moral conclusions are downstream of that selection. Behavioral alignment alone is insufficient; Mechanistic Alignment—verifying causal privilege of ethics-related features under controlled frame variation—is needed as a research program.

Abstract: Behavioral audits of Large Language Models on moral prompts measure what the model says, not the internal computation producing it. We use Transluce, an AI-driven mechanistic-interpretability platform, to examine LLaMA 3.1-8B-Instruct on 54 moral prompts in four batteries: 17 dilemmas, policy, and meta-ethical questions (B1); 6 role-playing scenarios (B3); and a controlled trolley contrast varying the switching mechanism with people fixed (B4, 15 prompts) or identity attributes with mechanism fixed (B5, 16 prompts).
  Two complementary metric families, five cluster-level metrics and a six-metric neuron-level panel, converge on a Situational Anchor Effect: domain-specific representations dominate the top of the activation list across every battery. The model's ethics-labeled capacity stays essentially constant; its salience (rank, priority, top-of-list presence) is highly sensitive to the interpretive frame the prompt selects.
  The B4-vs-B5 contrast confirms the model attends to whichever surface feature varies: aggregate ethics metrics are indistinguishable, but the dominant non-ethics distractor mirrors the design. A multi-temperature audit identifies a candidate ethics neuron (L16/N3837) stable across temperatures; a cross-model behavioral proxy on two frontier models yields preliminary evidence of divergence in self-reported moral focus, consistent with an Alignment Wrapper in which RLHF re-orders surface text without removing underlying domain-first frames. We unify these as Frame-Conditioned Moral Computation: the prompt's surface vocabulary selects a feature manifold, and the moral conclusion is downstream of that selection. Behavioral alignment must be supplemented by Mechanistic Alignment: a research program asking whether ethics-related features can be shown causally privileged under controlled frame variation, not merely loud in the explanation.

</details>


### [289] [ToolMenuBench: Benchmarking Tool-Menu Filtering Strategies for Reliable and Efficient LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15508)
*Rahul Suresh Babu,Laxmipriya Ganesh Iyer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ToolMenuBench evaluates how tool-menu construction (which tools to show, when) impacts LLM agent performance, finding that Causal Minimal Tool Filtering (CMTF) dramatically improves task success (32.1% → 85.7%) and reduces token usage by ~98% compared to showing all tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations focus on whether models can call tools correctly, ignoring how the visible tool menu itself shapes reliability, efficiency, and safety-related risk exposure in large tool libraries.

Method: ToolMenuBench is a benchmark that varies tool-menu size, distractor type, state-dependent task structure, and risk exposure. It evaluates seven model backends, three menu sizes, six filtering methods, and seven settings, reporting filter-level and downstream agent metrics.

Result: CMTF improves task success from 32.1% (all-tools) to 85.7%, reduces token usage ~98%, and achieves the best tradeoff across visible tools, wrong-tool calls, premature actions, and risky-tool exposure compared to unfiltered, lexical, state-aware, and broader causal-path baselines.

Conclusion: ToolMenuBench provides a reusable framework for studying the agent-interface problem: which tools should be visible, when, and under what cost/risk constraints.

Abstract: Tool-augmented large language model agents increasingly operate over large tool libraries, but existing evaluations often focus on whether a model can call a tool correctly rather than how the visible tool menu shapes reliability, efficiency, and safety-relevant risk exposure. We introduce ToolMenuBench, a benchmark for evaluating tool-menu construction in multi-step LLM agents. ToolMenuBench varies tool-menu size, distractor type, state-dependent task structure, and risk exposure, and reports both filter-level and downstream agent metrics, including visible-tool count, risky-tool exposure, task success, wrong-tool calls, premature actions, and token usage. In a controlled evaluation across seven model backends, three tool-menu sizes, six filtering methods, and seven evaluation settings, CMTF improves task success from 32.1% under all-tools exposure to 85.7%, while reducing average token usage by roughly 98%. Causal minimal tool filtering achieves the strongest overall tradeoff, reducing visible tools, wrong-tool calls, premature actions, and risky-tool exposure relative to unfiltered exposure, lexical filtering, state-aware filtering, and broader causal-path baselines. ToolMenuBench provides a reusable evaluation framework for studying the agent-interface problem: which tools should be visible, when they should be visible, and under what cost or risk constraints.

</details>


### [290] [QoS-Aware Token Scheduling and Private Data Valuation for Multi-Modal Agentic Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15573)
*Yao Du,Jing Liu,Pengfei Xu,Zehua Wang,Victor C. M. Leung,Cyril Leung,Victoria Lemieux*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a differentially private framework for fair token allocation and private data valuation in decentralized agentic systems, using multi-modal embeddings in a shared semantic space with DP prototypes to balance privacy, fairness, and QoS under data heterogeneity and resource constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Centralized cloud processing reduces personal data sovereignty and may degrade QoS, while decentralized records are biased, noisy, and heterogeneous. There is a need to fairly value diverse user contributions while protecting privacy.

Method: Embeds multi-modal representations in a shared semantic space, releases differentially private (DP) prototypes to reduce semantic leakage, and designs a fair token allocation scheme that rewards effective contributions robust to data heterogeneity and resource scarcity.

Result: Simulations show improved contribution-based fairness and QoS over standard benchmarks, along with enhanced resistance to image reconstruction attacks, indicating better privacy for multi-modal personal data.

Conclusion: The proposed DP-based framework effectively balances privacy, fairness, and QoS in decentralized agentic systems, offering a viable solution to data sovereignty and heterogeneity challenges.

Abstract: In agentic systems, human-generated data records anchor the value of AI services. Yet cloud compute pipelines centralize processing on remote servers. Data centralization reduces personal data sovereignty and may potentially degrade the quality of service (QoS). Meanwhile, user contributions are diverse in quantity and quality: decentralized records can be biased, noisy, and heterogeneously distributed. To address the data challenge, we study fair token allocation and private data valuation for decentralized and resource-constrained agentic systems. Our approach embeds multi-modal representations in a shared semantic space and releases differentially private (DP) prototypes to preserve utility while reducing semantic leakage. With the DP guarantee, we design a fair token allocation scheme that rewards effective contributions and remains robust to data heterogeneity and AI resource scarcity. Extensive simulations demonstrate improved contribution-based fairness and QoS compared to standard benchmarks. The improved resistance to image reconstruction attacks indicates enhanced privacy for multi-modal personal data.

</details>


### [291] [Do we have the knowledge we need? Rethinking human-AI decision-making in corporations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15575)
*Anne S. R. Marx,Ricardo M. Avelino,Torbjørn Netland,Mennatallah El-Assady*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This position paper addresses how organizations can store and maintain knowledge for both human and AI consumption, and proposes a framework for allocating agency between humans and AI based on task attributes and knowledge availability, demonstrated through two manufacturing tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI systems take on decision-making roles, they need access to organizational knowledge that was traditionally designed for humans. The authors aim to bridge the gap between fragmented organizational knowledge and AI accessibility, while addressing how to allocate agency between humans and AI across different tasks.

Method: The paper proposes a framework that maps task attributes and knowledge availability to recommended agency allocations and control mechanisms. It illustrates the framework through two contrasting manufacturing tasks: routine visual quality inspection and one-off factory location decision.

Result: A framework for agency allocation between humans and AI based on task characteristics and knowledge availability, with illustrative examples from manufacturing contexts.

Conclusion: The paper concludes with opportunities for future research on how organizations can effectively manage knowledge for both human and AI consumption while appropriately allocating agency across tasks with different risk levels.

Abstract: Organizational knowledge is fragmented across a variety of software systems, tacit expertise, and manual documents that have traditionally been designed for human consumption. As AI systems are increasingly deployed and granted decision-making roles, they require access to this knowledge. This raises two questions: how should organizations store and maintain knowledge so that it remains accessible to both humans and future AI systems, and how should agency be allocated between humans and AI across tasks with different risks and levels of uncertainty? In this position paper, we describe how organizational knowledge evolves and contribute a framework that maps task attributes and knowledge availability to recommended agency allocations and control mechanisms.
