TL;DR: Physics-conforming Latent Twins (PLT) is a framework that learns latent surrogate models for time-dependent physical systems, enforcing conservation laws, invariants, and dissipative structures by design through constrained latent dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard surrogate models for time-dependent systems may interpolate training trajectories accurately but fail to respect physical principles (conservation laws, invariants, admissibility), leading to physically meaningless predictions over long time horizons.

Method: PLT jointly learns an encoder, decoder, and latent flow map between time-indexed states, with constraints ensuring the latent dynamics preserve or dissipate prescribed structural quantities. A constraint-transfer framework connects physical structure in original space to compatible latent constraints, with theoretical bounds on structure preservation.

Result: Experiments on ODE and PDE benchmarks show improved constraint satisfaction, structural fidelity, and qualitative long-time behavior while maintaining accurate surrogate predictions.

Conclusion: Enforcing physical structure in latent space via constraint transfer is a principled and effective way to produce reliable surrogates that respect physical principles over long time horizons.

Abstract: Surrogate models are central to scientific machine learning, where they enable fast prediction, simulation, inference, and control for complex physical systems. For time-dependent problems, however, accurate interpolation of training trajectories is not sufficient: reliable surrogates should also respect the conservation laws, invariants, admissibility conditions, and dissipative structures that give those trajectories physical meaning. We introduce Physics-conforming Latent Twins, a framework for learning latent surrogate solution operators whose dynamics satisfy selected physical principles by design. The method builds on the Latent Twin formulation by jointly learning an encoder, a decoder, and a latent flow map between arbitrary time-indexed states, while constraining the latent dynamics to preserve or dissipate prescribed structural quantities. We develop a constraint-transfer viewpoint that connects physical structure in the original state space with compatible constraints in latent space, and prove structure-preservation bounds showing how latent enforcement improves control of physical defects after decoding. We also derive algebraic conditions for latent flow maps that preserve linear and quadratic invariants or enforce dissipative inequalities. Numerical experiments on representative ODE and PDE benchmarks demonstrate improved constraint satisfaction, structural fidelity, and qualitative long-time behavior while maintaining accurate surrogate prediction.

</details>


### [397] [Size Doesn't Matter: Cosine-Scored Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15054)
*Silen Naihin,Lev Stambler*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAEs using inner product scoring waste dictionary slots on norm detection when inputs are normalized. Replacing the score with learned cosine similarity eliminates this, yielding features that better align with human-recognizable concepts at matched reconstruction quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Under BatchTopK, high-norm tokens inflate all pre-activations simultaneously, claiming dictionary slots regardless of content alignment. Since sublayer normalization discards magnitude, the encoder detects a quantity the model does not read, motivating a better scoring function.

Method: Replace inner product score with a learned blend of cosine similarity and input magnitude, including a per-feature variant. The optimizer chooses how much norm to use; training is free to recover inner product but never does.

Result: No feature ever chooses more than half-magnitude dependence. At matched reconstruction, cosine encoder learns features that align with human-recognizable concepts far more often than standard SAEs, filling dictionary slots that inner product wastes on norm detectors. Loss reweighting barely closes the gap.

Conclusion: Cosine scoring should be the default for dictionary learning on normalized representations, though the advantage is not universal across all tasks or depths.

Abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) detect features via inner product, so a feature's activation scales with both its directional alignment and the input's norm. Under BatchTopK, high-norm tokens inflate all pre-activations simultaneously, claiming dictionary slots regardless of content alignment. This matters because sublayer normalization has already discarded the magnitude the score measures, so the encoder detects a quantity the model does not read. We replace the score with a learned blend of cosine similarity and input magnitude, letting the optimizer choose how much norm to use; a per-feature extension lets each feature decide independently. In both regimes, training is free to recover inner product but never does, with no feature ever choosing more than half-magnitude dependence. At matched reconstruction, the cosine encoder learns features that align with human-recognizable concepts far more often than standard, filling dictionary slots that inner product wastes on norm detectors. Loss reweighting that equalizes gradients barely closes the gap, confirming forward-pass score geometry as the lever. The advantage is not universal across tasks or depths, but we believe cosine scoring should be the default for dictionary learning on normalized representations.

</details>


### [398] [Machine Learning and the Random Walk Puzzle: Forecasting the CAD/USD Exchange Rate with Expanding Window Evaluation and SHAP Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15058)
*Louis Agyekum,Edmund Fosu Agyemang,Obu-Amoah Ampomah,Kofi Acheampong,Emmanuel Boadi,Priscilla Yaa Amakye,Fafa Shalom Tchorly,Enock Adu Bonsu,Eric Nyarko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Linear regression outperforms the naive random walk in forecasting USD/CAD exchange rates, while ML ensemble models (Random Forest, XGBoost, etc.) show only marginal improvements. SHAP analysis reveals that short-term lags dominate predictions, consistent with near-random-walk behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether ML models can beat the naive random walk benchmark in forecasting the USD/CAD exchange rate, contributing to the debate on exchange rate predictability.

Method: Five ML models (linear regression, random forest, gradient boosting, XGBoost, AdaBoost) evaluated against naive random walk and ETS using expanding-window framework, Diebold-Mariano test for significance, structural break detection, and SHAP analysis for interpretability.

Result: Only linear regression statistically outperforms the random walk (DM=3.0585, p=0.0071). Random Forest achieves lowest MAPE (1.17%) among non-random-walk models. Four structural breaks identified (2018 trade war, 2020 COVID recovery, 2022 rate hike peak, 2024 rate cut start).

Conclusion: The naive random walk remains a strong benchmark; linear regression can beat it statistically, but complex ML ensemble methods offer only marginal improvements. Short-term lags are the dominant predictors.

Abstract: This study examines whether machine learning (ML) models can outperform the naive random walk benchmark in forecasting the monthly USD/CAD exchange rate. Using daily data from the Bank of Canada spanning January 2017 to May 2026, resampled into 113 monthly observations, five ML models are evaluated: linear regression, random forest, gradient boosting, XGBoost, and AdaBoost. These models are benchmarked against the naive random walk model and exponential smoothing with Holt-Winters seasonality (ETS). All models are evaluated using an expanding-window framework to maintain strict out-of-sample integrity, and forecast-accuracy differences are assessed using the Diebold-Mariano (DM) test. Structural break detection identifies four significant breakpoints in the series, corresponding to the escalation of the US-China trade war in 2018, the COVID-19 economic recovery in 2020, the peak of the Bank of Canada rate-hiking cycle in 2022, and the start of the Bank of Canada rate-cutting cycle in 2024. SHAP, or Shapley Additive Explanations, analysis is applied to interpret the drivers of the best-performing ML model. The results show that the naive random walk model remains a formidable benchmark. Linear regression is the only model that statistically outperforms the naive random walk model, with a DM statistic of 3.0585 and a p value of 0.0071, whereas the ML ensemble models show only marginal differences. Random Forest with an expanding-window framework achieves the lowest MAPE of 1.17 percent among all models except the random walk. SHAP analysis confirms that short-term lags, particularly lag1 and lag2, and recent rolling means dominate predictions, consistent with the near-random-walk behavior of exchange rates.

</details>


### [399] [Phase-Localized Curation Does Not Help: A Negative Result on Per-Phase Metric Selection for Demonstration Filtering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15064)
*Aarav Bedi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper tests the hypothesis that demonstration curation for robotic manipulation should be done per-phase rather than globally, but finds phase-gated curation never outperforms global or uniform metrics, often performing worse due to signal dilution and lack of transferable metric selection across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work showed a single global metric can be the best defect detector yet worst curator, motivating the hypothesis that phase-based curation might resolve this paradox for manipulation demonstrations which have temporal phase structure.

Method: The authors test phase-gated curation against uniform and global metrics on three LIBERO pick-and-place tasks with controlled early-release defects, using five random seeds per condition, and compare performance across all strategies.

Result: Phase-gated curation was never the best strategy; it was worst on two of three tasks (Task 1: 86.0 vs 92.0 global; Task 3: 22.7 vs 48.0 uniform). Signal dilution from rank-aggregating across phases and non-transferable metric selection across tasks caused the failure.

Conclusion: Practitioners should prefer identifying a single defect-informative metric over decomposing curation by phase, as per-phase methods underperform and require costly re-derivation per task.

Abstract: Manipulation demonstrations have temporal phase structure, and a natural hypothesis is that demonstration-curation metrics should be applied within phases rather than globally. The idea is to segment each trajectory into phases, score each phase with the metric that is locally most informative, and then aggregate. This follows directly from prior work showing that a single global metric can be the best detector of a defect and yet the worst curator of the resulting policy. We test the per-phase hypothesis on three contact-rich LIBERO pick-and-place tasks with a controlled early-release structural defect, comparing phase-gated curation against the same metrics applied uniformly and against a strong single global metric. Across all three tasks and five random seeds per condition, phase-gated curation is never the best curation strategy, and it is the worst of the three on two of the three tasks (Task 1: 86.0 vs. 92.0 for global; Task 3: 22.7 vs. 48.0 for uniform). We trace the failure to a concrete mechanism. When the defect signal is concentrated in a single phase, rank-aggregating across phases dilutes that signal with uninformative scores from defect-free phases, selecting a worse demonstration subset than simply applying the defect-informative metric everywhere. We further show that the per-phase metric selection does not transfer across tasks, since no phase shares a winning metric between any two tasks, so the selection cannot be reused and must be re-derived per task from a noisy sweep. These results bound a plausible and previously untested method, and they argue that practitioners should prefer identifying a single defect-informative metric over decomposing curation by phase. We release the full pipeline, all metric implementations, and per-seed results.

</details>


### [400] [TriAdReview: Triangular Adversarial Review Architecture for Multi-Model Technical Document Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15074)
*Zhiqiang Zhou,Junliang Dai,Xu Ling*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TriAdReview is a triangular adversarial review system using two reviewer models and a judge to improve LLM outputs, achieving a 10.1% overall improvement but causing a -7.5% degradation on requirements analysis tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single LLM outputs suffer from over-engineering, security blind spots, and incomplete coverage, motivating the need for multi-model adversarial review.

Method: Proposes TriAdReview with two independent reviewer models (engineering and boundary perspectives) and a triangular judging mechanism to iteratively improve generator outputs across five benchmark tasks.

Result: Triple model achieves 10.1% overall improvement (26.2 vs 23.8/50), with strong gains in security audit (+27.6%), code generation (+20.8%), and architecture design (+15.6%), but -7.5% degradation on requirements analysis.

Conclusion: First empirical characterization of when multi-model adversarial review helps versus harms; adversarial architectures have a structural bias toward simplification counterproductive for completeness-oriented tasks, partially mitigated by reviewer prompt adaptation.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for technical document generation, yet single-model outputs often suffer from over-engineering, security blind spots, and incomplete coverage. We propose TriAdReview, a triangular adversarial review architecture that employs two independent reviewer models (engineering and boundary perspectives) and a triangular judging mechanism to iteratively improve a generator model's output. We evaluate TriAdReview across five benchmark tasks - architecture design, code generation, proposal review, security audit, and requirements analysis - using three configurations: single model (baseline), dual model (single review), and triple model (full system). Results across 75 experiments (n=5 per cell) show that the triple model configuration achieves a 10.1% overall improvement over the single model baseline (26.2 vs. 23.8 out of 50; p<0.05, paired t-test), with particularly strong gains on security audit (+27.6%), code generation (+20.8%), and architecture design (+15.6%). A second scorer (mimo-v2.5-pro) confirms the direction with a smaller effect (+2.7%), suggesting moderate inter-rater agreement. However, the system shows a -7.5% degradation on requirements analysis, revealing that adversarial review architectures have a structural bias toward simplification that is counterproductive for completeness-oriented tasks. We analyze this boundary condition through a task-type framework and demonstrate that reviewer prompt adaptation partially mitigates the issue. Our findings provide the first empirical characterization of when multi-model adversarial review helps versus harms, with implications for the design of collaborative AI systems.

</details>


### [401] [An Integrable Token Mixing Layer from the Generalized Yang Baxter Equation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15085)
*Snigdha Chandan Khilar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The YB Mixer is a mathematically grounded sequence token mixing layer built on free fermion and Yang-Baxter structures, providing norm-preserving orthogonal mappings, commuting transfer matrices for order-free inference, and spectral circulant generators for length generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a stable, mathematically principled sequence processing architecture by leveraging integrable systems theory, specifically local algebraic constraints that guarantee global computational stability.

Method: Uses Ising exchange algebra to create a free fermionic structure as an exactly norm-preserving orthogonal map; produces commuting transfer matrices for order-free adaptive inference; employs a spectral circulant generator to maintain orthogonality and commutativity for length generalization.

Result: A highly stable and mathematically grounded architecture for sequence processing with provable norm preservation, order-free inference capability, and length generalization.

Conclusion: The YB Mixer demonstrates that integrable systems principles can be effectively applied to design stable and adaptable sequence processing architectures.

Abstract: The YB Mixer is a sequence token mixing layer derived from free fermion and generalized Yang Baxter structures. It applies a core principle from integrable systems where a local algebraic constraint guarantees global computational stability. By using the Ising exchange algebra the mixer creates a free fermionic structure that acts as an exactly norm preserving orthogonal map. This algebra also produces commuting transfer matrices which allow inference to be order free and adaptable to any variable budget. To ensure the model can generalize to longer sequence lengths it uses a spectral circulant generator. This generator maintains the crucial orthogonal and commuting properties of the system. The result is a highly stable and mathematically grounded architecture for sequence processing.

</details>


### [402] [High-Dimensional Random Projection for Activation Steering in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15092)
*Minh-Hieu Pham,Bach Do,Laziz Abdullaev,Tan Minh Nguyen,Khoat Than*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HiDRA improves activation steering for LLMs by using random projections into high-dimensional space to capture nonlinear discriminative features, outperforming linear difference-in-means methods without added computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing difference-in-means activation steering methods are limited to capturing only mean differences between class activations, missing discriminative signals in nonlinear feature subspaces under the superposition hypothesis.

Method: HiDRA (High-Dimensional Random-projection for Activation Steering) performs activation addition in a randomly projected high-dimensional space to capture better discriminative structure beyond linear methods. It is training-free and integrates with existing activation steering methods.

Result: HiDRA consistently outperforms baseline counterparts across diverse LLM families and benchmarks, achieving stronger behavioral control without significant computational overhead.

Conclusion: High-dimensional random projection is an effective, low-cost way to enhance activation steering by accessing nonlinear discriminative features that linear methods cannot capture.

Abstract: Activation steering has emerged as a key methodology for controlling the behavior of large language models (LLMs). Existing difference-in-means based methods, however, are fundamentally limited: they capture only mean differences between class activations and fail to recover discriminative signals that naturally exist in the nonlinear feature subspace under the superposition hypothesis. Motivated by that, we propose High-Dimensional Random-projection for Activation Steering (HiDRA), a training-free approach that integrates seamlessly with existing activation steering methods. By performing activation addition in the projected high-dimensional space, HiDRA can provably capture a better discriminative structure beyond the reach of linear methods. Experiments across diverse LLM families and benchmarks demonstrate that HiDRA consistently outperforms baseline counterparts, achieving stronger behavioral control without significant computational overhead.

</details>


### [403] [Diversity-Driven Offline Multi-Objective Optimization via Nested Pareto Set Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15115)
*Yiyi Zhu,Yaolin Wen,Xiang Xia,Xin An,Hanyi Si,Xiang Shu,Yangde Fu,Liang Dou,Hong Qian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DOMOO is a method for offline multi-objective optimization that uses accumulative risk control, nested Pareto set learning, and diversity-driven selection to find diverse, high-quality Pareto solutions while avoiding out-of-distribution issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline MOO suffers from out-of-distribution (OOD) issues where surrogate models are inaccurate for unseen designs, causing solutions that are biased toward extremes and do not lie on the true Pareto front.

Method: (1) Accumulative risk control module to estimate risk and mitigate OOD issues; (2) Nested Pareto set learning (PSL) strategy for joint learning of preference and PSL parameters; (3) Diversity-driven selection strategy using IGD_offline indicator that considers both diversity and convergence.

Result: DOMOO achieves the best average rank across tasks in both convergence and diversity on synthetic and real-world benchmarks compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The proposed DOMOO framework effectively addresses OOD issues and bias in offline MOO, producing diverse and well-converged Pareto front solutions.

Abstract: Multi-objective optimization (MOO) has emerged as a powerful approach to solving complex optimization problems involving multiple objectives. In many practical scenarios, function evaluations are unavailable or prohibitively expensive, necessitating optimization solely based on a fixed offline dataset. In this setting, known as offline MOO, the goal is to find out the Pareto set without access to the true objective functions. This setting suffers from the out-of-distribution (OOD) issue, where the surrogate model is not accurate for unseen designs. Due to the OOD issue, surrogate errors may cause the optimizer to select solutions that do not lie on the true Pareto front and are biased toward its extremes. To address this, this paper proposes Diversity-driven Offline Multi-Objective Optimization (DOMOO), which aims to find out a diverse and high-quality set of solutions. First, DOMOO incorporates an accumulative risk control module that estimates the potential risk of candidate solutions and alleviates the OOD issue between the training data and the generated solutions. In addition, a nested Pareto set learning (PSL) strategy is proposed to jointly learn preference and PSL parameters, then optimize them, enabling adaptation to diverse Pareto front geometries. To further enhance solution quality, we design a diversity-driven selection strategy that extracts a representative and well-distributed set of final solutions. To achieve this diversity-driven selection strategy, we propose $\text{IGD}_\text{offline}$, a tailored indicator for the offline setting that considers both diversity and convergence, and avoids the bias of hypervolume indicator. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks show that DOMOO achieves the best average rank across tasks in both convergence and diversity among the compared methods.

</details>


### [404] [Beyond Accuracy: Measuring Bias Acknowledgment in Chain-of-Thought Reasoning for Responsible AI Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15127)
*Xian Sun,Wei Gao,Yingshuo Wang,Lingdong Kong,Yanhang Li,Zhichao Fan,Zexin Zhuang,Wenlong Dong,Zhiyuan Zheng,Hrishikesh Paranjape,Abhishek Mandal,Johnny R. Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies a blind spot in accuracy-only evaluation of reasoning models, showing that models can receive identical final-answer scores while dramatically differing in whether their traces acknowledge injected bias. The authors introduce a trace-level diagnostic measuring susceptibility (bias breaking correctness) and acknowledgment (trace explicitly flags bias). Testing on biased GSM8K, GPT-4o and Claude Sonnet 4 show similar susceptibility (~1.2-1.3%) but vastly different acknowledgment rates (13% vs 75%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation metrics focus only on final-answer accuracy, which fails to capture critical differences in model behavior when intermediate traces are used for education, oversight, or auditing. This creates a measurement blind spot for responsible AI evaluation.

Method: The authors propose a two-axis trace-level diagnostic: (1) susceptibility — whether injected bias breaks a previously correct answer, and (2) acknowledgment — whether the trace contains a rubric-defined surface reference to the injected content. They evaluate this on thousands of biased GSM8K trials across GPT-4o and Claude Sonnet 4.

Result: GPT-4o and Claude Sonnet 4 have nearly identical susceptibility rates (1.3% vs 1.2%) but dramatically different acknowledgment rates (13.0% vs 75.0%) under the same rubric.

Conclusion: Accuracy-only evaluation is insufficient for responsible evaluation of reasoning models; trace-level diagnostics reveal meaningful behavioral differences that accuracy metrics miss, particularly regarding how models handle biased inputs in their reasoning traces.

Abstract: Reasoning models are increasingly used in settings where the final answer is not the only object of review: educational tools may show students intermediate steps, decision-support systems may require human oversight, and audit workflows may inspect traces for misleading or biased input. In such settings, two responses can receive the same final-answer score while differing in whether the trace explicitly flags injected biasing content. Accuracy-only evaluation collapses these cases. We study this gap as a measurement blind spot for responsible evaluation and introduce a minimal trace-level diagnostic with two axes: \emph{susceptibility} (whether the bias breaks a previously correct answer) and \emph{acknowledgment} (whether the trace contains a rubric-defined surface reference to the injected content). Across thousands of biased GSM8K trials, GPT-4o and Claude Sonnet~4 have similar susceptibility rates ($1.3\%$ vs.\ $1.2\%$) but substantially different acknowledgment rates ($13.0\%$ vs.\ $75.0\%$) under the same rubric.

</details>


### [405] [Contextual Bandits for Maximizing Stimulated Word-of-Mouth Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15146)
*Ahmed Sayeed Faruk,Elena Zheleva*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a contextual multi-armed bandit framework that learns individual spillover probabilities to optimize stimulated word-of-mouth targeting in social networks, outperforming baseline methods that ignore spillover heterogeneity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Optimizing stimulated word-of-mouth requires identifying users most susceptible to spillover, but spillover probabilities vary across individuals and their connections. Accurately estimating this heterogeneity is crucial for improving targeting effectiveness.

Method: A contextual multi-armed bandit framework that learns individual spillover probabilities and ranks connected users to maximize rewards from stimulated word-of-mouth.

Result: Experiments on real-world network datasets show that accounting for spillover heterogeneity enhances targeting precision of top-k connected users, boosting rewards and outperforming baselines that do not learn individual spillover effects.

Conclusion: Learning individual spillover probabilities significantly improves stimulated word-of-mouth targeting effectiveness compared to ignoring heterogeneity.

Abstract: Stimulated word-of-mouth is a strategy that promotes information sharing through prompts or incentives. Optimizing stimulated word-of-mouth through social networks requires identifying and targeting connected users who are most susceptible to spillover, a phenomenon where the influence of recommendations extends beyond the immediate audience to impact their connected users. The probability of spillover varies across individuals, and their connections, leading to heterogeneity. Understanding and accurately estimating the spillover probabilities among users in social networks is crucial for improving the effectiveness of stimulated word-of-mouth. To address this, we present a novel contextual multi-armed bandit framework that learns individual spillover probabilities and ranks connected users to maximize rewards from stimulated word-of-mouth. Experiments on real-world network datasets demonstrate that accounting for spillover heterogeneity enhances the targeting precision of top-$k$ connected users, boosting rewards and outperforming baseline methods that do not learn individual spillover effects.

</details>


### [406] [False Sense of Safety in Selective Signal Classification: Auditing Bound Tightness and Exchangeability for Risk Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15153)
*Jingwen Zhou,Mingzhe Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper audits distribution-free risk control methods for selective prediction in anomaly detection and AI-generated image forensics, finding that uncertified thresholding frequently exceeds error budgets, certified methods like CP and WSR work under exchangeable splits, but all fail under grouped deployment due to broken exchangeability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate whether selective prediction with distribution-free risk control actually delivers its promised safety guarantees (error rate below budget alpha with confidence 1-delta) in real-world signal-domain detection tasks like machine anomalous-sound detection and AI-generated image forensics.

Method: The authors audit four calibration rules (NAIVE, Hoeffding, Clopper-Pearson/CP, betting/WSR) on signal-domain detectors for machine ASD and AI-generated image forensics, testing under synthetic trials (n=200), real-data splits, and grouped deployment scenarios with unseen machine types or generators.

Result: (i) NAIVE thresholding exceeds its declared budget in 49-73% of synthetic trials and up to 68% of real-data splits. (ii) CP and WSR certify substantial coverage with zero budget overruns under exchangeable splits, while Hoeffding certifies none. (iii) Under grouped deployment, all certified rules overrun in 9-30% of trials, far above delta, due to broken exchangeability.

Conclusion: Uncertified thresholding provides a false sense of safety. Certified bounds (CP, WSR) work under exchangeability but fail under grouped deployment due to broken exchangeability premise, where conservative per-group thresholding can restore validity at severe coverage cost.

Abstract: Selective prediction with distribution-free risk control promises that, with confidence 1-delta over the calibration draw, the error rate of accepted inputs stays below a user budget alpha. We audit this promise on signal-domain detectors -- machine anomalous-sound detection (ASD) and AI-generated-image forensics -- for four calibration rules: uncertified empirical thresholding (NAIVE) and certified Hoeffding, Clopper-Pearson (CP), and betting (WSR) upper confidence bounds. We report three findings. (i) NAIVE thresholding, common in practice, exceeds its declared budget in 49-73% of synthetic trials (n=200 calibration points) and in up to 68% of real-data splits: a false sense of safety rather than a broken theorem, since the rule never had a certificate. (ii) Tightness matters: CP and WSR certify substantial coverage where Hoeffding certifies none, with zero observed budget overruns under exchangeable splits. (iii) Under grouped deployment (unseen machine types or generators), certified rules overrun in 9-30% of trials -- far above delta -- showing the failure lies in the broken exchangeability premise, not in the bounds; a conservative per-group threshold restores validity at a severe coverage cost.

</details>


### [407] [Semantic Reasoning in Medicine: The Role of Knowledge Graphs Across Five Key Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15155)
*Haniye Sherafatmandjoo,Mohammad Akbari,Zahed Rahmati*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This survey reviews Knowledge Graphs (KGs) in healthcare, covering their applications (clinical decision support, prediction, recommendations, precision medicine, QA) and construction methods (from EHRs, literature, web sources using ontologies, deep learning, neuro-symbolic pipelines). It also identifies key challenges: fragmented knowledge, data alignment, reasoning fragility, and privacy/bias issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Knowledge graphs offer a promising semantic backbone for integrating complex biomedical data and enhancing interpretability, reasoning, and personalization in healthcare AI systems. The paper aims to systematically survey and categorize this growing field.

Method: The paper conducts a comprehensive survey that categorizes KG research in medicine along two dimensions: (1) application-oriented (clinical decision support, prediction, recommendations, precision medicine, QA) and (2) methodology-oriented (KG construction from EHRs, clinical narratives, biomedical literature, web resources using ontologies, semantic web, deep learning, neuro-symbolic pipelines).

Result: KGs enhance interpretability, semantic coherence, and patient-specific reasoning across diverse medical applications. Construction pipelines range from ontology-based to deep-learning and hybrid neuro-symbolic approaches. However, significant challenges persist: limited knowledge coverage, data heterogeneity, fragile reasoning on dense graphs, and privacy/bias/accountability concerns.

Conclusion: While KGs show strong utility in medicine, addressing challenges in knowledge coverage, alignment, reasoning robustness, and ethical issues is critical. Future work should focus on safer and more effective integration of KG-driven AI into clinical environments.

Abstract: Knowledge graphs (KGs) have emerged as a promising solution for integrating and reasoning over complex biomedical and clinical data in healthcare. By representing structured relationships among entities such as diseases, drugs, symptoms, and patient records, KGs provide a semantic backbone for decision-making, prediction, recommendation, and personalized care. Recent advances have demonstrated their utility across diverse medical applications--including clinical decision support systems, disease and treatment outcome prediction, health recommender systems, precision medicine, and medical question answering--where KGs often enhance interpretability, semantic coherence, and patient-specific reasoning. In parallel, a growing body of work focuses on medical KG generation itself, proposing frameworks that construct graphs from EHRs, clinical narratives, biomedical literature, and web resources using ontologies, semantic web technologies, deep-learning-based information extraction, and hybrid neuro-symbolic pipelines. Despite this progress, significant challenges remain, including limited and fragmented knowledge coverage, difficulties in aligning heterogeneous data sources, the fragility of current reasoning and representation-learning methods on dense multi-relational graphs, and unresolved issues related to privacy, bias, and accountability. This survey reviews and categorizes current research on KGs in medicine along both application-oriented and methodology-oriented dimensions, discusses their benefits and technical foundations, and outlines key limitations and open research directions. By analyzing trends, architectures, and evaluation practices, this work aims to guide future developments in KG-driven medical AI systems and support their safe and effective integration into healthcare environments.

</details>


### [408] [PolyKV: Heterogeneous Retention and Allocation for KV Cache Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15157)
*Chao Fei,Panos Kalnis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PolyKV is a layer-wise KV cache compression framework that assigns different compression policies and non-uniform cache budgets to different transformer layers, outperforming uniform single-policy methods by recovering 25.7%-54.5% of the performance gap to FullKV across various budget settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing KV cache compression methods apply a single compression policy and uniform cache budget across all transformer layers, ignoring that different layers play different roles during prefill and decoding and may require different eviction strategies and cache capacities.

Method: PolyKV is a layer-wise optimization framework that routes each layer to a suitable KV compression policy based on layer-level signals and assigns non-uniform budgets under a fixed total budget, enabling heterogeneous compositions of existing KV cache methods.

Result: On LLaMA-3.1-8B and Qwen3-8B under a 512-token average KV budget, PolyKV recovers 54.5% and 25.7% of the LongBench performance gap between the strongest single-policy baseline and FullKV. Across 128-1024 budget sweep, PolyKV consistently improves over the strongest baseline by 1.7%-6.4% (40.0%-54.5% recovery of FullKV gap).

Conclusion: Layer-wise heterogeneous KV compression with non-uniform budget allocation consistently outperforms uniform single-policy approaches across varying budget constraints.

Abstract: KV cache compression is essential for reducing the memory cost of long-context large language model inference. Existing approaches, however, typically apply a single compression policy and a uniform cache budget across all transformer layers. This uniform design ignores the fact that different layers can play different roles during prefill and decoding, and may therefore require different eviction strategies and cache capacities. We present PolyKV, a layer-wise KV cache optimization framework that considers design space with method selection and budget allocation. PolyKV routes each layer to a suitable KV compression policy based on layer-level signals, while assigning non-uniform budgets under a fixed total budget. This formulation enables heterogeneous compositions of existing KV cache methods. Experiments on LLaMA-3.1-8B and Qwen3-8B show that, under the same 512-token average KV budget, PolyKV recovers 54.5% and 25.7% of the LongBench performance gap between the strongest single-policy baseline and FullKV, respectively. Across 128-1024 budget sweep, PolyKV consistently improves over the strongest baseline by 1.7%-6.4%, corresponding to 40.0%-54.5% recovery of the FullKV gap.

</details>


### [409] [Towards a Unified Generative Model for Scarce Time Series with Domain Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15172)
*Zihao Yao,Qi Zheng,Jiankai Zuo,Yaying Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TimeMoDE integrates Diffusion Transformers with Mixture-of-Experts to generate realistic time series under data scarcity, using domain prompts and diffusion timestep signals for adaptive generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generative models for time series assume abundant training data, limiting their effectiveness in data-scarce settings.

Method: TimeMoDE combines Diffusion Transformers with Mixture-of-Experts, pre-trained on multi-domain datasets. It uses Domain Prompts for expert assignment and diffusion timestep signals for stage-dependent denoising.

Result: Outperforms existing methods under diverse low-data settings, establishing a paradigm for few-shot time series generation.

Conclusion: TimeMoDE effectively addresses data scarcity in time series generation through domain-adaptive and diffusion-stage-aware mechanisms.

Abstract: Synthesizing realistic time series with generative models has wide-ranging applications in real-world scenarios. Despite recent progress, most existing methods are trained under the assumption of abundant training data, which substantially limits their effectiveness in data-scarce settings. In this paper, we propose TimeMoDE, a novel framework that integrates Diffusion Transformers with Mixture-of-Experts to exploit both domain adaptability and diffusion-stage awareness for time series generation under data scarcity. It is pre-trained on a large-scale collection of multi-domain datasets to extract domain-agnostic temporal representations and domain-specific information benefiting generalization during fine-tuning. We propose Domain Prompts to condition expert assignment for indistinguishable noised tokens, mitigating the limitations of capturing inter-dataset relationships. Moreover, we incorporate diffusion timestep signals to equip the experts with awareness of time series degradation variations, facilitating adaptive calibrate to stage-dependent denoising requirements. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TimeMoDE outperforms existing methods under diverse low-data settings. It establishes an innovative paradigm for advanced time series few-shot generation.

</details>


### [410] [StarOR: Synergizing Tree Search and Test-Time Reinforcement Learning for Optimization Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15197)
*Jiajun Li,Yu Ding,Shisi Guan,Ran Hou,Wanyuan Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: StarOR is a search-and-adaptation framework combining MCTS with Test-Time Reinforcement Learning for hierarchical optimization modeling, achieving SOTA performance with a 4B backbone by using instance-specific policy refinement and unsupervised multi-faceted rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing learning-based methods for optimization modeling are costly to adapt to new problem distributions (training data dependency), while one-shot generation is brittle due to cascading symbolic errors. Search-based methods rely on fixed policies with limited credit assignment for intermediate decisions.

Method: StarOR decomposes optimization modeling into four stages, uses MCTS with a transient LoRA adapter updated via GRPO at each non-terminal node, leverages MCTS-generated siblings as local comparison sets for instance-specific policy refinement, and employs an unsupervised multi-faceted reward system for fine-grained feedback without ground-truth labels.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across five optimization benchmarks with a 4B backbone model, outperforming existing methods and frontier LLMs.

Conclusion: StarOR effectively addresses hierarchical modeling brittleness and fixed-policy limitations by synergistically combining search-time exploration with instance-specific policy adaptation, offering a promising test-time scaling approach for optimization modeling.

Abstract: Optimization modeling is inherently hierarchical, requiring a precise sequence of symbolic commitments. Traditional learning-based automated optimization modeling methods improve modeling policies through large-scale annotated or curated training data, but are costly to adapt to new problem distributions. Meanwhile, one-shot generation remains brittle in hierarchical modeling, where early symbolic errors can propagate into invalid formulations. Test-time scaling offers a promising alternative by enabling structural exploration with additional instance-level computation; however, existing search-based methods typically rely on a fixed policy, causing repeated rollouts to inherit similar modeling biases and providing limited credit assignment for intermediate decisions. To address these limitations, we propose StarOR, a synergistic search-and-adaptation framework that couples MCTS with Test-Time Reinforcement Learning for optimization modeling. StarOR decomposes the modeling process into four stages and updates a transient LoRA adapter via GRPO at each non-terminal node. By using MCTS-generated siblings as local comparison sets, StarOR transforms search-time exploration into instance-specific policy refinement. Moreover, an unsupervised multi-faceted reward system provides fine-grained feedback for intermediate formulation decisions without ground-truth labels. Experiments across five optimization benchmarks show that StarOR achieves state-of-the-art performance even with a 4B backbone, outperforming existing methods and the frontier LLMs.

</details>


### [411] [Controlled Dynamics Attractor Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15207)
*Cheng Zhang,Minnan Luo,Zesheng Yang,Ming Li,Yong-Jin Liu,Qinghua Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CDAT combines Mo-vMF attention energy with Hopfield refinement and CANN-inspired excitation-inhibition modulation, achieving SOTA performance in graph anomaly detection and classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between Transformer-based attention mechanisms and biologically plausible Continuous Attractor Neural Networks (CANNs) by introducing controlled, topology-constrained dynamical systems.

Method: CDAT couples a mixture von Mises-Fisher (Mo-vMF) attention energy with a Hopfield refinement energy, and augments energy descent with CANN-inspired excitation-inhibition modulation, forming a topology-constrained dynamical system with controlled inference dynamics.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks in graph anomaly detection and graph classification.

Conclusion: The controlled dynamics and structured energy landscapes of CDAT yield robust representation learning, linking attractor-style dynamics to modern energy-based attention.

Abstract: Transformer architectures have dramatically advanced representation learning and inference in deep models through self-attention mechanisms. In parallel,associative memory (AM) frameworks map representations onto energy landscapes, offering interpretable retrieval mechanisms. However, their continuous-time inference dynamics lack the biological plausibility of classical Continuous Attractor Neural Networks (CANNs). To bridge this gap, we propose Controlled Dynamics Attractor Transformer (CDAT), which couples a mixture von Mises-Fisher (Mo-vMF) attention energy with a Hopfield refinement energy, while augmenting energy descent with a CANN-inspired excitation-inhibition modulation. CDAT instantiates a topology-constrained dynamical system whose couplings encode relational structure among tokens, thereby linking attractor-style dynamics to modern energy-based attention. We further provide a constructive dissipation analysis to formally establish their controlled inference dynamics. Benefiting from these robust and structured dynamics, CDAT achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks in graph anomaly detection and graph classification.

</details>


### [412] [Can Neural Networks Achieve Optimal Computational-statistical Tradeoff? An Analysis on Single-Index Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15219)
*Siyu Chen,Beining Wu,Miao Lu,Zhuoran Yang,Tianhao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a gradient-based algorithm for training two-layer neural networks to learn Gaussian single-index models, achieving optimal sample complexity matching SQ lower bounds for both dense and sparse settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether neural networks trained with gradient methods can achieve the optimal computational-statistical tradeoff in learning Gaussian single-index models, which was previously only known for SQ algorithms.

Method: The authors design a unified gradient-based algorithm for training two-layer neural networks, incorporating label transformation and landscape smoothing techniques. For the sparse setting (k = o(√d)), they introduce a novel weight perturbation technique to exploit sparsity structure.

Result: The algorithm achieves sample complexity Õ(d^{s*/2} ∨ d), matching the SQ lower bound up to polylog factors for all s* ≥ 1. For sparse θ* with k = o(√d), sample complexity matches the derived SQ lower bound of Ω̃(k^{s*}) up to polylog factors.

Conclusion: Gradient-based neural network training can achieve optimal computational-statistical tradeoffs for Gaussian single-index models, and the weight perturbation technique may extend to other sparse problems like sparse tensor PCA.

Abstract: In this work, we tackle the following question: Can neural networks trained with gradient-based methods achieve the optimal computational-statistical tradeoff in learning Gaussian single-index models? Prior research has shown that any polynomial-time algorithm under the statistical query (SQ) framework requires $Ω(d^{s^\star/2}\lor d)$ samples, where $s^\star$ is the generative exponent representing the intrinsic difficulty of learning the underlying model. However, it remains unknown whether neural networks can achieve this sample complexity. Inspired by prior techniques such as label transformation and landscape smoothing for learning single-index models, we propose a unified gradient-based algorithm for training a two-layer neural network in polynomial time. Our method is adaptable to a variety of loss and activation functions, covering a broad class of existing approaches. We show that our algorithm learns a feature representation that strongly aligns with the unknown signal $θ^\star$, with sample complexity $\widetilde{O} (d^{s^\star/2} \lor d)$, matching the SQ lower bound up to a polylogarithmic factor for all generative exponents $s^\star\geq 1$. Furthermore, we extend our approach to the setting where $θ^\star$ is $k$-sparse for $k = o(\sqrt{d})$ by introducing a novel weight perturbation technique that leverages the sparsity structure. We derive a corresponding SQ lower bound of order $\widetildeΩ(k^{s^\star})$, matched by our method up to a polylogarithmic factor. Our framework, especially the weight perturbation technique, is of independent interest, and suggests potential gradient-based solutions to other problems such as sparse tensor PCA.

</details>


### [413] [Edu-Theater: A Data-Efficient Agent Framework for Scalable Learner Behavior Simulation through Staging Roll-Call](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15225)
*Weibo Gao,Qi Liu,Linan Yue,Zheng Zhang,Yichao Du,Fangzhou Yao,Ao Yu,Zhenya Huang,Shijin Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Edu-Theater proposes a cohort-aware roll-call simulation paradigm that uses LLM-powered agents to simulate learner behavior by first constructing cohort-level proficiency priors and then refining individual states with minimal diagnostic queries, achieving better simulation accuracy with fewer computational resources than individual-centric methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale learner-task interaction data are costly to collect and constrained by privacy and learner engagement. Existing learner simulators are individual-centric, requiring dense interaction histories for each learner, which is data- and computation-intensive and fragile in cold-start scenarios.

Method: Proposes a cohort-aware roll-call simulation paradigm that constructs cohort-level proficiency priors and refines individual learner states through targeted diagnostic queries. Introduces Edu-Theater, an LLM-powered agent system with a teacher agent and retrospective roll-call probing over learner logs.

Result: Achieves higher simulation accuracy with significantly fewer LLM calls on two real-world datasets, producing synthetic data that enhances downstream applications such as adaptive testing.

Conclusion: The cohort-aware paradigm is more efficient and scalable than individual-centric approaches, enabling effective learner simulation without dense per-learner histories, with practical benefits for downstream educational applications.

Abstract: Large-scale learner-task interaction data are crucial for intelligent educational systems but are costly to collect and constrained by privacy and learner engagement. Learner simulators play a critical role in simulating scalable learner behavior without the need for continuous involvement of real learners. However, existing methods are predominantly \textbf{individual-centric}, pairing a simulator with each learner to iteratively infer latent knowledge states from dense interaction histories, which is both data- and computation-intensive, and fragile in cold-start scenarios. We propose a \textbf{cohort-aware roll-call simulation paradigm} that first constructs cohort-level proficiency priors and refines individual learner states through a small number of targeted diagnostic queries. Based on this paradigm, we introduce \textbf{Edu-Theater}, an LLM-powered agent system that performs cohort-aware learner simulation via a teacher agent and retrospective roll-call probing over learner logs. Edu-Theater enables scalable future behavior simulation without the need for dense per-learner histories. Experiments on two real-world datasets demonstrate that Edu-Theater achieves higher simulation accuracy with significantly fewer LLM calls, producing synthetic data that enhances downstream applications such as adaptive testing.

</details>


### [414] [EnvShip-Bench: An Environment-Enhanced Benchmark for Short-Term Vessel Trajectory Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15240)
*Kun Ma,Qilong Han,Chengjing Song,Jingzheng Yao,Hao Wang,Changmao Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EnvShip-Bench is a unified benchmark for short-term vessel trajectory prediction built from large-scale AIS data (DMA & NOAA), featuring standardized protocols (10min observation, 10min prediction, 20s sampling), quality-focused subsets, and environmental/interaction context extensions for fair and reproducible research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing public AIS datasets suffer from inconsistent forecasting protocols, uneven data quality, and lack of contextual annotations, hindering fair comparison and context-aware modeling in vessel trajectory prediction.

Method: The authors constructed EnvShip-Bench by processing raw AIS data from DMA and NOAA through a common pipeline, standardizing the forecasting protocol (10min observe, 10min predict, 20s sampling in vessel-centric local metric coordinates), and providing both a core large-scale benchmark, a quality-first compact subset, plus synchronized environmental and nearby-vessel context extensions.

Result: EnvShip-Bench supports trajectory-only, environment-aware, and interaction-aware forecasting under a unified evaluation framework, with extensive benchmark statistics validating its standardized, extensible, and context-aware capabilities.

Conclusion: The benchmark provides a standardized, extensible, and context-aware foundation for maritime trajectory forecasting research, addressing key reproducibility and fairness issues in the field.

Abstract: Vessel trajectory prediction is important for intelligent shipping, maritime surveillance, and navigation safety. However, existing public maritime AIS resources are often limited by inconsistent forecasting protocols, uneven data quality, and the lack of benchmark-ready contextual annotations, which hinder fair comparison and context-aware modeling. To address this gap, we present EnvShip-Bench, a unified benchmark for short-term vessel trajectory prediction built from large-scale raw AIS data from the Danish Maritime Authority (DMA) and NOAA through a common processing pipeline. EnvShip-Bench adopts a standardized forecasting protocol with 10 minutes of observation, 10 minutes of prediction, and 20-second sampling in vessel-centric local metric coordinates. Beyond the large-scale core benchmark, it provides a quality-first compact subset for efficient and reproducible experimentation, together with synchronized environmental and nearby-vessel context extensions. As a result, EnvShip-Bench supports trajectory-only, environment-aware, and interaction-aware forecasting under a unified evaluation framework. Extensive benchmark statistics and analysis demonstrate that EnvShip-Bench offers a standardized, extensible, and context-aware foundation for maritime trajectory forecasting research.

</details>


### [415] [M-CTX: Exact and Scalable Spatial Context Retrieval for Trajectory Analytics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15244)
*Kun Ma,Qilong Han,Chengjing Song,Jingzheng Yao,Xiao Han,Yuee Zhou,Changmao Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: M-CTX is a scalable spatial context-retrieval framework that replaces brute-force spatial context construction in trajectory prediction with index-backed operators, achieving 226x speed-up (from 17 CPU-days to 1.8 hours) on a 5.48M-anchor maritime corpus while maintaining exact reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spatial context construction (map geometry, SDFs, nearby agents) for trajectory predictors has become a hidden systems bottleneck, requiring ~17 CPU-days for a 5.48M-anchor maritime corpus, dominating downstream predictor costs.

Method: M-CTX recasts context construction as an ingest-once, query-many spatial database workload with three composable, index-backed operators replacing brute-force OSM range retrieval, SDF computation, and moving-vessel neighbor lookup. The learned range-index backend BR-LZ provides recall-complete MBR-overlap range retrieval.

Result: 226x end-to-end speed-up (17 CPU-days to 1.8 hours) on 5.48M-anchor corpus; reproduces reference context exactly across four maritime regions, eight baselines, synthetic workloads up to 40M features, and 10^7-record AIS streams; optional storage compresses SDF context by 64x with only 0.04m ADE change.

Conclusion: Exact spatial context retrieval should be treated as a first-class database problem in modern trajectory analytics, as significant speed-ups are achievable without sacrificing accuracy.

Abstract: Modern trajectory predictors increasingly condition on external spatial context, such as map geometry, signed distance fields (SDFs), and nearby moving agents. While this context improves prediction quality, constructing it for every training anchor has become a hidden systems bottleneck. In a representative maritime AIS pipeline, spatial context construction requires roughly 17 CPU-days for a 5.48M-anchor corpus, dominating the cost of the downstream predictor. We present M-CTX, an exact and scalable spatial context-retrieval framework for trajectory analytics. M-CTX recasts context construction as an ingest-once, query-many spatial database workload and replaces three brute-force stages -- OSM range retrieval, SDF computation, and moving-vessel neighbour lookup -- with composable, index-backed operators. Its learned range-index backend, BR-LZ, provides recall-complete MBR-overlap range retrieval and reduces candidate amplification by 1.1x--2.7x relative to global-expansion one-curve baselines. Across four maritime regions, eight baseline systems, synthetic workloads with up to 40M spatial features, and 10^7-record AIS streams, M-CTX reproduces the reference context exactly. On the 5.48M-anchor corpus, it reduces context construction from about 17 CPU-days to 1.8 hours, a measured 226x end-to-end speed-up. An optional storage mode further compresses SDF context by 64x with only a 0.04 m ADE change. These results establish exact spatial context retrieval as a first-class database problem in modern trajectory analytics. Code and datasets are publicly available at https://github.com/mark000071/M-CTX-Traj.

</details>


### [416] [Exploring Starts Are Not Enough: Counterexamples and a Fix for Monte Carlo Exploring Starts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15247)
*Octave Oliviers,Glenn Vinnicombe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper settles a long-standing open question by showing that tabular Monte Carlo Exploring Starts (MCES) can converge to suboptimal solutions, and provides a convergence-restoring modification via state-by-state inverse-frequency learning rate scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve the long-standing open question of whether MCES converges to optimal solutions in the tabular setting.

Method: Constructs counterexamples for both initial-visit and first-visit MCES, and proposes scaling learning rates inversely to update frequencies on a per-state basis to restore convergence.

Result: Shows that both initial-visit and first-visit MCES can converge to suboptimal solutions, and provides a scalable modification that guarantees convergence to optimality.

Conclusion: Exploring starts alone do not guarantee optimality; convergence depends critically on the relative size and frequency of action updates, making learning rate choices and exploration-exploitation balance central to MCES analysis.

Abstract: The asymptotic behaviour of Monte Carlo Exploring Starts (MCES) is a long-standing open question in reinforcement learning, even in the tabular setting. We investigated the convergence properties of tabular MCES by constructing examples in which the algorithm converges to suboptimal solutions. This paper presents new counterexamples for both initial-visit and first-visit MCES and gives a convergence-restoring modification for the initial-visit case. We show that stable suboptimal solutions may exist for initial-visit MCES with sample-average updates even when greedy actions are updated more often than non-greedy actions on average. However, by scaling learning rates inversely to update frequencies on a state-by-state basis, convergence to optimality is guaranteed. Unlike previous uniformisation methods, this modification is applicable to large-scale problems that require approximating the estimated value function. We then extend the example to show that sample-average first-visit MCES may also converge to suboptimal solutions. This largely settles a fundamental open problem and shows that exploring starts alone do not guarantee convergence to optimality. More broadly, these results highlight that convergence depends critically on the relative size and frequency of updates applied to different actions, making the choice of learning rates and the balance between exploration and exploitation central to the analysis of MCES and the implementation of scalable Monte Carlo control methods.

</details>


### [417] [AI for Social Good: An Investigation of the Causal Relationship Between Environmental Regulations and Their Effects on Air Pollution in London, UK](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15257)
*Yang Han,Jacqueline CK Lam,Victor OK Li,Yiu-Wai Man*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a Bayesian deep learning framework to estimate that London's air pollution regulations reduced PM2.5 by 1.88 μg/m³ (12.35%) from 2010-2020, with effects strengthening over time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Estimating air pollution regulation effects is difficult due to non-random policy implementation and confounding factors like meteorology, socioeconomic changes, and overlapping interventions. Traditional methods struggle with these complexities.

Method: A Bayesian deep learning framework integrating Bayesian LSTM for temporal dependencies, Bayesian embedding layers for temporal/regulation inputs, and a regulation status prediction branch for propensity score adjustment. Counterfactual predictions under a no-regulation scenario are compared with observed PM2.5 concentrations.

Result: Average PM2.5 reduction of 1.88 μg/m³ (12.35%) with 95% CI [1.64-2.12]. Effects were limited before 2013, clearer from 2013-2017, and strongest in 2018-2019.

Conclusion: Sustained and cumulative regulatory interventions contributed to measurable improvements in London's air quality. The uncertainty-aware causal AI approach supports environmental accountability and evidence-based governance.

Abstract: Air pollution regulation is central to urban public health governance, but estimating its effects is difficult because policies are implemented non-randomly and pollution trajectories are shaped by meteorology, socioeconomic change, temporal trends, and overlapping interventions. This study develops an uncertainty-aware Bayesian deep learning framework to estimate the aggregate effect of air pollution regulations on PM$_{2.5}$ concentrations in London from 2010 to 2020. The framework integrates daily PM$_{2.5}$ observations from Inner London monitoring stations, meteorological covariates, annual socioeconomic indicators, month-of-year and day-of-week indicators, and daily regulation status data for 32 policy measures. A Bayesian LSTM captures temporal dependencies in environmental and socioeconomic covariates, Bayesian embedding layers represent temporal and regulation status inputs, and a regulation status prediction branch supports propensity score-based adjustment for non-random policy implementation. Regulatory effects are estimated by comparing observed PM$_{2.5}$ concentrations with counterfactual predictions under a hypothetical no-regulation scenario, with uncertainty summarized across repeated Bayesian training runs and bootstrap resampling. Results show that London's regulations were associated with an average PM$_{2.5}$ reduction of 1.88 $μ$g/m$^3$, a relative reduction of 12.35%, with a 95% confidence interval of 1.64-2.12 $μ$g/m$^3$. Estimated effects were limited before 2013, became clearer from 2013 to 2017, and were strongest in 2018 and 2019. The findings suggest that sustained and cumulative regulatory interventions contributed to measurable improvements in London's air quality. This study demonstrates how uncertainty-aware causal AI can support environmental accountability, public health protection, and evidence-based governance for environmental decision-making.

</details>


### [418] [Trust-Region Diffusion Policies for Massively Parallel On-Policy RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15260)
*Huy Le,Onur Celik,Denis Blessing,Tai Hoang,Claas A Voelcker,Axel Brunnbauer,Felix Richter,Michael Volpp,Gerhard Neumann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TruDi introduces trust-region optimization for diffusion policies in massively parallel on-policy RL, outperforming baselines on 73 tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing massively parallel RL methods rely on simple Gaussian policies, while diffusion models (expressive but mainly used in offline/off-policy settings) have not been effectively applied to on-policy massively parallel RL due to unstable training from rapidly changing data distributions.

Method: TruDi integrates a trust-region optimization rule that enforces a KL-divergence constraint over the entire diffusion trajectory, enabling stable training of diffusion policies in on-policy RL with massively parallel simulations.

Result: Evaluated on 4 massively parallel RL benchmarks (73 tasks total); consistently outperforms or matches strong baselines on standard tasks, with clear gains on challenging humanoid control tasks.

Conclusion: TruDi establishes a strong new baseline for massively parallel on-policy RL by successfully combining diffusion policies with trust-region optimization.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with massively parallel simulations has become a standard framework for developing robust, deployable policies; however, most existing approaches still rely on simple Gaussian policy parameterizations. Diffusion models provide a more expressive policy class and have shown strong performance on challenging control problems, yet most diffusion-based RL methods are designed for offline or off-policy training. In this work, we ask whether diffusion policies can be trained effectively in the massively parallel, on-policy regime. To this end, we introduce Trust-region Diffusion Policies (TruDi), which enables diffusion policies for on-policy RL with massively parallel simulations. This setting is particularly challenging because the data distribution changes quickly across updates, making stable training with complex policies difficult. TruDi addresses this by integrating a trust-region optimization rule to enforce a KL-divergence constraint over the entire diffusion trajectory. Empirically, we evaluate TruDi on a diverse set of 4 massively parallel RL benchmarks comprising a total of 73 tasks. Across these tasks, TruDi consistently outperforms or is on-par with strong baselines on standard tasks and achieves clear gains on more challenging humanoid control tasks, establishing a strong new baseline for massively parallel on-policy RL.

</details>


### [419] [When to use what Schatten-$p$ norm in deep learning?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15268)
*Thomas Pethick*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper resolves conflicting observations about Schatten-∞ optimizers (e.g., Muon) by showing their effectiveness is regime-dependent, with smaller Schatten-p geometries being optimal in low-dimensional regimes (including Chinchilla scaling), supported by a new noise-robust acceleration result for the SODA framework for p>2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve conflicting observations about whether Schatten-∞ based optimizers like Muon are beneficial.

Method: The authors show that the conclusion is regime-dependent, proving that smaller Schatten-p geometries can be optimal in low-dimensional regimes (including Chinchilla scaling). They derive a new noise-robust acceleration result for the SODA framework for p>2.

Result: Explains why Muon-like methods don't require warmup, why they naturally favor large batches, and yields a batch size scaling rule for arbitrary p.

Conclusion: The effectiveness of Schatten-∞ optimizers is regime-dependent; smaller Schatten-p geometries can be optimal in low-dimensional settings.

Abstract: Schatten-$\infty$ based optimizers such as Muon have shown promising empirical performance, but there remains seemingly conflicting observations regarding whether they are beneficial. We resolve this conflict by showing that the conclusion is regime dependent. Even when the objective is smooth in the Schatten-$\infty$ geometry, smaller Schatten-$p$ geometries can be optimal, specifically in the low-dimensional regime, which we show includes Chinchilla scaling. This conclusion follows from a new noise-robust acceleration result for the SODA framework for $p>2$. The same analysis explains why Muon-like methods do not require warmup, why they naturally favor large batches, and yields a batch size scaling rule for arbitrary $p$.

</details>


### [420] [RECTOR: Masked Region-Channel-Temporal Modeling for Affective and Cognitive Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15278)
*Jinhan Liu,Mahsa Shoaran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RECTOR is a self-supervised framework for EEG/sEEG analysis that jointly learns region-channel-temporal representations via masked modeling, achieving SOTA on emotion recognition and task-engagement classification with strong robustness to missing channels and cross-montage generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Affective and cognitive disorders involve complex, distributed, and time-varying brain network dynamics that are difficult to capture with conventional fixed anatomical priors, motivating a more flexible and robust representation learning approach from EEG/sEEG.

Method: RECTOR uses a hierarchical block-sparse self-attention mechanism (RECTOR-SA) induced by Adaptive Functional Partitioning, which evolves from static anatomical regions to adaptive functional regions. Self-supervision is achieved via Masked Topology and Representation Learning with three objectives: Masked Predictive Modeling, Topological Structure Modeling, and Cross-View Consistency.

Result: RECTOR achieves state-of-the-art performance on EEG emotion recognition and sEEG task-engagement classification benchmarks. It also demonstrates strong robustness to missing channels and cross-montage generalization.

Conclusion: RECTOR's strong robustness and generalization suggest its potential for large-scale pre-training on heterogeneous EEG/sEEG data, while providing interpretable insights at both region and channel levels.

Abstract: Affective and cognitive disorders manifest as distributed, time-varying brain network dynamics across regions, channels, and time, challenging robust representation learning from EEG/sEEG for clinical diagnosis. We propose RECTOR (Masked Region-Channel-Temporal Modeling), an end-to-end self-supervised framework that unifies joint region-channel-temporal representation learning beyond fixed anatomical priors. At its core, RECTOR-SA is a hierarchical, block-sparse self-attention induced by Adaptive Functional Partitioning that evolves region structures from static anatomical definitions to adaptive functional regions. The self-supervision is driven by Masked Topology and Representation Learning, which jointly optimizes three complementary objectives: Masked Predictive Modeling, Topological Structure Modeling, and Cross-View Consistency. Across diverse benchmarks, RECTOR sets a new state-of-the-art in EEG emotion recognition and sEEG task-engagement classification. Crucially, its strong robustness to missing channels and cross-montage generalization underscores its potential for large-scale pre-training on heterogeneous EEG/sEEG, providing interpretable insights at both region and channel levels.

</details>


### [421] [Rethinking Structural Anomaly Detection: From Decision Boundaries to Projection Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15280)
*Alexander Bauer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a geometric perspective for anomaly detection that learns a projection operator onto the manifold of normal samples and identifies anomalies based on projection residuals, unifying reconstruction-based methods and outperforming both boundary-based and existing reconstruction-based approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing anomaly detection methods (density estimation, decision boundary learning) assume normal data occupies a non-zero volume region, which mismatches the low-dimensional manifold structure of real data, leading to degraded performance. The authors aim to resolve this mismatch.

Method: Learn a projection operator onto the normal data manifold. A sample is anomalous if projection significantly alters it. Anomaly detection is reframed as computing projection residuals rather than probabilistic modeling. This unifies reconstruction-based methods and explains their success/failure via projection quality.

Result: Projection-aligned methods achieve strong empirical performance, outperforming boundary-based methods and improving upon existing reconstruction-based methods. They exhibit strong generalization due to contraction behavior toward the manifold and reduce misclassification of rare but normal samples.

Conclusion: The geometric perspective effectively addresses the manifold-data mismatch in anomaly detection. By decoupling detection from probabilistic modeling, it avoids pitfalls like misclassifying rare normal samples, and provides a unifying theoretical framework for understanding reconstruction-based approaches.

Abstract: Most existing anomaly detection methods rely on estimating a probability density or learning an enclosing decision boundary, implicitly assuming that normal data occupies a region of non-zero volume in the ambient space. In contrast, structural anomaly detection considers data that lies near a low-dimensional manifold, creating a mismatch between the inductive bias of existing methods and the structure of the data, often resulting in degraded performance. To address this mismatch, we introduce a geometric perspective. Specifically, we learn a projection operator onto the manifold of normal samples and define a sample as anomalous if it is altered by this projection. This formulation naturally integrates the inductive bias of manifold-supported data and reframes anomaly detection in terms of a projection residual, thereby resolving issues arising from modeling degenerate distributions. Notably, it provides a unifying interpretation of reconstruction-based methods by explaining their success and failure in terms of projection quality. In particular, it explains the strong generalization ability of projection-aligned models as a consequence of contraction behavior toward the manifold. Moreover, by decoupling anomaly detection from probabilistic modeling, it reduces the tendency to misclassify rare but normal samples, a widely recognized limitation of existing approaches. Empirically, we demonstrate that projection-aligned methods achieve strong performance, outperforming boundary-based methods while improving upon existing reconstruction-based approaches.

</details>


### [422] [Hybrid NARX-LLM for Greenland Iceberg Discharge: Prompt-Driven Residual Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15288)
*Yiquan Gao,Duohui Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A Hybrid NARX-LLM framework with Physics-Informed Prompt (PIP) improves Greenland iceberg discharge prediction by combining time-series modeling with LLM-based residual correction and physical knowledge reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Greenland iceberg discharge exhibits complex nonlinear dynamics with limited observability, making it challenging for traditional predictive models to handle extreme events, rare variations, and nonstationary trends.

Method: The framework combines a NARX model (capturing temporal dependencies) with an LLM for residual correction, guided by a Physics-Informed Prompt (PIP) method that transforms unstructured physical knowledge into structured prompts for zero-shot in-context reasoning.

Result: The approach enhances predictive accuracy, corrects systematic prediction errors, and produces interpretable residuals, particularly for extreme events that traditional methods struggle with.

Conclusion: By fusing structured time-series modeling with knowledge-driven foundation AI, the framework offers a scalable and interpretable pathway for data-limited climate forecasting, bridging physics-informed LLM reasoning with domain-specific prediction.

Abstract: Greenland iceberg discharge exhibits complex nonlinear dynamics with limited observability, challenging traditional predictive models. We present a Hybrid NARX-LLM framework that combines a nonlinear autoregressive model with exogenous inputs (NARX) and a large language model (LLM) for residual correction. We further propose a Physics-Informed Prompt (PIP) method that transforms unstructured physical knowledge into structured prompts for zero-shot in-context reasoning. The primary objective is to explore the corrective potential of this framework for modeling Greenland iceberg discharge, rather than merely optimizing predictive accuracy. The NARX component captures intrinsic temporal dependencies, while the LLM, guided by PIP, encodes glacier dynamics and environmental drivers and perceives key trend patterns to correct systematic prediction errors. This integration allows the model to reason about unmodeled factors and produce interpretable residuals, enhancing overall predictive accuracy. Applied to Greenland iceberg discharge time series, our approach addresses extreme events that are difficult to predict due to rare variations and nonstationary trends, a limitation often overlooked by traditional methods. By fusing structured time-series modeling with knowledge-driven foundation AI, the framework offers a scalable and interpretable pathway to bridge data-limited climate forecasting with physics-informed LLM reasoning. The code is available.

</details>


### [423] [Discovering Lattice Reduction Strategies via Self-Play](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15301)
*Mohamed Malhou,Kristin Lauter,Ludovic Perret*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DeltaStar uses deep reinforcement learning (AlphaZero-style) to discover better lattice reduction strategies than LLL, training on small 8D lattices and generalizing zero-shot to higher dimensions (up to 32D).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
