Method: FC-MoE employs (1) importance-aware weighting to prioritize reliable local updates, (2) gradient consensus projection to suppress conflicting updates, and (3) a local knowledge retention mechanism that re-anchors domain-specific residuals to preserve specialized client expertise.

Result: Extensive experiments show FC-MoE accelerates convergence and enhances both global and local model performance in non-IID federated environments.

Conclusion: FC-MoE effectively stabilizes the global optimization path in federated MoE fine-tuning under heterogeneous data distributions while preserving client-specific expertise.

Abstract: The continuous scaling of large language models (LLMs) incurs prohibitive computational costs, making Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) a scalable alternative for efficient fine-tuning via sparse activation. While federated learning (FL) emerges as the paradigm for privacy-preserving collaborative optimization, integrating MoE into FL under data heterogeneity may trigger conflicting expert optimizations. Client-specific data distributions force same-indexed experts to optimize under inconsistent or even conflicting feature-label correlations. This mismatch induces destructive interference during aggregation, thus destabilizing the optimization trajectory and degrading model performance. To address this issue, we propose FC-MoE, a federated conflict-aware framework for MoE fine-tuning. It employs an importance aware weighting scheme to prioritize reliable local updates and utilizes gradient consensus projection to suppress conflicting updates, ensuring a stable global optimization path. Moreover, a local knowledge retention mechanism further preserves specialized client expertise by re-anchoring domain-specific residuals. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FC-MoE accelerates convergence and enhances both global and local model performance in non-IID federated environments.

</details>


### [450] [Formalizing and Mitigating Structural Distortion in LLM Attention for Zero-Shot Graph Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15633)
*Donald Loveland,Puja Trivedi,Ari Weinstein,Edward W Huang,Danai Koutra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies that graph linearization (serializing graph structure into sequences) causes bandwidth-dependent attention decay due to rotary positional embeddings, suppressing attention between adjacent graph nodes that become far apart in the sequence. The authors propose GaLA, a lightweight inference-time attention modification that re-biases attention toward graph-adjacent nodes, improving TAG reasoning performance without significant overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based graph reasoning methods suffer from performance degradation when linearizing graph structures into sequences, but the root cause is unclear—often misattributed to prompt design or model scale rather than the fundamental distortion introduced by graph bandwidth constraints.

Method: The authors analyze how rotary positional embeddings (RoPE) cause attention decay proportional to the graph bandwidth (distance between adjacent nodes in the serialized sequence). They then propose GaLA (Graph-aligned Language Attention), a lightweight inference-time modification that biases attention weights toward graph-adjacent nodes while preserving the LLM's original sequential inductive biases.

Result: GaLA improves performance across Text-Attributed Graph (TAG) benchmarks with negligible computational overhead, demonstrating that the distortion bottleneck is correctable.

Conclusion: Graph linearization distortion is a fundamental but correctable bottleneck in LLM-based graph reasoning, shifting the focus from prompt engineering/scale toward fixing attention misalignment. GaLA offers a practical, lightweight solution.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown promise for reasoning over Text-Attributed Graphs (TAGs). However, applying LLMs to graphs requires linearizing their structure into sequences, introducing distortion rooted in the graph bandwidth problem. While this distortion has been shown to degrade performance, it is often attributed to prompt design or model scale, leaving the underlying mechanism unclear. In this work, we show \textit{how} rotary positional embeddings turn graph linearization into bandwidth-dependent attention decay, suppressing attention between graph-adjacent nodes that are forced far apart in the serialized sequence. This shifts the focus of LLM-based graph reasoning from prompt engineering and scaling toward correcting attention misalignment. Motivated by this analysis, we propose \textbf{G}raph-\textbf{a}ligned \textbf{L}anguage \textbf{A}ttention (\textbf{GaLA}), a lightweight, inference-time modification for LLMs. GaLA biases attention toward graph-adjacent nodes while preserving the LLM's sequential inductive biases. Across TAG benchmarks, GaLA improves performance with negligible overhead, demonstrating that distortion is a correctable bottleneck in LLM-based graph reasoning.

</details>


### [451] [HAPI-EP: Towards Hybrid, Adaptive, and Predictive Digital Twins of Cardiac Electrophysiology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15637)
*Sumeet Vadhavkar,Xiajun Jiang,Yubo Ye,Maryam Toloubidokhti,Linwei Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HAPI is an AI framework that builds hybrid, adaptive, and predictive digital twins for patient-specific hearts by combining mechanistic models with neural components, enabling rapid adaptation to live data via meta-learning, and achieving theoretical identifiability for strong predictive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current digital twins face challenges: (1) mechanistic vs. data-driven models have competing merits and limitations, and (2) optimization driven by reconstruction objectives leads to un-identifiable models. Rapid, dynamic adaptation to live data with predictive capability remains a central challenge.

Method: HAPI has three key enablers: (1) physics-integrated gray-box model combining a mechanistic backbone with a neural component for residual modeling; (2) rapid on-the-fly adaptation via feedforward meta-learners for amortized inference of both mechanistic and neural parameters; (3) construction of a conditional generative model ensuring theoretical identifiability.

Result: Demonstrated in cardiac electrophysiology using a hybrid monodomain model with mechanistic reaction kinetics and neural graph diffusion. Across synthetic and real-data studies, HAPI's hybridization and predictive adaptation achieve identifiable DTs with strong predictive and out-of-distribution capabilities.

Conclusion: Mechanistic-neural hybridization and predictive adaptation are critical for building identifiable digital twins with strong predictive performance, advancing personalized medicine applications.

Abstract: A digital twin (DT) of a patient-specific heart offers significant potential in personalized medicine. However, its rapid and dynamic adaptation to an individual's live data and its predictive capability after adaptation remains central challenges. We examine this challenge from its two building blocks: DT formulation where mechanistic and data-driven models show competing merits and limitations, and DT optimization strategies that are largely driven by a reconstruction objective leading to un-identifiable models. We address both bottlenecks via HAPI -- an AI framework for building hybrid, adaptive, and predictive DTs with three key enablers. First, HAPI constructs a physics-integrated gray-box model in which an interpretable mechanistic backbone is augmented by a neural component that models its residual to the observed data. Second, rather than attempting to pre-encode all possible variations in a static hybrid model, HAPI enables rapid on-the-fly adaptation of the hybrid model to few-shot live data, achieved by feedforward meta-learners realizing amortized inference of both mechanistic and neural parameters of the hybrid model trained with predictive objectives. Finally, we show that this adaptivity corresponds to the construction of a conditional generative model (i.e., the hybrid DT) that endows it with theoretical identifiability and thus strong performance in predictive scenarios. We demonstrate the proof-of-concept of HAPI in cardiac electrophysiology using a hybrid monodomain model with mechanistic reaction kinetics and neural graph diffusion. Across synthetic and real-data studies, we show that HAPI's mechanistic-neural hybridization and predictive adaptation are critical for obtaining identifiable DTs with strong predictive and out-of-distribution capabilities.

</details>


### [452] [Multi-Agent Framework for Audit Risk Assessment with Explicit Uncertainty and Evidence Conflict Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15640)
*Yuhan Wang,Manqing Wang,Yixuan Lu,Zhaoyue Peng,Shengda Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UMAR is a multi-agent framework that fuses risk scores from three specialized agents (MD&A Text, Financial Ratio, CAM) using Dempster-Shafer theory, achieving superior AUROC (0.782) and calibration (ECE=0.052) while quantifying inter-agent conflict for audit risk assessment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing audit risk assessment methods produce point predictions without quantifying agreement between different evidence streams, limiting interpretability and actionability for auditors.

Method: UMAR employs three specialized LLM-based agents (MD&A Text, Financial Ratio, CAM) that each output risk scores with calibrated uncertainty. An Uncertainty Aggregator based on Dempster-Shafer evidence theory fuses these scores and explicitly measures inter-agent conflict.

Result: Evaluated on 3,200 firm-year SEC 10-K filings (2019-2023), UMAR achieves AUROC=0.782, PR-AUC=0.341, lowest ECE=0.052, outperforming logistic regression, XGBoost, FinBERT, and single/dual-agent LLM baselines.

Conclusion: UMAR provides auditors with interpretable, well-calibrated risk signals by identifying evidence-conflict patterns correlated with actual restatement risk, offering actionable insights beyond point predictions.

Abstract: Audit risk assessment increasingly benefits from combining heterogeneous evidence sources, yet existing approaches typically produce point predictions without quantifying how well different evidence streams agree. We propose UMAR (Uncertainty-Aware Multi-Agent Risk Assessment), a framework that employs three specialized agents: an MD&A Text Agent, a Financial Ratio Agent, and a CAM Agent, each producing independent risk scores with calibrated uncertainty estimates. An Uncertainty Aggregator based on Dempster-Shafer evidence theory fuses these scores while explicitly measuring inter-agent conflict. We evaluate UMAR on a U.S. dataset of 3,200 firm-year observations from SEC 10-K filings (2019-2023), with financial restatement as the target label. Experimental results show that UMAR achieves an AUROC of 0.782 and a PR-AUC of 0.341, outperforming logistic regression, XGBoost, FinBERT, and single-agent and dual-agent LLM baselines. UMAR attains the lowest expected calibration error (ECE = 0.052) among all methods and identifies evidence-conflict patterns that correlate with actual restatement risk, offering auditors potentially actionable and interpretable risk signals.

</details>


### [453] [CIWI-CKT: Chaos-Informed Wave Interference Feature Fusion and Cross-City Knowledge Transfer for Traffic Flow Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15642)
*Abdul Joseph Fofanah,Lian Wen,David Chen,Shaoyang Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CIWI-CKT is a chaos-informed wave interference framework with cross-city knowledge transfer that outperforms SOTA methods on traffic flow prediction, especially in data-scarce scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traffic flow prediction is difficult in cross-city, data-scarce settings due to chaotic dynamics, complex spatio-temporal dependencies, and heterogeneous urban networks. Existing methods treat traffic as deterministic or fail to model wave-like interference patterns.

Method: Three innovations: (1) chaos-informed wave generation extracting measurable chaos invariants; (2) meta-interference processing capturing wave interactions between support and query regimes; (3) chaos-aware meta-learning for cross-city knowledge transfer with theoretical guarantees.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art spatio-temporal graph learning, transfer learning, prompt-based, and few-shot methods on four real-world datasets, improving accuracy while substantially reducing required training data.

Conclusion: Chaos-informed wave interference modeling is effective for cross-city few-shot traffic prediction, with theoretical guarantees on stability, dimension reduction, and generalization.

Abstract: Accurate traffic flow prediction remains challenging in cross-city, data-scarce scenarios where limited historical data hinders model generalisation. The chaotic nature of traffic dynamics, complex spatio-temporal dependencies, and heterogeneous urban networks complicate few-shot learning across cities. Existing deep learning approaches either treat traffic as purely deterministic or lack mechanisms to model wave-like interference patterns essential for cross-regime traffic dynamics. To address these limitations, this paper proposes CIWI-CKT, a novel Chaos-Informed Wave Interference Feature Fusion framework with Cross-City Knowledge Transfer. Our framework introduces three core innovations: chaos-informed wave generation that extracts measurable chaos invariants and models traffic as adaptive wave components; meta-interference processing that captures wave interactions between support and query regimes while producing a predictability score for confidence estimation; and chaos-aware meta-learning that enables efficient cross-city knowledge transfer while preserving chaotic characteristics. We establish theoretical guarantees including chaos-to-wave stability, wave-induced dimension reduction, and meta-learning generalisation bounds. Extensive experiments on four real-world traffic datasets demonstrate that CIWI-CKT significantly outperforms state-of-the-art spatio-temporal graph learning, transfer learning, prompt-based, and few-shot methods, improving prediction accuracy while substantially reducing required training data.

</details>


### [454] [MosaicQuant: Inlier-Outlier Disaggregation for Unified 4-Bit LLM Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15652)
*Yangjia Hu,Haodong Wang,Zicong Hong,Qianli Liu,Quanxin Shou,Jian Lin,Song Guo,Xiaowei Shen,Xiangjun Huang,Dian Wang,Jian Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MosaicQuant achieves near-FP16 accuracy in 4-bit LLM quantization by separating weight matrices into a dense 4-bit base (for inliers) and a sparse 4-bit residual (for outliers), then fusing both computations into a single low-bit inference kernel via ZipperEngine.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 4-bit quantization suffers accuracy loss from outliers. Mixed-precision methods break low-bit execution uniformity, introducing costly precision conversion and data movement that negate speedup.

Method: MosaicQuant disaggregates inliers/outliers: a dense 4-bit base captures inliers faithfully; a sparse 4-bit residual compensates quantization errors on outlier-critical weight blocks. ZipperEngine fuses sparse block computation into the dense 4-bit GEMM kernel via an overlapped pipeline for unified execution.

Result: MosaicQuant preserves near-FP16 accuracy on LLaMA3 and Qwen3, achieving up to 1.24× speedup over the W16A16 baseline.

Conclusion: Unified 4-bit representation and execution (representation + execution unification) can handle outliers effectively without breaking low-bit inference pipeline efficiency.

Abstract: 4-bit quantization significantly reduces the memory footprint and accelerates the inference of large language models (LLMs). However, its limited bit-width representation struggles to faithfully capture both dense common values (\emph{inliers}) and rare large-magnitude values (\emph{outliers}), causing substantial accuracy degradation. Existing mixed-precision methods mitigate this by retaining outliers in high precision, but at the cost of breaking the uniformity of low-bit execution, introducing precision conversion and extra data movement that undermine practical speedup. We propose \textbf{MosaicQuant}, a unified 4-bit LLM quantization paradigm built on a novel principle of \emph{inlier--outlier disaggregation}. Rather than elevating outlier precision, MosaicQuant quantizes the full weight matrix into a dense 4-bit base component, where inliers are captured faithfully while outlier are inevitably quantized. A sparse 4-bit residual component is then introduced to compensate for these quantization errors, selectively targeting the most error-critical weight blocks where output distortion is shown to be concentrated. However, a unified representation alone is insufficient, as naïvely executing the sparse residual as a separate kernel still breaks the unified low-bit inference pipeline. To bridge this gap, we introduce \textbf{ZipperEngine}, which fuses sparse block computation into the dense 4-bit GEMM kernel via an overlapped pipeline, unifying not only the representation but also the execution into a single coherent low-bit inference pipeline. Extensive experiments on LLaMA3 and Qwen3 demonstrate that MosaicQuant preserves near-FP16 accuracy while achieving up to $1.24\times$ speedup over the W16A16 baseline.

</details>


### [455] [Z-Plane Neural Networks: Bounded Geometric Activation Replaces ReLU and LayerNorm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15669)
*Sungwoo Goo,Hwi-yeol Yun,Sangkeun Jung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Z-Plane Neural Networks, which replace ReLU and LayerNorm with a geometric activation function (Radial Bounding) that maps neurons to 2D phasor bundles on a hypersphere, preserving directional information and ensuring 1-Lipschitz stability. A 100-layer MLP without ReLU or LayerNorm achieves 98.34% accuracy on MNIST.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current activation functions (ReLU) and normalization (LayerNorm) cause dead neurons, lose directional information, and break orthogonality in deep networks, leading to gradient instability.

Method: Z-Plane Neural Network: maps hidden states to 2D phasor bundles on a hypersphere using Radial Bounding activation ($\mathbf{x} / \max(1, \|\mathbf{x}\|_2)$), which bounds energy magnitude while preserving phase/direction, and maintains 1-Lipschitz continuity.

Result: A 100-layer Z-Plane MLP (no ReLU, no LayerNorm) converges on MNIST with 98.34% accuracy and absolute numerical stability.

Conclusion: Bounded geometric activation alone is sufficient for stable deep learning, eliminating the need for traditional scalar activations and normalization layers.

Abstract: Modern deep neural networks rely on Euclidean scalar activations (e.g., ReLU) and global normalization techniques (e.g., LayerNorm) to prevent gradient instability in deep architectures. However, these mechanisms inherently cause dead neurons, discard critical directional information, and destroy the orthogonality of feature representations. Inspired by the frequency-modulation transmission of biological axons, we propose the Z-Plane Neural Network, which maps hidden states into 2D phasor bundles on a hypersphere. We introduce a novel geometric activation function, Radial Bounding($\mathbf{x} / \max(1, \|\mathbf{x}\|_2)$), which limits the energy magnitude while preserving the phase (direction). We demonstrate mathematically that this isotropic activation maintains 1-Lipschitz continuity and prevents gradient vanishing by preserving tangential gradients. Empirically, a 100-layer Z-Plane Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)-entirely devoid of ReLU and LayerNorm-successfully converges on the MNIST dataset with 98.34% accuracy and absolute numerical stability, proving that bounded geometric activation alone is sufficient for stable deep learning.

</details>


### [456] [The Reservoir Attention Network: Cross-Pass State in Pretrained Transformers via Content-Addressable Reservoir Injection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15678)
*Emma Leonhart*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Reservoir Attention Network (RAN), which injects a fixed, randomly-initialized reservoir into pretrained transformers' mid-layer attention to carry state across forward passes. Experiments on GPT-2 and Qwen2.5 models show that untrained recurrent dynamics alone can carry usable cross-pass state, though the broader agent vision is left as future work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether untrained, fixed random recurrent dynamics can carry state across forward passes in transformer models, without the cost of training recurrent connections.

Method: Injection of a fixed, randomly-initialized reservoir into the mid-layer attention of pretrained transformers (GPT-2 124M/355M, Qwen2.5 0.5B/1.5B) to enable state-carrying across forward passes.

Result: Untrained recurrent dynamics alone can carry usable cross-pass state in transformer models of varying sizes.

Conclusion: Untrained reservoir dynamics offer a lightweight mechanism for cross-pass state propagation, with trained recurrence as a complementary and more expensive alternative for future work.

Abstract: A feasibility and dynamics study of the Reservoir Attention Network (RAN), an architecture that injects a fixed, randomly-initialized reservoir into the mid-layer attention of a pretrained transformer to carry state across forward passes. Experiments span GPT-2 (124M, 355M) to Qwen2.5 (0.5B, 1.5B) on a single consumer GPU. The tasks are minimal probes chosen to isolate individual mechanisms; the broader always-alive agent vision is treated throughout as compute-limited future work, not a claim of this paper. The reservoir is left untrained (fixed random) by design: this isolates whether untrained recurrent dynamics alone suffice to carry usable cross-pass state, leaving trained recurrence as a complementary, more expensive direction.

</details>


### [457] [ReQAT: Achieving Full-Precision Reasoning Accuracy with 4-bit Floating-Point Quantization-Aware Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15682)
*Janghwan Lee,Sihwa Lee,Jinseok Kim,Yongjik Kim,Jieun Lim,Jinwook Oh,Jungwook Choi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReQAT is an FP4 quantization-aware training framework for Large Reasoning Models that recovers reasoning accuracy by targeting low-entropy tokens (digits/operators), achieving up to 3.9x throughput speedup while surpassing BF16 fine-tuning accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying LRMs with full FP4 quantization (W4A4KV4) severely degrades reasoning performance, and existing PTQ and QAT methods fail to recover this degradation, primarily due to quantization noise on low-entropy tokens like digits and operators.

Method: ReQAT includes three components: (1) Trace-Aligned QAT (TAQ) that revisits identical reasoning traces to focus on low-entropy decisions; (2) Selective Entropy Minimization (SEM) that reinforces confidence at low-entropy positions; and (3) Q-FIT, a quantization-friendly initialization that calibrates RoPE-consistent KV cache transformations.

Result: ReQAT recovers and surpasses BF16 fine-tuning accuracy under the same training budget, achieving 3.9x throughput speedup on NVIDIA DGX Spark and 3.1x on B200.

Conclusion: Targeting low-entropy tokens with specialized QAT techniques is an effective strategy to enable high-performance FP4 quantization for reasoning-intensive LRMs.

Abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) achieve strong problem-solving through long chain-of-thought, but their deployment is constrained by the high cost of full-precision inference and growing KV cache footprints. Microscaled FP4 formats enable efficient FP4 deployment; however, fully quantizing weights, activations, and KV caches (W4A4KV4) causes severe reasoning degradation that existing PTQ and QAT fail to recover. We identify that FP4 failures concentrate on low-entropy tokens--precise symbolic commitments such as digits and operators--where quantization noise inflates sampling errors that cascade through reasoning traces. Based on this insight, we propose ReQAT, a reasoning-centric FP4 training framework with three components: (i) Trace-Aligned QAT (TAQ), which revisits identical reasoning traces to focus updates on critical low-entropy decisions; (ii) Selective Entropy Minimization (SEM), which reinforces confidence at low-entropy positions; and (iii) Q-FIT, a quantization-friendly initialization that jointly calibrates RoPE-consistent KV cache transformations to stabilize QAT. Under the same training budget, ReQAT not only recovers but surpasses BF16 fine-tuning accuracy, while delivering up to 3.9x throughput speedup on NVIDIA DGX Spark and 3.1x on B200.

</details>


### [458] [Multi-Fidelity SINDy: Sparse Discovery of Nonlinear Dynamical Systems with Fidelity-Weighted Measurements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15690)
*Filippo Zacchei,Ana Larrañaga,Attilio Frangi,Andrea Manzoni,Steven L. Brunton*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper extends SINDy to discover nonlinear dynamical systems from data with heterogeneous noise (heteroscedasticity) by combining Ensemble SINDy and Weak SINDy with weighted regression from generalized least squares.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Simulation and experimental data are rarely noise-free and often exhibit variable noise levels across measurements, sensing devices, or within a single experiment. Existing SINDy approaches do not handle such inhomogeneous (heteroscedastic) noise well.

Method: Extends SINDy by combining Ensemble SINDy and Weak SINDy within a weighted regression framework derived from generalized least squares to account for varying noise levels.

Result: Validated on benchmark systems (ODEs & PDEs) and double pendulum. Multi-fidelity integration benefits forecasting. The approach mitigates heteroscedastic noise effects; repeated low-cost, low-quality measurements can match or outperform high-fidelity-only reconstructions.

Conclusion: The proposed weighted SINDy approach effectively handles heterogeneous noise in dynamical system discovery, and multi-fidelity data integration can be beneficial for model recovery.

Abstract: Data from simulations and experiments are rarely noise-free and often exhibit heterogeneous levels of fidelity. Measurement uncertainty may vary across repeated observations, sensing devices, or even within a single experiment. This work addresses the problem of discovering nonlinear dynamical systems from such inhomogeneous data. We extend the Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamical Systems (SINDy) framework to account for variable noise levels by combining Ensemble SINDy and Weak SINDy within a weighted regression formulation derived from generalized least squares. A statistical justification for the weighting strategy is also provided. The methodology is validated on several benchmark systems, including ordinary and partial differential equations. In addition, we show the benefit of multi-fidelity integration for forecasting the dynamics of a double pendulum system. The results confirm that the proposed approach mitigates the adverse effects of heteroscedastic noise and that repeated, low-cost, low-quality measurements can improve model recovery, in some cases matching or outperforming reconstructions obtained using only high-fidelity data.

</details>


### [459] [When Generator Replay Degrades: Projected Rehearsal Orchestration for Heterogeneous Federated Class-Incremental Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15695)
*Thinh T. H. Nguyen,Khoa D. Doan,Binh T. Nguyen,Danh Le-Phuoc,Kok-Seng Wong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PRO and PRO-MAX replace synthetic input replay with projected rehearsal orchestration for federated class-incremental learning, achieving better retention and final utility under heterogeneous task streams across image, text, and graph benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated class-incremental learning (FCIL) becomes substantially harder when clients observe different label subsets, progress through tasks at different stages, and provide uneven supervision for the same semantic concepts. Existing FCIL methods that preserve old knowledge through input-space synthesis are fragile under heterogeneous task streams and difficult to transfer across modalities.

Method: PRO maintains compact class-level projected memories on the server, allowing clients to perform balanced pseudo multi-task training over current examples and old projected memories. PRO-MAX augments this with neighborhood-weighted memory alignment while keeping the server lightweight (only aggregating model updates and memory statistics). All methods are evaluated under the same warmup to remove external pretraining.

Result: PRO and PRO-MAX improve retention and final utility under heterogeneous streams while remaining competitive in homogeneous FCIL across image, text, and graph benchmarks. They outperform baselines even when given expanded replay budgets, which degrade under supervision imbalance and stage misalignment, indicating replay quantity alone does not resolve replay-quality failures.

Conclusion: Larger replay mismatch is associated with larger downstream degradation, while PRO keeps projected memories better aligned with the evolving representation. The server-light principle (server only aggregates model updates and memory statistics) is preserved.

Abstract: Federated class-incremental learning (FCIL) becomes substantially harder when clients observe different label subsets, progress through tasks at different stages, and provide uneven supervision for the same semantic concepts. Existing FCIL methods often preserve old knowledge through input-space synthesis, but they can be fragile under heterogeneous task streams and difficult to transfer across modalities. To alleviate such issues, we propose PRO, a framework that replaces synthetic input replay with projected rehearsal orchestration. To remove external pretraining, we evaluate all methods under the same warmup. After this, PRO maintains compact class-level projected memories on the server and allows clients perform balanced pseudo multi-task training over current examples and old projected memories. To handle stronger representation drift, we further introduce PRO-MAX, which augments PRO with neighborhood-weighted memory alignment while preserving the same server-light principle that the server only aggregates model updates and memory statistics. Across image, text, and graph benchmarks, PRO and PRO-MAX improve retention and final utility under heterogeneous streams while remaining competitive in homogeneous FCIL. Even when baselines are given expanded replay budgets, they degrade under supervision imbalance and stage misalignment, indicating that replay quantity alone does not resolve replay-quality failures. Additional weak-task diagnostics further show that larger replay mismatch is associated with larger downstream degradation, while our method keeps projected memories better aligned with the evolving representation.

</details>


### [460] [Robust Transformer-Based One-Step Stock Index Forecasting via Shifted Data Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15701)
*Tien Thanh Thach*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a modified Transformer with cosine annealing warmup scheduling and Shifted Data Augmentation (SDA) for stock index forecasting, achieving superior results on VN30 and S&P 500 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Direct application of Transformers to financial time series is challenging due to noisy signals, short-memory dynamics, and distributional shifts.

Method: A modified Transformer architecture for one-step stock index forecasting, combined with cosine annealing with warmup learning-rate scheduling and a novel Shifted Data Augmentation (SDA) technique.

Result: Cosine annealing with warmup outperforms generalized inverse-power scheduling; SDA reduces forecasting errors, run-to-run variability, and improves robustness to hyperparameter selection; the combined approach achieves best performance on both VN30 and S&P 500.

Conclusion: Data augmentation (SDA) can play a more important role than increasing model complexity in Transformer-based financial forecasting, providing a practical and computationally efficient approach.

Abstract: Transformers have shown remarkable success in sequence modeling, yet their direct application to financial time series remains challenging due to noisy signals, short-memory dynamics, and distributional shifts. This paper proposes a modified Transformer architecture for one-step stock index forecasting, combined with advanced learning-rate scheduling and a novel Shifted Data Augmentation (SDA) technique. We evaluate the proposed framework on two benchmark stock index datasets, VN30 and S&P 500. Experimental results demonstrate that cosine annealing with warmup consistently improves forecasting accuracy over the generalized inverse-power scheduler. Furthermore, SDA substantially reduces forecasting errors and run-to-run variability while improving robustness to hyperparameter selection. The combination of cosine annealing scheduling and SDA achieved the best performance on both datasets, indicating that data augmentation can play a more important role than increasing model complexity in Transformer-based financial forecasting. These findings provide a practical and computationally efficient approach for robust stock index forecasting in noisy financial environments.

</details>


### [461] [How to Score Experts for One-Shot MoE Expert Pruning: A Unified Formulation and Selection Principle](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15716)
*Zongfang Liu,Jinghui Zhang,Zijian Ma,Guangyi Chen,Xin Yuan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a unified formulation for one-shot expert pruning in MoE language models, deriving a principle for selecting pruning criteria based on deployment objectives, and introduces two new task-agnostic criteria (MAN and MSAN) that outperform existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing one-shot expert pruning criteria are largely heuristic and no single criterion is universally optimal, yet there is no established principle for selecting the right criterion for different deployment objectives.

Method: The authors introduce a unified formulation organized around three factors: routing frequency, gate weighting, and activation strength. From this, they derive a criteria selection principle and propose two new task-agnostic criteria: Mean Activation Norm (MAN) and Mean Squared Activation Norm (MSAN).

Result: Across 4 MoE models and 16 benchmarks, MAN and MSAN achieve the top-two average ranks in task-agnostic pruning, improving average performance by up to 8.8 points over the strongest baseline.

Conclusion: Task-agnostic pruning should favor routed-token-averaged, gate-free activation-based criteria, while task-specific pruning benefits from retaining routing-frequency and gate-weight information. The formulation also systematically unifies existing heuristic criteria.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models reduce per-token computation through sparse expert activation, yet deployment still requires storing the full expert pool, making one-shot expert pruning a practical approach for reducing memory usage. Although effective, existing criteria are largely heuristic, and no single criterion is universally optimal. Thus, establishing a principle for selecting pruning criteria suited to different deployment objectives remains an important yet largely underexplored problem in one-shot expert pruning. To this end, we introduce a unified formulation for one-shot MoE expert pruning organized around three factors: routing frequency, gate weighting, and activation strength. The formulation yields a criteria selection principle: task-agnostic pruning should favor routed-token-averaged, gate-free activation-based criteria, whereas task-specific pruning can benefit from retaining routing-frequency and gate-weight information. Beyond this principle, the formulation also provides a systematic view of existing heuristic criteria and gives rise to two new task-agnostic criteria, Mean Activation Norm (MAN) and Mean Squared Activation Norm (MSAN). Across four representative MoE models and 16 diverse benchmarks, MAN and MSAN are consistently strong in the task-agnostic setting, obtain the top-two average ranks, and improve average performance by up to 8.8 points over the strongest baseline.

</details>


### [462] [InstantForget: Update-Free Backdoor Unlearning with Inference-Time Feature Reset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15730)
*Zhenyu Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: InstantForget is an inference-time backdoor unlearning method that uses clean-calibrated gated reset with Mahalanobis scoring to detect and neutralize triggered features without model updates, achieving 0.071 average ASR and 0.981 detection AUROC, but fails under certain adaptive attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing backdoor defenses often require model updates or access to triggered samples; this work explores update-free inference-time unlearning and audits why a common projection-based assumption fails on non-BadNets triggers.

Method: InstantForget uses a clean-calibrated gated reset mechanism: it computes Mahalanobis scores to flag anomalous (triggered) features and resets only flagged features toward a neutral non-target representation, all without parameter updates or triggered samples during deployment.

Result: InstantForget reduces average ASR to 0.071 across four non-adaptive CIFAR-10 triggers, achieves 0.981 detection AUROC, transfers to six of eight backbones, but fails on WaNet, ModelNet10 point blend, two backbone geometries, and adaptive feature-compactness attacks.

Conclusion: Inference-time backdoor unlearning without parameter updates is feasible via feature-level gating and reset, but the method has clear scope limitations including failures under adaptive attacks and certain trigger types.

Abstract: Backdoor unlearning aims to remove a malicious trigger behavior from a deployed model while preserving clean utility. We study the update-free inference-time setting, where model parameters remain frozen. First, we audit a common projection assumption under oracle paired clean and triggered features. Projection succeeds mainly on BadNets and leaves WaNet, Blended, and SIG at 0.683, 0.888, and 0.941 ASR on CIFAR-10 ResNet-18. This failure is not explained by spectral compactness, spatial locality, or subspace misalignment. It is predicted by a logit-triplet gap involving the target margin, target-logit drop, and non-target logit rise. We then introduce InstantForget, a clean-calibrated gated reset that flags anomalous features with a Mahalanobis score and moves only flagged features toward a neutral non-target representation. With one fixed operating point selected on held-out triggered validation, InstantForget reduces average ASR to 0.071 across four non-adaptive CIFAR-10 triggers without triggered samples or parameter updates at deployment. It also reaches 0.981 detection AUROC and transfers to six of eight tested backbones. Reported failures under WaNet, ModelNet10 point blend, two backbone geometries, and adaptive feature-compactness attacks define the method's scope.

</details>


### [463] [Unsupervised Learning for Missing Modalities in Multimodal Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15743)
*Hassan Ismkhan,Hamid Bouchahcia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UL4M4 is a flexible unsupervised framework that imputes missing modality embeddings via clustering and greedy imputation, achieving robust multi-modal learning even when >50% of modalities are missing, with F1-Micro consistently above 0.7.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the missing-modality challenge in multi-modal learning without requiring supervised signals or task-specific imputation, enabling robust handling of arbitrary missing patterns.

Method: Proposes modality-specific normalization and a partial-modality distance metric for fair clustering of incomplete observations; uses cluster centers from unsupervised stage to guide iterative greedy imputation of missing modalities, decoupled from downstream tasks.

Result: First consistent F1-Micro scores above 0.7 under highly incomplete regimes (>50% missing modalities), stable across cluster sizes, and significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: UL4M4 offers a robust, lightweight, task-independent solution for missing modalities in multi-modal learning, easily integrable with any fusion/prediction architecture.

Abstract: This paper addresses the missing-modality challenge in multi-modal learning by introducing Unsupervised Learning for Missing Modalities in Multi-Modal Learning (UL4M4), a flexible framework that imputes missing feature embeddings in a task-independent manner before supervised prediction. We propose modality-specific
  normalization and a novel partial-modality distance metric to enable fair clustering of incomplete observations, capturing cross-modal structures while preserving scale-invariance across varying dimensionalities and modality counts. Cluster centers from this unsupervised stage guide an iterative greedy imputation process for any
  missing modalities during training or inference, supporting arbitrary numbers of modalities and arbitrary missing patterns per sample. The imputation module is lightweight, uses frozen encoders, and decouples from the downstream task, allowing easy integration with any fusion/prediction architecture. Extensive experiments under diverse and highly incomplete regimes demonstrate UL4M4's robustness, achieving, to
  the best of our knowledge, the first consistent F1-Micro scores above 0.7 on challenging missing configurations even when more than 50\% of modality slots are missing. Results are also stable across cluster sizes and significantly outperform state-of-the-art baselines. Code is available here: https://github.com/h-ismkhan/Multimodal-Learning-with-Missing-Modalities-via-Unsupervised-Learning.

</details>


### [464] [From Correlation to Causation in Lane Change Prediction for Automated Driving: A Causal Explanation Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15756)
*Mohamed Manzour,Aditya Kumar,Augusto Luis Ballardini,Miguel Ángel Sotelo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A causal-inference-based framework for lane-change prediction that achieves >95% F1-score and provides interpretable causal-chain explanations, moving beyond correlation-based approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing lane-change prediction methods learn statistical associations between driving variables and maneuvers but overlook causal dependencies among input variables (e.g., longitudinal gap, relative velocity, TTC are treated as independent flat inputs), limiting interpretability.

Method: Combines linguistic feature construction, expert-constrained causal discovery, deep structural causal modeling with DECI, intervention-based effect analysis, refutation testing, and recursive causal-chain explanation.

Result: Achieves average F1-scores above 95% during the first three seconds before lane-marking crossing. Distinguishes influential from weakly influential variables, separates direct contributors from mediated effects, and generates contrastive causal-chain explanations.

Conclusion: Provides a mechanism-aware lane-change prediction pipeline that moves beyond correlation-based classification toward more interpretable causal reasoning for maneuver prediction.

Abstract: Lane-change prediction is a central task in intelligent vehicles, where early maneuver anticipation can support safer decision-making. However, many existing approaches mainly learn statistical associations between observed driving variables and future maneuvers, while overlooking the causal dependencies among the input variables themselves. This limits interpretability, especially when physically related variables such as longitudinal gap, relative longitudinal velocity, and Time-To-Collision (TTC) are treated as independent flat inputs. This article presents a causal-inference-based framework for lane-change prediction and explanation. The proposed approach combines linguistic feature construction, expert-constrained causal discovery, deep structural causal modeling with Deep End-to-end Causal Inference (DECI), intervention-based effect analysis, refutation testing, and recursive causal-chain explanation. The objective is not only to predict the future maneuver, but also to identify candidate variables that directly contribute to the prediction, the upstream factors influencing them, and the causal chains through which these effects propagate. The framework achieves average F1-scores above 95% during the first three seconds before the lane-marking crossing event. Beyond prediction accuracy, the framework uses intervention-based effect analysis to distinguish influential from weakly influential variables under the learned causal structure. It further distinguishes candidate direct contributors from mediated effects and generates contrastive causal-chain explanations that clarify why the predicted maneuver is favored and why the alternative maneuvers are less supported. The main contribution is therefore a mechanism-aware lane-change prediction pipeline that moves beyond correlation-based classification toward more interpretable causal reasoning for maneuver prediction.

</details>


### [465] [The Data Manifold under the Microscope](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15760)
*Marios Koulakis,Constantin Seibold*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The authors introduce a benchmarking framework for studying data manifold geometry, repurposing dSprites and COIL-20 datasets with dense sampling and finite-difference estimators to recover curvature, reach, and volume accurately. They demonstrate its use by assessing theoretical bounds and tracking layer-wise geometry in a β-VAE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a gap between theory and practice in deep learning—theoretical bounds rely on manifold geometry (e.g., intrinsic dimension, curvature, reach) but there are no suitable benchmarks: analytic manifolds are too simple, real datasets too noisy for accurate geometric estimation.

Method: Repurpose and extend dSprites and COIL-20 with additional transformation dimensions and dense axis-aligned sampling; pair them with finite-difference estimators to recover curvature, reach, and volume at near-ground-truth accuracy.

Result: Finite-difference estimators achieve near-ground-truth accuracy where general-purpose estimators fail; the framework enables scaling analysis of theoretical bounds (Genovese et al., Fefferman et al.) and layer-wise geometric tracking of a β-VAE.

Conclusion: The proposed framework serves as a controlled testbed for calibrating geometric estimators and probing theoretical assumptions, highlighting the value of controlled benchmarks for guiding and validating future theory.

Abstract: A significant gap exists between theory and practice in deep learning. Generalization and approximation error bounds are often derived for simplified models or are too loose to be informative. Many rely on the manifold hypothesis and on geometric regularity such as intrinsic dimension, curvature, and reach. Progress requires insight into data-manifold geometry and suitable benchmarks, yet existing options are polarized: analytic manifolds with known geometry but limited applicability, or real-world datasets where geometry is only coarsely estimable. We introduce a benchmarking framework for studying data geometry. We repurpose and extend dSprites and COIL-20 with additional transformation dimensions and dense, axis-aligned sampling, and pair them with finite-difference estimators that recover curvature, reach, and volume at near-ground-truth accuracy in a regime where general-purpose estimators are unreliable or difficult to deploy. The framework is intended as a controlled testbed, useful as a calibration environment for geometric estimators and a sandbox for probing theoretical assumptions. To illustrate its use, we present two application studies, namely assessing the scaling behavior of the bounds of Genovese et al. and Fefferman et al., and tracking the layer-wise geometry of a $β$-VAE, highlighting the behavior of current bounds and the value of controlled benchmarks for guiding and validating future theory.
  A reference implementation is available at https://github.com/koulakis/manifold-microscope.

</details>


### [466] [Visualizing Uncertainty: Spatial Maps of Missing and Conflicting Evidence in Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15767)
*Dong Hyun Jeong,Feng Chen,Jin-Hee Cho,Lance M. Kaplan,Audun Jøsang,Soo-Yeon Ji*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Uncertainty Activation Maps (UAM), a visualization framework combining Evidential Deep Learning and FullGrad to generate spatial maps showing two types of uncertainty—vacuity (lack of evidence) and dissonance (conflicting evidence)—in image regions where deep neural networks are uncertain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing uncertainty quantification methods provide scalar measures but lack interpretability about which specific spatial regions contribute to different types of uncertainty. This limits trust and deployment in safety-critical domains.

Method: Combines Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) for principled uncertainty quantification via Subjective Logic with Full-Gradient Class Activation Mapping (FullGrad) for complete gradient decomposition. Generates vacuity and dissonance activation maps through belief-weighted attributions.

Result: Extensive evaluations across multiple benchmark datasets show the framework effectively bridges uncertainty quantification and explainability, producing intuitive visual feedback for model reliability assessment.

Conclusion: The UAM framework successfully addresses the gap between uncertainty quantification and explainability by providing spatially interpretable uncertainty maps that distinguish between lack of knowledge (vacuity) and ambiguous evidence (dissonance) in visual recognition tasks.

Abstract: Understanding when and why deep neural networks are uncertain is crucial for deploying reliable machine learning systems in safety-critical domains. While existing uncertainty quantification methods provide scalar measures of model confidence, they offer limited insight into which spatial regions of an input contribute to different types of uncertainty. We propose a novel visualization framework, Uncertainty Activation Map (UAM), that combines Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) with Full-Gradient Class Activation Mapping (FullGrad) to generate interpretable spatial uncertainty activation maps. Our approach distinguishes between two fundamental types of uncertainty: vacuity, representing lack of evidence, and dissonance, capturing conflicting evidence between competing hypotheses. By leveraging the complete gradient decomposition property of FullGrad and the principled uncertainty quantification of Subjective Logic, our method produces theoretically grounded visualizations that highlight specific image regions responsible for model uncertainty. With this framework, vacuity and dissonance activation maps are generated by computing belief-weighted attributions, enabling identification of where models lack knowledge versus where they encounter ambiguous evidence. Extensive evaluations across multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed framework effectively addresses the critical gap between uncertainty quantification and explainability, providing intuitive visual feedback to assess model reliability in complex visual recognition tasks.

</details>


### [467] [Bayesian Networks with Latent Time Embedding for Stage-Aware Causal Modeling of Alzheimer's Disease Progression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15784)
*Nguyen Linh Dan Le*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BN-LTE is a Bayesian structural framework that models Alzheimer's disease progression by estimating disease pseudotime from baseline biomarkers and using AT(N)-constrained spline-varying equations to forecast regional tau-PET change, achieving strong spatial reconstruction and identifying a mid-pseudotime window of amyloid sensitivity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing longitudinal AD models represent the AT(N) cascade either as a fixed biomarker sequence or as a black-box forecasting task, making it difficult to determine when biologically guided biomarker relationships influence future regional pathology.

Method: Bayesian Networks with Latent Time Embedding (BN-LTE): estimates disease pseudotime from baseline biomarker profiles, constrains directed dependencies by AT(N) ordering, and uses posterior spline-varying structural equations to link baseline multimodal measurements with future annualized regional tau-PET change.

Result: BN-LTE shows strong spatial reconstruction of tau progression compared to forecasting baselines. It recovers posterior stage-varying AT(N)-constrained effects and identifies a mid-pseudotime window of amyloid sensitivity, supported by g-formula contrasts, AIPW, ablations, and robustness analyses.

Conclusion: BN-LTE is a Bayesian structural framework capable of forecasting tau progression while examining stage-dependent AT(N)-cascade mechanisms in observational longitudinal neuroimaging data.

Abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) progression is often described through the amyloid-tau-neurodegeneration, or AT(N), cascade. However, most longitudinal models represent this cascade either as a fixed sequence of biomarkers or as a black-box forecasting task. This makes it difficult to determine when biologically guided biomarker relationships influence future regional pathology. In this study, we introduce Bayesian Networks with Latent Time Embedding (BN-LTE), a Bayesian structural framework for stage-aware modeling of AD progression. BN-LTE estimates disease pseudotime from baseline biomarker profiles and constrains directed dependencies according to biologically plausible AT(N) ordering. Posterior spline-varying structural equations are then used to link initial multimodal measurements with future annualized regional tau-PET change. Across repeated subject-disjoint evaluations using ADNI data, BN-LTE shows strong spatial reconstruction of tau progression compared with the included forecasting baselines. Beyond spatial reconstruction, BN-LTE recovers posterior stage-varying AT(N)-constrained effects and identifies a mid-pseudotime window of amyloid sensitivity. This window is supported by model-implied g-formula contrasts, root-adjusted AIPW, mechanism-sensitive ablations, and robustness analyses across spline and prior specifications. Overall, these findings position BN-LTE as a Bayesian structural framework for forecasting tau progression while examining stage-dependent AT(N)-cascade mechanisms in observational longitudinal neuroimaging data. Our code is available at https://github.com/danleneurocom/BN-LTE.

</details>


### [468] [Proximal Policy Optimization for Amortized Discrete Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15793)
*Anna Zykova-Myzina,Timofei Gritsaev,Daniil Tiapkin,Nikita Morozov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces policy gradient algorithms, including Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), to the GFlowNet framework for sampling from discrete probability distributions, demonstrating improved convergence and data efficiency over standard methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge GFlowNets with policy gradient methods from reinforcement learning, enabling more efficient and stable training for sampling from structured discrete distributions.

Method: Derives equivalents of standard policy gradient algorithms (e.g., REINFORCE, PPO) tailored for GFlowNets, with experimental exploration of baselines, advantage estimation, and a first-time application of PPO to GFlowNets.

Result: PPO-based GFlowNet training shows improved convergence speed and data efficiency compared to standard GFlowNet objectives across benchmarks including synthetic energies and molecular graph generation.

Conclusion: Policy gradient methods, especially PPO, are effective for GFlowNet training and offer practical advantages over existing objectives.

Abstract: This paper explores policy gradient algorithms for training stochastic policies to sample from structured discrete probability distributions under the Generative Flow Network (GFlowNet) framework. Building on extensive theoretical connections between GFlowNets and entropy-regularized reinforcement learning, we derive equivalents of standard policy gradient algorithms for training GFlowNets, as well as experimentally explore their various methodological aspects, including baseline training and advantage estimation. Most importantly, our work is the first to derive and successfully apply proximal policy optimization to GFlowNets, showing its improved convergence speed and data efficiency compared to standard GFlowNet training objectives on benchmarks ranging from synthetic energies to molecular graph generation.

</details>


### [469] [Mean-Field Parallel Decoding for Discrete Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15805)
*Tamim Zoabi,Ameen Ali,Liran Ringel,Lior Wolf*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a training-free decoding framework for discrete diffusion language models that uses pairwise interactions to coordinate parallel token generation, improving the quality-latency trade-off by suppressing conflicting simultaneous commitments within a single forward pass.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing discrete diffusion models select tokens independently by marginal confidence, which leads to incompatible token configurations when multiple positions are updated simultaneously, limiting effective parallelism in decoding.

Method: The method assigns a commit score to each masked position and refines these scores using pairwise interactions derived from the model's predictive distributions. A variational relaxation produces a simple fixed-point update that suppresses conflicting simultaneous commitments within a single forward pass.

Result: Experiments on reasoning and code-generation benchmarks show consistent improvements in the quality-latency trade-off.

Conclusion: The proposed framework is lightweight, requires no auxiliary model or retraining, and can be integrated into existing diffusion decoding pipelines without modification to improve parallel token generation coordination.

Abstract: Discrete diffusion language models enable parallel token generation, offering a pathway to low-latency decoding. However, selecting tokens independently by marginal confidence limits effective parallelism: tokens that appear reliable in isolation can form incompatible configurations when several positions are updated at once. We introduce a training-free decoding framework that coordinates these parallel updates. At each forward pass, the method assigns a commit score to each masked position and refines these scores using pairwise interactions derived from the model's predictive distributions. A variational relaxation yields a simple fixed-point update that suppresses conflicting simultaneous commitments within a single forward pass. This mechanism allows the decoder to commit more tokens in parallel while maintaining competitive generation quality. The method is lightweight, requires no auxiliary model or retraining, and drops into existing diffusion decoding pipelines without modification. Experiments on reasoning and code-generation benchmarks show consistent improvements in the quality-latency trade-off.

</details>


### [470] [Continuous Cross-Domain Traffic State Prediction via Memory-Augmented Graph Liquid Time-Constant Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15807)
*Jinrong Xiang,Ming Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MA-GLTC is a continuous cross-domain traffic prediction framework using memory-augmented graph liquid time-constant networks that decomposes traffic networks into spatio-temporal units for fine-grained knowledge transfer, models continuous traffic dynamics with graph-coupled liquid time-constant networks, and employs memory-based transfer storage for handling unseen patterns, outperforming baselines on five datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-domain traffic prediction faces limitations: coarse-grained source-target adaptation, inability to handle unseen target-domain patterns, and insufficient modeling of continuous traffic dynamics under irregular/heterogeneous temporal conditions.

Method: 1) Spatio-temporal units (STUs) decompose traffic networks into transferable local units for fine-grained alignment. 2) Graph Liquid Time-Constant Network (GLTC) models continuous graph-coupled traffic evolution with leakage, adaptive time constants, and neighborhood-aware feedback. 3) Memory-based Transfer Storage (MTS) preserves source knowledge, retrieves matched patterns, and updates target patterns for unseen states.

Result: Outperforms inner-domain and cross-domain baselines on five datasets. Reduces average prediction errors by 3.02%, 0.33%, 8.92%, 10.09%, and 2.11% compared to the second-best method.

Conclusion: MA-GLTC effectively addresses key limitations in cross-domain traffic prediction through fine-grained unit decomposition, continuous-time ODE-based graph dynamics with liquid time constants, and memory-augmented knowledge transfer, consistently achieving superior performance across diverse traffic datasets.

Abstract: Traffic state prediction is a fundamental task in intelligent transportation systems. In practical applications, some regions suffer from limited traffic observations due to insufficient sensing infrastructure, making cross-domain knowledge transfer an important solution for data-scarce traffic prediction. However, existing cross-domain traffic prediction methods still face several limitations, including coarse-grained source-target adaptation, limited capability in handling unseen target-domain patterns, and insufficient modeling of continuous traffic dynamics under irregular or heterogeneous temporal conditions. To address these issues, this paper proposes a continuous cross-domain traffic prediction framework, termed Memory-Augmented Graph Liquid Time-Constant Network (MA-GLTC). Specifically, we first construct spatio-temporal units (STUs) to decompose traffic networks into transferable local units, enabling fine-grained knowledge alignment across domains. Then, a graph liquid time-constant network (GLTC) is developed to model graph-coupled traffic evolution in continuous time. Different from generic graph neural ODE-based models, GLTC introduces graph-coupled recurrent conductance into liquid time-constant dynamics, allowing node states to evolve with leakage, adaptive time constants, and neighborhood-aware feedback. Furthermore, a Memory-based Transfer Storage (MTS) mechanism is designed to preserve source-domain knowledge, retrieve matched traffic patterns, and update reliable target-domain patterns when unseen states emerge. Experiments on five public traffic datasets demonstrate that MA-GLTC consistently outperforms representative innerdomain and cross-domain baselines in both short-term and longterm prediction tasks. Compared with the second-best method, MA-GLTC reduces the average prediction errors by 3.02%, 0.33%, 8.92%, 10.09%, and 2.11%, respectively.

</details>


### [471] [Brownian Kernel Ladders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15812)
*Mahdi Mohammadigohari,Giuseppe Di Fatta,Giuseppe Nicosia,Panos M Pardalos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Brownian Kernel Ladders (BKLs), a recursively defined hierarchy of function spaces built from Brownian-kernel integrals. The authors prove that BKL spaces form quasi-Banach spaces with depth-dependent regularity, monotonicity, and uniform Gaussian complexity bounds, enabling near-parametric excess-risk guarantees for regularized empirical risk minimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To construct mathematically tractable function spaces that capture hierarchical compositional representations, which remains a central challenge in statistical learning theory, particularly for understanding deep learning.

Method: Brownian Kernel Ladders (BKLs) are recursively defined by integrating Brownian kernels over probability measures supported on subsets of the previous layer. The analysis uses combinatorial proof techniques based on recursive subset decompositions and Brownian-kernel threshold representations.

Result: BKL spaces are quasi-Banach spaces with depth-dependent Hölder regularity, strict monotonicity in depth, and Gaussian complexity bounds uniformly controlled in both dimension and depth. Regularized ERM achieves near-parametric excess-risk guarantees.

Conclusion: BKLs provide a mathematically tractable hierarchical function-space framework for studying compositional representations in deep learning, with strong theoretical guarantees.

Abstract: Constructing mathematically tractable function spaces that capture hierarchical compositional representations remains a central challenge in statistical learning theory. We introduce Brownian kernel ladders (BKLs), a recursively defined hierarchy of integral reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces generated through Brownian-kernel integral constructions. Starting from linear functionals, each layer is obtained by integrating Brownian kernels over probability measures supported on subsets of the previous layer, yielding a recursive function-space model in which depth is encoded directly through the hierarchy.
  Based on this framework, we define canonical BKL spaces together with an associated complexity functional. We establish several analytical and statistical properties of these spaces. In particular, we show that BKL spaces form quasi-Banach spaces, satisfy depth-dependent Hölder regularity estimates, and exhibit strict monotonicity with respect to depth. We further prove existence results for regularized empirical risk minimization and derive Gaussian complexity bounds that remain uniformly controlled with respect to both the ambient dimension and the hierarchy depth.
  A key ingredient of the analysis is a combinatorial proof technique based on recursive subset decompositions and Brownian-kernel threshold representations. These estimates yield excess-risk guarantees of near-parametric order for regularized empirical risk minimization over BKL spaces. Our results provide a mathematically tractable hierarchical function-space framework for studying compositional representations in deep learning.

</details>


### [472] [SILAGE: Memory-Efficient, Full-Gradient-Free Nonconvex Optimization for Nested Finite Sums](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15832)
*Igor Sokolov,Laurent Condat,Peter Richtárik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SILAGE is a variance-reduced algorithm for nonconvex optimization on massive datasets with a nested double finite-sum structure. It eliminates costly global full-gradient refreshes, requires only O(n) memory (vs. O(nm) for SILVER), and adapts convergence rates to data heterogeneity via across-group (δ₁) and within-group (δ₂) similarities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing variance-reduced methods (PAGE, SILVER) suffer from scalability bottlenecks: either expensive periodic full-gradient refreshes over all N=nm samples (PAGE) or impractical O(nm) memory (SILVER). The paper aims to bridge this trade-off by exploiting the double-sum structure inherent in massive datasets.

Method: SILAGE actively leverages the nested double finite-sum structure (n blocks of size m). It eliminates global full-gradient refreshes over all nm components, evaluating at most one local group gradient per iteration, and requires only O(n) memory. Convergence analysis uses nested functional similarities (δ₁, δ₂) instead of worst-case Lipschitz constants.

Result: SILAGE achieves variance reduction without periodic global refreshes and with O(n) memory. Its complexity adapts to data geometry via across-group (δ₁) and within-group (δ₂) heterogeneity. The paper claims improved state-of-the-art bounds in several practically relevant regimes.

Conclusion: SILAGE effectively resolves the trade-off between computational cost and memory in variance-reduced optimization for nonconvex objectives with double-sum structure, offering scalable and memory-efficient training.

Abstract: Empirical risk minimization on massive datasets naturally exhibits a nested double finite-sum structure, where $N=nm$ total samples are logically or physically partitioned into $n$ blocks of size $m$ (e.g., in pooled data silos, out-of-core learning, or deliberate stratification). While variance-reduced methods achieve optimal oracle complexities for nonconvex objectives, they suffer from severe scaling bottlenecks in this centralized regime. Recursive estimators, such as PAGE, require periodic global full-gradient refreshes over all $nm$ samples, which are computationally expensive. Conversely, single-loop methods, such as SILVER, avoid such refreshes but require an impractical $\mathcal{O}(nm)$ memory footprint to store a control variate for every sample. In this paper, we propose SILAGE, a variance-reduced algorithm that addresses this trade-off. By actively exploiting the double-sum structure, SILAGE eliminates periodic global full-gradient refreshes over all $nm$ components (evaluating at most one local group gradient per iteration) while requiring only $\mathcal{O}(n)$ memory. Furthermore, we provide a tight convergence analysis that avoids pessimistic worst-case Lipschitz constants. Instead, SILAGE's complexity natively adapts to the underlying data geometry via nested functional similarities: across-group ($δ_1$) and within-group ($δ_2$) heterogeneity. Our results improve existing state-of-the-art bounds in several practically relevant regimes.

</details>


### [473] [Wasserstein Convergence of ODE-Based Samplers in Decentralized Diffusion Model via Velocity Field Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15835)
*Chencheng Tang,Xuanyu Xue,Fangyikang Wang,Chao Zhang,Hubery Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proves the first Wasserstein-2 convergence guarantee for decentralized diffusion models using ODE-based sampling, showing O(N^{-1/2}+ε) convergence rate despite stochastic expert switching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion convergence theory assumes a single global velocity field, but decentralized architectures with multiple local experts and stochastic routing fall outside these analyses. The paper aims to provide theoretical guarantees for this more practical setting motivated by privacy and scalability.

Method: The authors analyze a decentralized diffusion framework with stochastic velocity fields and ODE-based sampling, establishing convergence bounds in Wasserstein-2 distance using N-step discretization with neural approximation errors ε.

Result: The distribution converges at rate O(N^{-1/2}+ε) in Wasserstein-2 distance, where N is the number of discretization steps and ε captures neural approximation errors.

Conclusion: This is the first Wasserstein-2 convergence result for decentralized diffusion models with ODE-based sampling, bridging a gap between practical decentralized architectures and theoretical convergence guarantees.

Abstract: Diffusion models have achieved impressive empirical success in generative tasks, and their convergence theory is now relatively well understood. Motivated by privacy and scalability, recent decentralized diffusion architectures replace a single global velocity field with multiple local experts and a routing mechanism, yielding a sampling dynamics with stochastic expert switching that falls outside standard diffusion convergence analyses. In this work, We study a decentralized diffusion framework with stochastic velocity fields and ODE-based sampling. We establish a convergence guarantee in Wasserstein-2 distance, showing that the distribution of the $N$-step discretization converges to the analytical solution at rate $\mathcal{O}(N^{-1/2}+\varepsilon)$ in $W_2$, where $\varepsilon$ captures the neural approximation errors. To our knowledge, this is the first $W_2$ convergence result for decentralized diffusion models with an ODE-based sampling scheme.

</details>


### [474] [David vs. Goliath in Next Activity Prediction: Argmax vs. LSTM, Transformer, and LLM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15868)
*Hans Weytjens,Ingo Weber*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This benchmark study reveals that simple argmax baselines match or approach the performance of billion-parameter LLMs for next activity prediction, with pretraining and model size offering little consistent advantage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in systematic comparison of LLMs, Transformers, LSTMs, and simple baselines for NAP under a unified sequence modeling setting.

Method: Systematic benchmark comparing vocabulary-adapted LLMs, Transformers trained from scratch, LLM-distilled Transformers, LSTMs, and a counting-based argmax baseline across seven real-life event logs.

Result: Pretraining offers no consistent improvement; model size has little effect; argmax baseline matches or approaches billion-parameter LLM performance on most datasets.

Conclusion: Complexity does not guarantee superior performance for NAP; simple baselines remain highly competitive, challenging the field's trend toward larger models.

Abstract: Next activity prediction (NAP) is a cornerstone of predictive process monitoring (PPM), enabling organizations to move from retrospective analysis to proactive process steering. The PPM field has progressed from classical machine learning through deep learning architectures such as LSTMs and Transformers to large language models (LLMs). Despite growing model complexity, no benchmark jointly compares LLMs, Transformers, LSTMs, and simple baselines in a direct sequence modeling setting for NAP. In this paper, we fill this gap with a systematic benchmark. We compare vocabulary-adapted LLMs, Transformers trained from scratch, LLM-distilled Transformers, and LSTMs against a simple counting-based argmax baseline across seven real-life event logs. Our results tell a David vs. Goliath story: pretraining confers no consistent improvement over training from scratch, model size shows little effect on performance, and on most datasets the argmax baseline matches or approaches the performance of billion-parameter LLMs.

</details>


### [475] [Intelligence Is Not the Bottleneck: Validating an LLM First-Pass Manuscript Score Against Peer-Review Outcomes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15887)
*Costa Georgantas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AIPR, a prompt-only LLM system that evaluates paper quality across five dimensions and produces a weighted overall score, is validated against 300 ICLR submissions and achieves AUROC 0.82 in distinguishing accepted from rejected papers, with strongest signal at the lowest scores and high reliability across repeated runs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based peer review evaluations focus on prose quality of generated reviews rather than the validity of numeric scores, and there is a need to validate whether such systems can produce meaningful quality assessments that align with real publication outcomes.

Method: AIPR uses prompting alone (no fine-tuning) on a language model to read submissions and output five 0-100 quality dimensions plus a weighted overall score. It was evaluated on 300 ICLR submissions with public decision tiers and reviewer ratings, under a frozen pipeline with pre-registered hypotheses, comparing overall scores against actual acceptance outcomes.

Result: The overall score separates accepted from rejected submissions with AUROC 0.82 (95% CI 0.78-0.87), rises monotonically across decision tiers, and tracks mean reviewer ratings. The lowest-scoring fifth is rejected far above base rate with no oral papers. A one-paragraph prompt on the same model performs nearly as well (gap not statistically significant, p=0.09). AIPR shows much lower variance across repeated runs (0.7 vs 2.8 points within-paper SD).

Conclusion: The LLM model itself provides most of the discriminative signal, while the AIPR pipeline adds reliability (stable scores) and grounded, rubric-structured reviews. The system is intended to assist rather than replace human decision-making.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) systems are increasingly proposed to assist peer review, yet most evaluations judge the prose of machine-generated review text, not the validity of the numeric score a system assigns. We validate AIPR, which reads a submitted manuscript and emits five 0-100 quality dimensions and a weighted overall score, against the public decision outcomes of a major machine learning venue. AIPR grades by prompting alone, with no fine-tuning on reviews or decisions. Across 300 ICLR submissions with public decision tiers and reviewer ratings, graded under a frozen pipeline with hypotheses pre-registered before any score met any outcome, the overall score separates rejected from accepted submissions (AUROC 0.82, 95% CI 0.78-0.87), rises monotonically across tiers, and tracks the mean reviewer rating. The signal is strongest where we claim it: the lowest-scoring fifth is rejected far above the base rate, with oral papers absent. The validity comes mostly from the model: a one-paragraph prompt on the same model discriminates almost as well as the full pipeline (the small gap favours the pipeline but does not meet the pre-declared criterion, p = 0.09). What the engineering adds is reliability and a grounded review: AIPR's score barely moves across repeated runs (0.7 vs. 2.8 points within-paper SD) where the bare prompt swings, and the same pass returns a rubric-structured, evidence-grounded review rather than a bare number, with the human keeping the decision.

</details>


### [476] [Scalar-pathway fidelity improves physical accuracy in short-range equivariant interatomic potentials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15892)
*Jia Bi,Alin Marin Elena,Samuel Pinilla*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that improving scalar information processing in equivariant neural network potentials (via PAN pooling and PGS mixers) can reduce force errors by 22-27%, energy errors by 19-22%, and stress errors by 27-28% across diverse materials systems, with only ~5% extra computation cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Equivariant neural network potentials have advanced local geometry representation, but their final energy surfaces depend on invariant scalar channels, whose aggregation and spectral resolution remain underexamined and underoptimized.

Method: Two lightweight, symmetry-preserving modifications (PAN and PGS) that act only on ℓ=0 scalar channels: PAN adds coordination-sensitive amplitude modulation; PGS augments scalar features with radial and tapered spectral bases, tested on MACE, Allegro, and NequIP backbones.
