TL;DR: CVP efficiently propagates uncertainty through modern deep architectures (transformers, CNNs) in a single forward pass, achieving MC sampling-quality calibration at much lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bayesian deep learning is computationally expensive at test time due to needing many MC samples; prior variance propagation methods work for MLPs but fail for modern architectures with normalization layers and diverse activations.

Method: CVP introduces a new variance propagation method for normalization layers, combines with existing techniques for activation functions, and adds a light calibration step to absorb residual errors.

Result: CVP improves coverage at 0.5% risk from 8.2% to 14.6% with BEiT-3 on NLVR2 and from 2.6% to 10.8% with ViLT on VQAv2, with gains also in CNNs.

Conclusion: CVP provides accurate uncertainty estimates comparable to MC sampling at a fraction of the cost, bridging the gap for variance propagation in modern deep networks.

Abstract: Modern deep learning models remain notoriously prone to overconfidence, limiting their reliability in high-stakes applications. Bayesian methods aim to counter this by learning a distribution over model parameters, and recent advances now make this feasible for large-scale architectures at costs comparable to AdamW. However, a challenge remains at test time: predictions must be averaged across many forward passes with weights sampled from the posterior, which is prohibitively expensive. Variance propagation offers an efficient alternative, computing layer-wise analytical approximations of uncertainty in a single forward pass. While such techniques are effective for MLPs, their extension to modern architectures remains challenging, due to increased depth and diversity of layer types. To fill this gap, we propose Calibrated Variance Propagation (CVP), which introduces a new propagation method for normalization layers, combines it with recent techniques for handling activation functions, and absorbs residual error through a light calibration step. CVP yields comparably accurate uncertainty estimates to MC sampling across transformers and CNNs, at a fraction of the cost. Against prior variance propagation work, CVP improves coverage at $0.5\%$ risk from $8.2\%$ to $14.6\%$ with BEiT-3 on Visual Reasoning (NLVR2) and from $2.6\%$ to $10.8\%$ with ViLT on VQAv2, with gains extending to convolutional architectures.

</details>


### [504] [Prediction of Runtime Parameters of Parallel Chemistry Applications via Active and Generative Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16226)
*Tanzila Tabassum,Omer Subasi,Ajay Panyala,Epiya Ebiapia,Gerald Baumgartner,Erdal Mutlu,P Sadayappan,Karol Kowalski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two ML approaches (active + generative learning with gradient boosted regression trees) predict runtime parameters for parallel chemistry computations, achieving 0.023 MAPE and 99.9% R² on CCSD calculations, and 0.2 MAPE using only 20-25% of data via active learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To predict runtime parameters of highly scalable parallel chemistry computations, addressing the challenge of limited training data.

Method: Active learning and generative learning combined with gradient boosted regression tree models, selected from a suite of ML models.

Result: MAPE as low as 0.023 and R² up to 99.9% on CCSD computations; with active learning, MAPE ~0.2 using only 20-25% of the original dataset.

Conclusion: The proposed ML approaches effectively predict runtime parameters with high accuracy while mitigating data scarcity via active learning.

Abstract: In this work, we develop two main Machine Learning based approaches to predict the runtime parameters of highly scalable parallel chemistry computations.These approaches employ active and generative learning together with the empirically determined gradient boosted regression tree models chosen among a rich suite of machine learning models. When evaluated on Coupled-Cluster with Singles and Doubles computations, our models achieve a mean absolute error percentage (MAPE) as low as 0.023 and a coefficient of determination as high as 99.9%. Furthermore, when combined with active learning to mitigate the lack of large amounts of training data, our models score a MAPE about 0.2 with 20-25% of the original dataset.

</details>


### [505] [From Tokens to Regions: CUDA-Sensitive Instruction Tuning for GPU Kernel Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16231)
*Wentao Chen,Jiace Zhu,Xing Zhe Chai,Zeng Qu,Qiaoling Xiao,Liucheng Duan,An Zou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CuSeT proposes a low-cost SFT-based method for CUDA kernel generation that improves functional correctness by modeling CUDA sensitivity through adaptive token-level masking and region-aware sample reweighting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle to generate correct CUDA kernels due to strict execution constraints; existing methods (agentic/RL) are costly, and standard SFT fails to explicitly model CUDA-sensitive tokens/regions tightly coupled with these constraints.

Method: CuSeT (CUDA-Sensitive Instruction Tuning) combines adaptive token-level masking (to leverage high-confidence CUDA-sensitive tokens) with region-aware sample reweighting (to preserve low-confidence CUDA-sensitive regions), following a "from tokens to regions" principle within a simple SFT framework.

Result: CuSeT consistently improves functional correctness across multiple model families and scales, outperforms standard SFT and advanced SFT variants, and achieves competitive performance against frontier CUDA kernel generation models with substantially lower inference cost.

Conclusion: Modeling CUDA sensitivity from token confidence patterns enables effective and low-cost CUDA kernel generation, bridging the gap between lightweight SFT and expensive RL/agentic approaches.

Abstract: High-performance CUDA kernels are essential for scalable AI systems, while Large Language Models (LLMs) still struggle to generate correct kernels due to strict and implicit execution constraints. Existing LLM-based approaches either rely on costly agentic or reinforcement-learning (RL) pipelines, or adopt supervised fine-tuning (SFT) objectives that fail to explicitly model CUDA sensitivity, namely code tokens or regions tightly coupled with execution constraints. In this work, we investigate CUDA sensitivity from the perspective of token confidence patterns, showing that CUDA sensitivity appears at both token and region levels, where most CUDA-sensitive tokens are predicted with high confidence, while a smaller low-confidence subset forms regions corresponding to execution-critical structures. These findings suggest that effective CUDA kernel generation should both leverage high-confidence CUDA-sensitive tokens and preserve low-confidence CUDA-sensitive regions. Building on these insights, we propose \textbf{\underline{CU}DA-\underline{Se}nsitive Instruction \underline{T}uning (CuSeT)}, a low-cost post-training method within a simple SFT framework. CuSeT follows the principle of ``from tokens to regions'' by combining \emph{adaptive token-level masking} with \emph{region-aware sample reweighting}. Experiments show that CuSeT consistently improves functional correctness across multiple model families and scales, outperforming standard SFT and advanced SFT variants, while achieving competitive performance against frontier CUDA kernel generation models with substantially lower inference cost.

</details>


### [506] [Evolutionary Bilevel Reward Shaping for Generalization in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16236)
*Ekasit Usaratniwart,Xilin Gao,Marc Ong,Youhei Akimoto*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GERS is a bilevel optimization method using CMA-ES to shape rewards, improving RL generalization to unseen environments with only scalar feedback (no trajectory access) from validation environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL agents degrade when deployed in environments unseen during training; existing methods like domain randomization need diverse training environments and full trajectory observability, which is infeasible in privacy-preserving or restricted scenarios.

Method: Bilevel optimization: lower level trains an RL agent with shaped rewards on limited training environments (trajectory accessible); upper level uses CMA-ES to optimize reward shaping parameters to maximize scalar cumulative reward on validation environments (no trajectory access).

Result: GERS outperforms standard RL baselines on unseen test environments; achieves performance comparable to domain randomization despite using less data (no validation trajectory access).

Conclusion: GERS effectively enhances RL generalization under restricted data access constraints where only scalar feedback is available.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) often suffers from performance degradation when deployed in environments that differ from those encountered during training. Existing techniques such as domain randomization (DR) mitigate this, but require access to diverse training environments and full trajectory observability, assumptions that fail in privacy-preserving or restricted scenarios where only scalar performance metrics are available. We propose Generalization via Evolutionary Reward Shaping (GERS), a bilevel optimization approach to improve generalization on unseen test environments using only scalar feedback from validation environments. At the lower level, an RL agent guided via a reward function shaped by the upper level learns a policy on a limited set of training environments with accessible trajectory data; at the upper level, CMA-ES optimizes the reward shaping parameters to maximize the cumulative unshaped reward on separate validation environments for which trajectory access is unavailable. Results on continuous control tasks indicate that GERS outperforms the standard RL baseline on unseen test environments. GERS performance is comparable to DR, despite DR treating the combined set of training and validation environments of GERS as a single training set that requires trajectory access, whereas GERS cannot access validation trajectories. These results confirm that GERS effectively enhances generalization under restricted data access constraints.

</details>


### [507] [Rapid Poison: Practical Poisoning Attacks Against the Rapid Response Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16242)
*David Huang,Jaewon Chang,Avidan Shah,Prateek Mittal,Chawin Sitawarin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper reveals that prompt injection can poison the Rapid Response (RR) jailbreak-detection training pipeline, enabling targeted false-positive attacks and concept-based backdoor attacks (via the novel Omission Attack) that cause near-complete label flipping at just 1% poisoning rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To expose a security vulnerability in production RR frameworks (used in systems like Anthropic's ASL-3 safeguards), where prompt injection can infiltrate the classifier training pipeline, and to demonstrate two attack objectives previously unexplored under the realistic constraint that attackers can only modify jailbreak samples (not benign data or labels).

Method: The authors propose the Omission Attack, exploiting a phenomenon where training on concept-absent unsafe samples causes the classifier to misassociate that concept's presence with the safe label. Both attack objectives (targeted poisoning for false positives and concept-based backdoor for false negatives) are tested at a 1% poisoning rate.

Result: Both attacks achieved substantial label flipping: up to 100% false positive rates and up to 96% false negative rates at only a 1% poisoning rate.

Conclusion: The RR framework's training pipeline is vulnerable to poisoning via prompt injection, enabling dangerous backdoor attacks even under realistic constraints, and highlights a critical security gap that needs mitigation.

Abstract: The Rapid Response (RR) framework, deployed in production systems, including Anthropic's ASL-3 safeguards, continuously improves jailbreak-detection classifiers. When new jailbreaks emerge that bypass these classifiers, Rapid Response generates synthetic variants for training, helping the model generalize from the new attacks and quickly adapt. We reveal that prompt injection can infiltrate this pipeline to deliver poisoned samples into the classifier's training set, enabling two attack objectives: (I) targeted poisoning attacks that create false positives on harmless samples by categorizing them as a jailbreak, with a specific desired feature (e.g., certain formatting, subject, or keyword), (II) concept-based backdoor attacks that induce false negatives on jailbreak inputs, generalizing even to jailbreaks from attack strategies the defender explicitly trained against, when the backdoor trigger is present. Importantly, our threat model restricts adversaries to modifying only jailbreak samples (not benign data or labels), a constraint unexplored by prior work that makes the second objective particularly challenging. We address this with Omission Attack, which exploits a new phenomenon: when training on concept-absent unsafe samples, the classifier misassociates that concept's presence with the safe label. Both attacks cause substantial and in some cases near-complete label flipping at only a 1% poisoning rate, achieving up to 100% false positive rates and up to 96% false negative rates.

</details>


### [508] [LiFT: Local Search via Linear Programming for Overfitting-Controlled Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16243)
*Abhishek Shukla,Anikeit Khanna,Ankur Sinha,Faiz Hamid*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LiFT is an LP-based local search framework for fine-tuning transformers that jointly optimizes parameters and regularization hyperparameters using validation gradients and Hessian information, reducing overfitting without full retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address overfitting in transformer fine-tuning by moving beyond heuristic or grid-based hyperparameter selection, and to provide a principled optimization framework that explicitly controls overfitting during adaptation.

Method: Formulates fine-tuning as bilevel optimization-based regularization, uses validation gradients and training Hessian from warm-up iterations to construct a local descent direction via Linear Programming, enabling joint updates of parameters and regularization hyperparameters.

Result: On GPT-2 Small fine-tuned on WikiText-2, LiFT yields consistent improvements in test perplexity across multiple layer configurations and regularization settings, with pronounced gains in overfitting-prone scenarios.

Conclusion: LiFT establishes a principled connection between transformer fine-tuning, bilevel optimization, local search, and regularization theory, offering effective adaptation with selective tuning and reduced overfitting.

Abstract: This paper proposes a Linear Programming (LP)-based local search framework for fine-tuning pretrained transformer models with explicit control against overfitting. The approach formulates transformer fine-tuning as a bilevel optimization-based regularization problem, in which model parameters and regularization hyperparameters are jointly updated. Information collected during initial warm-up iterations, including validation gradients and training Hessian information, is used to construct a local descent direction by solving an LP that minimizes a scaled directional derivative while preserving training optimality. This validation-aware descent direction enables focused local updates of both parameters and regularization hyperparameters, reducing overfitting without requiring repeated full retraining cycles. The resulting method, termed Linear Programming-based Fine-Tuning (LiFT) for transformers, differs from conventional fine-tuning by systematically identifying task-specific updates rather than relying on heuristic or grid-based hyperparameter selection. Experiments on GPT-2 Small fine-tuned on WikiText-2 demonstrate that LiFT enables effective adaptation through selective tuning of transformer blocks and regularization parameters, yielding consistent improvements in test perplexity across multiple layer configurations and regularization settings, with particularly pronounced gains in overfitting-prone scenarios. Beyond empirical performance, LiFT establishes a principled connection between transformer fine-tuning, bilevel optimization, local search, and regularization theory.

</details>


### [509] [Data Augmentations for Data-Constrained Language Model Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16246)
*Michael K. Chen,Xikun Zhang,Zhen Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates data augmentation techniques to mitigate overfitting in autoregressive language model pretraining under data-constrained, compute-abundant conditions. Three augmentation categories (token-level noise, sequence permutations, target offset prediction) are explored, with combinations proving most effective.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI labs face a data ceiling where compute outpaces new high-quality text generation, language model pretraining enters a data-constrained regime where multi-epoch training on fixed corpora is needed. Standard autoregressive pretraining severely overfits in this setting, motivating the need for regularizers like data augmentation.

Method: Three orthogonal augmentation categories for AR pretraining: (1) token-level noise (masking, random replacement), (2) sequence permutations (right-to-left prediction, Fill-in-the-Middle), and (3) target offset prediction (predicting x_{t+i} for i>1). Systematic ablations are conducted to evaluate individual and combined effects.

Result: Individual augmentations delay overfitting and lower validation loss vs baseline. Random token replacement achieves the best minimum loss among individual methods. Combining augmentation categories further lowers the minimum validation loss.

Conclusion: Data augmentations effectively mitigate AR pretraining's data inefficiency and offer a promising solution to the data-constrained regime where multi-epoch training on fixed corpora is required.

Abstract: As AI labs approach a data ceiling where compute capacity outpaces the rate of new high-quality text generation, language model pretraining is shifting toward a data-constrained, compute-abundant regime that demands productive multi-epoch training on fixed corpora. Standard autoregressive (AR) pretraining overfits severely in this setting, reaching its optimum early and then continuously deteriorating. We investigate data augmentation as a regularizer to mitigate this overfitting and enable productive training for hundreds of epochs on the same data. We introduce three orthogonal categories of augmentation for AR pretraining: token-level noise (masking, random replacement), sequence permutations (right-to-left prediction, Fill-in-the-Middle), and target offset prediction ($x_{t+i}$ for $i > 1$). Through systematic ablations, we find that individual augmentations delay overfitting and lower validation loss relative to the baseline, with random token replacement achieving the best minimum loss among individual methods. Combining augmentation categories further lowers the minimum validation loss. Our experiments demonstrate that data augmentations mitigate AR pretraining's data inefficiency and offer a promising solution to the data-constrained regime. All code and data are available at https://github.com/michaelchen-lab/data-augmentations-for-pretraining

</details>


### [510] [Variance Reduction for Non-Log-Concave Sampling with Applications to Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16257)
*M. Berk Sahin,Ahmet Ege Tanriverdi,Behzad Sharif,Abolfazl Hashemi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides the first unified analysis of variance reduction techniques (momentum, STORM, PAGE) for sampling from high-dimensional non-log-concave distributions using stochastic gradients, establishing improved convergence rates and validating them on imaging inverse problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sampling from high-dimensional non-log-concave distributions is challenging when exact gradients are unavailable and stochastic gradients have high variance. Variance reduction methods exist for optimization but their potential for sampling remains unexplored.

Method: The authors develop a unified theoretical analysis of variance reduction estimators (SGD with momentum, STORM, PAGE) for sampling from non-log-concave distributions, establishing convergence rates in Fisher information and total variation distance, with extensions to score-based generative priors for inverse problems.

Result: Improved non-asymptotic convergence rates in ε-relative Fisher information and squared total variation distance (under Poincaré inequality), weak convergence to target distribution, and empirical validation showing variance-reduction improves sample quality in imaging applications.

Conclusion: Variance reduction techniques consistently improve sampling quality under fixed gradient computation budgets per iteration, with theoretical guarantees for non-log-concave distributions and practical benefits in imaging inverse problems.

Abstract: Sampling from high-dimensional, non-log-concave distributions with unnormalized densities is a fundamental challenge in machine learning, particularly when the exact gradient of the potential is unavailable and must be approximated via stochastic gradients that exhibit high variance under a fixed budget of gradient computations per iteration. Although variance reduction techniques such as SGD with momentum, STORM, and PAGE have demonstrated improved convergence properties in non-convex optimization, their implications for sampling from non-log-concave distributions remain largely unexplored. In this work, we develop the first unified analysis of these estimators for sampling from non-log-concave distributions. We establish improved non-asymptotic convergence rates in $\varepsilon$-relative Fisher information and, under a Poincaré inequality assumption, in squared total variation distance, and further prove weak convergence to the target distribution. We extend our analysis to solving inverse problems with score-based generative priors. We empirically validate our theory and demonstrate that, under a fixed gradient computations per iteration, variance-reduction techniques consistently improve sample quality in two standard imaging applications.

</details>


### [511] [An affordable hardware-aware neural architecture search for deploying convolutional neural networks on ultra-low-power computing platforms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16290)
*Andrea Mattia Garavagno,Edoardo Ragusa,Antonio Frisoli,Paolo Gastaldo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a hardware-aware NAS method that generates tiny CNNs for ultra-low-power microcontrollers, featuring a lightweight search procedure executable on embedded devices while maintaining competitive classification accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing HW-NAS methods target high-performance microcontrollers with high power consumption, which are unsuitable for sensing nodes. There is a need to generate tiny CNNs that can run on ultra-low-power microcontrollers.

Method: A lightweight HW-NAS approach that generates tiny CNNs compatible with ultra-low-power microcontrollers, with a search procedure efficient enough to run on embedded devices themselves.

Result: The proposed HW-NAS successfully generated tiny CNNs on three well-known tiny computer vision benchmarks while preserving state-of-the-art classification accuracy.

Conclusion: The proposed HW-NAS enables deployment of CNNs on ultra-low-power microcontrollers, expanding the applicability of edge AI to power-constrained sensing nodes.

Abstract: Hardware-aware neural architecture search (HW-NAS) allows the integration of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in microcontrollers devices by automatically designing neural architectures that can fit prearranged hardware constraints. However, state-of-the-art HW-NAS target high-performance microcontrollers, whose power consumption does not meet sensing nodes requirements. This work presents a HW-NAS generating tiny CNNs that can run on ultra-low-power microcontrollers, featuring a lightweight search procedure enabling its execution even on embedded devices. Empirical results on three well-known benchmarks for tiny computer vision proved that the proposed HW-NAS was able to generate tiny CNNs while preserving state-of-the-art classification accuracy.

</details>


### [512] [One-Step Generalization Ratio Guided Optimization for Domain Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16301)
*Sumin Cho,Dongwon Kim,Kwangsu Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GENIE is a new optimizer that dynamically equalizes parameter contributions via OSGR to prevent overfitting to domain-specific features, outperforming existing DG methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DG methods overfit to domain-specific (spurious) correlations because gradients are driven by a dominant subset of parameters, and dropout-based regularization does not explicitly adjust gradient alignment or balance parameter updates.

Method: Proposes GENIE optimizer that uses One-Step Generalization Ratio (OSGR) to quantify each parameter's contribution to loss reduction and gradient alignment. It dynamically equalizes OSGR via a preconditioning factor to prevent a few parameters from dominating optimization.

Result: GENIE achieves higher OSGR while retaining SGD's convergence rate, and empirically outperforms existing optimizers and enhances DG/single-DG methods.

Conclusion: Balancing parameter contributions via OSGR equalization effectively promotes domain-invariant feature learning and improves generalization.

Abstract: Domain Generalization (DG) aims to train models that generalize to unseen target domains but often overfit to domain-specific features, known as undesired correlations. Gradient-based DG methods typically guide gradients in a dominant direction but often inadvertently reinforce spurious correlations. Recent work has employed dropout to regularize overconfident parameters, but has not explicitly adjusted gradient alignment or ensured balanced parameter updates. We propose GENIE (Generalization-ENhancing Iterative Equalizer), a novel optimizer that leverages the One-Step Generalization Ratio (OSGR) to quantify each parameter's contribution to loss reduction and assess gradient alignment. By dynamically equalizing OSGR via a preconditioning factor, GENIE prevents a small subset of parameters from dominating optimization, thereby promoting domain-invariant feature learning. Theoretically, GENIE balances convergence contribution and gradient alignment among parameters, achieving higher OSGR while retaining SGD's convergence rate. Empirically, it outperforms existing optimizers and enhances performance when integrated with various DG and single-DG methods.

</details>


### [513] [pFedUL: Layer-Aware Federated Unlearning for Personalized Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16304)
*Zhuodong Liu,Xiangyu Li,Zhihao Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces pFedUL, a layer-aware selective unlearning framework for personalized federated learning that balances unlearning completeness with personalization preservation, achieving 97.3% accuracy retention for remaining clients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing federated unlearning methods are designed for FedAvg (single global model), but personalized FL methods (FedPer, FedRep, Ditto, FedBN) decompose models into shared global layers and client-specific layers, creating a tension between unlearning completeness and personalization preservation that has been largely overlooked.

Method: pFedUL is a layer-aware selective unlearning framework with three components: (1) gradient-based layer-wise contribution attribution to quantify target client influence on shared vs. personalized parameters, (2) adaptive selective unlearning with differentiated forgetting strategies per layer type, and (3) a lightweight recalibration protocol for remaining clients to restore personalization.

Result: Experiments on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and FEMNIST show pFedUL achieves unlearning effectiveness comparable to full retraining while maintaining 97.3% personalized accuracy for remaining clients, outperforming six adapted state-of-the-art FU methods.

Conclusion: The paper formalizes federated unlearning under the personalized FL paradigm and proposes pFedUL, which effectively resolves the tension between unlearning completeness and personalization preservation, validated by two new metrics (PPS and CFI).

Abstract: Federated unlearning (FU) enables the removal of specific data contributions from federated learning (FL) models to comply with regulations such as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). However, most existing FU methods are designed for the FedAvg paradigm, where all clients share a single global model. In practice, personalized federated learning (pFL) methods such as FedPer, FedRep, Ditto, and FedBN have become widely adopted due to their superior handling of non-IID data. These methods decompose the model into shared global layers and client-specific personalized layers, fundamentally altering the semantics of unlearning, yet this setting has received little attention. We formalize FU under the pFL paradigm, identifying a tension between unlearning completeness on shared layers and personalization preservation for remaining clients. We then propose pFedUL, a layer-aware selective unlearning framework comprising three components: (1) gradient-based layer-wise contribution attribution that separately quantifies the target client's influence on shared and personalized parameters, (2) adaptive selective unlearning that applies differentiated forgetting strategies across layer types, and (3) a lightweight recalibration protocol enabling remaining clients to restore personalization with minimal overhead. We further introduce two new metrics, Personalization Preservation Score (PPS) and Cross-client Fairness Index (CFI), to evaluate pFL-specific unlearning quality. Experiments on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and FEMNIST under varying non-IID settings indicate that pFedUL achieves unlearning effectiveness comparable to full retraining while maintaining an average of 97.3\% personalized accuracy for remaining clients. Compared with six state-of-the-art FU methods adapted to the pFL setting, pFedUL consistently achieves superior personalization preservation.

</details>


### [514] [QK-Normed MLA: QK normalization without full key caching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16310)
*Yizhou Han,Yao Zhao,Jun Zhou,Longfei Li,Ruoyu Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QK normalization can be applied to Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA) without full-key caching by decomposing RMSNorm into static and dynamic components, achieving lower training loss and less than 2% latency overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: QK normalization stabilizes attention but was thought incompatible with MLA's efficient latent state caching. The authors wanted to show this is merely an implementation artifact, not an architectural constraint.

Method: RMSNorm is decomposed into static affine weight (absorbed into query projection) and dynamic scalar RMS statistic (reduced to one inverse-RMS scalar per token/KV group). This reformulation is exactly equivalent to explicit post-projection QK RMSNorm while preserving MLA's latent decode path.

Result: 400M parameter models trained up to 100B tokens: QK-Normed MLA achieves lower training loss and better downstream accuracy than QK clipping. H800 decode benchmarks show <2% latency overhead up to 256k context.

Conclusion: QK normalization is a practical stabilization option for MLA models that does not require full-key caching, contrary to prior belief.

Abstract: Query-key (QK) normalization stabilizes attention by controlling the scale of queries and keys before the dot product, but is not immediately compatible with Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA). MLA achieves efficient decoding by caching low-dimensional latent states instead of full keys, whereas post-projection QK RMSNorm appears to require the fully projected key for every cached token. We show this apparent incompatibility is an implementation artifact, not an architectural constraint. RMSNorm decomposes into a static affine weight and a dynamic scalar RMS statistic. The static key-side weight can be absorbed into the MLA query-side projection; the dynamic key statistic reduces to one inverse-RMS scalar per token and KV group. The resulting formulation is exactly equivalent to explicit post-projection QK RMSNorm in exact arithmetic and preserves MLA's latent decode path. In our 400M runs trained for up to 100B tokens, QK-Normed MLA achieves lower training loss and better downstream accuracy than QK clipping, while H800 decode benchmarks show less than 2% latency overhead up to 256k context. These results make QK normalization a practical stabilization option for MLA models without requiring full-key caching.

</details>


### [515] [Diffusion Offline Reinforcement Learning for Fair and Energy-Efficient UAV-Assisted Wireless Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16331)
*Eslam Eldeeb,Hirley Alves*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Diffusion-SAC, an offline RL framework combining conservative Q-learning with denoising diffusion probabilistic models, to optimize UAV trajectory and scheduling in 6G wireless networks, achieving over 35% improvement in throughput and energy efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing offline RL methods (e.g., CQL) struggle to generalize in low-data or dynamic wireless conditions, limiting their applicability in future 6G UAV networks where data-efficient and robust policy learning is critical.

Method: A Diffusion Soft Actor-Critic (Diffusion-SAC) approach that integrates denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) with conservative Q-learning (CQL) to enable expressive, signal-aware offline RL policy learning.

Result: Diffusion-SAC outperforms standard offline RL baselines with more stable convergence, higher rewards on limited datasets, over 35% increase in throughput, reduced energy consumption, and improved fairness among devices.

Conclusion: The proposed generative offline RL framework demonstrates strong potential for robust, data-efficient policy learning in next-generation wireless control systems.

Abstract: The integration of generative artificial intelligence with wireless communication and signal processing systems has opened new avenues for intelligent, data-driven decision-making in future 6G networks. This work proposes a diffusion soft actor-critic (Diffusion-SAC) approach that leverages offline reinforcement learning (RL) enhanced by denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) to optimize trajectory and scheduling control in unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) networks. While offline RL methods, such as conservative Q-learning (CQL), can learn from static datasets, they often struggle to generalize in low-data or dynamic conditions. To address this, we combine the robustness of CQL with the generative power of diffusion models, enabling expressive and signal-aware policy learning that generalizes beyond behavior policies. Applied to a UAV-assisted wireless network, the proposed framework minimizes transmission energy and improves fairness among devices. Simulations show that Diffusion-SAC outperforms standard offline RL baselines, achieving more stable convergence and higher rewards even with limited datasets. The method enhances data efficiency, reduces energy consumption, and increases throughput by more than 35 % compared to existing algorithms, demonstrating its potential for robust policy learning in next-generation wireless control systems.

</details>


### [516] [Filtered ANN as a Phase Transition: When Selectivity-Estimation Error Causes Plan Regret](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16341)
*Madhulatha Mandarapu,Sandeep Kunkunuru*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper characterizes the performance of filtered ANN queries under selectivity uncertainty, showing that plan regret concentrates at phase boundaries between pre-filter, post-filter, and in-filter strategies. The regret forms a universal wedge governed by order statistics and percolation theory, confirmed via scaling collapses on synthetic and real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how selectivity-estimation error impacts filtered ANN query performance across different execution strategies (pre-filter, post-filter, in-filter), and to characterize where and why plan regret occurs.

Method: The problem is modeled as an argmax over a landscape with phases separated by boundaries. Phase boundaries are derived mathematically: post-filter cliff from order statistics (s ~ k/K), in-filter cliff from site percolation (s_c ~ 0.83/M). Regret is analyzed as a wedge with log-width proportional to estimation error epsilon and height proportional to |V'(s*)| epsilon. Pre-registered experiments validate the theory on synthetic sweeps and SIFT1M.

Result: Regret concentrates ~290x at phase boundaries. Regret curves collapse onto a single universal wedge across two decades of corpus size. A biased cost model creates a persistent miscalibration band that estimation-error robustness cannot fix. Criticality exists only under budget B < sqrt(k n).

Conclusion: The paper provides a theoretical characterization of filtered ANN query regret rather than proposing a new index. The findings highlight that selectivity estimation error is problematic only near phase boundaries, and that unbiased cost models are crucial for robustness.

Abstract: A filtered approximate-nearest-neighbor (ANN) query returns the k nearest vectors among those satisfying an attribute predicate P of selectivity s. The best execution strategy -- pre-filter, post-filter, or in-filter -- changes with s, so a system must estimate s and choose. We model this as an argmax over a landscape with phases (regions where each strategy wins) separated by boundaries, and show that selectivity-estimation error produces plan regret -- recall lost versus the oracle strategy -- only in the critical regions around those boundaries. The regret is a wedge of log-width equal to the multiplicative estimation error epsilon and height equal to the local cliff |V'(s*)| epsilon; the flip-margin 1/|V'(s*)| is the condition number of a sibling cardinality-estimation study reappearing as the local boundary theory. The two phase boundaries follow from independent mathematics: order statistics place the post-filter cliff at s ~ k/K, and site percolation places the in-filter cliff at s_c ~ 0.83/M for graph degree M (corpus-size independent). Criticality exists only under a constrained budget B < sqrt(k n). Under pre-registered decision rules we confirm, on synthetic sweeps and real SIFT1M, that regret concentrates ~290x at the boundary and that the regret curves obey a finite-size scaling collapse onto one universal wedge across two decades of corpus size. A real approximate index does not mis-locate the boundary, but a biased cost model opens a persistent miscalibration band that estimation-error robustness cannot fix. The contribution is a characterization, not a new index. Code and the full pre-registration are public.

</details>


### [517] [Communication-Efficient Verifiable Attention for LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16352)
*Ziqun Chen,Ming Wu,Michael Heinrich,Jason Zeng,Huiying Lan,Tianwei Zhang,Rui Tan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VeriAttn is a communication-efficient TEE-GPU attention mechanism that accelerates verifiable LLM inference by offloading attention computations to GPU while TEE handles verification, achieving 2.6-5.42x speedup over TSDP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Directly applying existing TEE-shielded DNN partitioning (TSDP) to Transformer-based LLMs causes significant TEE computation and TEE-GPU communication overhead, making verifiable LLM inference inefficient.

Method: VeriAttn offloads both linear and non-linear attention computations to GPU while TEE performs verification only. For prefill, it uses a two-level pipeline to overlap data movement, TEE pre-/post-processing, and GPU computation. For decoding with KV cache exceeding GPU memory, it partitions attention across TEE and GPU to reduce repeated key-value transfers.

Result: On Intel TDX platform, VeriAttn achieves 2.60-3.38x acceleration over TSDP for 6k-token prompts during prefill, and 3.86-5.42x acceleration for 10k-token outputs during decoding.

Conclusion: VeriAttn significantly improves verifiable LLM inference efficiency by reducing TEE computation and communication overhead through strategic offloading and pipelining of attention mechanisms.

Abstract: Computation integrity of remote large language model (LLM) serving can be questionable. For conventional deep neural networks (DNNs), the existing TEE-shielded DNN partitioning (TSDP) approach uses Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) to compute non-linear components and verify the integrity of linear components offloaded to an untrusted GPU. However, directly applying TSDP to Transformer-based LLMs incurs significant TEE computation and TEE-GPU communication overhead. This paper presents Communication-efficient TEE-GPU Attention (\textsc{VeriAttn}) for accelerating verifiable LLM inference. \textsc{VeriAttn} offloads both linear and non-linear computations of attention to the GPU, while TEE performs verification. Moreover, for prefill, \textsc{VeriAttn} uses a two-level pipeline to overlap data movement, TEE pre-/post-processing, and GPU computation. For decoding, when the key-value cache exceeds available GPU memory, \textsc{VeriAttn} partitions attention across TEE and GPU to reduce repeated key-value transfers. Evaluation on an Intel TDX platform shows that \textsc{VeriAttn} achieves 2.60-3.38$\times$ and 3.86-5.42$\times$ acceleration over TSDP for 6k-token prompts and 10k-token outputs during prefill and decoding, respectively.

</details>


### [518] [Simulation-Augmented Multi-Step Split Conformal Prediction for Aggregated Forecasts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16356)
*Andro Sabashvili*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SA-MSCP is a simulation-augmented conformal method that uses block bootstrap residuals to construct prediction intervals for aggregated time-series forecasts (e.g., annual totals, growth rates), improving coverage over standard baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide reliable uncertainty quantification for aggregated forecasting targets (annual totals, growth rates) where standard conformal methods may undercover.

Method: Simulation-Augmented Multi-Step Split Conformal (SA-MSCP): generates future paths from cross-validated residuals using a block bootstrap, then constructs prediction intervals from empirical quantiles.

Result: SA-MSCP improves empirical coverage over the simulated-path baseline for both aggregated and growth-rate forecasting targets.

Conclusion: Simulation-enhanced conformal calibration is an effective and general framework for UQ in aggregated time-series forecasting.

Abstract: We study uncertainty quantification for aggregated forecasting tasks such as annual totals and year-over-year growth rates. We propose SA-MSCP, a simulation-augmented multi-step split conformal method that generates future paths from cross-validated residuals using a block bootstrap and constructs prediction intervals from empirical quantiles. Experiments show that SA-MSCP improves empirical coverage over a simulated-path baseline for aggregated and growth-rate targets. Our results demonstrate that simulation-enhanced conformal calibration is an effective and general framework for uncertainty quantification in aggregated time-series forecasting.

</details>


### [519] [CacheMuon: Using Temporal Preconditioning To Approximate Polar Factor](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16371)
*Bishnu Dev,Sushil Bohara,Martin Takáč,Samuel Horváth*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CacheMuon speeds up the Muon optimizer by reusing previous polar factor computations across optimization steps, reducing expensive orthogonalization while maintaining model quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Muon's Newton-Schulz iteration for computing the polar factor is applied at every optimization step and is computationally expensive, despite the momentum matrix changing slowly over time.

Method: CacheMuon is a temporal preconditioning method that reuses polar factor information from previous optimization steps to approximate the current polar factor, reducing redundant orthogonalization across iterations.

Result: Conservative CacheMuon thresholds closely match fresh Muon on language-model and vision training while reducing orthogonalization FLOPs; aggressive thresholds yield greater savings with modest quality degradation.

Conclusion: CacheMuon provides a controllable quality-efficiency frontier for the Muon optimizer, offering practical speedups with tunable trade-offs between computation and validation performance.

Abstract: Muon is an optimizer that computes updates using the polar factor of the momentum matrix and has shown strong empirical performance across a range of training settings. A key component of Muon is the Newton-Schulz iteration used to compute this polar factor. Although this avoids the cost of an exact singular value decomposition, it remains expensive in practice because it is applied at every optimization step. At the same time, the momentum matrix changes smoothly over training, suggesting strong temporal correlation in the corresponding polar factors. In this paper, we exploit this structure and propose CacheMuon, a temporal preconditioning method that reuses information from previous optimization steps to approximate the polar factor at the current step. This reduces redundant orthogonalization computation across iterations. We analyze CacheMuon as an inexact Muon update, with error controlled by fresh-solver error and cache staleness. Empirically, CacheMuon provides a controllable quality-efficiency frontier: conservative thresholds closely match fresh Muon on language-model and vision training while reducing orthogonalization FLOPs, whereas more aggressive thresholds yield larger arithmetic savings at the cost of modest validation-quality degradation.

</details>


### [520] [Scalable and Interpretable Representation Alignment with Ordinal Similarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16379)
*Diogo Soares,Pankhil Gawade,Andrea Dittadi,Ewa Szczurek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces ordinal-similarity metrics (TSI and QSI) that measure representation alignment via ordinal relationship consistency, offering interpretability, robustness, and computational efficiency compared to existing similarity metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing representation similarity metrics suffer from shifting baselines (lack of interpretability), sensitivity to outliers, and computational intractability for large datasets, necessitating heuristic approximations.

Method: Develops an ordinal-similarity framework with Triplet Similarity Index (TSI) and Quadruplet Similarity Index (QSI), which quantify alignment by measuring consistency of ordinal relationships across representations.

Result: Theoretically demonstrates interpretability, outlier robustness, and computational efficiency; establishes formal equivalence between TSI and local neighborhood alignment via Mutual Nearest Neighbors.

Conclusion: Ordinal similarity provides a scalable, principled approach to measuring representation alignment, enabling better understanding and design of representations.

Abstract: Evaluating representation similarity is fundamental to representation learning. However, existing metrics suffer from significant limitations: they lack interpretability due to shifting baselines, lack robustness to outliers, and are computationally intractable for large datasets, forcing reliance on heuristic approximations. To address this, we develop an ordinal-similarity framework, instantiated by the Triplet (TSI) and Quadruplet (QSI) Similarity Indices, which measure alignment by quantifying the consistency of ordinal relationships. We theoretically demonstrate this formulation is inherently interpretable, robust to outliers, and computationally efficient. Finally, we establish a formal equivalence between TSI and local neighborhood alignment, measured by Mutual Nearest Neighbors. Empirically, we validate these properties and show that ordinal similarity offers a scalable approach to measuring alignment, enabling practitioners to better understand and design representations.

</details>


### [521] [Mixtures of Subspaces for Bandwidth Efficient Context Parallel Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16384)
*Sameera Ramasinghe,Ajanthan Thalaiyasingam,Hadi Mohaghegh Dolatabadi,Gil Avraham,Violetta Shevchenko,Yan Zuo,Chamin Hewa Koneputugodage,Alexander Long*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a compression method for communication-efficient context parallelism in decentralized LLM training, achieving 95%+ compression rate and matching centralized training speed even on slow networks (300Mbps) by exploiting low-rank activation structures via dynamic subspace constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing context parallelism methods split sequences across devices and compute attention block-by-block, causing significant communication overhead that is practical only in high-speed clusters but infeasible for decentralized training over low-bandwidth connections.

Method: Exploits intrinsic low-rank structure of activation outputs by dynamically constraining them to learned mixtures of subspaces via efficient reparameterizations, achieving over 95% compression rate.

Result: Scales billion-parameter decentralized models to context lengths exceeding 100K tokens on networks as slow as 300Mbps, matching wall-clock convergence speed of centralized models on 100Gbps interconnects.

Conclusion: The proposed compression method makes decentralized training of long-context language models practical even over low-bandwidth connections, with negligible overhead and no convergence loss.

Abstract: Pretraining language models with extended context windows enhances their ability to leverage rich information during generation. Existing methods split input sequences into chunks, broadcast them across multiple devices, and compute attention block by block which incurs significant communication overhead. While feasible in high-speed clusters, these methods are impractical for decentralized training over low-bandwidth connections. We propose a compression method for communication-efficient context parallelism in decentralized settings, achieving a remarkable compression rate of over 95\% with negligible overhead and no loss in convergence. Our key insight is to exploit the intrinsic low-rank structure of activation outputs by dynamically constraining them to learned mixtures of subspaces via efficient reparameterizations. We demonstrate scaling billion-parameter decentralized models to context lengths exceeding 100K tokens on networks as slow as 300Mbps, matching the wall-clock convergence speed of centralized models on 100Gbps interconnects.

</details>


### [522] [Robust Neural Tucker Factorization with Bias Correction and Adaptive Initialization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16388)
*Yuchao Su,Yixin Ran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes KaBiN, a neural Tucker factorization model with Kaiming initialization and bias correction, to improve HDI tensor completion by addressing optimization instability from poor initialization and missing translation parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HDI tensor completion faces challenges from non-linear dynamics and non-stationary variations. Neural Tucker factorization suffers from suboptimal initialization causing gradient saturation and omitted bias parameters forcing interaction weights to absorb global statistical shifts.

Method: The KaBiN model applies Kaiming uniform initialization to embedding and Tucker linear parameters, and adds a simple bias correction to the output mapping to decouple global mean shifts from local structural representations.

Result: Experiments on three real-world HDI tensor datasets show KaBiN achieves better completion performance than the original NeuTucF with minimal computational overhead.

Conclusion: Simple implementation-level fixes (Kaiming init + bias correction) can significantly improve neural Tucker factorization stability and accuracy for HDI tensor completion without complex architectural changes.

Abstract: High-dimensional incomplete (HDI) tensors are widely used in traffic and climate applications, but sparse observations make accurate completion difficult. The intrinsic non-linear dynamics and non-stationary variations across distinct multi-modal fields severely hinder the efficacy of conventional linear reconstruction frameworks. Neural Tucker factorization provides an effective framework for modeling high-order interactions among tensor modes. By parameterizing underlying structural characteristics into continuous latent spaces, neural representations circumvent the rigid low-rank constraints of classical algebra. However, its performance can still be affected by implementation-level choices, especially parameter initialization and the bias configuration of the final output mapping. Suboptimal initializations frequently lead to variance explosion across the cubically expanded interaction spaces, driving the subsequent non-linear activation boundaries into severe gradient saturation zones, while the omission of a dedicated translation parameter forces interaction weights to implicitly absorb global statistical deviations. This paper proposes a simple yet effective neural Tucker factorization model with Kaiming initialization and bias correction (KaBiN) for HDI tensor completion. The proposed model utilizes Kaiming uniform initialization for the embedding and Tucker linear parameters, and adopts a simple bias correction in output mapping. By elegantly decoupling global mean shifts from local structural representations, the framework provides a highly stable and well-conditioned optimization landscape. Experiments on three real-world HDI tensor datasets show that KaBiN achieves better performance than the original NeuTucF, while introducing minimal computational overhead.

</details>


### [523] [MUNI: Multimodal Unified Latent Diffusion for Coherent Any-to-Any Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16408)
*Kyeongmin Yeo,Yunhong Min,Minhyuk Sung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MUNI is an end-to-end multimodal latent diffusion framework for any-to-any generation using a shared stochastic latent and a routed training objective, achieving strong performance on conditional and unconditional generation across image, text, and audio modalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal generative models are largely LLM-based, which limits leveraging modality-specific generators and requires text-paired data. Recent diffusion/flow-based any-to-any extensions still rely on text-aligned embeddings, fully-paired training, or matched-dimensionality deterministic mappings. MUNI aims to overcome these limitations.

Method: MUNI extends latent diffusion to end-to-end multimodal any-to-any generation by jointly training modality-specific encoders, expressive decoders, and a single shared flow-based prior. It introduces a routed training objective that ensures the shared latent satisfies coherence, predictive sufficiency, and minimality criteria.

Result: On PolyMNIST-Quadrant-Labels and a large-scale image-text-audio benchmark, MUNI matches or exceeds strongest baselines on conditional generation and achieves largest margins on unconditional coherence.

Conclusion: MUNI demonstrates that end-to-end multimodal latent diffusion with a properly regularized shared latent can outperform existing approaches in both conditional and unconditional multimodal generation tasks.

Abstract: We introduce MUNI, an end-to-end multimodal latent diffusion framework for any-to-any generation that unifies subset-conditioned cross-modal generation and unconditional joint sampling through a shared stochastic latent. Existing multimodal generative models are largely LLM-based, which limits leveraging modality-specific generators and requires text-paired data for training. Recent diffusion- and flow-based any-to-any extensions take a different direction but still rely on text-aligned embeddings, fully-paired training, or matched-dimensionality deterministic mappings. MUNI rests on two complementary contributions, one architectural and one in the training objective. First, we extend latent diffusion to multimodal any-to-any generation end-to-end: instead of the standard two-stage recipe that precomputes a frozen latent space and then fits a prior over it, MUNI jointly trains modality-specific encoders, expressive decoders, and a single shared flow-based prior under one objective. Second, we identify that the standard aggregation rules of multimodal variational inference are insufficient once coupled with a learned prior and expressive decoders. A suitable shared latent must simultaneously satisfy coherence across generated modalities, predictive sufficiency of subset latents, and minimality of the latent content. We propose a routed training objective whose structural choices align the latent with these criteria and admit a minimal-sufficiency characterization in the realizable setting. Experiments on PolyMNIST-Quadrant-Labels and a large-scale image-text-audio benchmark show MUNI matching or exceeding the strongest baselines on conditional generation while opening its largest margins on unconditional coherence. Project page: https://muni-proj.github.io/.

</details>


### [524] [Not all Jensen-Shannon Divergence Estimators are Equal](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16411)
*Alba Garrido,Alejandro Almodóvar,Mar Elizo,Patricia A. Apellániz,Santiago Zazo,Juan Parras*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper reveals that the Jensen-Shannon divergence for synthetic tabular data is estimation-protocol-dependent, leading to non-comparable empirical values. The authors identify key pitfalls (dependence blindness, prior-shift bias, estimator sensitivity), derive a closed-form posterior correction for classifier-based estimators, and provide practical guidelines and an open-source tool.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The Jensen-Shannon divergence is widely used to measure synthetic tabular data fidelity, but its estimation from finite samples is often underspecified, creating a measurement problem where different protocols yield non-comparable values.

Method: The authors systematically study JS divergence estimation across controlled settings with reference divergences and real-world benchmarks, analyzing marginal-based vs. classifier-based estimators. They derive a closed-form posterior correction to address prior-shift bias in classifier-based estimation.

Result: Marginal estimators suffer from dependence blindness (underestimating divergence). Classifier estimators capture joint structure but exhibit strong estimator dependence and prior-shift bias under class imbalance. Empirical JS divergence values are inherently protocol-dependent.

Conclusion: Explicit specification of the estimation procedure is necessary for meaningful JS divergence comparison. The authors provide practical guidelines and an open-source tool for estimator-aware evaluation.

Abstract: The Jensen-Shannon divergence is widely reported as a scalar measure of fidelity for synthetic tabular data. Yet, in practice, it is estimated from finite samples using protocols that are often underspecified. This creates a measurement problem. Although the population divergence is well defined, the empirical value depends on the estimator family, sampling protocol, calibration, dimensionality, and class balance. We show that different protocols can yield non-comparable values: marginal-based estimators ignore dependencies in the joint distribution and can severely underestimate divergence, while classifier-based estimators capture joint structure but exhibit strong estimator dependence. We systematically study this behavior across controlled settings with reference divergences and real-world synthetic tabular benchmarks. Our analysis reveals dependence blindness in marginal estimators, prior-shift bias under class imbalance, and estimator sensitivity in high dimensions. To address prior shift, we derive a closed-form posterior correction for classifier-based Jensen-Shannon estimation. Our results show that empirical Jensen-Shannon divergence values are inherently protocol-dependent, making explicit specification of the estimation procedure necessary for meaningful comparison. We provide practical guidelines and an open-source tool for estimator-aware Jensen-Shannon evaluation.

</details>


### [525] [Taylor-Calibrate: Principled Initialization for Hybrid Linear Attention Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16429)
*Zhongzhu Zhou,Qingyang Wu,Junxiong Wang,Mayank Mishra,Shuaiwen Leon Song,Ben Athiwaratkun,Chenfeng Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Taylor-Calibrate is a lightweight initialization method for converting pretrained Transformers into hybrid Gated DeltaNet models, achieving up to 88x improvement and 4.9x-9.2x fewer training tokens than naive conversion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Converting pretrained Transformers to hybrid linear attention models (like Gated DeltaNet) is brittle because naive parameter copying doesn't specify the new recurrent dynamics, causing poor initialization that requires many distillation tokens to repair.

Method: Taylor-Calibrate uses Taylor-guided teacher attention statistics to set value projection, memory timescale, write gates, and output gate, followed by a short per-layer alignment step to match converted layer outputs to the teacher.

Result: Up to 88x improvement in zero-shot performance over naive conversion in ablations; reaches matched recovery targets with 4.9x-9.2x fewer training tokens; consistently strong across four teacher settings and three retained-layer policies.

Conclusion: Taylor-Calibrate provides a principled and efficient initialization for hybrid model conversion, dramatically reducing the token budget needed to recover teacher-level performance.

Abstract: Hybrid linear attention models offer an appealing path to faster long-context inference: they reduce the quadratic cost and KV-cache burden of full softmax attention while retaining much of the quality of Transformer models. A practical way to obtain such models is to convert a pretrained Transformer instead of pretraining a new architecture from scratch, but this conversion is still brittle. Simply copying the teacher attention projections into a Gated DeltaNet (GDN) student does not specify the new recurrent decay, write, and output-gating dynamics. As a result, the converted model often starts in a poor dynamical regime and must spend many distillation tokens repairing initialization rather than learning the remaining teacher behavior. We propose Taylor-Calibrate, a lightweight initialization method for hybrid GDN students. The method uses Taylor-guided teacher attention statistics to set the value projection, memory timescale, write gates, and output gate, then applies a short per-layer alignment step to match each converted layer to the teacher output. Across four teacher settings and three retained-layer policies, Taylor-Calibrate gives substantially stronger zero-shot students, with up to an 88x improvement in a representative ablation, and reaches matched recovery targets with 4.9x--9.2x fewer training tokens than naive conversion.

</details>


### [526] [Autonomous End-to-End SOH Prediction Services for Battery Systems via Temporal-Contrastive Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16434)
*Junting Wen,Dan Li,Qihao Quan,Xiwen Wang,Hang Yang,Zhaohong Meng,Zigui Jiang,Changlin Yang,Tianle Liu,Diego Muñoz-Carpintero,Jian Lou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TC-SOH is a modular, plug-and-play service for autonomous lithium-ion battery state of health (SOH) estimation. It uses temporal-contrastive learning and cross-window prediction to extract degradation features from raw data, improving transparency via representation diagnostics, and outperforms baselines by ~2x on MAPE and RMSE across four datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual feature engineering and opaque black-box models hinder scalable industrial deployment of battery SOH estimation. The authors aim for an autonomous, end-to-end solution that is both accurate and interpretable.

Method: TC-SOH introduces a temporal-contrastive mechanism and a cross-window prediction pretext task to learn degradation-relevant representations directly from raw operational data. Transparency is enhanced via visualization, sensitivity analysis, redundancy analysis, bidirectional probing, future-SOH probing, and temporal shuffling diagnostics.

Result: Across four public datasets, TC-SOH reduces MAPE by 1.91× and RMSE by 2.13× compared to physics-informed and data-driven baselines. Learned features overlap with expert descriptors while retaining additional SOH-relevant variation, and ordered temporal context improves prediction.

Conclusion: TC-SOH provides an accurate, transparent, and scalable solution for autonomous battery SOH estimation, bridging the gap between data-driven methods and industrial deployment requirements.

Abstract: Accurate state of health (SOH) estimation is a critical diagnostic service for lithium-ion battery management. However, reliance on labor-intensive manual feature engineering and opaque black-box models hinders scalable industrial deployment. To address this, we introduce TC-SOH: a modular, plug-and-play service architecture for autonomous, end-to-end SOH prediction. TC-SOH employs a temporal-contrastive mechanism and a cross-window prediction pretext task to extract degradation-relevant representations directly from raw operational data. To improve transparency, we connect model efficacy with representation diagnostics: visualization, sensitivity analysis, redundancy analysis, bidirectional probing, future-SOH probing, and temporal shuffling show that learned features overlap with selected expert descriptors while retaining additional SOH-relevant variation, and that ordered temporal context improves subsequent-SOH prediction. Across four public datasets, TC-SOH outperforms the considered physics-informed and data-driven baselines, reducing MAPE by 1.91 times and RMSE by 2.13 times.

</details>


### [527] [SDS-LoRA: Overcoming Anisotropic Gradient Scaling in Low-Rank Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16454)
*Junghun Oh,Sungyong Baik,Kyoung Mu Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies anisotropic gradient scaling in LoRA caused by singular values, which distorts gradients and reduces adaptation quality. The authors propose SDS-LoRA, which decouples singular values from the backward pass to preserve gradient fidelity, achieving better convergence and closer performance to full fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LoRA's low-rank parameterization causes anisotropic scaling of the full fine-tuning gradient due to singular values, skewing it toward dominant directions and suppressing others, which exacerbates the performance gap compared to full fine-tuning.

Method: SDS-LoRA structurally decouples singular values from the backward pass, allowing the full fine-tuning gradient to backpropagate only through orthonormal bases of the low-rank matrices' subspaces, independent of their scale.

Result: SDS-LoRA shows improved loss convergence, reduced gap to full fine-tuning, and convergence rate independent of the condition number of low-rank matrices, validated across NLP and vision benchmarks.

Conclusion: Decoupling singular values from gradient backpropagation in LoRA effectively mitigates anisotropic gradient distortion, yielding better adaptation performance and closer alignment with full fine-tuning.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) enables efficient adaptation of large pre-trained models to downstream tasks by parameterizing weight updates with low-rank matrices. In this paper, we investigate the limitations of the LoRA parameterization from a geometric perspective. Specifically, we show that when a full fine-tuning gradient is backpropagated to the low-rank matrices, it undergoes anisotropic scaling driven by their singular values. We argue that this phenomenon is undesirable because it distorts the full fine-tuning gradient by skewing it toward dominant singular directions while suppressing others. Our analyses demonstrate that anisotropic gradient scaling reduces the effective rank of the low-rank matrices' gradients and results in suboptimal alignment between the full fine-tuning gradient and its low-rank approximation in LoRA, thereby exacerbating the gap to full fine-tuning. To address these limitations, we propose a new low-rank parameterization, SDS-LoRA, which structurally decouples singular values from the backward pass. Our method ensures that the full fine-tuning gradient backpropagates only through the orthonormal bases of the low-rank matrices' subspaces, independent of their scales. Convergence analysis demonstrates that while LoRA's convergence rate degrades with the condition number of the low-rank matrices, SDS-LoRA remains independent of it. Experimental results across natural language and vision benchmarks show that SDS-LoRA improves loss convergence and reduces the gap to full fine-tuning, significantly enhancing adaptation performance.

</details>


### [528] [SPRI: SVD-Partitioned Residual Initialization for Data-Constrained MoE Upcycling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16456)
*Weiqiao Shan,Ruixiang Mao,Yuang Li,Yuhao Zhang,Yingfeng Luo,Tong Zheng,Chen Xu,Yucheng Qiao,Chunxiang Jin,Yi Yuan,Jingdong Chen,Tong Xiao,Jingbo Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPRI is a new method for MoE upcycling that uses SVD-partitioned residual initialization from pretrained FFN weights, combined with two-stage training, to effectively convert dense models into sparse MoE models under data-constrained conditions, achieving significant gains on multilingual speech-to-text translation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training MoE models from scratch is too expensive. MoE upcycling (converting dense to sparse MoE) is cheaper, but existing methods struggle under data-constrained supervised adaptation due to either homogeneous experts or disruptive perturbations to pretrained parameters.

Method: SPRI distributes SVD-partitioned residuals from pretrained FFN weights across routed experts to introduce controlled diversity grounded in pretrained spectral structure, plus a two-stage training strategy for stability.

Result: On CoVoST2 (15 En-to-XX directions), SPRI improves average BLEU and COMET over fully fine-tuned dense models by 2.58 and 3.32 points, and outperforms prior best MoE upcycling baseline by 3.39 BLEU and 4.34 COMET points.

Conclusion: SVD-based residual initialization with two-stage training enables effective MoE upcycling under data scarcity, leveraging pretrained weight structure while ensuring expert diversity.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models enable efficient scaling, but training them from scratch remains prohibitively expensive. MoE upcycling mitigates this cost by converting pretrained dense models into sparse MoE models. However, existing upcycling methods typically rely on large-scale continued training and often perform poorly under data-constrained supervised adaptation, due to either homogeneous experts or overly disruptive perturbations to pretrained parameters. In this setting, effective upcycling must leverage pretrained weight structure while introducing sufficient diversity among routed experts. To this end, we propose SVD-Partitioned Residual Initialization (SPRI), which distributes SVD-partitioned residuals derived from pretrained feed-forward network (FFN) weights across routed experts, introducing controlled expert diversity grounded in pretrained spectral structure. We further introduce a two-stage training strategy to improve adaptation stability. We evaluate SPRI on multilingual speech-to-text translation, where limited supervised data challenges MoE upcycling and multiple target languages provide natural routing heterogeneity. On CoVoST2 across 15 En-to-XX directions, SPRI improves average BLEU and COMET over fully fine-tuned dense models by 2.58 and 3.32 points, respectively, and outperforms the prior best MoE upcycling baseline by 3.39 BLEU and 4.34 COMET points.

</details>


### [529] [Privacy from Symmetry: Orthogonally Equivariant Transformers for LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16461)
*Alexander Yukhimchuk,Andrey Shulga,Mladen Kolar,Martin Takáč*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes orthogonal obfuscation for privacy-preserving LLM inference, where clients rotate embeddings by a secret orthogonal matrix before sending them to the server. They introduce ConjFormer, an O(d)-equivariant transformer that operates entirely in the rotated space, preventing hidden state recovery without noise or cryptography. Experiments show token recovery drops from 35% to 1.3% with minimal perplexity increase (0.4%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Running LLMs locally is often impractical, forcing sensitive text to be processed by third-party providers. Split inference (sending hidden representations instead of tokens) is vulnerable to nearest-neighbor attacks that recover tokens from public embedding tables. The goal is to prevent this reconstruction without heavy cryptographic overhead or noise injection.

Method: The client multiplies input embeddings by a secret orthogonal matrix before sending them to the server. To enable correct inference under any rotation, the authors introduce ConjFormer, a transformer variant that is exactly O(d)-equivariant. This is achieved via scalar RMSNorm (a lightweight normalization change) and blockwise orthogonal conjugation of all linear weights. The server performs the full forward pass in the rotated basis, never seeing unrotated hidden states.

Result: On GPT-2 and Llama 3.2 1B models fine-tuned on PubMed, orthogonal obfuscation eliminates direct cosine nearest-neighbor inversion and reduces token recovery from over 35% top-10 to at most 1.3%. Perplexity increases by only 0.4% after fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Enforcing O(d) symmetry at the architectural level provides a practical defense for privacy-preserving LLM inference without noise injection or heavy cryptographic machinery. The approach is effective, lightweight, and maintains model utility.

Abstract: Running large language models locally is often impractical, pushing inference on sensitive text to third-party providers. Split inference partially mitigates this by keeping tokens on the client and sending only hidden representations, but these representations can still be recovered via nearest-neighbor search against the public embedding table. We propose an orthogonal obfuscation procedure in which the client multiplies embeddings by a secret orthogonal matrix before transmission. To enable correct inference under arbitrary rotations, we introduce ConjFormer, a transformer variant that is exactly $\mathrm{O}(d)$-equivariant via a lightweight normalization change (scalar RMSNorm) together with blockwise orthogonal conjugation of all linear weights. As a result, the server performs the full forward pass entirely in the rotated basis and never observes unrotated hidden states. Experiments on GPT-2 and Llama 3.2 1B models fine-tuned on PubMed show that orthogonal obfuscation eliminates direct cosine nearest-neighbor inversion and reduces token recovery from over 35% top-10 to at most 1.3%, while increasing perplexity by only 0.4% after fine-tuning. These results indicate that enforcing symmetry at the architectural level can provide a practical defense for privacy-preserving LLM inference without noise injection or heavy cryptographic machinery.

</details>


### [530] [Learning aligned EEG representations with subject-specific encoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16462)
*Bruna J. Lopes,Gabriel Schwartz,Sylvain Chevallier,Raphael Y. de Camargo,Bruno Aristimunha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates cross-subject EEG decoding by comparing subject-specific encoders (hybrid model) against shared encoders with Euclidean Alignment (EA). The hybrid model learns subject-aligned representations that internalize the role of EA, improving class distinctiveness and per-subject latent manifolds, though head selection for unseen subjects remains a bottleneck.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-subject EEG decoding suffers from strong inter-subject distribution shifts. The authors explore whether task supervision and architecture alone can learn subject-aligned representations without explicit alignment methods.

Method: Replace a shared EEG encoder with subject-specific encoders followed by a common classifier (hybrid model). Compare against standard EEGNet, AttentionBaseNet, and CTNet baselines with/without Euclidean Alignment (EA) on four motor-imagery datasets.

Result: Hybrid encoders internalize the role of EA—latent distances and validation curves change little when EA is removed. Subject-specific heads increase class distinctiveness and place each subject near its own latent manifold, improving most subjects but leaving a method-sensitive subset.

Conclusion: Subject-specific encoders serve as a learned alignment mechanism for EEG decoding. The main remaining bottleneck is head selection for unseen subjects.

Abstract: Cross-subject EEG decoding promises more training data, but it also exposes neural networks to strong inter-subject distribution shifts. We study whether task supervision and architecture alone can learn subject-aligned representations. We replace a shared EEG encoder with subject-specific encoders followed by a common classifier, and compare this hybrid model with standard EEGNet, AttentionBaseNet, and CTNet baselines with Euclidean Alignment (EA) on four motor-imagery datasets. EA improves shared encoders by recentering subject covariances, but the hybrid encoder largely internalises this role: validation-loss curves and latent-distance analyses change little when EA is removed. Subject-specific heads increase class distinctiveness and place each subject close to its own latent manifold, improving most subjects while leaving a method-sensitive subset. These results support subject-specific encoders as a learned alignment mechanism for EEG decoding and identify head selection for unseen subjects as the remaining bottleneck.

</details>
