TL;DR: Proposes Decision-Weighted Flow Matching (DW-FM), a regret-aligned training framework that reweights flow matching objectives using decision-sensitive information to improve downstream decision quality in stochastic optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard training of conditional generative models for scenario generation optimizes for uniform distributional fit, ignoring downstream decision regret. This causes an objective mismatch where errors in decision-sensitive regions are not prioritized.

Method: DW-FM reweights the velocity-regression objective of standard flow matching using decision-sensitive endpoint information. It connects downstream regret to pathwise velocity mismatch via a loss-induced decision discrepancy and adjoint transport argument, yielding a regret-aligned surrogate with theoretical guarantees.

Result: Evaluated on three CVaR-based contextual stochastic optimization benchmarks (synthetic portfolio, semi-real financial, traffic-CVaR), DW-FM demonstrates improved downstream regret compared to standard baselines.

Conclusion: Aligning generative model training with downstream decision regret is effective and theoretically grounded; DW-FM offers a simple yet principled approach to bridge the objective mismatch in scenario generation for stochastic optimization.

Abstract: Conditional generative models are increasingly used as scenario generators for stochastic optimization, but standard training objectives emphasize uniform distributional fit rather than the downstream decisions induced by generated scenarios. This creates an objective mismatch: errors in statistically common regions may have little effect on decision regret, whereas errors in decision-sensitive regions can substantially change the optimal action. We propose Decision-Weighted Flow Matching (DW-FM), a regret-aligned training framework that preserves the simplicity of standard flow matching while reweighting its velocity-regression objective using decision-sensitive endpoint information. Theoretically, we connect downstream regret to pathwise velocity mismatch through a loss-induced decision discrepancy and an adjoint transport argument, yielding an ideal regret-aligned surrogate and practical endpoint-weighted objectives with regret guarantees. Empirically, we demonstrate the effectiveness of DW-FM on three CVaR-based contextual stochastic optimization benchmarks spanning synthetic portfolio, semi-real financial, and traffic-CVaR tasks, where DW-FM improves downstream regret over standard baselines.

</details>


### [559] [Deep Q-Learning on Hölder Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16846)
*Qian Qi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper analyzes the operator-theoretic foundations of Q-learning in continuous-time stochastic control, showing that Bellman updates map bounded inputs into anisotropic regularity classes, motivating a tensor-product DeepONet architecture with explicit approximation bounds and a stiffness-complexity trade-off as the time step diminishes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the operator-theoretic core of Q-learning in continuous-time stochastic control with continuous states and actions, particularly isolating and analyzing the Bellman optimality target's regularity and approximation complexity.

Method: The authors study the Bellman optimality target under uniform ellipticity and Hölder-regular coefficients, proving that Bellman updates map bounded inputs into an anisotropic regularity class (smoothing state variables while maintaining Lipschitz action dependence). They propose a tensor-product DeepONet architecture exploiting this mixed regularity.

Result: Explicit approximation and resource bounds are derived, along with a stiffness-complexity trade-off as the time step δ→0. The analysis yields a compact family of Bellman iterates.

Conclusion: The work contributes to Q-learning theory at the level of Bellman target regularity and approximation in continuous stochastic control, though it does not claim a full convergence theorem for practical sampled Q-learning with exploration, replay, and SGD updates.

Abstract: We study the operator-theoretic core of Q-learning in continuous-time stochastic control with continuous states and actions. In value-based reinforcement learning, each Q-learning or DQN update is built from a Bellman optimality target; our analysis isolates this target in a diffusion setting and studies its regularity and approximation complexity. Under uniform ellipticity and Hölder-regular coefficients, we show that a Bellman update maps bounded inputs into an anisotropic regularity class, smoothing the state variable while leaving only Lipschitz dependence on the action variable. This yields a compact family of Bellman iterates and motivates a tensor-product DeepONet architecture adapted to the mixed regularity of the problem. We then derive explicit approximation and resource bounds, together with a stiffness--complexity trade-off as the time step $δ\to 0$. The resulting theory makes a direct contribution to Q-learning theory at the level of Bellman target regularity and approximation in continuous stochastic control. At the same time, we do not claim a full convergence theorem for practical sampled Q-learning with exploration, replay, and stochastic gradient updates.

</details>


### [560] [HawkesNest: A Multi-Axis Synthetic Benchmark for Spatiotemporal Pattern Complexity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16863)
*Yahya Aalaila,Sumantrak Mukherjee,Gerrit Großmann,Sebastian Vollmer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HawkesNest is a benchmark for evaluating spatiotemporal point process models using controlled complexity axes (entanglement, heterogeneity, interaction, topology) with deterministic indices, revealing failures in both Hawkes-family and neural models under specific structural stresses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing STPP evaluation relies on opaque real-world datasets where latent structure is unknown, making it hard to diagnose model failures.

Method: HawkesNest builds a multivariate Hawkes backbone with four complexity axes (space-time entanglement, background heterogeneity, cross-type interaction, domain topology), each quantified by a deterministic index. It varies axes independently while controlling global rate, stability, and budget.

Result: Indices are monotone and nearly orthogonal. Hawkes-family baselines degrade under joint heterogeneity-entanglement. AutoSTPP (neural model) is vulnerable to isolated space-time entanglement increases.

Conclusion: HawkesNest provides a principled, interpretable benchmark for diagnosing STPP model weaknesses under controlled complexity.

Abstract: Evaluation of spatiotemporal point process (STPP) models relies heavily on opaque real-world datasets, where latent generative structure is unknown and model failures are difficult to attribute. We introduce HawkesNest, a generator-aligned benchmark for controlled spatiotemporal pattern complexity built on a multivariate Hawkes backbone. HawkesNest defines four complexity axes: space--time entanglement, background heterogeneity, cross-type interaction, and domain topology. Each axis is associated with a deterministic index computed from the latent data-generating mechanism. By varying these axes while holding global rate, stability, and simulation budget fixed, HawkesNest enables diagnostic stress tests of STPP models under known structural difficulty. We verify that the indices are monotone and nearly orthogonal under controlled sweeps. We illustrate its use by showing that Hawkes-family baselines degrade under joint heterogeneity--entanglement complexity, even though they are structurally aligned with the Hawkes data-generating backbone. We further show that HawkesNest exposes neural-model sensitivity: AutoSTPP remains vulnerable under isolated increases in space--time entanglement. Code. Available at https://github.com/YahyaAalaila/HawkesNest

</details>


### [561] [Integrated Marketing Attribution: A Bayesian Framework for Privacy-Safe Granular Measurement Anchored in MMM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16878)
*Meghana R. Bhat,Ankit Umare,Utsav Aggarwal,Richard Vecsler,Arunkumar Mani,Karthik Nair,Chandhu Nair*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Integrated Marketing Attribution (IMA), a unified framework combining Marketing Mix Modeling (MMM) with Bayesian attribution models to deliver granular, privacy-safe campaign-level insights while maintaining consistency with MMM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches (MMM and MTA) produce fragmented insights: MMM is privacy-safe but too coarse for campaign optimization, while MTA is granular but unreliable under privacy restrictions. A unified solution is needed.

Method: IMA combines MMM with channel-specific Bayesian attribution models, using MMM-informed priors to derive campaign-level effects from aggregated data.

Result: IMA delivers granular, privacy-safe attribution while preserving consistency with MMM, enabling campaign-level optimization without user-level tracking.

Conclusion: IMA offers a privacy-compliant, unified attribution framework that bridges the gap between channel-level planning and campaign-level optimization.

Abstract: Retail marketing measurement increasingly requires granular campaign-level insights without relying on user-level tracking. However, the two dominant approaches, Marketing Mix Modeling (MMM) and Multi-Touch Attribution (MTA), often produce fragmented insights. MMM is privacy-safe and robust for channel-level planning but is too coarse for campaign optimization, while MTA provides granular attribution but has become less reliable under increasing privacy restrictions. We propose Integrated Marketing Attribution (IMA), a unified framework that combines MMM with channel specific Bayesian attribution models to derive campaign-level effects from aggregated data. By leveraging MMM-informed priors, IMA delivers granular, privacy-safe attribution while preserving consistency with MMM.

</details>


### [562] [Upper Bounds on the Generalization Error of Deep Learning Models via Local Robustness and Stability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16883)
*Abdul-Rauf Nuhu,Parham M. Kebria,Vahid Hemmati,Mahmoud N. Mahmoud,Edward Tunstel,Abdollah Homaifar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes tighter, non-vacuous generalization bounds for robust deep neural networks by scaling the robustness term according to stable/unstable samples within sub-regions of the input space, achieving substantially tighter upper bounds than existing methods on ImageNet.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing robustness-based generalization bounds are often vacuous (loose upper bounds greatly exceeding actual error rates) in practical settings; this vacuousness partly originates from the robustness term itself treating it as a global measure that ignores variation across different input sub-regions.

Method: The authors propose scaling the robustness term according to the number of stable and unstable samples within each sub-region of the input space, creating bounds that incorporate both data- and model-dependent factors.

Result: Experiments on ImageNet-trained models show the proposed bounds remain consistently non-vacuous and achieve the tightest estimates among existing methods, closely aligning with empirical performance across various robust deep neural networks.

Conclusion: Addressing the local variation of robustness across input sub-regions is a key factor for obtaining practically meaningful generalization bounds, and the proposed region-aware scaling approach significantly improves bound tightness.

Abstract: Generalization is a critical property of data-driven models, particularly deep learning models deployed in safety-critical applications. Robustness-based generalization bounds have gained attention as a principled way to link robustness properties to generalization performance, often in a data-dependent manner. However, most existing bounds suffer from vacuousness in practical settings, yielding loose upper bounds that greatly exceed the actual error rates and limiting their usefulness for real-world evaluation. While this issue is often attributed to the uncertainty term, a substantial part of the problem originates from the robustness term itself, particularly for the 0-1 loss. Existing approaches typically treat the robustness term as a global measure, ignoring its variation across different sub-regions of the input space. In this work, we propose a generalization bound that addresses this limitation by scaling the robustness term according to the number of stable and unstable samples within each sub-region. Our bounds incorporate both data- and model-dependent factors while maintaining practical relevance (yielding tighter upper bounds on true error). Experiments on models trained on the ImageNet dataset show that our bounds remain consistently non-vacuous and achieve the tightest estimates among existing methods, closely aligning with empirical performance across a range of robust deep neural networks.

</details>


### [563] [Beyond Weights and Gradients: A Taxonomy of Federated Learning Messages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16891)
*Alvaro Javier Vargas Guerrero,Xinguang Wang,Quang Manh Doan,Guy Nagels*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a formal definition of "federated messages" beyond traditional model weights, categorizing them into three types (model structures, statistical summaries, data-conditioned representations) and analyzing their trade-offs in computation, communication, and privacy based on a review of 202 publications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing definitions of Federated Learning fail to capture modern payloads like synthetic data and federated analytics; the paper aims to fill this gap with a formal mathematical framework.

Method: The authors propose a formal mathematical definition of federated messages, introduce a three-category taxonomy (model structures, statistical summaries, data-conditioned representations), and analyze them across computational costs, communication costs, and privacy risks. They also conducted a systematic review of 202 recent publications.

Result: The review of 202 publications reveals a significant shift since 2021 toward more diverse and specialized messaging paradigms, moving beyond standard deep learning updates.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a structured path for future research to optimize federated systems according to varying hardware constraints and security requirements.

Abstract: Federated Learning is rapidly evolving beyond the exchange of traditional model weights and gradients, yet existing definitions fail to capture the full scope of modern payloads like synthetic data and federated analytics. This paper addresses the gap by proposing a formal mathematical definition of a federated message that accounts for both utility and privacy. We introduce a taxonomy that organizes these exchanges into three categories: model structures, statistical summaries, and data-conditioned representations. By evaluating these groups based on computational demands, communication costs, and privacy risks, we provide a clearer understanding of the trade-offs involved in decentralized training. Our review of 202 recent publications highlights a significant shift since 2021 toward diverse messaging paradigms, signaling a move away from standard deep learning updates toward more specialized information sharing. This framework provides a structured path for future research to optimize federated systems for varying hardware and security requirements.

</details>


### [564] [Fantastic Pretraining Optimizers and Where to Find Them II: Hyperball Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16899)
*Kaiyue Wen,Xingyu Dang,Kaifeng Lyu,Tengyu Ma,Percy Liang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hyperball is an optimizer wrapper that fixes weight matrix norms and update norms to constants, improving upon weight decay by achieving 20-30% token speedup on Qwen3 models up to 1.2B parameters and improving learning rate transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Matrix-based optimizers like Muon show diminishing gains over AdamW at larger model/data scales when using standard constant decoupled weight decay; Hyperball addresses this issue.

Method: Hyperball wraps a base optimizer (Adam or Muon) by fixing the Frobenius norms of weight matrices and their corresponding optimizer updates to predetermined constants.

Result: On Qwen3 models up to 1.2B parameters, Muon + Hyperball achieves 20-30% token equivalent speedup over weight decay baselines, and improves learning rate transfer across widths and depths.

Conclusion: Fixing weight norms is more effective than weight decay for large-scale training, as weight decay indirectly controls angular learning rate via equilibrium norm, while Hyperball directly enforces it.

Abstract: Matrix based optimizers such as Muon can substantially speed up language model pretraining, but their gains over AdamW are observed to shrink as model size and data scale grow when using standard constant decoupled weight decay. We propose Hyperball, a simple optimizer wrapper that addresses this issue. Given a base optimizer such as Adam or Muon, Hyperball sets the Frobenius norms of weight matrices and their corresponding optimizer updates to fixed constants. On Qwen3 style models up to 1.2B parameters, Muon Hyperball achieves 20--30% token equivalent speedup over weight decay baselines. Hyperball also improves learning rate transfer across widths and depths compared to decoupled weight decay. This method is motivated by prior theory showing that training with weight decay leads to an equilibrium weight norm that only depends on the training hyperparameters. Through this mechanism, the weight decay then decides the angular learning rate, i.e. how fast the direction of the weight matrix changes.

</details>


### [565] [Factorized Neural Operators Decompose Dynamic and Persistent Responses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16900)
*Hao Tang,Yuechen Duan,Jiongyu Zhu,Zimeng Feng,Hao Li,Chao Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FaNO decomposes neural operator learning into two branches—equivariant (rapid dynamics) and invariant (persistent structures)—achieving better interpretability, accuracy, and cross-scale generalization in modeling multiscale physical systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Physical systems exhibit heterogeneous multiscale behaviors (fast dynamics + persistent structures), but existing neural operators rely on a single inductive bias, coupling distinct physical responses into a shared representation.

Method: FaNO (Factorized Neural Operators) decomposes spectral representations into equivariant dynamic responses and invariant persistent responses via a Unified Green's Function Framework.

Result: Improved prediction accuracy, parameter efficiency, and cross-scale generalization; consistent long-horizon rollout, cross-resolution extrapolation, and regime-shift robustness.

Conclusion: Moving beyond single-inductive-bias formulations toward factorized operator representations better reflects the heterogeneous organization of physical systems, accelerating reliable ML deployment in scientific computing.

Abstract: Physical systems often exhibit heterogeneous mechanisms, where rapidly evolving dynamics coexist with persistent structures. Capturing such multiscale physical behavior remains challenging for existing neural operators, which typically rely on single dominant inductive bias and therefore couple distinct physical responses into a shared representation. We introduce the Unified Green's Function Framework across domains and propose the Factorized Neural Operators (FaNO), which decompose spectral representations into equivariant dynamic responses and invariant persistent responses, leading to better interpretability and generalization. Mechanistically, we show that the two operator branches spontaneously specialize into distinct physical roles that remain consistent across scales and domains: the equivariant branch captures rapidly varying transient dynamics, whereas the invariant branch extracts coherent persistent structures. This factorized mechanism of FaNO improves prediction accuracy, parameter efficiency and cross-scale generalization across physical systems and domains. In particular, it maintains consistent predictions under long-horizon autoregressive rollout, cross-resolution extrapolation and physical-regime shifts. These findings suggest that scalable physical modeling may benefit from moving beyond single-inductive-bias formulations toward factorized operator representations that better reflect the heterogeneous organization of physical systems, accelerating the reliable deployment of machine learning for scientific computing and discovery.

</details>


### [566] [Demystifying Variance in Circuit Discovery of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16920)
*Frank Zhengqing Wu,Francesco Tonin,Volkan Cevher*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies and analyzes three types of variance in circuit discovery (resampling, rephrasing, sample-wise) and proposes CEAP, a method with theoretical guarantees that reduces resampling variance. It argues rephrasing variance reveals LLMs may be inherently hard to steer, and sample-wise variance is largely benign.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the roots of substantial variability in circuit discovery methods (resampling, rephrasing, sample-wise variance), which undermine the reliability of mechanistic interpretability findings.

Method: The authors propose CEAP, an improvement over EAP-IG with theoretical guarantees, and conduct systematic analysis of three variance types through controlled experiments with different data batches, rephrased prompts, and individual sample evaluations.

Result: CEAP substantially reduces resampling variance. Rephrasing variance stems from different templates activating different circuits, suggesting steering LLMs may be inherently difficult. Sparsity fails to solve this. Sample-wise variance is largely benign, with poor scores often due to definitional issues and selective contribution scaling.

Conclusion: Circuit discovery faces fundamental challenges: rephrasing variance may be irreducible due to template-dependent circuit activation, making comprehensive task circuits elusive. Better to focus on benign sample-wise variance and improve resampling stability via methods like CEAP.

Abstract: Circuit discovery is a key technique in mechanistic interpretability to pinpoint the model components that are crucial for performing a given task. Although the current state-of-the-art method (EAP-IG) performs well on the metric of (un)faithfulness, it suffers from substantial variability. This includes resampling variance, where the circuit changes when we probe with a new batch of data from the same distribution; rephrasing variance, where the discovered circuit shifts when the prompts are rephrased; and sample-wise variance, where a circuit with low population unfaithfulness exhibits large fluctuations in unfaithfulness across individual samples.
  This paper studies the roots of these variances. We demonstrate that CEAP, our new circuit discovery method that improves upon EAP-IG with a theoretical guarantee, can substantially lessen resampling variance. We further show that rephrasing variance arises because prompts with different templates tend to activate different circuits in the model. This leads us to argue that it may be challenging to find a comprehensive circuit that explains and controls the model's behavior on a task, which can be expressed in countless templates, suggesting that LLMs may be inherently hard to steer. We show that sparsity, which has been claimed to form more compact and interpretable task circuits, fails to solve this problem. Regarding sample-wise variance, we argue that it is largely benign: extremely poor unfaithfulness scores often stem from how unfaithfulness is defined, rather than from defects in the measured circuits. We show that the magnitude of unfaithfulness is affected by selective contribution scaling, a neural mechanism that accounts for the extremely poor scores sometimes observed.

</details>


### [567] [A Unified Causal-Origin Taxonomy of Distributional Shifts in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16933)
*Ardianto Wibowo,Paulo E Santos,Amer Baghdadi,Matthew Stephenson,Karl Sammut,Jean-Philippe Diguet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a unified causal-origin taxonomy for distributional shifts in RL, connecting ID/OOD generalization to non-stationary settings through a POMDP-based decomposition, and introduces an evaluation framework for measuring shift impact and adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL systems degrade under distributional shifts, but the formal relationship between ID/OOD generalization and non-stationary settings remains unclear, and prior work focuses on mitigation rather than the causal origin of shifts within agent-environment interactions.

Method: The authors transfer the classical dataset-shift principle from supervised learning to RL by reformulating distributional shift via the generative interaction process. Using a POMDP, they decompose the interaction into structural components (state distribution, observation process, policy, reward, transition dynamics, shifted-time boundary) and propose a taxonomy distinguishing internal (agent-driven) vs. external (environment-driven) shifts, along with explicit, implicit, and hybrid shifts based on the shifted-time boundary.

Result: A unified causal-origin taxonomy that characterizes sources of distributional shift in RL, relating ID/OOD generalization to non-stationary settings as structured changes in the underlying generative process, plus an evaluation framework with performance degradation and recovery metrics.

Conclusion: By grounding distributional shift in the causal-origin structure of RL, this work supports systematic analysis of robustness under distributional shift, offering a principled foundation for future research on RL generalization and adaptation.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) systems often degrade when operating conditions differ from those previously encountered, reflecting distributional shifts in the underlying data-generating process. Such shifts may occur between training and evaluation, as in In-Distribution (ID) and Out-of-Distribution (OOD) generalization, or within non-stationary settings where environment dynamics evolve over time. However, the formal relationship between these views remains unclear, and existing work mainly focuses on mitigation rather than the causal origin of shift within the agent-environment interaction. This work develops a unified causal-origin taxonomy that characterizes sources of distributional shift in RL and relates ID/OOD generalization to non-stationary settings. We transfer the classical dataset-shift principle from supervised learning to RL by reformulating distributional shift in terms of the generative interaction process. Using a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP), we decompose the interaction into structural components, including the state distribution, observation process, policy, reward, and transition dynamics, together with the shifted-time boundary. The proposed taxonomy distinguishes internal, agent-driven, and external, environment-driven, distributional shifts. The shifted-time boundary perspective further characterizes explicit, implicit, and hybrid shifts. This formulation unifies ID/OOD generalization and non-stationarity as structured changes in the underlying process. We also introduce an evaluation framework for measuring shift impact and adaptation through performance degradation and recovery metrics. By grounding distributional shift in the causal-origin structure of RL, this work supports systematic analysis of robustness under distributional shift.

</details>


### [568] [Scalable Circuit Learning for Interpreting Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16939)
*Naiyu Yin,Dennis Wei,Tian Gao,Amit Dhurandhar,Karthikeyan Natesan Ramamurthy,Yue Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CircuitLasso uses sparse linear regression to efficiently learn interpretable circuits over SAE features in LLMs, matching state-of-the-art accuracy at lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing circuit-learning methods are computationally prohibitive when applied to high-dimensional SAE features, and raw neuron polysemanticity hinders interpretability.

Method: Sparse linear regression (Lasso) to learn circuits over SAE features, replacing expensive intervention-based approaches.

Result: Matches structural accuracy of state-of-the-art intervention-based methods on benchmarks at a fraction of computational cost; reveals relationships among semantic SAE features.

Conclusion: CircuitLasso enables scalable and interpretable circuit discovery, with practical utility demonstrated via improved cost-performance trade-off on a domain-generalization task.

Abstract: A prominent research direction in mechanistic interpretability is learning sparse circuits over LLM components to reveal how they jointly produce model behavior. However, raw neurons are polysemantic, making learned circuits hard to interpret. Sparse autoencoder (SAE) features alleviate this, but their high dimensionality makes existing intervention-based circuit learning methods computationally prohibitive. We propose CircuitLasso, a scalable circuit-learning approach based on sparse linear regression. CircuitLasso recovers circuits whose structural accuracy matches that of state-of-the-art intervention-based methods on the benchmark data, at a fraction of the computational cost. For interpretability, CircuitLasso efficiently uncovers relationships among SAE features, showing how human-interpretable semantic features propagate through the model and influence its predictions. Finally, we validate the utility of our learned circuits by leveraging their insights to achieve comparable performance at substantially lower cost on a domain-generalization task.

</details>


### [569] [Phantoms and Disclosures: a Causal Framework for Auditing Synthetic Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16952)
*Kareem Amin,Rudrajit Das,Alessandro Epasto,Adel Javanmard,Dennis Kraft,Mónica Ribero,Sergei Vassilvitskii*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents a customizable auditing framework to detect and explain private data disclosures in synthetic data generated by LLMs, distinguishing true from phantom disclosures via statistical hypothesis testing using only synthetic outputs and a held-out set, requiring no model access or canary insertion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Synthetic data from LLMs risks memorizing and leaking private training data; existing auditing methods require model access, canary insertion, or reference models, which are computationally expensive and inflexible.

Method: The framework partitions data into training/holdout sets, applies statistical hypothesis testing to compare observed disclosures against privacy baselines (zero-learning or DP bounds), and distinguishes true disclosures from phantom disclosures using only synthetic outputs and a held-out control set.

Result: The framework functions as a membership inference attack, providing tighter empirical lower bounds on privacy leakage than prior data-based auditing methods, while requiring orders of magnitude fewer computational resources than shadow-model or canary-based alternatives.

Conclusion: This model-agnostic approach offers a lightweight, effective, and scalable method for auditing privacy leakage in any synthetic data generation mechanism without intrusive model access.

Abstract: The rapid adoption of generative AI and Large Language Models (LLMs) has spurred interest in synthetic data as a privacy-preserving alternative to sensitive real-world datasets. However, generating high-utility synthetic data often carries the risk of memorizing and regurgitating private information from the training corpus. In this work, we present a customizable empirical auditing framework designed to detect and explain such data disclosures. Our framework introduces a mechanism to distinguish between "true disclosures"-where the system directly reproduces a user's information-and "phantom disclosures''-where the system incidentally generates a user's data. By partitioning input data into training and holdout sets and applying rigorous statistical hypothesis testing, we determine if observed disclosures are consistent with strict privacy baselines, such as zero-learning or specific Differential Privacy (DP) bounds. Crucially, this approach requires no model access, no canary insertion, and no reference model training -only the synthetic output and a held-out control set. We demonstrate that this framework effectively functions as a membership inference attack, providing empirical lower bounds on privacy leakage that are tighter than prior data-based auditing methods. Our approach is model-agnostic, applies to any synthetic data generation mechanism, and requires orders of magnitude fewer computational resources than shadow-model or canary-based alternatives.

</details>


### [570] [Beyond the Smile: A Hybrid Convolutional VAE for Crypto Volatility Surfaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16961)
*Sadanand Singh,Allam Reddy,Manan Chopra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A convolutional variational autoencoder predicts crypto implied-volatility surfaces, achieving 8x error reduction over parametric smile re-fit alone, with joint BTC/ETH training improving both markets by 9-27%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current parametric methods (e.g., quadratic smile re-fit) fail at high masking rates and under structurally-correlated hole patterns, motivating a learned generative model that captures the shared manifold across cryptocurrency volatility surfaces.

Method: A convolutional variational autoencoder (CVAE) trained on 6,034 Binance hourly BTC/ETH surfaces (6x7 tenor-delta grid), combined with a quadratic smile re-fit via a deterministic per-tenor routing rule to form a hybrid predictor that ensures no arbitrage.

Result: Hybrid predictor achieves 0.83 vol points at 50% masking vs. 7.00 for smile re-fit (8x reduction); under structured holes, generative model maintains 1.5-1.9 vol points error while parametric fails at 9.6-13.1; joint training improves both markets by 9-27%.

Conclusion: The hybrid model is calendar- and butterfly-arbitrage-free at listed strikes, flags market events (ETF rally, flash crash) via reconstruction error, and all code/data is released for reproducibility.

Abstract: We present a convolutional variational autoencoder for cryptocurrency implied-volatility surfaces, together with a deployable predictor that combines it with a quadratic smile re-fit through a deterministic per-tenor routing rule. Trained on 6,034 fully-filled hourly Binance Options surfaces of BTC and ETH spanning May-October 2023 and parameterised on a common $6 \times 7$ tenor-delta grid, the model attains a hidden-cell surface-completion RMSE in the 0.94-1.56 vol-point range across both markets and mask rates 10-50%. The hybrid predictor attains 0.83 vol points at 50% masking against 7.00 for the smile re-fit alone, an eightfold reduction obtained at no additional inference cost. Under structurally-correlated hole patterns that emulate the withdrawal of an entire tenor of strikes, the smile re-fit incurs 9.6-13.1 vol points of error while the learned model remains at 1.5-1.9, isolating a regime in which the generative model is the only viable predictor. Joint training on BTC and ETH improves the in-distribution model on both markets by 9-27% relative to the better-performing single-symbol counterpart, indicating a substantially shared vol-surface manifold across the two largest cryptocurrencies over the observation window. The hybrid is calendar- and butterfly-arbitrage-free at the listed strikes, a property that the parametric smile re-fit alone fails at high mask rates. The per-snapshot reconstruction error of the trained model flags the late-October ETF-anticipation rally and the August $17$, $2023$ flash crash as elevated-error periods without supervision. All training and evaluation infrastructure is released to support reproducible follow-on work.

</details>


### [571] [Scalable Pairwise Kernel Learning with Stochastic Vec Trick](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16979)
*Napsu Karmitsa,Tapio Pahikkala,Antti Airola*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPaiK is a scalable kernel learning method for pairwise settings that uses a stochastic generalized vec trick (sGVT) to enable efficient large-scale training with pairwise kernels, achieving strong results on drug-target affinity datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pairwise learning is important but traditional kernel methods have high computational and memory costs that limit scalability to large datasets.

Method: Introduces SPaiK with stochastic generalized vec trick (sGVT), a stochastic extension of the sparse Kronecker product multiplication algorithm, enabling efficient kernel-based pairwise learning.

Result: Evaluated on seven real-world drug-target affinity datasets, outperforming or matching state-of-the-art pairwise learning methods.

Conclusion: SPaiK makes kernel-based pairwise learning scalable to previously unreachable dataset sizes while preserving expressive power.

Abstract: Pairwise learning is a specialized form of supervised learning that focuses on predicting outcomes for pairs of objects. In this work, we introduce SPaiK, a new scalable kernel learning method tailored for pairwise settings. Our approach preserves the expressive power of kernel methods while substantially reducing computational and memory requirements. The key innovation is the stochastic generalized vec trick (sGVT), a stochastic extension of the sparse Kronecker product multiplication algorithm, which enables efficient large-scale training with pairwise kernels. By incorporating sGVT, SPaiK makes it possible to apply kernel-based pairwise learning to datasets of a size previously out of reach. We evaluate the performance of SPaiK on seven real-world drug-target affinity datasets and compare the results with state-of-the-art methods in pairwise learning.

</details>


### [572] [Analytic Torsion and Spectral Gap Capture Persistent-Laplacian Performance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16990)
*Jernej Grlj,Aaron D. Lauda*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a compact spectral representation of persistent Laplacians using three invariants (Betti numbers, spectral gap, analytic torsion) that preserves essential predictive signal while reducing computational overhead, outperforming full spectrum in some cases across benchmark datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Persistent Laplacians offer rich geometric data representation but suffer from high dimensionality and varying length across filtration scales, hindering their use in learning tasks.

Method: The method distills persistent Laplacians into three mathematically grounded invariants: Betti numbers, spectral gap, and analytic torsion, creating a fixed-length representation.

Result: On MNIST, QM-3D, and SKEMPI WT datasets, the reduced feature space captures essential predictive signals of the full spectrum, sometimes outperforms it, and reduces computational overhead while avoiding noise from high-frequency eigenvalues.

Conclusion: The three invariants provide a principled, fixed-length interface between spectral geometry and topological learning, offering an efficient alternative to full persistent Laplacian spectra.

Abstract: While persistent Laplacians (PL) offer a richer geometric representation of data than persistent homology, utilizing their full eigenspectrum for learning tasks is often hampered by high dimensionality and the ``varying length'' problem across different filtration scales. We propose a compact spectral representation that distills the persistent Laplacian into three mathematically grounded invariants: Betti numbers, the spectral gap, and analytic torsion. Across benchmark datasets including MNIST, QM-3D, and SKEMPI WT, we demonstrate that this reduced feature space captures the essential predictive signal of the full spectrum, and in some cases outperforms it, while significantly reducing computational overhead and preventing the noise introduced by higher-frequency eigenvalues. Our results suggest that these invariants provide a principled, fixed-length interface between spectral geometry and topological learning.

</details>


### [573] [From Tokens to Policy: Causal and Interpretable Heterogeneous Treatment Effects Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17010)
*Riccardo Cadei,Frank Otchere,Nyasha Tirivayi,Gustavo Angeles Tagliaferro,Falco J. Bargagli-Stoffi,Francesco Locatello*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces NEXIS, a method for identifying heterogeneous treatment effects through latent interactor discovery using multi-modal pre-treatment data, with applications to anti-poverty programs in Africa.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing HTE methods trade off interpretability for expressivity and can produce spurious causal characterizations when active heterogeneity drivers are unmeasured; the authors aim to address this gap using richer pre-treatment data.

Method: NEXIS reframes HTE identification as a Markov-blanket discovery problem on a sufficient and aligned pre-treatment representation, using an iterative procedure with provable consistency for identifying latent interactors.

Result: Validated consistency through both theoretical proofs and empirical experiments; deployed on two anti-poverty programs in Africa with satellite imagery augmentation, yielding novel and prescriptive policy guidelines.

Conclusion: With more extensive pre-treatment measurements and scalable representations, oracle-level HTE causal characterization via latent interactors is achievable, enabling interpretable and actionable policy optimization.

Abstract: Heterogeneous Treatment Effect (HTE) identification is crucial to explain the impact of an intervention and optimize our policies accordingly. Existing approaches trade expressivity for interpretability, but, if some active heterogeneity drivers are unmeasured, methods at both ends of this spectrum allow for spurious HTE characterization with no causal reading. In this work, we focus on controlled experiments and argue that an oracle HTE causal characterization via the latent interactors is now within reach, thanks to (i) more extensive pre-treatment measurements, i.e., multi-modal and multi-view, and (ii) scalable representations with minimal human supervision. We then re-frame HTE identification as a Markov-blanket discovery problem on a sufficient and aligned pre-treatment representation, and introduce Neural EXposure Interaction Search (NEXIS), an iterative procedure with provable and empirically validated consistent selection. We deploy NEXIS on two anti-poverty programs in Africa, augmenting each with satellite imagery capturing previously unmeasured environmental effect modifiers, leading to novel, interpretable and prescriptive guidelines to optimize the programs' next iterations.

</details>


### [574] [Filtered Conformal Ellipsoids for Graph-Native Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17014)
*Yannick Limmer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes filtered conformal ellipsoids for multivariate time series prediction, combining state-space filtering with conformal calibration to create prediction sets that adapt to cross-coordinate dependence while maintaining coverage guarantees under dependent data conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To control joint prediction events for multivariate time series while adapting to cross-coordinate dependence, without relying on Gaussian tail probabilities for coverage guarantees.

Method: Uses a frozen state-space filter to emit one-step predictive mean and covariance, applies split-conformal calibration to Mahalanobis scores, and provides theoretical analysis of contraction properties under a stable Bayes Gaussian-projection filter with observability conditions.

Result: Achieves Chebyshev-type and Bernstein-type approximate coverage bounds under dependence; near-oracle log-volume comparison under Gaussian realisability; empirical results show learned filter outperforms static-covariance baselines on graph-native traffic benchmarks.

Conclusion: The filtered conformal ellipsoid framework effectively combines learned predictive covariance with distribution-free coverage calibration, though performance varies across different dataset types and scales.

Abstract: Joint prediction sets for multivariate time series should control a single event while adapting to cross-coordinate dependence. We study filtered conformal ellipsoids: a frozen state-space filter emits a one-step predictive mean and covariance, and split-conformal calibration is applied to the resulting Mahalanobis scores. The filter is used to choose the ellipsoid shape; conformal calibration chooses the scalar radius, so the construction benefits from a learned predictive covariance without relying on Gaussian tail probabilities for coverage. The main difficulty is that filtered scores are dependent and learned recurrent filters need not contract in their raw hidden state; we therefore analyse contraction in an observable predictive-law quotient that identifies hidden states producing the same future sequence of emitted Gaussian laws. Under a stable Bayes Gaussian-projection filter, covariance bounds, and a finite-horizon observability Fisher condition, small excess Gaussian negative log-likelihood implies contraction of the learned emitted laws. Combined with a threshold-autocovariance envelope this yields a Chebyshev-type approximate coverage bound for filtered split-conformal prediction under dependence; a sharper Bernstein-type bound requires an additional geometric-mixing concentration assumption. Under Gaussian oracle realisability we also obtain a near-oracle log-volume comparison within the class of conditionally valid Gaussian ellipsoid rules. We instantiate the framework with a GCN-GRU filter with diagonal-plus-low-rank covariance. On moderate-size graph-native traffic benchmarks (METRLA-$20$ and PEMSBAY-$50$), the learned filter gives sharper at-target ellipsoids than static-covariance and non-filter baselines; at full-graph scale and on non-graph-native datasets, factor and copula baselines can be stronger.

</details>


### [575] [ExpRL: Exploratory RL for LLM Mid-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17024)
*Violet Xiang,Amrith Setlur,Chase Blagden,Nick Haber,Aviral Kumar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ExpRL is a method that uses human-written reference solutions as reward scaffolds (not imitation targets) for RL-based mid-training of LLMs, providing dense rewards via LLM judges to reinforce partial progress and productive reasoning behaviors, outperforming SFT and sparse-reward RL on math reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse reward RL for LLM reasoning depends on base model coverage, and mid-training with curated traces requires manual specification of primitive skills like decomposition or verification. It's unclear if such primitive coverage suffices for harder problems requiring combined strategies, motivating a more automated approach.

Method: ExpRL uses human-written question-answer data as reward scaffolds: references are hidden from the policy and used to construct grading rubrics. An LLM judge compares sampled reasoning traces against the reference solution to assign dense rewards (outcome-level or process-level), reinforcing partial progress and productive behaviors that sparse final-answer rewards miss.

Result: ExpRL yields stronger RL priming than SFT, sparse-reward GRPO, and self-distillation on challenging math reasoning tasks. It provides a better initialization for subsequent sparse-reward RL, and mixed-domain experiments suggest it extends beyond math-only settings.

Conclusion: Using reference solutions as reward scaffolds rather than imitation targets enables more effective automated mid-training for LLM reasoning, improving performance on hard problems and generalizing to mixed domains.

Abstract: Sparse reward reinforcement learning (RL) has become a standard tool for improving LLM reasoning, but its success depends critically on the coverage present in the base model. In practice, models are often primed for RL through \emph{mid-training} on curated reasoning traces that teach useful primitive skills such as decomposition, verification, or self-correction. Although effective, this strategy requires manually specifying what the model should learn, and it remains unclear whether such primitive coverage is enough for much harder problems, which require combining these skills into broader solution strategies. We study a more automated approach: \emph{RL-based mid-training} using large corpora of human-written question-answer data. Rather than treating reference solutions as targets to imitate, our method, ExpRL, uses them as \emph{reward scaffolds}: references are hidden from the policy and used only to construct problem-specific grading rubrics for judging on-policy reasoning traces. The policy samples from the original problem prompt, while an LLM judge compares the sampled reasoning trace against the reference solution and assigns outcome-level or process-level dense rewards. This lets ExpRL reinforce partial progress, useful intermediate reductions, and productive reasoning behaviors that sparse final-answer rewards often fail to upweight. On challenging math reasoning tasks, ExpRL yields stronger RL priming than SFT, sparse-reward GRPO, and self-distillation, and provides a better initialization for subsequent sparse-reward RL. Additional mixed-domain experiments further suggest that ExpRL can extend beyond the original math-only setting.

</details>


### [576] [HAMON: Passive Optical Sequence Mixing for Long-Horizon Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17028)
*Alper Yıldırım*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HAMON is a passive diffractive optical forecasting system that uses phase masks and free-space diffraction to perform time-series prediction without digital sequence-mixing layers, achieving competitive or superior results on several benchmarks while offering a concrete path toward optical hardware for forecasting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent evidence suggests that long-horizon time-series forecasting may not require the complex representations of transformers, and the core operator is often low-complexity and approximately linear, raising the question of whether it needs to be implemented as learned digital temporal mixing.

Method: HAMON encodes historical values onto an optical aperture, keeps future positions dark, and uses cascaded trainable phase masks with free-space diffraction to shape the forecast directly in the output field via a single passive optical propagation pass.

Result: Outperforms strongest digital baselines on ETTm2 (all horizons) and ETTh2 (all but longest), with up to 14% MSE improvement; competitive on Weather; trails on Traffic and Electricity. Ablations confirm forecasts come from the optical field, not a digital head.

Conclusion: Because the passive core uses standard Fourier optics, HAMON defines a concrete target for optical hardware and passive physical sequence mixing.

Abstract: Simple linear and frequency-domain models remain surprisingly competitive in long-horizon time-series forecasting, and recent mechanistic evidence suggests that standard forecasting benchmarks may not require the dense superposed representations that make transformers powerful in other domains. This raises a substrate-level question: if the core forecasting operator is often low-complexity and approximately linear, does it need to be implemented as learned digital temporal mixing? We introduce HAMON, a passive diffractive optical forecasting core in which historical values are encoded onto an optical aperture, future positions are left dark, and cascaded trainable phase masks with free-space diffraction shape the forecast directly in the output field. At inference, prediction is performed by a single passive optical propagation pass with no trainable digital sequence-mixing layer.
  Across standard benchmarks, HAMON outperforms the strongest digital baselines considered on ETTm2 at all horizons and on ETTh2 at all but the longest horizon, improving MSE by up to 14\% and doing so consistently across horizons rather than at isolated points. It is competitive on Weather and trails the strongest baselines on the remaining ETT settings and on the high-channel-count Traffic and Electricity datasets. Phase encoding, intensity-compatible readout, and phase-scrambling ablations, together with a TorchOptics cross-simulator check, indicate that the forecasts arise from the data-bearing optical field rather than from a digital forecasting head. Because the passive core uses standard Fourier optics, HAMON defines a concrete target for optical hardware and for passive physical sequence mixing.

</details>


### [577] [Your Privacy My Cloak: Backdoor Attacks on Differentially Private Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17035)
*Xiaolin Li,Ning Wang,Ninghui Li,Wenhai Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RING attack exploits differential privacy's masking effect in federated learning, achieving 90.3% attack success against state-of-the-art defenses by using adversarial perturbations to hide backdoor signals within DP noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the common assumption that differential privacy inherently enhances robustness against backdoor attacks in federated learning, revealing a fundamental tension where DP's noise masks malicious updates from detection.

Method: RING collaboratively crafts adversarial perturbations that allow compromised clients to reconstruct strong backdoor signals during aggregation while evading anomaly detection, operating as a perturbation layer agnostic to underlying backdoor techniques.

Result: 90.3% average attack success rate across four datasets and six defenses under moderate privacy budgets, with up to 26.08x improvement over baseline strategies.

Conclusion: DP-FL has a fundamental security gap: mitigating RING attacks incurs significant utility trade-offs, suggesting that DP alone does not guarantee security against backdoor attacks.

Abstract: Prior research suggests that differential privacy (DP) inherently enhances the robustness of federated learning (FL) against backdoor attacks. In this paper, we challenge this assumption. Through an empirical analysis of two baseline attack strategies, we uncover a fundamental tension in DP-FL: while bypassing DP allows state-of-the-art defenses to detect and filter malicious updates, complying with DP inadvertently masks their distinguishing statistical characteristics. Consequently, existing defenses become ineffective as DP reduces the raw backdoor signal. Building on this masking effect, we propose RING, a novel attack that explicitly exploits DP to conceal malicious contributions while maximizing attack impact. By collaboratively crafting adversarial perturbations, compromised clients reconstruct a strong backdoor signal during aggregation without triggering anomaly detection. RING operates as a perturbation layer that is agnostic to the underlying backdoor technique, making it broadly applicable and composable with existing attacks -- a property that significantly amplifies the threat it poses to DP-FL. Extensive evaluations across four image and text datasets under non-iid distributions show that RING achieves an average attack success rate of 90.3% against six state-of-the-art defenses under a moderate privacy budget, an improvement of up to 26.08x over baseline strategies. Finally, we evaluate potential countermeasures and find that mitigating this threat incurs significant utility trade-offs, exposing a fundamental security gap in the deployment of differentially private FL.

</details>


### [578] [Exact Posterior Score Estimation for Solving Linear Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17048)
*Abbas Mammadov,Ozgur Kara,Kaan Oktay,Iskander Azangulov,Adil Kaan Akan,Hyungjin Chung,James Matthew Rehg,Yee Whye Teh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Exact Posterior Score (EPS), a method that derives the exact posterior score in closed form for linear Gaussian inverse problems, enabling posterior sampling by simply using a denoiser trained with a modified objective that preserves the standard denoising structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for solving linear inverse problems with diffusion/flow-based priors either use approximate measurement-matching corrections on a fixed pretrained denoiser (which is inaccurate) or train conditional restoration models that lose the denoising structure. There is a need for a method that preserves the denoising prior structure while exactly handling the posterior score.

Method: The authors derive the exact posterior score in closed form for linear Gaussian inverse problems under general Gaussian interpolants. They show posterior sampling reduces to a denoising problem at an operator-dependent shifted pivot with anisotropic noise covariance. This leads to EPS, a denoising training objective that preserves the standard input/output structure of pretraining, enabling training from scratch or fine-tuning from a pretrained denoiser.

Result: EPS outperforms both training-free and training-based baselines on fidelity, perceptual, and distributional metrics across five linear inverse problems on FFHQ and ImageNet, while using roughly an order of magnitude fewer denoiser evaluations than gradient-based posterior samplers.

Conclusion: Exact Posterior Score provides a principled way to convert posterior sampling into a denoising problem, eliminating the need for likelihood gradients or projections at inference while achieving superior performance with significantly fewer computational resources.

Abstract: Diffusion and flow-based models learn powerful data priors by training a denoiser to reverse Gaussian corruption. To use this prior to solve a linear inverse problem, one needs to sample from the posterior, but the score that the prior provides is the unconditional score, not the posterior score. Existing methods either steer a fixed pretrained denoiser with approximate measurement-matching corrections, or train a conditional restoration model that abandons the denoising structure of the prior. We derive the exact posterior score in closed form for linear Gaussian inverse problems under general Gaussian interpolants, and show that posterior sampling reduces to a denoising problem at an operator-dependent shifted pivot under an anisotropic noise covariance. We turn this identity into Exact Posterior Score (EPS), a denoising training objective that preserves the input/output structure of standard pretraining and can therefore be trained from scratch or fine-tuned from a pretrained denoiser. At inference, EPS uses the same sampler as the underlying backbone, with no likelihood gradients or projections. We evaluate EPS on five linear inverse problems across FFHQ and ImageNet, where it outperforms training-free and training-based baselines on fidelity, perceptual, and distributional metrics, while using roughly an order of magnitude fewer denoiser evaluations than gradient-based posterior samplers.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [579] [Sparse Solution Trade-offs in GMP DPD: A Least Squares Thresholding Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14907)
*Ellison Murray*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes using Least Squares Thresholding (LST) for digital pre-distortion to linearize satellite communication power amplifiers, achieving significant complexity reduction (2.77x) compared to Orthogonal Matching Pursuit while maintaining similar linearization performance under SWaP constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Power amplifiers in satellite systems introduce nonlinear distortion, and full-complexity digital pre-distortion solutions are impractical due to strict size, weight, and power (SWaP) constraints.

Method: The authors propose Least Squares Thresholding (LST) and compare it against Orthogonal Matching Pursuit (OMP) and Matching Pursuit for digital pre-distortion.

Result: LST achieves a 2.77x complexity reduction while maintaining near-identical linearization performance to OMP.

Conclusion: LST is a more efficient alternative for PA linearization in SWaP-constrained satellite communication systems.

Abstract: Power amplifiers (PAs) in satellite communication systems introduce nonlinear distortion, degrading spectral fidelity. Digital pre-distortion linearizes the PA response, but full-complexity solutions are prohibitive under strict size, weight, and power (SWaP) constraints. We propose the use of Least Squares Thresholding (LST) and compare it against Orthogonal Matching Pursuit (OMP) and Matching Pursuit. LST achieves a 2.77x complexity reduction while maintaining near-identical linearization performance to OMP.

</details>


### [580] [Moving Target SAR Imaging Using Planar Arrays And Multidimensional Chinese Remainder Theorem (MD-CRT)--Part I: A General Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14910)
*Guangpu Guo,Xiang-Gen Xia*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This two-part paper proposes a multi-subarray planar antenna framework for SAR moving target imaging, using 2D-DFT processing and multidimensional Chinese remainder theorem to resolve ambiguity and enlarge the unambiguous range for joint estimation of target height and motion-induced cross-range shift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate localization of moving targets over 3D terrain requires joint estimation of motion-induced cross-range shift and target height, which is challenging due to ambiguity issues when using a single planar array.

Method: The authors unify cross-range shift and target height into a 2D parameter vector, develop a 2D-DFT matrix modulus framework, derive optimal modulus construction, and propose a multi-subarray framework using MD-CRT (including robust MD-CRT for noisy settings) to resolve ambiguity and recover the true parameter vector.

Result: The proposed multi-subarray framework enlarges the unambiguous range compared to a single planar array, with established sufficient conditions and explicit reconstruction error bounds for both noiseless and noisy settings.

Conclusion: The multi-subarray approach with MD-CRT effectively resolves ambiguity in SAR moving target imaging, providing robust parameter estimation with enlarged unambiguous range.

Abstract: In this two-part paper, we investigate synthetic aperture radar (SAR) moving target imaging using planar antenna arrays. For a target moving over a three-dimensional terrain, its accurate localization requires the joint estimation of the motion-induced cross-range shift and the target height. In Part I of this two-part paper, starting from the planar array imaging geometry and the corresponding signal model, we show that these two quantities can be unified into a two-dimensional parameter vector and represented, after two-dimensional discrete Fourier transform (2D-DFT) processing across the planar array, through a natural vector remainder formulation. We first develop a general 2D-DFT matrix modulus framework and show that, in the two-dimensional setting, the associated 2D-DFT matrix modulus affects the propagation of vector remainder errors. Under a fixed array geometry and antenna number constraint, we derive an optimal construction of this matrix modulus and adopt it in the subsequent analysis. Under this construction, a single planar array provides only a folded estimate when the true parameter vector lies outside its unambiguous range. To resolve this ambiguity, we develop a multi-subarray framework in which multiple planar subarrays generate multiple vector remainders with different matrix moduli, and the desired parameter vector is recovered through the multidimensional Chinese remainder theorem (MD-CRT). To account for practical errors introduced by 2D-DFT quantization and additive noise, we further introduce an approximate 2D-DFT peak model for non-integer frequency vectors, incorporate robust MD-CRT, and establish sufficient conditions together with explicit reconstruction error bounds for both noiseless and noisy settings. Numerical results verify that the proposed multi-subarray framework enlarges the unambiguous range compared with a single planar array.

</details>


### [581] [Moving Target SAR Imaging Using Planar Arrays And Multidimensional Chinese Remainder Theorem (MD-CRT)--Part II: Two Subarray Designs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14911)
*Guangpu Guo,Xiang-Gen Xia*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This Part II paper investigates two-subarray designs for moving target SAR imaging using planar antenna arrays and MD-CRT, focusing on a common-scaling design that improves robustness and reconstruction error bounds compared to conventional separated schemes and separable array geometries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve moving target SAR imaging performance by analyzing two-subarray planar array designs, achieving better robustness and tighter error bounds than conventional one-dimensional CRT-based and separable array approaches under fixed platform constraints.

Method: Proposes a common-scaling two-subarray design under planar antenna arrays with MD-CRT, where subarrays share the same scaling factor, enabling ambiguity resolution on a common integer frequency vector; compares with conventional separated scheme and various planar array geometries.

Result: The common-scaling design preserves unambiguous range, weakens sufficient conditions for robust recovery, and yields tighter reconstruction error bounds. Non-separable planar array geometries outperform separable ones with comparable antenna numbers.

Conclusion: The proposed planar array framework with common-scaling design outperforms conventional one-dimensional schemes, and non-separable geometries provide better robustness, demonstrating that array geometry matters beyond just antenna count.

Abstract: Based on the framework proposed in Part I, the Part II of this two-part paper investigates two-subarray designs for moving target SAR imaging using planar antenna arrays and the multidimensional Chinese remainder theorem (MD-CRT). In this Part II, we focus on the performance analysis and the detailed two planar subarray designs. In particular, we study a common-scaling two-subarray design, under which the two subarrays share the same scaling factor in the MD-CRT formulation. Under this design, ambiguity resolution can be performed on a common integer frequency vector. As a result, the same unambiguous range as in the general two-subarray framework in Part~I is preserved, while the sufficient conditions for robust recovery become weaker and the corresponding reconstruction error bounds become tighter. Within this common-scaling design, we compare the proposed planar array framework with a conventional separated scheme, in which the motion-induced cross-range shift is recovered by a one-dimensional CRT-based method and the target height is estimated by cross-track interferometric processing. Under the same platform size and minimum antenna spacing constraints, the proposed planar array framework can realize the common-scaling design, whereas the corresponding one-dimensional non-uniform linear array scheme does not admit such a design. With this design, the planar array framework leads to a weaker sufficient condition for robust recovery and thus performs better in moving target imaging. We also compare several planar array designs under fixed platform size and minimum antenna spacing. The analysis shows that recovery performance depends not only on the number of antennas but also on the array geometry. In particular, non-separable planar array geometries can provide better robustness than separable ones when their antenna numbers are comparable.

</details>


### [582] [Prototype-Aware Fundamental Electromagnetic Limits on Wavefront Synthesis with Programmable Metasurfaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14944)
*Philipp del Hougne*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper derives prototype-aware, electromagnetically consistent bounds on wavefront synthesis for programmable metasurfaces using multiport network theory, semidefinite relaxation, and experimentally estimated parameters. The bounds account for mutual coupling, binary programmability, and lossy loads, and are validated on RIS systems with up to 100 1-bit elements across different radio environments, enabling identification of unattainable performance regions and certification of near-optimality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fundamental limits on wavefront synthesis for real-world programmable metasurface prototypes remain largely unknown, hindering performance assessment and optimization.

Method: Combines multiport network theory (MNT), experimentally estimated proxy MNT parameters, and semidefinite relaxation to derive bounds, accounting for mutual coupling, binary programmability, and lossy tunable loads. Evaluates bounds via threshold sweeps on four experimental RIS-based MIMO systems.

Result: Bounds yield practical insights including unattainable performance regions and close-to-optimality certification of optimization outcomes. Comparisons with discrete-optimization benchmarks show bounds can be closely approached, indicating tightness.

Conclusion: This work contributes to prototype-aware electromagnetic information theory for reconfigurable wave systems, applicable broadly to lumped-element-reconfigurable systems like dynamic metasurface antennas.

Abstract: Wavefront synthesis is a central objective in many applications of programmable metasurfaces (PMs), ranging from electromagnetic holography and computational imaging to massive backscatter communications. Yet, fundamental limits on the ability of a given real-world PM prototype to synthesize a desired output wavefront remain largely unknown. Here, we derive prototype-aware and electromagnetically consistent bounds on target-wavefront synthesis in reconfigurable MIMO wave systems whose programmability stems from tunable lumped elements. Our approach combines multiport network theory (MNT), experimentally estimated proxy MNT parameters, and semidefinite relaxation. We account for relevant practical aspects of typical real-world PMs, such as mutual coupling, binary programmability, and lossy tunable loads. We derive bounds on strength-agnostic wavefront-synthesis fidelity, shape-agnostic target-mode strength, and the strength--fidelity Pareto frontier using two complementary threshold sweeps. We evaluate these bounds for four experimental MIMO systems whose transfer functions are parametrized by a reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS), involving up to 100 1-bit-programmable elements and radio environments ranging from rich scattering to free space. Our bounds yield practical insights such as the identification of unattainable performance regions and the close-to-optimality certification of certain optimization outcomes. Comparisons with feasible discrete-optimization benchmarks show that the bounds can often be closely approached in practice, indicating tightness. While demonstrated with a RIS prototype, our methodology applies broadly to lumped-element-reconfigurable wave systems, including dynamic metasurface antennas. Altogether, this work contributes to the development of a prototype-aware electromagnetic information theory for reconfigurable wave systems.

</details>


### [583] [Symbol Error Analysis of Linear Receivers in Terahertz Channels under Channel-Noise Dependence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15006)
*Almutasem Bellah Enad,Jihad Fahs,Hadi Sarieddeen,Hakim Jemaa,Tareq Y. Al-Naffouri*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper develops a framework to analyze ZF and MMSE linear detectors under THz channel conditions, considering Rayleigh, α-μ, and mixture-gamma fading models. It derives SER expressions accounting for channel-noise correlation, showing that at SER=10⁻³, 70% correlation causes ~6.5 dB SNR degradation, with MMSE offering better numerical stability despite mismatched detection loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how the correlation between channel and additive noise (due to hardware impairments) impacts the performance of linear detectors in terahertz communication systems, which is critical for THz-band receiver design.

Method: Semi-analytical, approximate, and asymptotic SER expressions are derived for ZF and MMSE detectors under Rayleigh, α-μ (indoor THz), and mixture-gamma (outdoor THz) fading. Channel-noise correlation is characterized via statistical approaches and copula-based methods.

Result: At SER=10⁻³, 70% correlation causes ~6.5 dB SNR degradation; mismatched MMSE incurs an additional ~1 dB loss over ZF. MMSE provides better numerical stability under severe fading where ZF suffers noise amplification.

Conclusion: Channel-noise dependence significantly impacts THz receiver performance. Despite some SNR loss under correlated conditions, MMSE detectors are preferable for severe fading scenarios due to improved numerical stability.

Abstract: This paper develops a comprehensive framework for the performance analysis of linear detectors, namely zero-forcing (ZF) and minimum mean-square error (MMSE), under diverse terahertz (THz) channel conditions. Three fading models are considered: Rayleigh fading, the $α$--$μ$ distribution for indoor THz environments, and the mixture-gamma (MG) distribution for outdoor THz scenarios. Semi-analytical, approximate, and asymptotic expressions for the symbol error rate (SER) are derived, explicitly incorporating the correlation between the channel and the additive noise arising from hardware impairments. This correlation is characterized using both statistical approaches and copula-based methods to effectively capture complex dependency structures. The theoretical findings are validated through simulations, demonstrating strong agreement with the derived expressions and confirming the accuracy and robustness of the proposed framework. The results demonstrate the significant impact of channel--noise dependence on THz-band receiver performance and verify the expected performance degradation of biased MMSE receivers in point-to-point links employing higher-order quadrature amplitude modulation. Specifically, at a target SER of $10^{-3}$, a 70\% correlation results in approximately a 6.5~dB degradation in the effective signal-to-noise ratio, with mismatched MMSE detection incurring an additional 1~dB loss compared to ZF. Nonetheless, MMSE offers enhanced numerical stability under severe channel fading conditions, where channel inversion causes noise amplification.

</details>


### [584] [Interpretable and Frugal Learning Systems Employing Multiresolution Pyramids and Volterra Kernels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15011)
*Kishore Kumar Tarafdar*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This thesis develops signal-theoretic deep learning systems combining wavelets, filter banks, Volterra kernels, and neural computation graphs into differentiable operator modules for inverse modeling, classification, and segmentation across atmospheric, audio, texture, and medical imaging domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning models lack explicit signal structure and are difficult to inspect, motivating the development of model-based, signal-theoretic learning systems that keep representations tied to interpretable signal concepts like kernels, subbands, and transform coefficients rather than opaque feature channels.

Method: The approach combines multiresolution analysis, wavelets, filter banks, Volterra systems, and neural computation graphs into differentiable operator modules trainable by backpropagation, including D-dimensional convolution layers, multirate sampling, Volterra-kernel layers, rational polynomial cascade heads, stability-constrained IIR filters, wavelet banks, and digital shearlet layers.

Result: The modules are composed into task-specific architectures including InVeRt (microwave radiometric inversion), multiresolution filter-bank encoders with Volterra heads for classification, WaveletViT/ShearViT (subband transformer blocks for WaveNETR/ShearNETR segmentation), and MRILong (3D brain MRI segmentation for ischemic stroke analysis).

Conclusion: Signal-theoretic differentiable operator modules enable interpretable, task-driven deep learning systems that maintain structural transparency while achieving strong performance across diverse signal processing and medical imaging problems.

Abstract: Deep learning models are widely used to process multidimensional signals such as time series, images, and volumetric medical images, but their learned representations often lack explicit signal structure and are difficult to inspect. This thesis develops model-based, signal-theoretic learning systems guided by data and task objectives. It combines multiresolution analysis, wavelets and filter banks, multirate representations, nonlinear Volterra systems, and neural computation graphs. Scale, directional geometry, memory, and nonlinear input-output interactions are represented as differentiable operator modules trainable by backpropagation. The design keeps intermediate variables tied to kernels, subbands, recursions, and transform-domain coefficients rather than only to opaque feature channels. The thesis formulates fast GPU-compatible D-dimensional convolution layers, multirate sampling layers, Volterra-kernel layers in natural and wavelet coefficient domains, rational polynomial cascade heads, stability-constrained multidimensional IIR filters, wavelet banks, and digital shearlet layers with learnable gains. These modules are composed into task-specific architectures for inverse modeling, classification, and segmentation across atmospheric, audio, texture, and medical-imaging problems. In microwave radiometric inversion, InVeRt retrieves vertical temperature and humidity profiles from microwave brightness temperature observations using learnable Volterra kernels in wavelet bases. Multiresolution filter-bank encoders with Volterra heads are used for efficient classification. WaveletViT and ShearViT serve as subband transformer blocks for WaveNETR and ShearNETR, direction-sensitive segmenters for image and MRI segmentation. MRILong deploys trained 3D T1-weighted brain MRI segmenter checkpoints for automatic segmentation and longitudinal analysis of ischemic stroke MRI volumes.

</details>


### [585] [Vertical Sub-THz Channel Characterization: Sounder Implementation and Initial Measurements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15114)
