*Ali Al-Ameri,Xuesong Cai,Juan Sanchez,Aleksei Fedorov,Buon Kiong Lau,Ove Edfors,Fredrik Tufvesson*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents measurements of indoor vertical ceiling-to-ground sub-THz channels (136-144 GHz) using a VNA-based sounder with virtual antenna arrays. Measurements in an office, laboratory, and ventilation room show strong LOS dominance but clear environmental differences. RMS delay spreads (0.44-2.57 ns) are smaller than horizontal sub-THz channels, though second-order reflections and spatial non-stationarity across large apertures are observed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To characterize vertical ceiling-to-ground sub-THz channels for ceiling-mounted radio-unit deployments in future distributed indoor networks, as existing work has focused primarily on horizontal channels.

Method: VNA-based channel sounder with mechanically scanned planar virtual antenna array (VAA) at the receiver. Conducted SISO, small-array SIMO (2×2 cm URA), and large-array SIMO (30×1 cm URA) measurements in three indoor environments (office, laboratory, ventilation room) at 136-144 GHz.

Result: Vertical links are dominated by LOS component. RMS delay spreads: 0.55-1.74 ns (small-array) and 0.44-2.57 ns (large-array), smaller than horizontal sub-THz channels. Stronger multipath in ventilation room due to metallic ceiling/structures. Repeatable second-order reflections observed. Large-aperture measurements reveal spatial non-stationarity.

Conclusion: Ceiling materials, obstructions, and aperture-dependent variations are important factors in vertical sub-THz channel modeling; conventional free-space assumptions are insufficient.

Abstract: We present a measurement-based characterization of indoor vertical ceiling-to-ground sub-THz channels in the 136-144 GHz band, motivated by ceiling-mounted radio-unit deployments for future distributed indoor networks. The measurements are performed using a vector network analyzer (VNA)-based channel sounder with a mechanically scanned planar virtual antenna array (VAA) at the receiver, enabling single-input single-output (SISO), small-array single-input multiple-output (SIMO), and large-array SIMO measurements in three indoor environments: an office, a laboratory, and a ventilation room. The small-array and large-array SIMO measurements synthesize 2 X 2 cm and 30 X 1 cm uniform rectangular arrays (URAs), respectively. The results show that the vertical links are generally dominated by a strong Line-of-Sight (LOS) component close to the ceiling-to-ground direction, but with clear environmental differences. The office and laboratory exhibit relatively limited delay dispersion, whereas the ventilation room shows stronger delayed multipath due to its corrugated metallic ceiling and surrounding metallic structures. The measured root mean square (RMS) delay spreads are 0.55-1.74 ns for the small-array measurements and 0.44-2.57 ns for the large-array measurements, smaller than those reported in several horizontal indoor sub-THz measurement campaigns at similar frequencies. However, the channel is not purely free-space. Repeatable second-order reflections involving the receiver table, ceiling, transmitter structure, and ceiling-mounted objects are observed in all environments. The large-array measurements further reveal spatial non-stationarity along the 30 cm aperture, with several multipath components visible only over limited parts of the array. These results show that ceiling materials, obstructions, and aperture-dependent variations matter in vertical sub-THz channel modeling.

</details>


### [586] [On the Feasibility of Human Presence Detection Using Ceiling-Mounted Sub-THz Channel Sounding: Conference Room Measurement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15147)
*Ali Al-Ameri,Xuesong Cai,Aleksei Fedorov,Fredrik Tufvesson*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper experimentally investigates human presence detection using a ceiling-mounted sub-THz channel sounder (134–146 GHz) in an indoor conference room. Results show detection performance depends heavily on target position, antenna beamwidth, and sensitivity to small environmental changes. The directive antenna offers strong but spatially selective detection, while the wide-beam configuration shows more ambiguous results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the feasibility of human presence detection using ceiling-mounted sub-THz sensing for future integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems.

Method: Wideband channel measurements at 134–146 GHz using a VNA with sub-THz extenders, comparing empty-room, human-present, and water-filled mannequin scenarios across five positions, with two antenna beamwidth configurations (directive horn vs. open-waveguide).

Result: Human detection is strongly dependent on target position and antenna beamwidth. With the directive antenna, human presence was detectable only at favorable positions (P1, P2). With the wide-beam antenna, human-present measurements produced lower delay-domain variation than empty-room baselines, while the mannequin produced higher values. Small furniture displacement caused non-negligible baseline variations.

Conclusion: Ceiling-mounted sub-THz sensing can detect human presence but is highly spatially selective and sensitive to environmental changes, which must be accounted for in ISAC system design.

Abstract: This paper presents a measurement-based investigation on the feasibility of human presence detection using a ceiling-mounted sub-THz channel sounder operating from 134 to 146~GHz. Wideband channel measurements were conducted in an indoor conference room under empty-room, human-present, and water-filled mannequin scenarios across five spatial positions. The measurements were performed using a vector network analyzer combined with sub-THz frequency extenders. Two antenna beamwidth configurations were used, one with a highly directive horn antenna on the transmitter side and one with a less directive, open-waveguide transmitter. The measured channel responses were transformed into calibrated power delay profiles and analyzed using normalized channel variation metrics in the delay domain. The results show that human detection is strongly dependent on target position relative to the ceiling-mounted transmitter and receiver as well as on antenna beamwidth. Furthermore, repeated empty-room measurements reveal that small environmental changes, such as slight furniture displacement, introduce non-negligible channel variations that must be considered when evaluating detection performance. In the wide-beam open-waveguide configuration, the human-present measurements produced lower values of the delay-domain variation metric than the repeated empty-room baseline, whereas the water-filled mannequin produced values at or above this baseline across all positions. With the directive transmitter, the human response exceeded the baseline significantly but only at favorable positions, especially P1 and P2, showing that the sensing response remains spatially selective. These findings provide experimental insight into the capabilities and limitations of ceiling-mounted sub-THz sensing for future integrated sensing and communication systems.

</details>


### [587] [Surrogate-Assisted Framework for SI-Compliant Interconnect Design Optimization Using the Earth Mover's Distance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15234)
*Emre Ecik,Werner John,Julian Withöft,Ralf Brüning,Jürgen Götze*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes a deterministic, interpretable framework for SI-compliant PCB design using neural surrogate predictors, a decision tree for quality filtering, and Earth Mover's Distance for ranking designs against ideal signals, demonstrated on DDR3 fly-by waveforms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To replace conventional black-box surrogate-based optimization methods with an explainable, deterministic alternative for signal integrity (SI) compliance in PCB design, avoiding iterative stochastic search and inverse modeling.

Method: (1) Neural surrogate models predict waveform features from design parameters. (2) A decision tree acts as a quality gate filtering SI-compliant waveforms. (3) Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) ranks valid designs by similarity to an ideal reference signal.

Result: The framework enables deterministic identification of admissible parameter regions and transparent prioritization of physically superior solutions, demonstrated on large-scale DDR3 fly-by waveform simulations.

Conclusion: The approach provides an explainable, computationally efficient alternative to conventional optimization strategies for AI-based PCB development.

Abstract: This work presents a deterministic, machine-assisted framework for SI-compliant PCB design based on the Earth Mover's Distance (EMD). In contrast to conventional surrogate-based optimization methods that rely on iterative black-box search procedures, the proposed approach follows an interpretable, sequential evaluation strategy. Neural surrogate models are first used to efficiently predict waveform describing features from topology-dependent design parameters. A decision tree then acts as a physically motivated quality gate that identifies SI-compliant waveforms according to predefined SI criteria. Within the resulting valid solution space, the Earth Mover's Distance is employed as a similarity metric to rank candidate designs according to their proximity to an ideal reference signal. This enables not only the deterministic identification of admissible parameter regions but also a transparent prioritization of physically superior solutions without inverse modeling or stochastic search procedures. The methodology is demonstrated using a large-scale set of simulated DDR3 fly-by waveforms. By combining surrogate prediction, interpretable classification, and EMD-based waveform evaluation, the framework provides an explainable and computationally efficient alternative to conventional optimization strategies for supporting PCB development with AI-based methods.

</details>


### [588] [CAP: Towards PPG Universal Representation Learning with Patient-level Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15284)
*Chenyang He,Xinyi Shao,Shun Huang,Bosong Huang,Daoqiang Zhang,Ming Jing,Cheng Ding*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: CAP proposes a pretraining method that aligns PPG signals with patient-level clinical semantics via cross-modal contrastive learning on a large-scale PPG-EHR dataset, achieving significant improvements across multiple downstream tasks, especially respiratory rate prediction (+87.6% relative improvement).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PPG representation learning focuses on signal-level objectives and overlooks patient-level health context, limiting generalization to complex clinical tasks and heterogeneous patient populations.

Method: Constructs a large-scale paired PPG-EHR multimodal dataset by distilling fragmented medical histories into cohesive patient-level records. Then proposes Clinical Anchored Pretraining (CAP) that performs cross-modal contrastive alignment to anchor PPG representations to patient-level clinical semantics during pretraining.

Result: CAP consistently outperforms strong baselines on four downstream tasks, with up to +87.6% relative improvement on respiratory rate prediction and an average +26.7% relative improvement across all tasks.

Conclusion: Incorporating patient-level clinical context into PPG pretraining through cross-modal alignment significantly improves representation quality, robustness, and transferability for downstream clinical tasks.

Abstract: Photoplethysmography (PPG) plays a central role in wearable health monitoring and clinical decision support. Yet existing approaches to universal PPG representation learning largely focus on signal-level objectives and often overlook patient-level health context, which limits generalization to complex clinical tasks and heterogeneous cohorts. To address this gap, we construct a large-scale paired PPG-EHR multimodal dataset by distilling fragmented medical histories and clinical records into cohesive, patient-level electronic health records (EHR). Building on this resource, we propose Clinical Anchored Pretraining for PPG (CAP). During pretraining, CAP performs cross-modal contrastive alignment that anchors PPG representations to patient-level clinical semantics, guiding the encoder beyond waveform fitting toward modeling consistency in a patient's overall physiological state. During downstream adaptation, the pretrained PPG encoder provides clinically grounded representations that strengthen inductive bias and improve robustness and transferability. Experiments demonstrate that CAP consistently outperforms strong baselines on four diverse downstream tasks. CAP achieves a particularly large gain on respiratory rate prediction (up to +87.6% relative improvement over the state-of-the-art baseline) and delivers an average relative +26.7% across all tasks. We further enhance the interpretability of our approach through comprehensive analyses, including ablations and multiple complementary visualizations of the learned representations. The code for our experiments is available at: https://github.com/gody123gody/CAP .

</details>


### [589] [Generalized likelihood ratio test for magnetic anomaly detection: a geometrical approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15343)
*C. Chenevas-Paule,S. Zozor,L. -L. Rouve,O. J. J. Michel,O. Pinaud,R. Kukla*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper improves magnetic anomaly detection by constraining GLRT signal parameters to a semi-algebraic space (a cone for dipole models), outperforming state-of-the-art methods and approaching optimal receiver performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current GLRT-based detection methods use parametric models expressed in functional bases, but they do not enforce that estimated signals satisfy physical constraints (e.g., the dipole model equations). This leads to suboptimal detection performance.

Method: The signal is identified as belonging to a semi-algebraic space (a cone for the dipole model). The GLRT is then constrained so that estimated signal parameters are forced to lie within this physically valid subspace.

Result: Numerical simulations show the proposed constrained GLRT outperforms conventional state-of-the-art methods and achieves performance close to the optimal clear-seeing receiver.

Conclusion: Exploiting the semi-algebraic structure of the signal subspace significantly improves magnetic anomaly detection by ensuring physical consistency of the estimated parameters.

Abstract: State-of-the-art approaches to magnetic anomaly detection rely on the generalized likelihood ratio test (GLRT). These approaches are based on the formulation of a parametric model of the source to be detected, expressed in a suitable functional basis. One of the primary objectives of this study is to demonstrate that, for a given measurement configuration, the signal is constrained to evolve within a restricted subset of the space generated by these functional bases. The parametric representation of the signal is identified as a semi-algebraic space which, for the dipole model used in this article, turns out to be a cone outside of which the estimated signal does not satisfy the physical equations. Thus, a second objective is to exploit this property to constrain the signal parameters in the GLRT to belong to the semi-algebraic space, in order to improve detection performance. The performance gain of the proposed algorithm is compared to the one of conventional approaches; numerical simulations show that the proposed approach not only outperforms state-of-the-art methods but can even provide results close to those of the clear-seeing (optimal) receiver.

</details>


### [590] [Universal adaptive beamforming: A Bayesian approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15477)
*Diego A. Cuji,Andrew C. Singer,John R. Buck*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A Bayesian universal beamforming framework for adaptive array processing in dynamic underwater acoustic environments, using posterior probability updates over steering hypotheses, achieving reliable communication with zero bit errors in MACE experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address adaptive array processing in underwater acoustic environments with unknown, time-varying propagation geometry, where classical beamformers struggle due to dynamic and unpredictable channel conditions.

Method: Discretizes the angular domain into steering hypotheses, recursively computes posterior probabilities via exponential-weights recursion with hypothesis-dependent evidence metrics, and extends to broadband via frequency-domain synthesis and adaptive equalization with branch-specific error updates.

Result: MACE experimental data shows reliable communication with low overhead, low data detection mean-squared error, and zero observed bit errors.

Conclusion: The proposed Bayesian universal beamforming framework effectively handles dynamic underwater acoustic environments, naturally connects to classical beamformers, and demonstrates strong empirical performance.

Abstract: We present a Bayesian universal beamforming framework for adaptive array processing in dynamic underwater acoustic environments with unknown and time-varying propagation geometry. Motivated by ideas from universal prediction and estimation, the proposed approach discretizes the angular domain into a finite set of steering hypotheses and recursively computes posterior probabilities over competing spatial models using observation-dependent likelihood functions. For Gaussian observation models, the posterior update reduces to an exponential-weights recursion driven by hypothesis-dependent beamformer evidence metrics. The resulting framework performs soft spatial inference and adaptive beamforming by continuously redistributing posterior probability across competing steering hypotheses while forming posterior-weighted combinations of branch outputs. The formulation naturally connects to classical adaptive beamformers including matched filtering and minimum mean-square error (MMSE) beamforming.
  In addition, the framework is extended toward broadband underwater acoustic communication receivers through frequency-domain beamformer synthesis and adaptive equalization. Posterior probabilities are updated according to branch-specific equalization errors, enabling joint spatial-temporal adaptation under multipath propagation, Doppler-induced distortions, and time-varying channel conditions. Experimental results using MACE data demonstrate reliable communication performance with low overhead, low data detection mean-squared error, and zero observed bit errors.

</details>


### [591] [Deep Learning-Based Automatic Modulation Classification Using GRU Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15742)
*Osman Tokluoglu,Emin Keresteci*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a GRU-based deep learning framework for automatic modulation classification (AMC) that directly processes I/Q signals without handcrafted features. Evaluated on BPSK, QPSK, and 16PSK under AWGN, the model achieves 55.3% accuracy at -10 dB SNR and 98.5% at 15 dB SNR, demonstrating strong performance especially at moderate to high SNR levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable reliable modulation classification in non-cooperative wireless scenarios where no prior signal knowledge is available, and to move beyond traditional handcrafted feature extraction methods by leveraging deep learning for automatic feature learning.

Method: A GRU-based deep neural network operating directly on raw in-phase and quadrature (I/Q) signal representations for temporal feature learning in a data-driven manner.

Result: Overall classification accuracy improves from 55.3% (-10 dB SNR) to 98.5% (15 dB SNR). Strong performance at moderate/high SNR levels with reasonable accuracy maintained under low SNR conditions.

Conclusion: GRU-based architectures offer a promising and computationally efficient solution for AMC tasks. This work serves as an initial step toward extensions to fading channels, more modulation schemes, and real-time hardware implementations.

Abstract: Automatic modulation classification (AMC) plays a critical role in modern wireless communication systems, particularly in non-cooperative scenarios where prior knowledge of the transmitted signal is unavailable. In this study, a gated recurrent unit (GRU)-based deep learning framework is investigated for the classification of digital modulation schemes by exploiting the temporal characteristics of received signals. The proposed approach operates directly on in-phase and quadrature (I/Q) signal representations and aims to learn discriminative features in a data-driven manner without relying on handcrafted feature extraction. The performance of the proposed model is evaluated for BPSK, QPSK, and 16PSK modulation schemes under additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) channel conditions across a wide range of signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) levels. The obtained results demonstrate that the GRU-based model achieves reliable classification performance, with overall accuracy improving from 55.3% at -10 dB SNR to 98.5% at 15 dB SNR. In particular, the model exhibits strong performance at moderate and high SNR levels, while maintaining reasonable accuracy even under challenging low SNR conditions. These findings suggest that GRU-based architectures provide a promising and computationally efficient solution for modulation classification tasks. The presented results represent an initial step toward more comprehensive studies, including extensions to fading channel environments, additional modulation schemes, and real-time implementations using hardware platforms.

</details>


### [592] [Uncertainty-Aware Haptic Signal Estimation for Reliable and Resource Efficient Tactile Internet](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15775)
*Georgios Kokkinis,Alexandros Iosifidis,Qi Zhang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The A2HAP framework uses a probabilistic neural network (VarxHAP) with hierarchical gating to reduce packet rates by 45% and resource block consumption by 25% in multi-user haptic communication, increasing user capacity by 20% while maintaining teleoperation reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High sampling rates for transparent haptic control cause severe congestion in multi-user wireless environments, limiting the scalability of the Tactile Internet.

Method: A2HAP integrates VarxHAP (a probabilistic neural network for joint force and uncertainty estimation) with an error-resilient controller, using a hierarchical gating architecture that dynamically adjusts transmission thresholds based on model confidence vs. reliability trade-offs.

Result: Packet rates suppressed by up to 45% during peak traffic, 25% average reduction in resource block consumption, and 20% increase in user capacity over state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: The A2HAP framework effectively balances communication efficiency and haptic reliability, enabling scalable multi-user teleoperation in the Tactile Internet.

Abstract: The Tactile Internet aims to enable real-time remote haptic interaction; however, the high sampling rates required for transparency in haptic control often lead to severe congestion in multi-user wireless environments. This paper proposes the Agile AI-empowered Haptic (A2HAP) framework, which integrates VarxHAP, a novel probabilistic neural network for joint force and uncertainty estimation, with an error-resilient controller. By employing a hierarchical gating architecture, the system dynamically adapts transmission thresholds to balance model confidence against reliability targets. Simulation results demonstrate that A2HAP suppresses packet rates by up to 45% during peak traffic and reduces resource block consumption by 25% on average. Consequently, the framework supports a 20% increase in user capacity compared to state-of-the-art methods while maintaining the ultra-reliability required for stable teleoperation.

</details>


### [593] [Early Anomaly-Onset Detection based on Wigner--Ville Distribution Slice Spectra: A Transmission-Grid Test Case](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15856)
*Eduardo Jr Piedad,Eduardo Prieto-Araujo,Oriol Gomis-Bellmunt*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper evaluates WVDS spectra for online anomaly detection in power grid waveforms, finding that WVDS-BND offers the lowest false alarm rate (0.69%) at the cost of lower sensitivity compared to FFT-BND.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Operational disturbance monitoring requires sequential real-time decisions from arriving waveform windows, not post-event analysis. False alarms are costly, so a selective, low false-alarm method is needed.

Method: Full-vector WVDS spectra represent 128-sample voltage windows as 128-dimensional slices, preserving bilinear Wigner-Ville structure. WVDS-BND is compared against FFT-BND, raw-window autoencoders, FFT autoencoders, and WVDS autoencoders under identical thresholding and three-window persistence rules. A synthetic autoencoder-clustering teacher filters RTE fault records.

Result: FFT-BND achieves highest sensitivity; WVDS-BND achieves the lowest false-alarm operating point (0.69% pre-onset false alarms). Autoencoder comparison follows the same pattern: WVDS lowers false alarms but misses more anomalies than FFT.

Conclusion: Preserved WVD cross-term information enables selective representation for online grid-waveform anomaly monitoring when minimizing false alarms is prioritized over catching every anomaly.

Abstract: Operational disturbance monitoring in power networks requires decisions to be made from waveform windows as they arrive, rather than from completed records after the event. This study evaluates full-vector Wigner--Ville Distribution Slice (WVDS) spectra for sequential anomaly-onset detection in high-voltage grid-voltage waveforms. The approach keeps the bilinear midpoint interaction structure of the Wigner--Ville distribution and represents each 128-sample voltage window by a 128-dimensional slice spectrum, avoiding manually selected fault-frequency markers. WVDS is used with a baseline-normalized deviation (BND) score and is compared against the BND of Fast Fourier Transform (FFT-BND), raw-window autoencoders, FFT autoencoders, and WVDS autoencoders under the same thresholding and three-window persistence rule. A synthetic autoencoder--clustering teacher is used to select RTE fault records that start from an initially normal region and then transition to anomalous behavior. On the filtered test set, FFT-BND achieves the highest sensitivity, whereas WVDS-BND provides the lowest false-alarm operating point, reducing record-level pre-onset false alarms to 0.69%. The autoencoder comparison follows the same selectivity pattern: WVDS reconstruction decreases false alarms relative to FFT reconstruction but misses more examples. The results indicate that preserved WVD cross-term information can form a selective representation for online grid-waveform anomaly monitoring when false alarms are costly.

</details>


### [594] [An auscultation location specific study on the relationship between expiratory-to-inspiratory acoustic patterns and spirometric airflow limitation across age and gender in asthmatic patients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15973)
*Dheeraj Harish Kumar,Sanjana M C,Keerthi Priya,K V Nikhath Khanam,Uma Maheshwari Krishnaswamy,Prasanta Kumar Ghosh*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This study of 141 participants found that the expiratory-to-inspiratory (E/I) spectral power ratio of respiratory sounds correlates with FEV1/FVC, particularly in the 100-200 Hz and 200-400 Hz bands, with lower posterior lung sites showing stronger associations. Age and gender differences were observed in correlation patterns across lung regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether respiratory sound recordings at posterior chest sites can serve as a non-invasive proxy for spirometric assessment (FEV1/FVC) of asthma-related airflow limitation, potentially offering an alternative to conventional spirometry.

Method: 141 participants aged 20-60 years underwent spirometry and respiratory sound recording at four posterior sites (Left Lower, Left Upper, Right Upper, Right Lower). The E/I spectral power ratio was computed across frequency subbands and correlated with FEV1/FVC using Spearman correlation. Subgroup analyses were performed by age and gender.

Result: Significant correlations between E/I ratio and FEV1/FVC were found in the 100-200 Hz and 200-400 Hz bands. Lower posterior sites showed stronger associations. Younger adults had stronger correlations at Left Lower; older adults at Left Upper. Males showed stronger Left Lower correlations; females showed stronger Left Upper correlations.

Conclusion: Respiratory sound analysis, particularly at specific frequency bands and posterior lung sites, correlates with spirometric measures of airflow limitation. Age and gender influence the optimal recording location, suggesting that personalized site selection may improve acoustic-based assessment of asthma.

Abstract: Asthma causes expiratory airflow limitation and is clinically assessed using spirometry, which provides the FEV1/FVC ratio representing the proportion of air exhaled in the first second relative to total forced vital capacity. Prior studies suggest that respiratory sounds recorded at posterior sites (Left Lower, Left Upper, Right Upper, Right Lower) reflect regional airflow patterns. In this study, we investigate the relationship between the expiratory-to-inspiratory (E/I) spectral power ratio and FEV1/FVC in 141 participants aged 20-60 years using Spearman correlation across frequency subbands. The 100-200 Hz and 200-400 Hz bands showed significant correlations. Overall, lower posterior sites showed stronger associations; younger adults showed stronger correlations at the Left Lower site, whereas older adults showed stronger correlations at the Left Upper site. Gender-stratified analysis showed stronger Left Lower correlations in males and stronger Left Upper correlations in females.

</details>


### [595] [Data Scarcity in Gas Load Profiling: Generalized Proxy-Guided Load and Temporal Disaggregation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16069)
*Lucas Krome,Mahtab Aboufazeli,Soosan Beheshti,Bala Venkatesh*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Generalized Proxy-Guided Load and Temporal Disaggregation framework to reconstruct high-resolution thermal load profiles from scarce, low-frequency utility data, validated on 11 multi-unit residential buildings over 18 months with a 6.37% MSPE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Electrification of heating systems is critical for building decarbonization, but sustainable strategy development is hindered by a lack of granular thermal load profiles due to extremely scarce, irregular, and low-frequency data.

Method: A four-stage framework: (i) Generalized Occupancy Proxy Extraction via weather normalization from hourly electricity data; (ii) Unified Segmentation and Normalized Pooling to handle sparse billing data; (iii) Unified Baseload Parameter Estimation with local calibration; (iv) Component-Wise Temporal Disaggregation to reconstruct baseload and heating profiles.

Result: Validated on 11 multi-unit residential buildings over 18 months, achieving a mean squared percentage error (MSPE) of 6.37% for reconstructed total gas consumption.

Conclusion: The framework effectively bridges the resolution gap, transforming low-frequency utility data into high-fidelity training sets suitable for scalable, data-driven decarbonization modeling.

Abstract: The electrification of heating systems is a critical pathway for decarbonizing the building sector; however, the development of sustainable strategies is often hindered by the lack of granular thermal load profiles. The nature of this problem is such that available data are extremely scarce, irregular, and low-frequency, rendering conventional data-hungry machine learning approaches impractical or unreliable. High-resolution gas metering is rarely available, as it falls outside standard utility business requirements. To bridge this gap and enable data-driven AI analytics under severe data limitations, this paper presents the Generalized Proxy-Guided Load and Temporal Disaggregation framework. Validated on a dataset of 11 multi-unit residential buildings over 18 months, the framework achieves a mean squared percentage error (MSPE) of 6.37% for reconstructed total gas consumption. The methodology operates through a four-stage process: (i) Generalized Occupancy Proxy Extraction via weather normalization to isolate behavioral signals from hourly electricity data; (ii) Unified Segmentation and Normalized Pooling to mitigate the statistical limitations of sparse billing data; (iii) Unified Baseload Parameter Estimation enhanced by local calibration to ensure building-specific accuracy; and (iv) Component-Wise Temporal Disaggregation to reconstruct distinct baseload and heating profiles. Overall, the proposed framework effectively bridges the resolution gap, transforming low-frequency utility data into high-fidelity training sets suitable for scalable, data-driven decarbonization modeling.

</details>


### [596] [Neural Network-Enabled Codebook Design for Phased Array Calibration with Arbitrary Array Sizes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16132)
*Xiangyu Chen,Hao Sun,Weiming Wang,Yuanan Liu,Wei Fan*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A neural network-enabled codebook generation method for mmWave phased array calibration that achieves low condition numbers with minimum measurements, validated on a 26-GHz AiP array.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Array calibration is critical for accurate beamforming in mmWave AiP phased arrays, requiring OTA calibration in ALL-ON mode with high efficiency (short measurement time) and reliability (robustness via low condition number).

Method: A neural network-enabled codebook generation method that works for arbitrary array sizes, producing codebooks with low condition numbers using the minimum number of measurements.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art calibration approaches. Validated on a 26-GHz AiP phased array with superior array calibration accuracy and beamforming quality.

Conclusion: The proposed neural network-based codebook generation method achieves efficient and robust phased array calibration with minimal measurements, validated experimentally.

Abstract: Array calibration is critical to achieving accurate beamforming in millimeter-wave (mmWave) antenna-in-package (AiP) phased arrays, where over-the-air (OTA) calibration in ALL-ON mode is a standard requirement. For practical calibration measurements, two core metrics are paramount: efficiency (defined by measurement time) and reliability (robustness, governed by the condition number of the phased array calibration codebook). In this work, we propose a neural network-enabled codebook generation method for phased array calibration compatible with arrays of arbitrary sizes. Codebooks generated via the proposed method achieve low condition numbers while requiring the minimum number of measurements, outperforming state-of-the-art calibration approaches. Practical measurements on a 26-GHz AiP phased array validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method, with superior performance in both array calibration accuracy and beamforming quality.

</details>


### [597] [Context-Aware Markov VAE for CSI Compression in Wireless Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16607)
*Efstathios Chatziloizos,Konstantinos Vandikas,Aneta Vulgarakis Feljan,Zheng Chen,Nikolaos Pappas*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes a k-memory Markov variational autoencoder (k-MMVAE) for efficient CSI compression in massive MIMO FDD systems by explicitly modeling temporal dynamics in latent space, outperforming memoryless and weakly sequential baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing memoryless compression models fail to exploit strong temporal correlations in time-varying massive MIMO channels, and simple temporal extensions incorporate multiple observations without explicitly modeling latent dynamics.

Method: A context-aware compression framework using a k-memory Markov variational autoencoder (k-MMVAE) that captures CSI evolution via finite temporal windows and Markov-structured latent dynamics with finite memory.

Result: The proposed k-MMVAE improves target CSI reconstruction over memoryless and weakly sequential baselines, especially at low and moderate compression rates.

Conclusion: Explicit latent temporal modeling is effective for CSI compression under limited feedback constraints in FDD massive MIMO systems.

Abstract: This paper considers neural channel state information (CSI) compression for time-varying massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) channels in frequency division duplex (FDD) systems with limited feedback resources. The main challenge lies in obtaining a compact and efficient representation of the CSI given that it exhibits strong temporal correlation across successive snapshots. Existing memoryless compression models do not exploit this property, while simple temporal extensions often incorporate multiple observations without explicitly modeling the latent dynamics. We propose a context-aware compression framework based on a k-memory Markov variational autoencoder (k-MMVAE), which uses a finite temporal window to capture the evolution of CSI in the latent space. The model introduces Markov-structured latent dynamics with finite memory, enabling efficient use of temporal dependencies for compression. Simulation results show that the proposed approach improves target CSI reconstruction performance compared to memoryless and weakly sequential baselines, particularly at low and moderate compression rates. These results suggest that explicit latent temporal modeling can provide an effective mechanism for CSI compression under limited feedback constraints.

</details>


### [598] [PALM: Single-Station Super-Resolved Small-Scale Radio-Map Localization by Path-Atom Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16157)
*Xiucheng Wang,Nan Cheng*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: PALM uses ray-traced radio maps and atomic matching to achieve 1.7m median localization from a single base station, significantly outperforming classical received-power methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve localization from a single base station without synchronized anchors, moving beyond coarse received-power radio maps that ignore fine-grained path structure.

Method: Builds a small-scale ray-traced radio map, then matches coarse angle-delay observations via exact marginal likelihood scoring of path atoms, with careful handling of clock nuisance on absolute delay.

Result: 1.7m median error on DeepMIMO; 34-62% reduction in 90th-percentile error vs. classical RM; 7m median with single snapshot.

Conclusion: Cell identification is the bottleneck in single-station localization; PALM's atomic matching with exact marginal likelihood outperforms posterior-scaled surrogates, especially when strong paths exist.

Abstract: Localizing from a single base station is a longstanding goal, since it removes the synchronized anchors that geometric methods require. A radio map (RM) answers a position query from this one-station survey, yet classical RMs store coarse received power and match it by correlation, ignoring the small-scale path structure a ray tracer provides. We instead build a small-scale RM and show that cell identification, rather than candidate generation, is its information-limited bottleneck. We propose path-atom localization by matching (PALM), which super-resolves a coarse angle-delay observation into scored atoms and matches them to a ray-traced RM by an exact marginal likelihood. The score marginalizes atom reality inside the logarithm, and we prove that the common posterior-scaled surrogate is a Jensen lower bound whose deficit grows with the number of strong paths. We match on the absolute delay axis under a clock nuisance, since relative delays jump across shadowing boundaries, and we prove a unit-gradient law, a capped miss cost, a minimum-mean-square local centroid, and finite-sample conformal coverage. On the real DeepMIMO campus scenario, PALM localizes to a 1.7 meter median from a single base station, cuts the ninetieth-percentile error of received-power RM matching by 34 to 62 percent, and halves the single-snapshot median to 7 meters.

</details>


### [599] [Synergizing Global Pattern Learning and Time Order Characterization in Mobile Channel Prediction: An RWKV-Based Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16170)
*Zili Wang,Zirui Chen,Ridong Li,Zhaoyang Zhang,Zhaohui Yang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a task-driven channel prediction network using RWKV as the backbone, which synergizes global pattern learning and temporal order encoding for improved wireless channel prediction. The method achieves 1.84~4.29 dB gains in NMSE and 2.6~10.5 percentage point gains in cosine similarity over LSTM and Transformer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Channel prediction is important for reducing pilot overhead and mitigating channel aging in wireless communications. Existing data-driven methods (LSTM, Transformer) lack task-specific designs for effectively capturing both global patterns and temporal causality in channel dynamics.

Method: The paper employs RWKV as the network backbone, adapted for channel prediction by: (1) using its deep interleaved learning architecture to extract global patterns and shareable features across adjacent time slots, and (2) leveraging its unique exponential decay mechanism to structurally encode temporal order and characterize causality in channel dynamics.

Result: The proposed method outperforms LSTM and Transformer baselines, achieving 1.84~4.29 dB improvement in normalized mean squared error (NMSE) and 2.6~10.5 percentage point improvement in cosine correlation.

Conclusion: Task-driven design significantly improves learning efficiency for channel prediction, with RWKV-based architecture proving superior to conventional data-driven approaches in this domain.

Abstract: Owing to the potential to reduce pilot overhead and mitigate channel aging, channel prediction is emerging as an important research topic in wireless communications. Meanwhile, deep neural networks are becoming a foundational technology for high-precision prediction thanks to their excellent non-linear representation capabilities. In this paper, we conceive a task-driven prediction network, which aims to deeply synergize the following two functions: learning global patterns for shareable features across adjacent time slots and structurally encoding time order to characterize the inherent causality within the channel dynamics. To implement channel prediction accuracy, we employ RWKV (receptance weighted key value) as network backbone and adapt it to the task's specific characteristics, utilizing its deep interleaved learning architecture to extract global patterns across multiple channel samples and leveraging its unique exponential decay to characterize temporal order. These task-driven unique designs significantly improve the learning efficiency of prediction network. Comprehensive experimental evaluations demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method over current data-driven methods, such as long short-term memory and Transformer, in the channel prediction task, including 1.84~4.29 dB gains in normalized mean squared error and 2.6~10.5 percentage point gains in cosine correlation.

</details>


### [600] [Graph Diffusion-Advection Operator for Directed Graph Signal Processing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16306)
*CHM. Chan,A. Cionca,V. Škultéty,D. Van De Ville*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper extends graph signal processing to directed graphs by linking the directed graph Laplacian to diffusion-advection operators, introducing frequency orderings based on modulus/argument of eigenvalues, defining heat and transport kernels, and proposing composite filters that combine diffusive and advective processes for better signal evolution on directed graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical GSP focuses on undirected graphs (symmetric relationships), but many real-world systems involve asymmetric interactions. The paper aims to extend GSP to directed graphs by establishing a link between the directed graph Laplacian and the diffusion-advection operator, addressing challenges like frequency ordering, smoothness definition, and filter design.

Method: (1) Establishes correspondence between directed graph Laplacian and diffusion-advection operator. (2) Introduces two frequency orderings based on eigenvalue modulus and argument, leading to new smoothness definitions. (3) Proposes heat kernel (diffusive) and transport kernel (advective). (4) Designs composite graph filters by combining diffusive and advective parts to approximate ideal spectral filters.

Result: The proposed frequency orderings, kernels, and composite filters are illustrated on both synthetic and real graphs, including a temperature graph and signals application.

Conclusion: The diffusion-advection perspective successfully addresses key challenges in directed GSP (frequency ordering, smoothness, filter design) and provides richer characterization of graph signal evolution on directed graphs.

Abstract: Graph signal processing (GSP) provides a framework for analyzing data on irregular domains, with applications in neuroscience, finance, chemistry, and social sciences. Classical GSP primarily models symmetric relationships using undirected graphs, yet many real-world systems exhibit asymmetric interactions, motivating extensions to directed graphs. Central to directed GSP is the graph shift operator, typically defined via the directed graph Laplacian. Building on the well-known link between the undirected graph Laplacian and the diffusion operator, we establish a correspondence between the directed graph Laplacian and the diffusion-advection operator. This perspective opens new avenues for addressing crucial points such as frequency ordering, smoothness definition, and the design of spectral and graph filters. Specifically, we introduce two new orderings of frequencies based on the modulus and argument of the eigenvalues, naturally leading to new definitions of smoothness. Then we present two kernels reflecting diffusive and advective processes, namely the heat and transport kernels, respectively. Finally, we propose novel graph filters obtained by composing diffusive and advective parts, which approximate ideal spectral filters accurately and characterize the evolution of graph signals in richer ways. All aforementioned developments are illustrated on both synthetic and real graphs, including an application to temperature graph and signals.

</details>


### [601] [Performance Analysis of AFDM Under In-Phase and Quadrature Imbalance at Receiver](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16422)
*Zhenfeng Huang,Yitong Liu,Hongwen Yang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper mathematically models receiver IQ imbalance in AFDM systems, derives closed-form BER expressions, and shows via analysis and simulation that AFDM suffers more severe BER degradation than OFDM under identical IQ imbalance conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AFDM is a promising 6G waveform for high-mobility scenarios, but its practical deployment faces hardware impairments like IQ imbalance. Understanding IQ imbalance's impact on AFDM is essential for real-world system design.

Method: The authors establish a mathematical model of AFDM under receiver IQ imbalance, characterize ICI in the DAFT domain, and derive closed-form BER expressions for M-QAM-AFDM over AWGN channels.

Result: Under identical IQ imbalance conditions, AFDM exhibits more pronounced BER degradation compared to OFDM; numerical simulations validate the theoretical BER analysis.

Conclusion: The results provide fundamental insights into AFDM's sensitivity to receiver IQ imbalance and offer guidance for practical 6G system design.

Abstract: Affine Frequency Division Multiplexing (AFDM) is a chirp-based multicarrier waveform that achieves full diversity in doubly selective channels while requiring reduced pilot overhead. It is regarded as a highly promising candidate for sixth-generation (6G) mobile communication waveforms in high-mobility scenarios. However, AFDM deployment remains subject to hardware impairments, particularly the in-phase and quadrature (IQ) imbalance commonly encountered in direct conversion transceivers. This paper investigates the impact of receiver IQ imbalance on the bit error rate (BER) performance of AFDM systems. A mathematical model of AFDM under receiver IQ imbalance is first established, where the resulting inter-carrier interference (ICI) in the discrete affine Fourier transform (DAFT) domain is explicitly characterized. Moreover, a closed-form expression for the BER is derived under the influence of receiver IQ imbalance in an M-QAM-AFDM system over an AWGN channel. Numerical simulation results validate the accuracy of the theoretical analysis, while also indicating that under identical IQ imbalance conditions, AFDM exhibits more pronounced BER degradation compared to OFDM. The results provide fundamental insights into the sensitivity of AFDM to receiver IQ imbalance and offer guidance for practical system design.

</details>


### [602] [Acoustic, VOC, and Multimodal Stress Source Localization in the Internet of Plants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16618)
*Ahmet B. Kilic,Ozgur B. Akan*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes a two-stage localization pipeline for the Internet of Plants (IoP) that combines acoustic TDOA multilateration (Stage 1) and VOC dispersion modeling (Stage 2) to locate stress sources in plant networks, finding acoustic methods achieve sub-meter accuracy while VOC fusion offers negligible improvement and remains futuristic.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The Internet of Plants (IoP) treats distributed plant networks as bio-sensing infrastructure for environmental monitoring, but spatial localization of stress sources within such networks remains unaddressed.

Method: A two-stage coarse-to-fine pipeline: Stage 1 uses TDOA multilateration on acoustic time-of-arrival readings; Stage 2 refines estimates using a closed-form Green's function model of VOC dispersion, with an informativeness gate and inverse-variance fusion rule to combine both estimates.

Result: TDOA multilateration achieves sub-meter MAE with 3+ agents in acoustic range, far outperforming VOC-only localization (MAE >3m). Fusion gains are negligible and statistically indistinguishable from noise. The pipeline is robust to perturbations.

Conclusion: TDOA localization is deployable with current hardware, while VOC localization remains a forward-looking capability pending advances in compact biochemical sensors.

Abstract: The Internet of Plants (IoP) treats distributed plant networks as bio-sensing infrastructure for environmental monitoring, but spatial localization of stress sources within such networks remains unaddressed. Plant stress signals have fundamentally different spatial dynamics: acoustic emissions propagate omnidirectionally and independently of wind, while volatile organic compound (VOC) plumes are narrow and advection-dominated. We propose a two-stage, coarse-to-fine localization pipeline for a network of ``agent plants'' -- bio-hybrid sensing nodes embedded in the canopy. Stage 1 localizes the source via time-difference-of-arrival (TDOA) multilateration on acoustic time-of-arrival (ToA) readings; Stage 2 refines this estimate using a closed-form, steady-state Green's function model of VOC dispersion. A VOC informativeness gate and an inverse-variance fusion rule combine the two estimates according to their across-trial reliability, with graceful degradation to the TDOA-only estimate when no informative VOC signal is detected. We evaluate TDOA-only, VOC-only, and fused approaches on a new open-source dataset of 52 scenarios generated via a finite-volume advection-diffusion solver and a ray-based acoustic attenuation model. Across network densities of 1 to 50 agent plants, TDOA multilateration achieves sub-meter mean absolute error (MAE) once three or more agents are within acoustic range, far outperforming VOC-only localization (MAE $> 3$ m at all densities). Fusion differences from the TDOA-only estimate are small and statistically indistinguishable from noise in most cases. The pipeline is robust to physical parameter perturbations, ToA noise, the VOC gate threshold, and the bounding radius. TDOA localization is deployable with current acoustic hardware, whereas VOC localization remains a forward-looking capability pending advances in compact biochemical sensors.

</details>


### [603] [XL-ChannelDiff: An Efficient Diffusion-Based Multi-Domain Near-Field Channel Extrapolation Framework for XL-MIMO Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16628)
*Mengyuan Li,Yu Han,Hao Xu,Yongxu Zhu,Chao-Kai Wen,Shi Jin*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a multi-domain near-field channel extrapolation framework for XL-MIMO systems using a conditional denoising diffusion implicit model (CDDIM) with WGAN adversarial supervision and RePaint-style refinement, achieving accurate CSI acquisition across antenna, frequency, and spatial domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate CSI acquisition is critical for XL-MIMO systems but is extremely challenging in near-field regions due to high-dimensional channel representation and spherical wavefront propagation, motivating the need for an efficient channel extrapolation approach.

Method: The framework uses a physics-aware CDDIM backbone with position-embedded patch tokenization and mask-guided multi-head attention, enhanced by a WGAN discriminator for adversarial supervision and a RePaint-style refinement scheme for optimizing the sampling trajectory.

Result: The proposed framework achieves superior extrapolation accuracy and robust generalization across diverse domains, varied configurations, and severe masking conditions.

Conclusion: The multi-domain near-field channel extrapolation framework effectively addresses the challenges of CSI acquisition in XL-MIMO systems, outperforming existing methods in accuracy and robustness.

Abstract: Accurate channel state information (CSI) acquisition is essential for unleashing the performance gains of extremely large-scale multiple-input multiple-output (XL-MIMO) systems. However, in near-field regions, CSI acquisition is much more challenging than in the far field due to the high-dimensional channel representation and spherical wavefront propagation. To address this, in this paper, we propose an efficient multi-domain near-field channel extrapolation framework for XL-MIMO systems. Leveraging the conditional denoising diffusion implicit model (CDDIM), our approach enables accurate channel extrapolation across the antenna, frequency, and spatial domains. Specifically, we design a physics-aware CDDIM backbone that incorporates position-embedded patch tokenization and a mask-guided multi-head attention mechanism, enabling the model to exploit position-dependent channel correlations induced by near-field spherical-wave propagation. To ensure high-fidelity extrapolation, we incorporate a Wasserstein GAN (WGAN) discriminator that provides adversarial supervision to the CDDIM during both the training and reverse sampling phases. Additionally, a RePaint-style refinement scheme is introduced to optimize the sampling trajectory, further boosting extrapolation accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of the proposed framework, achieving superior extrapolation accuracy and robust generalization across diverse domains, varied configurations, and severe masking conditions.

</details>


### [604] [Towards mm-Level Accurate UWB Radar: High-Accuracy Phase-Based Obstacle Detection through Multi-Channel Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16657)
*Jelle De Moerloose,Adnan Shahid,Eli De Poorter*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents the first method to exploit phase information for tag-free distance estimation in passive UWB radar, achieving centimeter-level accuracy (median error 1.69 cm) through combining coarse amplitude estimates with high-resolution phase changes across multiple frequency channels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tag-free distance estimation with UWB radar is crucial for robotics and autonomous vehicles, but existing passive radar approaches using only amplitude information achieve only ~10 cm accuracy, while phase-based techniques that enable sub-wavelength precision are limited to tag-based (active) systems due to challenges like weak reflections, multipath, and lack of known ToF first-path reference.

Method: The framework combines coarse amplitude-based estimates with high-resolution phase changes across multiple frequency channels. Phase measurements are referenced to the line-of-sight component to compensate for hardware drift, and multichannel frequency diversity is used to resolve phase periodicity ambiguity and mitigate frequency-specific degradation (e.g., Fresnel zones).

Result: Validated on a robot with bistatic UWB radar (DW3000 devices) in a metallic industrial environment. Achieves median error of 1.69 cm at high speeds, outperforming existing ~10 cm amplitude-only methods. Multi-channel fusion reduces error by >40% compared to single-channel operation.

Conclusion: Phase information can be effectively exploited in passive UWB radar for tag-free distance estimation, achieving centimeter-level accuracy. The approach shows potential for sub-centimeter accuracy with further phase modeling and fusion improvements.

Abstract: Accurate, tag-free distance estimation with ultrawideband (UWB) radar is essential for applications such as autonomous guided vehicles, robotics, and environment characterization. For tag-based localization systems, phase-based UWB signal processing techniques have demonstrated sub-wavelength ranging precision, but these approaches are not applicable for passive (tagless) radar setups with weak reflections, mixed multipath conditions, and the absence of a known time-of-flight (ToF) first-path reference. This paper demonstrates for the first time that phase information can be effectively exploited in a fully passive UWB radar setting. We introduce a signal processing framework that extracts reliable distance information by combining coarse amplitude-based estimates with high-resolution phase changes across multiple frequency channels. By referencing phase measurements with the line-of-sight component, the method compensates for hardware-induced phase drift, while the use of multichannel frequency diversity enables disambiguation of periodic phase information and improves robustness against frequencyspecific channel degradation such as Fresnel zones. The proposed approach is validated on a robot equipped with a bistatic UWB radar using DW3000 devices and evaluated in a realistic metallic industrial environment. Experimental results show that our work consistently achieves centimeter-level accuracy even at high speeds, with a median error of 1.69 cm, significantly outperforming existing ~10cm accuracy UWB radar approaches relying only on amplitude-information. We further show how multi-channel fusion exploits uncorrelated channel degradation to reduce the error by more than 40% compared to single-channel operation, and outline how phase modeling and fusion can be pushed toward sub-centimeter accuracy.

</details>


### [605] [Sensing-Assisted Predictive Beamforming for UAV-Enabled Ocean Monitoring Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16717)
*Bohan Li,Guangfei Gao,Jinpeng Zhang,Min Ye,Qian Li,Huaming Yu,Jingjing Wang,Pei Xiao,Sheng Chen*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a sensing-assisted predictive beamforming framework for UAV–buoy maritime monitoring, using integrated sensing and communication signals, a correlated-acceleration motion model, and joint optimization of sensing power and UAV position to achieve robust performance under challenging sea conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address wave-induced buoy dynamics and residual sea clutter that hinder accurate beamforming for UAV–buoy maritime communication, requiring a sensing-assisted approach for state refinement in dynamic maritime environments.

Method: A frame-based UAV workflow with integrated sensing and communication signals, a correlated-acceleration state-space model (Singer process + current-drift term), posterior Cramér–Rao bound derivation, and a per-frame worst-buoy joint optimization of sensing power and UAV position solved via Schur-complement reformulation and lagged successive convex approximation.

Result: The proposed scheme maintains robust prediction and communication performance under denser buoy deployments and harsher sea conditions, outperforms several baselines, and the communication-only benchmark shows significant RMSE degradation, confirming the necessity of sensing-assisted state refinement.

Conclusion: The proposed framework offers a more attractive performance–complexity tradeoff for online deployment compared to full first-order Taylor expansion methods, and is essential for accurate predictive beamforming in dynamic maritime environments.

Abstract: This paper investigates a sensing-assisted predictive beamforming framework for UAV--buoy maritime monitoring by explicitly accounting for wave-induced buoy dynamics and residual sea clutter. A frame-based UAV mission workflow is first established, where the UAV transmits integrated sensing and communication signals to acquire buoy echoes and to support subsequent uplink beam alignment. To characterize short-horizon buoy motion, a correlated-acceleration state-space model is developed by combining a Singer process for wave-driven excitation with a slowly varying current-drift term. Given the resulting nonlinear reflection, Doppler, and delay measurements, the posterior Fisher information matrix and the corresponding posterior Cramér--Rao bound (PCRB) are derived, and the predicted horizontal-position PCRB is adopted as the sensing metric. A per-frame worst-buoy design is then formulated to jointly optimize sensing power allocation and UAV position under uplink-rate, UAV-power, and mobility constraints. By exploiting a Schur-complement reformulation and a lagged successive convex approximation, the resulting subproblem is converted into a convex conic program with tractable complexity. Simulation results show that the proposed scheme maintains robust prediction and communication performance under denser buoy deployments and harsher sea conditions, and outperforms several baseline designs. In particular, the pronounced root mean square error (RMSE) degradation of the communication-only benchmark confirms that sensing-assisted state refinement is essential for accurate predictive beamforming in dynamic maritime environments. Compared with a full first-order Taylor expansion method, it achieves a more attractive performance--complexity tradeoff for online deployment.

</details>


### [606] [Data-Aided Channel and Doppler Estimation for mMIMO LEO SatComs with Uncompensated Doppler](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16750)
*Abdollah Masoud Darya,Saeed Abdallah*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a framework for mMIMO LEO satellite channel estimation under uncompensated Doppler, using pilot-based MMSE, Doppler estimation, and data-aided DD-MMSE or EM estimators, achieving improved accuracy with trade-offs between complexity and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of accurate channel estimation for massive MIMO LEO satellite systems under uncompensated Doppler effects, which degrade conventional estimation methods.

Method: The framework uses pilot-based MMSE estimation, followed by Doppler estimation and data-aided estimation via either decision-directed MMSE (DD-MMSE) or expectation-maximization (EM) methods.

Result: The proposed framework achieves improved channel and Doppler estimation accuracy over existing methods. DD-MMSE offers lower complexity, while EM provides higher estimation accuracy.

Conclusion: The framework effectively balances complexity and accuracy for mMIMO LEO satellite channel estimation, with DD-MMSE suitable for low-complexity scenarios and EM for high-accuracy needs.

Abstract: This paper presents a framework for estimating and tracking massive multiple-input multiple-output (mMIMO) low-Earth-orbit (LEO) satellite channels under uncompensated Doppler. The approach begins with a pilot-based minimum mean square error (MMSE) estimate, followed by Doppler estimation and data-aided channel estimation using either a decision-directed MMSE (DD-MMSE) or an expectation-maximization (EM)-based estimator. The proposed framework achieves improved channel and Doppler estimation accuracy compared to existing methods. Results demonstrate that the DD-MMSE variant offers lower complexity, while the EM variant provides higher estimation accuracy.

</details>


### [607] [A Perception vs. Distortion Perspective on Score-Based Generative Channel Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16815)
*Marco Skocaj,Lukas Eller,Mate Boban*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper provides a rigorous analysis of when score-based models outperform discriminative learning for wireless channel estimation, using the perception-distortion tradeoff to identify that score-based methods excel under high predictive uncertainty for downstream tasks like precoding, while traditional discriminative approaches are preferable under low uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of rigorous analysis in current literature regarding when score-matching offers tangible advantages over traditional discriminative learning in wireless communications, specifically for physical-layer tasks like channel estimation.

Method: The paper presents a theoretically grounded interpretation of score-based channel estimation through the lens of the perception-distortion tradeoff, and models downstream wireless tasks (e.g., capacity maximization) as functionals of channel estimation to quantify the excess risk incurred by standard distortion-minimization approaches.

Result: Under high predictive uncertainty, score-based estimation offsets large excess risk gaps and enables near Bayesian-optimal precoding; under low predictive uncertainty, discriminative distortion-minimization approaches are preferable due to lower complexity and more efficient model capacity use.

Conclusion: Score-based estimation is advantageous in high-uncertainty regimes for wireless channel estimation, but discriminative approaches remain preferable in low-uncertainty settings, offering practical guidance for method selection.

Abstract: Driven by their remarkable success in computer vision and inverse problem solving, score-based models are increasingly applied to wireless communications, where they show promise across a range of physical-layer tasks. However, despite this growing interest, the current literature often lacks a rigorous analysis of when score-matching offers a tangible advantage over traditional discriminative learning. This paper aims to address this gap through the use-case of channel estimation, a fundamental inverse problem in wireless systems. We present a theoretically grounded interpretation of score-based channel estimation through the lens of the perception-distortion tradeoff, identifying the conditions where score matching excels as well as its key limitations. In particular, by modeling downstream wireless tasks (e.g., capacity maximization) as functionals of the channel estimation process, we quantify the excess risk incurred by standard distortion-minimization approaches. Extensive numerical results show that under high predictive uncertainty, the large excess risk gap can be offset by score-based estimation, enabling near Bayesian-optimal precoding via the learned posterior, whereas in the low predictive uncertainty regime, discriminative distortion-minimization approaches are preferable due to lower complexity and more efficient use of model capacity.

</details>


### [608] [Communication Channel Modelling of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16927)
*Necati Kagan Erkek,Emre Balcı,Berkin Halay,Hakan Ali Çırpan*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper uses an SDR-based channel sounding system to measure UAV wireless communication channels across G2G, A2G, and A2A scenarios, finding that both large-scale (path loss) and small-scale (CIR, PDP) characteristics are needed for realistic UAV channel models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable wireless communication is critical for UAV control, data transmission, and mission performance, yet channel behavior needs better characterization through measurement-based approaches.

Method: An SDR-based channel sounding system collects IQ data to extract path loss (large-scale) and CIR/PDP (small-scale) parameters, verified through measurements in G2G, A2G, and A2A scenarios.

Result: Path loss alone is insufficient; CIR and PDP provide crucial multipath and delay-domain insights. Realistic UAV channel models require both large-scale and small-scale statistics.

Conclusion: UAV channel modeling should integrate both propagation effect types, with future work including wider bandwidth, better synchronization, diverse environments, and Doppler analysis.

Abstract: Wireless communication channel characterization for unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) is essential for reliable control, data transmission, and mission performance in civil, industrial, and defence applications. Channel behaviour is examined using a measurement-based approach that captures both large-scale propagation effects, represented by path loss, and small-scale characteristics, represented by the channel impulse response (CIR) and power delay profile (PDP). An SDR-based channel sounding system is employed to collect and process in-phase and quadrature (IQ) data, enabling the extraction of key channel parameters. Following system verification, measurements are conducted in ground-to-ground (G2G), air-to-ground (A2G), and air-to-air (A2A) scenarios. The results demonstrate that path loss alone is insufficient to describe UAV communication channels, as CIR and PDP provide additional insight into multipath propagation and delay-domain behaviour. The findings indicate that realistic UAV channel models should incorporate both large-scale and small-scale channel statistics. Further improvements may be achieved through increased sounding bandwidth, enhanced synchronization, measurements in a wider range of environments, and more detailed analysis of Doppler effects.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [609] [Joint 3D Trajectory Design and Resource Allocation for Secure Dual-UAV-aided Underlay Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15042)
*Hongjiang Lei,Xiaqiu Wu,Ki-Hong Park,Gaojie Chen,Gaofeng Pan*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a secure underlay data collection system with dual UAVs, jointly optimizing 3D flight paths, jamming power, GD transmit power, and scheduling to maximize average secrecy spectral efficiency (ASSE) against an aerial eavesdropper, using block coordinate descent and convex approximations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UAVs are increasingly used for data collection, but security against aerial eavesdroppers in underlay systems is a critical concern that needs addressing.

Method: The problem is decomposed into four subproblems via block coordinate descent, each solved using slack variables and successive convex approximation methods.

Result: Numerical results validate the approach, showing that optimizing UAV 3D trajectories significantly improves the average secrecy spectral efficiency.

Conclusion: Joint design of UAV 3D trajectories, jamming power, GD power, and scheduling effectively enhances security in underlay UAV-assisted data collection systems against aerial eavesdroppers.

Abstract: Uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) are increasingly being employed for data collection tasks, thanks to their high mobility and easy deployment, acting as aerial platforms to collect data from ground devices (GDs). This study considers a secure underlay data collection system assisted by dual UAVs and focuses on the joint design of the UAVs' three-dimensional (3D) flight paths, the power of the jamming UAV, the power of GDs, and the scheduling of the underlay GDs in the context of an aerial eavesdropper. The highly coupled objective function and non-convex constraints make the formulated problem more complicated to solve. We first utilize an approximate lower bound on the expected spectral efficiency to streamline the solution process. The average secrecy spectral efficiency (ASSE) is maximized by jointly designing the 3D trajectory of the UAVs, the transmit power of GDs, and the user scheduling. The optimization problem is decomposed into four subproblems using block coordinate descent, with each of them into manageable convex optimization tasks by incorporating slack variables and employing successive convex approximation methods. The numerical results validate the effectiveness of our proposed approach, demonstrating that the design of UAV 3D trajectories remarkably improves the ASSE of the considered system.

</details>


### [610] [Two-Timescale Design for Downlink Multiuser Transmission with Dynamic Metasurface Antennas](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15183)
*Hao Xu,Angel Lozano,Hongwen Yang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a two-timescale design for downlink multiuser transmission via Dynamic Metasurface Antennas (DMAs), where DMA coefficients are configured using statistical CSI at the frame level and digital precoders are updated per slot using instantaneous CSI, balancing pilot overhead, complexity, and spectral efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To relieve massive MIMO architectures from high energy consumption and hardware costs by leveraging DMAs, while balancing pilot overhead, complexity, and spectral efficiency in multiuser transmission.

Method: A two-timescale design: (1) long-term DMA coefficients optimization using statistical CSI via a novel optimization framework outperforming stochastic successive convex approximation; (2) short-term digital precoder updates using weighted minimum mean-squared error (WMMSE) and a closed-form solution for single-user case.

Result: The proposed optimization framework for DMA coefficients outperforms the widely used stochastic successive convex approximation method. Performance evaluations show the two-timescale design is attractive for future wireless networks.

Conclusion: The two-timescale DMA-based design effectively balances pilot overhead, complexity, and spectral efficiency, making it a promising approach for future wireless communication networks.

Abstract: Dynamic metasurface antennas (DMAs) promise to relieve massive multiple-input multiple-output architectures from their high energy consumption and hardware costs. This paper proposes a two-timescale design for downlink multiuser transmission via DMAs, a design that balances pilot overhead, complexity, and spectral efficiency. At the onset of each frame, the DMA coefficients are configured based only on statistical channel-state information (CSI), a process for which the paper introduces an optimization framework that is shown to outperform the widely used stochastic successive convex approximation method. Then, within each frame, the digital precoder is updated at each slot, based on the optimized DMA coefficients and the effective lower-dimensional instantaneous CSI. The weighted minimum mean-squared error method is applied for this short-term optimization and, for the special case of single-user transmission, a closed-form solution for the digital precoder is provided. Performance evaluations demonstrate that the proposed two-timescale design can be an attractive ingredient for future wireless networks.

</details>


### [611] [New bounds for covering codes under insertions or deletions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15379)
*Chengfei Xie,Yubo Sun,Gennian Ge*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper studies covering codes under insertion and deletion operations, showing key differences between insertion and deletion covering, establishing bounds and constructions, and providing optimal codes including perfect binary codes for two-burst deletions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Covering codes for insertions and deletions arise naturally in synchronization errors but differ substantially from Hamming metric counterparts. The paper aims to understand these differences and develop effective codes.

Method: The authors use combinatorial approaches, hypergraph covering theory, differential VT codes, and completion arguments to construct and bound insertion/deletion covering codes.

Result: Key results include: (1) insertion covering ≠ deletion covering; (2) new lower bounds for insertion covering codes; (3) asymptotic tightness of counting lower bound for deletion covering; (4) optimal non-binary single-deletion covering codes; (5) binary perfect codes for two-burst deletions; (6) explicit q-ary two-burst-deletion covering codes.

Conclusion: The paper establishes fundamental differences between insertion and deletion covering, provides tight bounds and constructions, and extends results to burst deletions, including perfect codes.

Abstract: Covering codes for insertions and deletions arise naturally in the study of synchronization errors and differ substantially from their classical counterparts in the Hamming metric. In this paper, we study covering codes under insertion and deletion operations. We first show that, in contrast to the equivalence between insertion and deletion correction, insertion covering and deletion covering are not equivalent. We then develop bounds and constructions for insertion and deletion covering codes, with particular emphasis on the large-alphabet regime. For insertion covering codes, we extend a recent combinatorial approach for single insertions and establish a new lower bound for arbitrary fixed insertion radius. For deletion covering codes, we relate the problem to hypergraph covering and prove that the elementary counting lower bound is asymptotically tight when the alphabet size tends to infinity. We further provide a construction of asymptotically optimal non-binary single-deletion covering codes by using differential Varshamov--Tenengolts (VT) codes together with a completion argument. In addition, we study covering codes for burst deletions. We prove that binary differential VT codes are not only capable of correcting two-burst deletions but also have the corresponding covering property, and hence form binary perfect codes for two-burst deletions.
