Finally, we extend this construction to non-binary alphabets and obtain explicit $q$-ary two-burst-deletion covering codes.

</details>


### [612] [Sparse Channel Estimation for SIM-based mmWave Near-Field Communications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15634)
*Jiancheng An,Enyu Shi,Jiayi Zhang,Lu Gan,Michail Matthaiou,Symeon Chatzinotas,Marco Di Renzo*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a compressed sensing-based channel estimation protocol for SIM-based multi-user millimeter-wave near-field communication systems, introducing a polar-domain transform matrix for UPAs and a low-complexity polar-domain SBL algorithm to address underdetermined CE problems and severe energy spread in near-field channels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address channel estimation challenges in SIM-based MU mmWave near-field systems, where many meta-atoms create an underdetermined problem (fewer RF chains than meta-atoms) and the expanded near-field region requires spherical wavefront modeling instead of planar wavefront assumptions.

Method: Introduces a CS-based CE protocol with a polar-domain channel representation and novel polar-domain transform matrix for UPAs, transforming CE into a sparse recovery problem. Proposes an LCPD-SBL algorithm to reduce computational complexity compared to standard SBL.

Result: The LCPD-SBL algorithm significantly reduces computational complexity without compromising estimation accuracy compared to traditional SBL frameworks.

Conclusion: The polar-domain approach effectively handles the near-field energy spread problem, and the proposed low-complexity algorithm makes CS-based CE practical for SIM-based systems with high-dimensional dictionaries.

Abstract: In this paper, we address the channel estimation (CE) problem in SIM-based multi-user (MU) millimeter-wave (mmWave) near-field communication systems. To address the severe path loss and blockage in mmWave communication systems, many meta-atoms are typically integrated into each layer of the SIM. Then, the number of radio frequency (RF) chains at the base station (BS) is fewer than that of meta-atoms per layer, resulting in an underdetermined problem. Additionally, the increase in the number of meta-atoms in each layer expands the SIM's near-field region, leading to the user equipment (UEs) being mostly situated in this region, necessitating precise modeling of the channel under the spherical wavefront assumption. To address these issues, we introduce a compressed sensing (CS)-based CE protocol to tackle the underdetermined problem. In contrast to the traditional CS-based estimation framework, we investigate a polar-domain channel representation to tackle the severe energy spread effect of the classical angular-domain channel representation in near-field communication systems. Specifically, we design a novel polar-domain transform matrix for uniform planar arrays (UPAs), thereby transforming the CE problem into a sparse recovery task of the paths' support set and complex gains. To overcome the limitations of the sparse Bayesian learning (SBL) framework in tackling high-dimensional dictionaries, we propose a low-complexity polar-domain SBL (LCPD-SBL) algorithm, which significantly reduces computational complexity without compromising estimation accuracy.

</details>


### [613] [Twin-in-the-Loop Optimization and Fundamental Limits of Position--Velocity Estimation in Cell-Free ISAC Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15688)
*Changhao He,Xiaojuan Zhang,Geoffrey Ye Li*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper establishes a joint position-velocity CRB framework for cell-free ISAC systems with FIM, revealing spatio-temporal decoupling and proposing a closed-loop DT architecture with confidence-aware scheduling that reduces update rates while improving accuracy over single-BS systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Digital twin networks require tight integration with wireless sensing, but fundamental limits of this coupling in cell-free ISAC systems with fluid intelligent metasurfaces remain unexplored.

Method: Derives joint position-velocity CRB via scatter-matrix decomposition of velocity Fisher information, analyzes rank-deficiency in single-BS vs. full observability in cell-free, and develops closed-loop DT framework with closed-form mismatch angle and confidence-aware scheduling.

Result: Single-BS systems are rank-deficient for 2D velocity estimation while cell-free achieves full observability. FIM shape optimization improves position accuracy but not velocity CRB under isotropic waveforms. Angular diversity in cell-free mitigates DT prediction errors. Proposed scheduling reduces DT update rate.

Conclusion: Cell-free ISAC with FIM significantly outperforms single-BS systems, with gains from angular diversity, Doppler-position coupling, and FIM adaptation.

Abstract: Digital twin (DT) networks require tight integration with wireless sensing, yet the fundamental limits of such coupling in cell-free integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems remain largely unexplored, particularly in the presence of fluid intelligent metasurfaces (FIM). This paper establishes a joint position-velocity Cramer-Rao bound (CRB) framework, operationalized through a twin-in-the-loop architecture. By leveraging a scatter-matrix decomposition of the velocity Fisher information, we show that single-base-station systems are inherently rank-deficient for two-dimensional velocity estimation, whereas cell-free deployments with multiple access-point pairs achieve full observability. The resulting CRB reveals a spatio-temporal decoupling: FIM shape optimization significantly improves position accuracy but does not affect the velocity CRB under isotropic waveforms while Doppler coupling asymmetrically enhances position estimation accuracy. Building on this analysis, we develop a closed-loop DT framework, deriving the critical mismatch angle in closed form and showing that angular diversity in cell-free systems mitigates DT prediction errors. We further characterize the optimal synchronization period and propose a confidence-aware scheduling strategy that reduces the DT update rate. Numerical results demonstrate substantial performance gains over single-base-station systems, with improvements attributed to angular diversity, Doppler-position coupling, and FIM adaptation.

</details>


### [614] [Information aging in massive MIMO systems affected by phase noise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16466)
*Alberto Tarable,Francisco J. Escribano*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper investigates phase noise issues in massive MIMO systems, specifically focusing on information aging of pilot-based phase-noise estimates. It proposes an iterative expectation-maximization (EM) receiver that is robust to such aging in a realistic 5G uplink scenario.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Phase noise degrades receiver performance in massive MIMO systems due to aging of pilot-based phase-noise information.

Method: An iterative receiver based on expectation-maximization (EM) is proposed for realistic 5G uplink scenarios.

Result: The iterative EM-based receiver demonstrates robustness to information aging caused by phase noise.

Conclusion: The proposed EM-based iterative receiver effectively mitigates phase-noise-induced information aging in massive MIMO 5G uplink systems.

Abstract: In massive MIMO systems, phase noise can spoil the performance of the usual receiver techniques. The problem arises because of the aging of phase-noise information based on pilots. In this paper, in a realistic 5G uplink scenario, we quantify the impact of information aging and we propose an iterative receiver based on expectation-maximization (EM). Simulation results show that the iterative receiver is robust to information aging related to phase noise.

</details>


### [615] [Rate-Distortion for Reversible Causal Nets under Closure-Preserving Fidelity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16592)
*Jianfeng Xu*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper develops a semantic rate-distortion theory for reversible logging under a closure-preserving fidelity criterion, showing that under admissible reconstructions, redundant facts become information-theoretically invisible, enabling core-only compression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish a formal information-theoretic framework for understanding compression limits of reversible logging systems where execution histories must be recoverable after rollback, particularly when meaning is defined by logical closure under rule systems like Datalog.

Method: Models execution history as finite logged facts with monotone semantic closure; introduces bounded distortion measuring closure change from single-fact edits; uses canonical deletion scan to decompose logs into irredundant core and redundant remainder; constructs confusability hypergraph from zero-distortion reconstruction overlaps.

Result: Redundant facts become information-theoretically invisible under admissible reconstructions; core-only rate-distortion reduction is achieved; at perfect fidelity, confusability hypergraph determines minimum rate; validated numerically on reversible causal nets and reversible prime event structures.

Conclusion: The framework provides a principled compression theory for reversible logging, demonstrating that only the irredundant core is essential for perfect reconstruction, with practical applicability to reversible causal nets and event structures under multiple reversing disciplines.

Abstract: We develop a semantic rate-distortion theory for reversible logging under a closure-preserving fidelity criterion. An execution history is modeled as a finite set of logged facts, and rollback-relevant meaning is captured by a monotone semantic closure induced by an effective rule system such as Datalog. We introduce a bounded distortion that edits one logged fact and measures the resulting change in closure. A canonical deletion scan decomposes the log into an irredundant core and a redundant remainder; under admissible reconstructions, redundant facts become information-theoretically invisible, yielding a core-only rate-distortion reduction. At perfect fidelity, overlaps among zero-distortion reconstructions induce a confusability hypergraph that determines the minimum rate. We instantiate the framework on reversible causal nets and reversible prime event structures under multiple reversing disciplines, and validate the predictions numerically.

</details>


### [616] [Enhancing Secret Key Generation for UAV Communications via Codeword Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16644)
*Yizhuo Wang,Qinghe Du,Ning Shen,Shijiao Zhang,Lei Zhao,Yang Hu*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a physical layer key generation scheme for UAV communications using channel codeword reconstruction with polarization characteristics to improve key agreement and security despite channel estimation errors from high mobility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UAV communications face high mobility causing short channel coherence time and estimation errors, which degrade conventional wireless channel-based key generation performance.

Method: A codeword construction algorithm achieving polarization characteristics is proposed to separate reliable from unreliable keys, using soft-decision decoding to maximize raw channel information utilization.

Result: The scheme reduces key disagreement rate for legitimate users, increases the number of consistently generated keys, and ensures a lower key consistency rate for eavesdroppers.

Conclusion: The proposed codeword reconstruction-based key generation scheme outperforms quantization-based methods and guarantees system security in UAV communications.

Abstract: With the rapid advancement of unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV), ensuring the security of communication links among UAVs has become crucial. In this paper, we propose a novel physical layer key generation scheme based on channel codeword reconstruction. In UAV communications, the high mobility of aerial nodes leads to short channel coherence time, which together with noise causes inevitable channel estimation errors. These errors significantly degrades the performance of wireless channel-based key generation. Therefore, we propose a codeword construction algorithm that achieves a polarization characteristic, which effectively segregates reliable keys from unreliable ones. Compared to the existing quantization-based key generation scheme, our approach maximize the utilization of raw channel information and employ soft-decision decoding to generate key. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme reduces the key disagreement rate for legitimate users and increases the number of consistently generated keys. Furthermore, our method ensures a lower key consistency rate for eavesdropper, which guarantees system security.

</details>


### [617] [A Lean-Certified Proof of $K_8(4, 2) = 23$](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16688)
*Andreas Florath*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper proves that the octonary covering-code value K₈(4,2) = 23 using Lean 4, combining an explicit 23-word code for the upper bound with a combinatorial lower bound argument that rules out 22-word covers, all verified within Lean without external SAT solvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish the exact covering-code value K₈(4,2) = 23 through a fully verified, formal proof in Lean 4, eliminating reliance on untrusted external solvers.

Method: Upper bound: explicit 23-word radius-two code checked over all 8⁴ words. Lower bound: fiber-counting and missing-pair arguments rule out ≤21 words; for the 22-word case, the proof reduces to six missing-pair graphs, uses fiber-counting constraints and two Lean-checked LRAT refutations of CNF instances to force a 3+3+2 block structure, which is then shown impossible via binary array arguments.

Result: K₈(4,2) = 23 is proven in Lean 4, with all upper and lower bound reasoning, CNF instances, and LRAT refutations checked internally.

Conclusion: The exact covering-code value is established via a fully formalized, self-contained proof artifact in Lean 4, demonstrating that complex combinatorial proofs can be mechanically verified without external solvers.

Abstract: We prove the exact octonary covering-code value $K_8(4, 2) = 23$ in Lean 4. The upper bound is given by an explicit 23-word radius-two code in $(Fin\:8)^4$ , checked over all $8^4$ ambient words. The lower bound excludes covers with at most 22 words. A fiber-counting and missing-pair argument first rules out covers with at most 21 words. In the remaining 22-word case, the proof reduces a hypothetical cover to six missing-pair graphs coming from the coordinate-pair projections. Fiber-counting arguments constrain these graphs, and two Lean-checked Linear RAT (LRAT) refutations of stored conjunctive-normal-form (CNF) instances force a common 3 + 3 + 2 block structure. This structure is incompatible with a 22-word cover: the two three-symbol components already force 18 codewords, while the remaining two-symbol component would require a binary strength-two array of length four with at most four rows, which is impossible. The result is packaged as a proof-carrying Lean artifact: the explicit upper bound, structural lower bound, CNF instances, and LRAT refutations are checked inside Lean, with no external SAT solver used during proof replay.

</details>


### [618] [Testing for a Hidden Geometry in Random Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16715)
*Amit Silber,Mor Oren-Loberman,Wasim Huleihel*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper studies detecting a hidden random geometric graph planted in an Erdős–Rényi random graph. It characterizes statistical and computational limits, revealing an easy–hard–impossible phase transition, and proves low-degree polynomial algorithms fail in the computationally hard regime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the fundamental statistical and computational limits of detecting hidden geometric structure in random graphs, and to characterize when detection is possible, efficiently or otherwise.

Method: The authors derive information-theoretic lower bounds, propose algorithms achieving those limits, prove computational hardness via low-degree polynomial analysis, and characterize the easy–hard–impossible phase transition.

Result: Sharp information-theoretic limits are identified; detection is impossible in some regimes, possible but computationally intractable in others, and efficiently detectable in still others. Low-degree polynomial algorithms provably fail in the conjectured hard regime.

Conclusion: There exists a sharp gap between statistical feasibility and computational feasibility for detecting hidden geometry in random graphs, characterized by an easy–hard–impossible phase transition.

Abstract: We study the problem of detecting a faint geometric signal hidden in an otherwise random graph. Formally, we consider a hypothesis testing problem in which, under the null, the observed graph is an Erdős--Rényi random graph $\mathcal{G}(n,q)$, while under the alternative a random geometric graph $\mathcal{G}(k,q,d)$ is planted on $k\le n$ vertices. The planted subgraph is generated from independent random points on the unit sphere $\mathbb{S}^{d-1}$, with edges determined by latent geometric proximity and calibrated to have edge density $q$. Our goal is to characterize the statistical and computational limits of detecting this hidden geometry.
  We derive sharp information-theoretic lower bounds that identify regimes where detection is impossible and provide algorithms that achieve these limits whenever detection is feasible. We further investigate the computational complexity of the problem and determine when efficient polynomial-time tests exist. The model exhibits an \emph{easy--hard--impossible} phase transition: some regimes allow efficient detection, others permit detection only with computationally intractable procedures, and still others render detection impossible even with unlimited computational power. As evidence for the computational barrier, we prove that all low-degree polynomial algorithms fail throughout the conjecturally hard regime, demonstrating a sharp gap between statistical and computational feasibility.

</details>


### [619] [Set Shaping Theory Applied to Universal Coding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16746)
*Alix Petit,Aida Koch,Logan Lewis,Lily Scott*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper shows that a Set Shaping Theory (SST) transformation reduces the average universal coding length of uniformly generated random sequences below the Krichevsky-Trofimov baseline, by mapping sequences into expanded representations with an extra symbol. The improvement holds across multiple compression architectures (adaptive arithmetic coding, enumerative coding, LZ78, adaptive Huffman, adaptive ANS), suggesting SST works as a general preprocessing layer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uniformly generated random sequences are the hardest case for universal coders since they contain no exploitable bias. The authors aim to show that even in this worst-case scenario, a representation-level transformation (SST) can systematically reduce coding length below the standard Krichevsky-Trofimov baseline.

Method: The SST transformation maps each input sequence s ∈ A^N to an expanded sequence f(s) ∈ A^(N+1), using the extra symbol to store the transformation index for reversibility. The baseline Krichevsky-Trofimov cost N H_0(s) + R_KT(s) is compared against the shaped cost (N+1)H_0(f(s)) + R_KT(f(s)), and tested across multiple compression architectures.

Result: The SST transformation systematically reduces average universal coding length for uniformly generated sequences below the Krichevsky-Trofimov baseline. The improvement holds consistently across adaptive arithmetic coding, enumerative coding, LZ78, adaptive Huffman coding, and adaptive ANS.

Conclusion: SST functions as a representation-level preprocessing layer that structurally improves existing universal coders without requiring internal modifications. All results are reproducible with the provided simulator.

Abstract: Universal coders process individual sequences without assuming that the source distribution is known. In this setting, uniformly generated sequences represent the most difficult test case: the source simulates pure randomness, contains no exploitable bias, and forces a frequency-estimating universal coder to infer the empirical composition entirely from the sequence itself. This paper reports that a Set Shaping Theory (SST) transformation systematically reduces the average universal coding length of uniformly generated sequences below the Krichevsky-Trofimov baseline N H_0(s) + R_KT(s). The transformation maps each input sequence s in A^N into an expanded sequence f(s) in A^(N+1), while storing the transformation index in the additional symbol in order to preserve reversibility. The comparison evaluates the exact Krichevsky-Trofimov baseline N H_0(s) + R_KT(s) against the shaped score (N+1)H_0(f(s)) + R_KT(f(s)), where H_0 is computed from the empirical frequencies of the individual sequence. A single unified transformation also yields reductions across distinct compression architectures, including adaptive arithmetic coding, enumerative coding, LZ78, adaptive Huffman coding, and adaptive ANS. These results support the interpretation of SST as a representation-level preprocessing layer that can structurally improve existing universal coders without requiring internal modifications to their coding mechanisms. All results reported in the article can be reproduced with the simulator available at https://sst-simulator.github.io/Set-Shaping-Theory-Simulator/.

</details>


### [620] [Mixed Block Markov Superposition Transmission Codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16831)
*Philipp Mohr,Jasper Brüggmann,Viet Hoang Le,Gerhard Bauch*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper proposes mixed BMST (mBMST) codes that combine recursive and non-recursive components via parallel/serial concatenation, achieving better FER/BER performance with lower memory than recursive BMST.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing BMST variants have complementary limitations: recursive BMST suffers from error propagation but avoids high error floors, while non-recursive BMST shows the opposite behavior. The paper aims to combine both to get the best of both worlds.

Method: The authors combine recursive and non-recursive BMST components through parallel and serial concatenation, creating a mixed BMST (mBMST) framework that generalizes existing BMST variants.

Result: Simulations show that mBMST structures improve both FER and BER performance while requiring lower memory than recursive BMST codes.

Conclusion: The proposed mBMST framework subsumes existing BMST variants and enables new structures that outperform prior approaches, particularly in terms of error performance and memory efficiency.

Abstract: Block Markov superposition transmission (BMST) codes provide a flexible framework for constructing codes with near-capacity performance and low-complexity sliding-window decoding. However, existing BMST variants show contrasting performance limitations: recursive BMST (rBMST) codes suffer from error propagation but avoid high error floors, whereas non-recursive BMST codes exhibit the opposite behavior. Motivated by these complementary characteristics, we combine recursive and non-recursive components through parallel and serial concatenation, yielding mixed BMST (mBMST) codes. The proposed framework subsumes existing BMST variants and enables new BMST structures. Simulations show that these structures improve FER and BER performance with lower memory requirements than rBMST.

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [621] [Explainable Task-Oriented Token Communication for AI-Native 6G Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14808)
*Feibo Jiang,Lei Mao,Li Dong,Kezhi Wang,Cunhua Pan,Jiangzhou Wang*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: The paper proposes ET-TokenCom, an explainable task-oriented token communication framework that integrates Foundation Models with wireless communications by using Visual Tokens for perception, Task Tokens for intent, and Cross-Modal Attention fusion, with explainable attention heatmaps for interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing task-oriented image communication methods face three challenges: insufficient task-oriented Token representation, inadequate collaboration between Visual Tokens and Task Tokens, and limited interpretability of task decisions.

Method: The ET-TokenCom framework: (1) extracts Visual Tokens from images for low-level perception; (2) introduces FM-generated Task Tokens to represent target information and decision intent; (3) uses Cross-Modal Attention (CMA) fusion for Task Tokens to guide Visual Token selection/weighting/transmission; (4) employs explainable output with attention heatmaps highlighting critical perceptual regions.

Result: Simulation results validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed ET-TokenCom framework, though specific metrics are not detailed in the abstract.

Conclusion: The ET-TokenCom framework effectively addresses token representation, token collaboration, and interpretability challenges in task-oriented image communication by leveraging Foundation Models and attention-based fusion mechanisms.

Abstract: The integration of Foundation Models (FMs) and wireless communications is driving the evolution of image communication from bit-accurate transmission toward task-oriented transmission. However, existing task-oriented image communication methods still face three major challenges: insufficient task-oriented Token representation, inadequate collaboration between Visual Tokens and Task Tokens, and limited interpretability of task decisions. To address these challenges, we propose an Explainable Task-Oriented Token Communication (ET-TokenCom) framework. By treating Tokens as unified units for information representation and transmission, the proposed framework constructs an end-to-end communication link that spans visual perception, wireless transmission, and task reasoning. At the transmitter, the ET-TokenCom framework extracts Visual Tokens from images to preserve low-level visual information. Meanwhile, Task Tokens generated by the FM are introduced to represent the target information and decision intent required by the current task. A Cross-Modal Attention (CMA) fusion mechanism is further designed, enabling Task Tokens to explicitly guide the selection, weighting, and transmission of Visual Tokens. At the receiver, the framework integrates Token decoding with an explainable output mechanism, where attention heatmaps are generated to highlight critical perceptual regions under different task objectives and reveal the influence of Task Tokens on the outputs. Finally, simulation results validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed ET-TokenCom framework.

</details>


### [622] [Leptomeningeal Collateral Detection on DSA via Vessel-Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14828)
*Junyong Cao,Hakim Baazaoui,Chinmay Prabhakar,Suprosanna Shit,Lukas Bastian Otto,Susanne Wegener,Bjoern Menze,Ezequiel de la Rosa*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: A hybrid graph-pixel deep learning framework detects leptomeningeal collaterals at individual vessel level on DSA, outperforming graph-only and pixel-only baselines with 0.434 PR-AUC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Leptomeningeal collaterals are important prognostic factors in acute ischemic stroke. Existing automated methods rely on CTA which cannot resolve individual collaterals, while DSA offers superior resolution but current assessment is subjective with poor inter-rater agreement.

Method: Formulates collateral detection as vessel segment classification on a DSA-derived graph. Uses a hybrid architecture combining a topology-aware graph branch with a dense pixel branch, fused in a shared node-probability space.

Result: In five-fold cross-validation, the fused model achieves PR-AUC of 0.434, outperforming graph-only (0.403) and pixel-only (0.362) baselines.

Conclusion: First method enabling individualization of LMCs in DSA for precise per-vessel quantitative assessment, shifting DSA evaluation toward objective measurement and supporting future biomarker discovery.

Abstract: Leptomeningeal collaterals (LMCs) are an important prognostic factor in acute ischemic stroke. Existing automated methods rely on CT angiography (CTA), but individual LMCs are often too small to be resolved on CTA, limiting these methods to coarse collateral scoring. Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) visualizes individual collaterals at superior resolution, yet current assessment remains subjective, relying on manual grading scales that suffer from poor inter-rater agreement. We present a framework that formulates collateral detection as the classification of individual vessel segments on a graph derived from DSA. A hybrid graph-pixel architecture combines a topology-aware graph branch with a dense pixel branch, fused in a shared node-probability space. In a five-fold cross-validation setting, the fused model achieves a PR-AUC of 0.434, outperforming the graph-only (0.403) and pixel-only (0.362) baselines. To our knowledge, this is the first method to enable the individualization of LMCs in DSA, allowing for precise per-vessel quantitative assessment. This integration shifts DSA assessment toward objective evaluation, supporting future biomarker and pattern discovery for individual LMCs.

</details>


### [623] [Polyp-D2ATL: Deep Domain-Adaptive Transfer Learning for Colorectal Polyp Classification under Label Distribution Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15000)
*Sajad Jabarzadeh Ghandilu,Maryam Sadat Hosseini Azad,Shahriar Baradaran Shokouhi,Emad Fatemizadeh*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: Polyp-D2ATL is a novel framework for automated colorectal polyp classification that handles imbalanced data, label distribution shift, and cross-modality generalization, achieving state-of-the-art results on the PICCOLO dataset (82.38% accuracy, 77.49% Macro-F1, 87.47% specificity).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early and highly accurate prediction of colorectal polyps can save lives, but existing automated systems struggle with difficult-to-predict polyps due to imbalanced data, label distribution shift, and cross-modality generalization challenges.

Method: The authors propose Polyp-D2ATL, a framework with a specific training strategy designed to mitigate limitations of existing models and effectively classify polyps according to the NICE classification system.

Result: On PICCOLO validation set: accuracy 82.38%, Macro-F1 77.49%, specificity 87.47%. Consistent improvements on held-out test set demonstrate generalization and clinical applicability.

Conclusion: Polyp-D2ATL significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art models across various reliable metrics, showing strong generalization capacity and potential for clinical deployment.

Abstract: Early and highly accurate prediction of colorectal polyps, as an important sign of one of the most dangerous types of cancer, will result in saving more lives. Despite the advancements in colorectal polyp classification, many challenges remain in obtaining an automated polyp prediction system that is able to diagnose the difficult-to-predict polyps accompanied by different features in real scenarios, where the model can handle imbalanced data, label distribution shift, and cross-modality generalization successfully. In this study, we propose Polyp-D2ATL, a novel framework accompanied by a specific training strategy, which mitigates these limitations and effectively predicts the different classes of polyps belonging to the NICE classification. Our extensive experiments on the PICCOLO validation and test sets demonstrate that the proposed Polyp-D2ATL significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art models across various reliable metrics, achieving an accuracy of 82.38%, a Macro-F1 of 77.49%, and a specificity of 87.47% on the validation set, alongside consistent improvements on the held-out test set which demonstrates the generalization capacity and clinical applicability of the proposed approach.

</details>


### [624] [Chroma-gated, differentiable OKLCH interpolation: Continuous Oklab fallback for color-cast reduction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15352)
*Naoyuki Uchida*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: The paper introduces Continuous Oklab Fallback (COFb), a differentiable chroma gate that blends OKLCH path toward linear Oklab path as chroma falls, reducing inter-hue detours in color gradients while unifying handling of both achromatic and chromatic endpoint cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OKLCH's polar parameterization causes two issues: (1) inter-hue detour between chromatic endpoints (e.g., blue to yellow passing through green), and (2) off-line bow when one endpoint is achromatic. Existing two-valued threshold fixes only address (2), leaving (1) untreated.

Method: COFb uses a one-parameter, differentiable chroma gate w(C)=C^n/(C^n+σ^n) that continuously blends OKLCH path toward linear Oklab path as chroma falls, with a default of n=1 (Michaelis-Menten form) and σ≈0.19 for typical sRGB palette.

Result: At default settings, COFb halves the inter-hue path detour: mean lateral deviation -49.5%, chroma-weighted hue excursion -35.5%. However, on pure achromatic endpoints the two-valued switch performs better, and COFb does not preserve chroma like any Cartesian blend.

Conclusion: COFb runs entirely in plain Oklab (a,b) to sRGB, serving as a fallback for cast-reduced gradients where modern CSS color interpolation is unavailable, such as in older engines, image/video pipelines, or GPU shaders.

Abstract: OKLCH -- the cylindrical (lightness, chroma, hue) form of Ottosson's Oklab color space -- is the interpolation space recommended by CSS Color 4 for gradients and color-mix(), and it is now broadly deployed. Its polar parameterization, however, casts color near the neutral axis in two ways: (1) an inter-hue detour between two chromatic endpoints that sweeps through an unintended hue (blue to yellow visibly passing through green), and (2) an off-line bow when one endpoint is achromatic. Existing remedies are uniformly two-valued -- a threshold switch that fires only at an achromatic endpoint -- so they address only (2); on chromatic pairs every one of them reduces to raw OKLCH, leaving the (1) inter-hue cast untreated. We introduce Continuous Oklab fallback (COFb), a one-parameter, differentiable chroma gate $w(C)=C^n/(C^n+σ^n)$ that continuously blends the OKLCH path toward the linear Oklab path as chroma falls. A single gate reduces the (1) cast that the two-valued family leaves untreated and unifies the handling of (1) and (2) without any endpoint test. We characterize a cast-hue trade-off frontier, adopt a default ($n=1$, the rational Michaelis-Menten form; $σ\approx0.19$ for a typical sRGB palette, from a normalization-independent cast-half criterion), and verify the gate's properties symbolically. At the default, COFb halves the inter-hue path detour (mean lateral deviation -49.5%, chroma-weighted hue excursion -35.5%). We also state the method's limits: on (2) alone the two-valued switch remains better, and like any Cartesian blend COFb does not preserve chroma. In deployment, COFb runs entirely in plain Oklab (a,b) to sRGB, so it serves as a fallback that delivers the same cast-reduced gradients where modern CSS color interpolation (color-mix(in oklch) and the like) is unavailable -- older engines, image and video pipelines, or GPU shaders.

</details>


### [625] [A Surface-based Multimodal Framework for Multitask Analysis in Alzheimer's Disease](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15692)
*Shuo Huang,Jianwei Zhang,Lujia Zhong,Jiaxin Yue,Yihao Xia,Xinkai Wang,Yonggang Shi*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: Proposes an augmented spherical multimodal framework for Alzheimer's Disease analysis using spherical diffusion for data augmentation, contrastive learning for cross-modal alignment, and in-context learning for multitask classification and regression, showing improvements on ADNI dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in multimodal AD analysis including cross-modal misalignment, non-Euclidean cortical surface representations, and limited data in small-sample clinical settings.

Method: (1) Spherical diffusion model to generate paired cortical thickness and Tau PET SUVR data; (2) Contrastive learning for aligned cross-modal representations; (3) In-context learning model integrating imaging features with tabular data for multitask classification and regression.

Result: Consistent performance improvements across five diagnostic and longitudinal tasks on ADNI (n=802), outperforming six baseline models.

Conclusion: The proposed multimodal framework effectively enhances AD analysis by improving cross-modal alignment, data augmentation, and multitask learning without task-specific fine-tuning.

Abstract: Alzheimer's Disease (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder, and longitudinal analysis is critical for early detection and effective intervention. Developing models capable of multimodal and multitask analysis enables a more comprehensive understanding of AD progression. However, multimodal learning remains challenged by cross-modal misalignment, non-Euclidean surface representations of cortical data, and limited data availability in small-sample clinical settings. In this work, we propose an augmented spherical data-driven multimodal framework for multitask AD analysis. A spherical diffusion model is first trained to generate paired cortical thickness and Tau PET Standardized Uptake Value Ratio (SUVR) data, enabling structurally consistent multimodal augmentation on cortical surfaces while preserving anatomical correspondence. The augmented data are subsequently used to train a contrastive learning model that learns aligned and fused cross-modal representations. This design strengthens multimodal integration and encourages more balanced representation learning. The learned imaging features are further integrated with tabular cognitive assessments and demographic variables, and processed using an in-context learning model to perform both classification and regression tasks without task-specific fine-tuning. Experiments on the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) dataset ($n = 802$) demonstrate consistent performance improvements across five diagnostic and longitudinal tasks, outperforming six baseline models.

</details>


### [626] [Variable-Rate Deep Image Compression based on Low-Rank Adaptation by Progressive Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16107)
*Xing-Yu Xu,Chen-Hsiu Huang,Ja-Ling Wu*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: Proposes LoRAM, a progressive learning approach using Low-Rank Adaptation for variable-rate image compression that adds no inference cost, achieves competitive performance, and saves significant storage, data, and training resources compared to multi-model methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Variable-rate image compression remains an unresolved issue; existing methods either use multiple networks (high storage cost) or single models (higher computational complexity and reduced performance).

Method: Introduces LoRA Rate-Adaptive Module (LoRAM) based on parameter-efficient fine-tuning (Low-Rank Adaptation). LoRA weights are merged via re-parameterization, adding no extra inference cost.

Result: Competitive performance while saving 99% in parameter storage, 90% in datasets, and 97% in training steps compared to multi-model methods.

Conclusion: LoRAM enables efficient variable-rate compression without additional computational overhead during inference, offering a practical solution for resource-constrained scenarios.

Abstract: In the digital age, image compression is crucial for numerous applications, including web media, streaming services, high-resolution medical imaging, and connected vehicle networks, enabling efficient data storage and transmission. With the increasing demand for high-quality image communication, the need for advanced compression techniques becomes increasingly critical. Numerous Deep Image Compression (DIC) techniques have recently been introduced, showing impressive performance compared to traditional standards. However, variable-rate image compression remains an unresolved issue. Specific DIC methods deploy multiple networks to attain different compression rates, whereas others use a single model, which often results in higher computational complexity and reduced performance. This work proposes a progressive learning approach for variable-rate image compression based on the parameter-efficient fine-tuning method, the Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). We introduce an additional LoRA Rate-Adaptive Module (LoRAM) in DIC methods. Due to the re-parameterized merging of LoRA, our proposed method does not introduce additional computational complexity during inference. Compared to methods utilizing multiple models, comprehensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves competitive performance, saving 99\% in parameter storage, 90% in datasets, and 97% in training steps.

</details>


### [627] [MOSAIC: Mobile Object Segmentation under Adverse Imaging Conditions for Rapid L-PBF Keyhole Behavior Characterization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16186)
*Garrett Mathesen,Kyle Mumm,Carter Taylor,Jian Cao*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: MOSAIC is a fast, accurate segmentation algorithm for analyzing keyhole dynamics in L-PBF processes, achieving F1=0.894 and processing at 19.9ms per frame on CPU—outperforming YOLO and SAM in speed and accuracy without requiring manual labeling or training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable rapid, real-time analysis of keyhole dynamics in L-PBF processes during active beamline experiments, overcoming the limitations of slow manual labeling and computationally expensive ML models.

Method: MOSAIC (Mobile Object Segmentation algorithm for experiments under Adverse Imaging Conditions) performs segmentation on moving-window cropped frames (~150x250 px) using CPU-based processing, without requiring manual labeling or model training.

Result: Validated on 12 samples with average F1=0.894 and precision=0.953; processes at 19.9ms per frame on CPU, compared to 54ms (YOLO) and 5284ms (SAM) per frame.

Conclusion: MOSAIC is a robust, efficient alternative to ML-based methods for analyzing keyhole dynamics in L-PBF, enabling real-time monitoring without manual labeling or GPU dependence.

Abstract: In laser powder bed fusion (L-PBF) processes, the rapid evolution of gas and fluid interactions complicates our ability to properly monitor or control the process, with unstable keyholes leading to porosity and spatter formation. High-speed operando x-ray imaging of the keyhole has been used to better understand the impact of these interactions on the monitoring and control of the L-PBF process. MOSAIC, a Mobile Object Segmentation algorithm for experiments under Adverse Imaging Conditions, is designed to perform rapid analysis of keyhole dynamics during active beamline experimentation without needing time consuming manual labeling or model training. Validation studies performed on 12 unique samples proved the robustness of MOSAIC with an average F1 score of 0.894 and a precision of 0.953 when compared to manually segmented images, performing equally or better than the SAM and YOLO machine learning methods tested. MOSAIC is efficient, processing frames cropped to a moving window approximately 150x250 pixels at 19.9 milliseconds per image on CPU, compared to 54 and 5284 milliseconds per image for inference on CPU for YOLO and SAM models.

</details>


### [628] [Input-Dependent Fisher Information for Local Sensitivity Analysis of Medical Image Classifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16362)
*Sourya Sengupta. Mark A. Anastasio*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a local sensitivity analysis framework using the input-dependent Fisher Information Matrix (iFIM) to interpret deep neural networks for medical image classification, showing that high-sensitivity iFIM components are more strongly tied to changes in predictive confidence than low-sensitivity ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep neural networks perform well in medical image classification but are black-box; existing post-hoc interpretation methods provide heuristic visualizations with indirect relation to the classifier's predictive distribution.

Method: The framework uses the input-dependent Fisher Information Matrix (iFIM) with a Gram-matrix formulation to recover its nonzero eigenspectrum without forming the full matrix, then projects input images into high-sensitivity and orthogonal low-sensitivity components.

Result: High-sensitivity iFIM components are more strongly coupled to changes in predictive confidence and classification performance than low-sensitivity complementary components, validated on controlled and clinical medical image classification tasks.

Conclusion: The iFIM framework is a principled tool for analyzing local decision sensitivity and complements existing attribution-based interpretability methods in medical imaging.

Abstract: Deep neural networks have achieved strong performance in medical image classification, but often work like black-box. Commonly used post-hoc interpretation methods often provide heuristic visualizations whose relationship to the classifier's predictive distribution is indirect. This work introduces a local sensitivity analysis framework based on the input-dependent Fisher Information Matrix (iFIM) of a trained classifier. The iFIM characterizes how the classifier's predictive distribution changes under infinitesimal perturbations of the input image. By using a Gram-matrix formulation, the nonzero eigenspectrum of the iFIM can be recovered without explicitly forming the full image-dimensional Fisher matrix. The leading iFIM eigenspace is then used to project an input image into a high local-sensitivity component and its orthogonal component. These components provide a model-intrinsic description of local predictive sensitivity, rather than a conventional pixel-wise attribution heatmap or a causal segmentation of task-relevant anatomy. The framework is evaluated on controlled and clinical medical image classification tasks using multiple classifier architectures. Perturbation-based experiments show that high-sensitivity iFIM components are more strongly coupled to changes in predictive confidence and classification performance than lower-sensitivity complementary components. The results support the iFIM framework as a principled tool for analyzing local decision sensitivity and for complementing existing attribution-based interpretability methods in medical imaging.

</details>


### [629] [Optimizing Multiple Feature Types for Image Inpainting in the Linear and Nonlinear Setting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16581)
*Vassillen Chizhov,Ferdinand Jost,Joachim Weickert*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a framework for inpainting-based image compression that allows arbitrary features (derivatives, integrals, etc.) beyond pixel values, with automatic optimization of feature type and location, achieving 1.82-2.76 dB PSNR improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing inpainting-based compression relies almost exclusively on pixel locations and color values, limiting its flexibility and performance. The authors aim to develop a more general paradigm by incorporating arbitrary features described by linear or nonlinear equations.

Method: The authors propose a general theory and practical framework that enables arbitrary features (derivatives of arbitrary order, local integrals) combinable with linear or nonlinear inpainting operators, along with an algorithm that automatically optimizes both location and type of selected features.

Result: Increasing feature types from 1 to 5 yields consistent quality gain: 2.76 dB PSNR improvement for harmonic inpainting and 1.82 dB for edge-enhancing diffusion inpainting with the same amount of stored data.

Conclusion: Allowing different types of optimized features transforms inpainting-based compression into a more general, versatile, and powerful paradigm with significant practical performance gains.

Abstract: Inpainting-based compression stores a carefully optimized subset of the full image data and reconstructs the missing data by inpainting. The quality of these lossy codecs depends decisively on the stored data. So far, these data consist almost exclusively of pixel locations along with their grayscale or color values. In the present paper, we present a general theory and a practical framework that allows to incorporate arbitrary features which can be described by linear or nonlinear equations. This includes e.g. derivatives of arbitrary order or local integrals. Our features can be combined with linear or nonlinear inpainting operators. Moreover, we present an algorithm that automatically optimizes the location and the type of the selected feature. The approach of allowing different types of optimized features turns inpainting-based compression into a more general, versatile and powerful paradigm. Our experiments report a consistent quality gain when increasing the number of feature types from 1 to 5. With the same amount of stored data, the average peak signal-to-noise improvement is 2.76 dB for harmonic (homogeneous diffusion) inpainting, and 1.82 dB for edge-enhancing diffusion inpainting.

</details>


### [630] [Conditioning Deep Anatomical Prior Knowledge for Reconstruction of Multispectral Optoacoustic Tomography Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16835)
*Sarah Franceschin,Lukas Imanuel Scheel-Platz,Philipp Haim,Guillaume Zahnd,Vasilis Ntziachristos,Dominik Jüstel*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: APRECOT uses probabilistic anatomical priors to simultaneously segment tissues and reconstruct chromophore compositions from MSOT images, improving accuracy over sequential methods in silico.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MSOT analysis lacks probabilistic anatomical priors to guide tissue segmentation and chromophore reconstruction, and most methods handle these tasks sequentially, causing error accumulation.

Method: APRECOT incorporates probabilistic models of anatomical structures and tissue properties to enable joint (simultaneous) segmentation and bulk chromophore composition reconstruction.

Result: In silico proof-of-concept showed that APRECOT with probabilistic anatomical context significantly improves bulk chromophore concentration estimation over reference methods (no anatomical context or sequential strategies).

Conclusion: This work is an essential step toward an MSOT imaging mode that directly provides clinically relevant information like tissue oxygenation dynamics or disease-related changes.

Abstract: Accurately delineating tissues and reconstructing their chromophore compositions from Multispectral Optoacoustic Tomography (MSOT) images is a key challenge in optoacoustic imaging. The difficulty arises because light fluence distributions within tissue intrinsically depend on spectral optical properties, making the inverse problem inherently ill-posed. Currently, there is a lack of studies leveraging a priori probabilistic anatomical knowledge to guide tissue segmentation and infer chromophore composition. Moreover, most current studies address these two tasks sequentially, which can result in errors accumulating. through the process. To address these issues, we present Anatomical Priors for Reconstruction of Optoacoustic Tomography (APRECOT), a method that leverages probabilistic models of anatomical structures and tissue properties, to enable simultaneous segmentation of tissues and reconstruction of their bulk chromophore compositions. In this proof-of-concept using in-silico data, we show that incorporating probabilistic anatomical context strongly improves the accuracy of bulk chromophore concentration estimation compared to reference methods that do not use any anatomical context or use sequential strategies. This work represents an essential step towards an MSOT imaging mode that directly provides clinically relevant information, such as imaging tissue oxygenation dynamics or disease-related changes in tissue composition.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [631] [Bayesian Optimization for Learning Nonlinear MPC in Autonomous Agent Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14763)
*Lorenzo Ortolani,Gabriel Voss,Gabriele Beltrami,Francesco Dorati,Tommaso Felice Banfi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a map-free, LiDAR-based navigation framework for mobile robots that integrates reactive rolling-horizon planning with nonlinear MPC, uses Bayesian optimization (TPE) for parameter tuning, and achieves up to 90% navigation success rate on a Unitree Go2 quadruped in both simulation and real-world deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time autonomous navigation in dynamic, unknown environments remains a fundamental challenge for mobile robotics, especially regarding parameter sensitivity and transfer from simulation to real hardware.

Method: The framework combines rolling-horizon planning, LiDAR-based Gaussian occupancy representation, A* search for collision-free trajectories, nonlinear MPC with sigmoid obstacle barrier using CasADi/IPOPT, and offline Bayesian optimization via Tree-structured Parzen Estimators (TPE) with Gaussian Process surrogate analysis.

Result: Achieves up to 90.0% navigation success rate on physical hardware, with 38.9% average improvement in evaluation metrics across simulated environments. Parameters tuned in simulation transfer effectively to real-world deployment without additional tuning.

Conclusion: The proposed map-free, robot-agnostic framework demonstrates robust navigation performance in dynamic environments, with effective sim-to-real transfer of optimized parameters.

Abstract: Real-time autonomous navigation in dynamic, unknown environments remains a fundamental challenge for mobile robotics. We propose a map-free framework that tightly integrates reactive rolling-horizon planning with nonlinear Model Predictive Control (MPC). At each control cycle, a LiDAR-based Gaussian occupancy representation is constructed and used to generate collision-free trajectories via A* search, which are then tracked by a CasADi/IPOPT MPC formulation incorporating a smooth sigmoid obstacle barrier. To improve robustness to parameter sensitivity, we adopt an offline Bayesian optimization scheme based on Tree-structured Parzen Estimators (TPE), which identifies near-optimal controller parameters with respect to a composite navigation objective. In addition, a Gaussian Process surrogate is used to analyze parameter sensitivity and provide insight into the optimization landscape.
  The proposed framework is robot-agnostic and is evaluated on the Unitree Go2 quadruped in simulation using Gazebo, followed by deployment on the physical robot. Experimental results show that parameters tuned in simulation transfer effectively to hardware, maintaining comparable performance without additional tuning. The full system achieves up to a 90.0\% navigation success rate when deployed, along with a 38.9\% average improvement in the evaluation metrics across simulated environments.

</details>


### [632] [Synthetic-to-Real Pipeline for Safe Landing Zone Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14767)
*Shrikant Banerjee,Reza Faieghi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a synthetic data generation pipeline and Transformer-based segmentation model (OneFormer) for autonomous UAV landing, using procedural simulation with domain randomization to eliminate manual annotation. A Euclidean Distance Transform-based landing module identifies safe zones. Validated on UAVid dataset and real-world footage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UAVs need autonomous landing in unstructured environments but lack annotated aerial datasets. Manual annotation is costly and scarce, hindering development of safe landing perception systems.

Method: 1) Procedural synthetic data engine with photorealistic urban scenes and automated semantic annotations via domain randomization. 2) Fine-tuning Transformer-based OneFormer on synthetic data only. 3) Deterministic landing module using Euclidean Distance Transform (EDT) and dynamic inference to find largest inscribed safe landing zones with obstacle clearance buffers.

Result: Quantitative: Robust semantic segmentation on UAVid benchmark. Qualitative: Successfully identifies collision-free landing sites in unseen real-world UAV footage.

Conclusion: High-fidelity procedural simulation can eliminate manual annotation needs while enabling edge-deployable situational awareness for autonomous UAV recovery, bridging the sim-to-real gap.

Abstract: As Uncrewed Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) transition toward higher levels of autonomy, the ability to perform unassisted recovery in non-cooperative, unstructured environments becomes critical. Achieving safe autonomous landing requires high-fidelity semantic resolution to distinguish navigable terrain from hazardous obstacles, yet development is often hindered by the scarcity of annotated aerial datasets. This work proposes a comprehensive perception and data generation pipeline designed to bridge the sim-to-real gap for autonomous landing tasks. We introduce a procedural synthetic data engine that generates photorealistic urban environments with automated semantic annotations through domain randomization. A Transformer-based OneFormer architecture is fine-tuned exclusively on this synthetic data, leveraging multi-head self-attention mechanisms for global context resolution. To ensure operational safety, a deterministic landing module utilizes a Euclidean Distance Transform (EDT) and dynamic inference logic to identify the largest inscribed safe landing zones while maintaining strict clearance buffers around obstacles. Quantitative benchmarking against the UAVid dataset demonstrates robust semantic segmentation performance, while qualitative validation on real-world UAV footage confirms the system's ability to identify collision-free landing sites in unseen environments. Our results highlight the potential of high-fidelity procedural simulation to eliminate the need for manual annotation while providing robust, edge-deployable situational awareness for autonomous UAV recovery.

</details>


### [633] [Deep Learning-Based Lunar Crater Terrain Relative Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14776)
*Batu Candan,Simone Servadio*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A deep-learning crater detector combined with an Extended Kalman Filter enables terrain relative navigation for lunar landings, recovering from initial 5 km errors to reduce navigation error to a few hundred meters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate position estimation is critical for autonomous lunar landings in dangerous environments with sparse terrain features, motivating the need for robust terrain relative navigation.

Method: The method combines a deep-learning crater detector (designed for the NASA Crater Detection Challenge) with an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF). Crater features from monocular orbital images are matched to a global database using Hungarian assignment and consensus-based outlier removal, then fed into the EKF for spacecraft pose estimation in the LCLF frame with altitude aiding.

Result: Simulations show that even with an initial 5 km position offset, the TRN system can recover and reduce navigation error to a few hundred meters.

Conclusion: The proposed TRN algorithm effectively recovers from large initial position errors. However, maintaining crater feature correspondences requires matching image resolution and scene scales to the detector's training distribution.

Abstract: Accurate position estimation is crucial for the successful implementation of future lunar landings using autonomous vehicles, especially in dangerous environments with sparse terrain features. In this paper, we propose a terrain relative navigation (TRN) algorithm combining our deep-learning crater detector, which was designed specifically for the NASA Crater Detection Challenge problem, and an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF). Our detector analyzes crater features from the monocular images acquired from orbit, and their matches with craters from a global database are identified via a Hungarian assignment approach followed by the consensus-based outliers removal method. The estimated measurements are then used to refine an EKF, where spacecraft pose estimation in the Lunar-Centered Lunar-Fixed (LCLF) frame of reference, augmented with altitude aiding information, constrains radial drift. The simulation results indicate that even if the spacecraft is off from its actual location up to 5 km, TRN could recover from this situation, achieving navigation error reduction to a few hundred meters. It should be noted that in order to maintain crater feature correspondences, it is important to match the image resolution and the scales within the scene to the detector training set distribution.

</details>


### [634] [Computing Smooth Geodesics under Two-Sided Curvature Bounds with Applications to Robotics and Image Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14794)
*Da Chen,Zhenjiang Li,Jean-Marie Mirebeau,Xuecheng Tai,Jinglin Zhang,Wei Zhang,Laurent D. Cohen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a curvature-bounded geodesic model under the HJB PDE framework that enforces arbitrary upper and lower bounds on curvature, enabling smooth path tracking with curvature constraints for applications like robot path planning and image structure tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenging problem of tracking minimal paths where curvature is constrained by arbitrary upper and lower bounds, providing stronger geometric control than existing curvature regularization approaches.

Method: A curvature-bounded geodesic model is developed within the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) PDE framework, along with a discretization scheme for the Hamiltonian and HJB PDE that incorporates curvature bounds for efficient numerical solving.

Result: Numerical experiments demonstrate the model as a powerful and robust tool for finding satisfactory paths in robot path planning and curvilinear structure tracking from images.

Conclusion: The proposed curvature-bounded geodesic model provides strong geometric control over minimal paths with bounded curvature, serving as an effective solution for path planning and image analysis applications.

Abstract: Curvature of planar curves serves as a key regularization term for computing second-order minimal paths, due to its tight relevance to desirable geometric properties such as smoothness, rigidity, and elasticity. In this paper, we tackle a more challenging problem in computational physics and geometry problem: tracking minimal paths whose curvature is constrained by arbitrary upper and lower bounds. For that purpose, we propose a new curvature-bounded geodesic model, developed under the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) partial differential equation (PDE) framework. It provides strong geometric control over minimal paths by enforcing curvature range constraints, whose paths are smooth and of bounded curvature limitation. We also present a discretization scheme for the Hamiltonian and the HJB PDE incorporating curvature bounds, allowing efficient solver for estimating numerical solutions to the model. Finally, we illustrate the capability of the proposed curvature-bounded geodesic model in applications of robot path planning and curvilinear structures tracking from images. Numerical experiments demonstrate that the proposed curvature-bounded geodesic model serves as a powerful and robust tool for finding satisfactory paths.

</details>


### [635] [TacStyle: Personalizing Tactile Robot Policies using Structured Behavior Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14862)
*Kevin Robledo,Matías I. Torres Galaz,Kumar Dixhant Rai,Shelly Sara Ulman,Tasmia Tasrin,Heramb Nemlekar*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes using language to reason over preferred robot behaviors rather than directly generating them, by learning a structured latent representation of user preferences and using a foundation model to interpret this space for fine-grained behavioral adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots need to adapt behaviors to individual user preferences (e.g., force applied while folding laundry), but existing language-conditioned policies require detailed preference labels and struggle to provide fine-grained control over continuous behaviors.

Method: Learn a structured latent representation organizing user preferences by trajectory differences, then use a foundation model to interpret this latent space and select appropriate values for desired behaviors based on preference prompts.

Result: Simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that selecting behaviors from an intuitively structured latent space enables more precise adaptation to user preferences with fewer preference labels compared to language-conditioned policies.

Conclusion: Using language to reason over preferred behaviors (via latent space interpretation) rather than directly generating them allows for finer control and greater label efficiency in preference-adaptive robotic systems.

Abstract: Robotic systems that assist humans should be capable of adapting their behaviors to individual user preferences. For instance, users may want a robot arm to adjust the amount of force it applies while folding their laundry or cleaning furniture. Natural language provides an intuitive way for humans to communicate such preferences. Recent progress in language-conditioned robot policies has shown that robots can successfully use language prompts to determine what task to perform. However, extending the same approach to realize how the task should be performed requires detailed labels describing the preferences or styles of trajectories in the task data. Not only is collecting such annotations challenging, but conditioning directly on these labels may also fail to provide fine-grained control over a continuous range of behaviors. For example, it can be difficult to convey the exact force that a robot must apply through abstract instructions like "apply a bit more pressure than before". Therefore, in this work, we propose using language to reason over preferred behaviors instead of directly generating them. We first learn a structured latent representation that organizes user preferences according to differences in the corresponding trajectories. Then, given a preference prompt, we use a foundation model to interpret this latent space and choose a value that produces the desired behavior. Through both simulation and real-world experiments, we show that selecting robot behaviors from an intuitively structured latent space enables more precise adaptation to user preferences while requiring significantly fewer preference labels than language-conditioned policies.

</details>


### [636] [VANDERER: Map-Free Exploration using Future-Aware and Visual-Curiosity-Guided Diffusion Policy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14879)
*Venkata Naren Devarakonda,Raktim Gautam Goswami,Prashanth Krishnamurthy,Farshad Khorrami*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VANDERER is an exploration framework for mobile agents that uses a Visual Curiosity Module with pre-trained diffusion policies to guide exploration using only monocular cameras, outperforming baselines by 13.4% more area explored.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile agents need efficient exploration strategies, but traditional occupancy-map-based methods struggle with sensor-constrained settings like monocular cameras, motivating the need for an alternative approach.

Method: VANDERER uses a Visual Curiosity Module (VCM) that predicts action outcomes via a navigation world model, evaluates them with a curiosity cost, and guides a diffusion policy to generate exploration-maximizing actions using only monocular image data.

Result: VANDERER explores 13.4% more area than NoMaD across diverse simulated environments, and reveals a direct correlation between visual and geometric curiosity in outdoor settings.

Conclusion: VANDERER demonstrates that visual curiosity can effectively drive exploration in sensor-constrained agents, leveraging the relationship between visual and geometric cues for efficient mapping.

Abstract: Mobile agents require efficient exploration strategies to map unseen environments and autonomously plan tasks. Traditional methods rely on generating occupancy maps and optimizing the sequence in which unexplored regions are visited. However, in sensor-constrained settings, such as those limited to monocular cameras, generating accurate occupancy maps is challenging. To address this, we propose VANDERER, an exploration framework that leverages a Visual Curiosity Module (VCM) to guide pre-trained diffusion policies using only monocular image data. This curiosity module predicts the outcomes of proposed actions via a navigation world model and evaluates them through a curiosity cost. The cost then guides the diffusion process toward generating actions that maximize exploration. Evaluated across diverse simulated environments, VANDERER consistently outperforms established baselines, exploring an average of 13.4% more area than NoMaD. Our results reveal a direct correlation between visual and geometric curiosity in outdoor environments, demonstrating that VANDERER can effectively leverage this relationship for efficient exploration using sensor-constrained agents.

</details>


### [637] [DynaHMRC: Decentralized Heterogeneous Multi-Robot Collaboration for Dynamic Tasks with Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14882)
*Wenhao Yu,Yu'ang Xie,Yifan Duan,Jie Peng,Guanting Ye,Ka-Veng Yuen,Yanyong Zhang,Jianmin Ji*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DynaHMRC is a decentralized framework using role-aware LLM agents for coordinating heterogeneous multi-robot systems in long-horizon tasks, outperforming baselines with higher success rates and fewer action/communication steps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based multi-robot coordination faces three key limitations: centralized schedulers scale poorly with team size and complexity, task formulations lack dynamic adaptation, and domain-specific data scarcity hinders specialized robotic reasoning.

Method: A decentralized framework where each robot acts as a role-aware LLM agent, organized as a four-stage closed-loop process: self-description, task allocation with leadership bidding, leader election, and reflective execution, supported by executable robot interfaces.

Result: DynaHMRC achieves higher success rates than strong baselines with fewer action and communication steps, and demonstrates promising scalability trends as team size grows.

Conclusion: Decentralized role-aware LLM agent design effectively mitigates context bottlenecks and supports flexible collaboration for heterogeneous multi-robot teams, with empirical analysis guiding domain-specific dataset construction and fine-tuning.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) provide robots with richer task understanding and adaptability, making them promising for coordinating heterogeneous multi-robot systems in long-horizon tasks. Despite this potential, several challenges remain underexplored: (1) Centralized LLM schedulers scale poorly as team size and environmental complexity increase. A single model must process excessive contextual information, and long-context approximation may degrade reasoning quality; (2) Existing task formulations insufficiently consider dynamic settings, while robust adaptation to evolving task conditions is essential for real-world deployment; (3) Domain-specific data scarcity limits specialized robotic reasoning, making proprietary general-purpose models inefficient for expert tasks. To address these limitations, we propose DynaHMRC, a decentralized framework in which each robot acts as a role-aware LLM agent. This design mitigates the single-model context bottleneck and supports flexible collaboration across heterogeneous team configurations. DynaHMRC organizes collaboration as a four-stage closed-loop process: self-description, task allocation with leadership bidding, leader election, and reflective execution, supported by executable robot interfaces. We further develop a benchmark covering three task families, four dynamic variations, and six team configurations to systematically study dynamic task modeling. In addition, we conduct an empirical analysis to guide the construction of domain-specific expert datasets and fine-tune pretrained LLMs to improve specialized competence. Experiments show that DynaHMRC achieves higher success rates than strong baselines with fewer action and communication steps, while demonstrating promising scalability trends as team size grows within the evaluated settings.

</details>


### [638] [Multimodal Physiological Assessment of Contact-Rich Physical Human-Robot Interaction Under Varying Environmental Conditions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14969)
*Yanyi Chen,Xi Wang,Min Deng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A multimodal study of contact-rich pHRI tasks under 18 environmental conditions reveals that operators maintain stable task performance by increasing physiological effort (especially autonomic workload via SCL) to compensate for thermal stress, motivating physiology-aware robot control systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional task-centric evaluations of pHRI overlook physiological burdens imposed by environmental stressors (temperature, noise, illuminance) in real-world settings.

Method: Multimodal empirical study with 18 combinations of temperature, noise, and illuminance; synchronous recording of EDA, sEMG, eye-tracking, and subjective comfort ratings during contact-rich tracing tasks.

Result: Task performance remained stable across all conditions; tonic SCL increased with temperature while physical/cognitive workload unchanged; perceived comfort showed no significant association with performance metrics.

Conclusion: Operators employ a compensatory mechanism—increasing physiological effort to suppress thermal discomfort—which motivates physiology-aware control architectures that use real-time physiological metrics to reduce operator workload.

Abstract: Physical human-robot interaction (pHRI) in real-world settings exposes operators to fluctuating environmental conditions during contact-rich tasks. Traditional task-centric evaluations overlook the physiological burdens imposed by these stressors. Therefore, we conducted a multimodal empirical study involving contact-rich tracing tasks under 18 distinct combinations of temperature, acoustic noise, and illuminance. Synchronously, we recorded electrodermal activity (EDA), surface electromyography (sEMG), eye-tracking data, and subjective environmental comfort ratings. Evaluating these physiological signals alongside execution data revealed hidden physiological costs not captured by objective performance. The results revealed that task performance remained stable across all environmental conditions. Autonomic workload, indexed by tonic skin conductance level (SCL), increased with temperature, while physical and cognitive workload were unaffected. Perceived environmental comfort showed no significant association with tracing error or completion time. These findings reveal a compensatory mechanism where operators maintain consistent performance by increasing their physiological effort to suppress thermal discomfort. Such insight motivates the development of physiology-aware control architectures that leverage real-time physiological metrics to reduce operator workload in unstructured environments.

</details>


### [639] [Inference-time Policy Steering via Vision and Touch](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14981)
*Yilin Wu,Zilin Si,Zeynep Temel,Oliver Kroemer,Andrea Bajcsy*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ViTaL is a visuo-tactile inference-time steering framework for contact-rich robot manipulation that uses a bi-level optimization (visual high-level mode selection + tactile low-level action editing) with a learned visuo-tactile latent world model, achieving 51% improvement over base policy and outperforming unimodal steering by 33%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior inference-time steering methods use only visual verification, which is insufficient for contact-rich manipulation that requires understanding both global task progress and subtle local interactions like contact force.

Method: ViTaL formulates multimodal guidance as a bi-level optimization: (1) high-level visual sampling-and-verification for long-horizon mode selection, and (2) low-level tactile-guided diffusion editing for short-horizon action refinement. It uses a learned visuo-tactile latent world model with semantically aligned visual and tactile verifiers, including a text-conditioned tactile reward that scores predicted tactile futures in latent space.

Result: Across three real-world contact-rich manipulation tasks, ViTaL improves overall success by 51% over base policy, outperforms unimodal steering by at least 33%, and exceeds naive multimodal fusion by at least 20%.

Conclusion: Visuo-tactile multimodal steering significantly improves contact-rich manipulation, with the bi-level optimization framework effectively leveraging complementary strengths of vision (global task progress) and touch (local contact requirements).

Abstract: Inference-time steering adapts pre-trained generative robot policies during deployment by verifying candidate actions before execution. While prior methods typically perform this verification only with visual observations, vision alone is often insufficient for contact-rich manipulation, where success depends on both global task progress and subtle local interactions such as contact force. We introduce ViTaL, a visuo-tactile inference-time steering framework that formulates multimodal guidance as a bi-level optimization problem. At the high level, visual sampling-and-verification performs long-horizon mode selection, deciding what behavior the robot should execute. At the low level, tactile-guided diffusion editing refines the selected action sequence over a shorter horizon to satisfy local contact requirements. To support outcome-based steering, ViTaL learns a visuo-tactile latent world model and employs semantically aligned visual and tactile verifiers, including a novel text-conditioned tactile reward that scores predicted tactile futures directly in latent space. Across three real-world contact-rich manipulation tasks, ViTaL improves overall success by 51% over the base policy, outperforms unimodal steering by at least 33%, and exceeds naive multimodal fusion by at least 20%. Website: https://yilin-wu98.github.io/vital_website.
