Method: SAPS: a shared autonomy framework with three arbitration strategies (including dynamic Cosine-similarity) that blend human and policy actions at the action level, requiring no retraining, auxiliary models, or architectural changes.

Result: Up to 82% improvement in task success rates over autonomous execution in simulation (LIBERO, LIBERO-PRO, CALVIN) and real-world; reduced human intervention vs pure teleoperation; faster task completion than both autonomous and pure teleoperation.

Conclusion: Action-level shared autonomy is a practical, model-agnostic approach for reliably deploying generalist robot policies with human operators, with applications in assistive teleoperation and scalable data collection.

Abstract: Recent advancements in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated impressive generalist capabilities in robot manipulation, yet these policies can be brittle under out-of-distribution spatial and semantic perturbations. While human teleoperation offers reliable recovery, it can demand high cognitive load and precise manual control, and existing policy steering methods often require auxiliary models or sampler modifications. In this work, we introduce Shared Autonomy for Policy Steering (SAPS), a framework that blends real-time human teleoperation commands with pretrained policy actions at the action level. SAPS requires no policy retraining, auxiliary dynamics models, or architectural modifications. We propose and evaluate three arbitration strategies to balance human and VLA policy control, including a dynamic Cosine-similarity arbitration strategy that computes the geometric agreement between human and policy actions. Across evaluations in simulation (LIBERO, LIBERO-PRO, CALVIN) and on real-world robot hardware, SAPS improves task success rates over autonomous execution by up to 82% in both simulation and the real world. Furthermore, our approach drastically reduces human intervention compared to pure teleoperation, while simultaneously achieving faster task completion times than both autonomous execution and pure teleoperation. These results demonstrate that action-level shared autonomy is a practical, model-agnostic approach for reliably deploying generalist robot policies in real-world contexts involving a human operator,with promising applications in assistive teleoperation and scalable data collection.

</details>


### [668] [Perfect Demo Makes Poor Teacher: Learning Robust Alignment from Critical Motion Segments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15587)
*Mingyu Liu,Zeju Li,Jiuhe Shu,Hanqing Wang,Yuhao Chao,Hao Chen,Chunhua Shen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Fluent expert demonstrations can be poor teachers for fine-grained robot manipulation because they compress critical decision moments. The paper proposes STAIR, a spatio-temporal dynamic feature that extracts motion-aware supervision from trajectories, recovering performance close to using deliberate (slowed-down) demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper identifies a counterintuitive failure: fluent demonstrations, typically considered gold standard, actually hinder robot learning for precision tasks because they compress alignment and recovery actions into too brief a window, starving the policy of supervision exactly where it matters most.

Method: Two-level solution: (1) Data level - slowing down near alignment and resampling critical segments helps mainly by broadening recovery state coverage; (2) Representation level - STAIR, a compact spatio-temporal dynamic feature that bridges vision-language models and action experts, distilling short-horizon motion from trajectories into dense, motion-aware supervision.

Result: STAIR trained on fluent data achieves 50.0% to 62.2% overall performance, approaching the 64.4% achieved with deliberate (slowed-down) demonstrations, recovering most of the deliberate-demonstration gain.

Conclusion: The results advocate for a more pedagogical approach to robot data collection, optimizing for machine learnability rather than just human efficiency. Demonstrations should be designed to teach effectively, not just be executed fluently.

Abstract: Expert demonstrations are widely assumed to be the gold standard for robot imitation learning. Yet for fine-grained manipulation such as insertion, stacking, and alignment, we uncover a counterintuitive failure mode: fluent demonstrations can be poor teachers. A skilled teleoperator compresses the decisive moments of alignment and recovery into a brief temporal window, leaving the policy flooded with redundant free-space motion and starved of supervision exactly where precision determines success. We address this bottleneck at two levels. At the data level, slowing down near alignment and resampling critical segments both help, yet the gain comes mainly from broadening the coverage of recovery states the policy must learn, not from reweighting frames it already has. Such data-side fixes, however, leave the policy's per-frame view untouched: a single image still maps directly to an action, and the local motion that governs correction stays implicit. We therefore turn to the representation level and introduce STAIR (\textbf{S}patio-\textbf{T}emporal feature \textbf{A}s an \textbf{I}nterface for \textbf{R}obot learning), a compact dynamic feature that bridges the vision-language model and the action expert, distilling the short-horizon motion already recorded in each trajectory into dense, motion-aware supervision. Trained on fluent data alone, STAIR recovers most of the deliberate-demonstration gain ($50.0$ to $62.2\%$ overall, approaching the $64.4\%$ of deliberate demonstrations). These results call for a more pedagogical view of robot data, optimized for machine learnability rather than human efficiency alone.

</details>


### [669] [Pixels to Proofs: Probabilistically-Safe Latent World Model Control via Parallel Conformal Robust MPC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15594)
*Devesh Nath,Anutam Srinivasan,Haoran Yin,Ruitong Jiang,Jeffrey Fang,Glen Chou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SLS² is a framework for safe vision-based robot control that combines learned latent world models with conformal prediction-calibrated robust MPC, using GPU-accelerated system level synthesis to enforce safety constraints during closed-loop execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable safe feedback motion planning from pixel observations, addressing the challenge that learned latent world models have imperfect predictions which can lead to unsafe behavior in the real system.

Method: 1) Trains an action-conditioned joint-embedding world model with compact Markovian latent states. 2) Uses conformal prediction to obtain calibrated latent error bounds and robust constraint sets. 3) Employs GPU-accelerated system level synthesis (SLS) robust MPC for trajectory optimization. 4) Learns and conformalizes a latent constraint checker for probabilistic safety constraints.

Result: Outperforms latent world-model and safe-planning baselines in both goal-reaching performance and safety on vision-based control tasks.

Conclusion: Combining learned latent dynamics with conformal prediction-based calibration and robust MPC is an effective approach for safe vision-based control, improving both task performance and safety guarantees.

Abstract: We present SLS^2, a framework for safe feedback motion planning from pixels using robust model predictive control (MPC) in learned latent world models. Our approach trains an action-conditioned joint-embedding world model with compact Markovian latent states, enabling efficient gradient-based trajectory optimization through learned latent dynamics. To enforce safety for the true system despite imperfect latent predictions, we inform a GPU-accelerated system level synthesis (SLS) robust MPC scheme with conformal prediction to obtain calibrated latent error bounds and robust latent-space constraint sets. We further learn and conformalize a latent constraint checker, allowing the SLS planner to impose probabilistic safety constraints during closed-loop execution. We evaluate our method on vision-based control tasks, where it improves both goal-reaching performance and safety over latent world-model and safe-planning baselines.

</details>


### [670] [Retrieve, Don't Retrain: Extending Vision Language Action Models to New Tasks at Test Time](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15631)
*Jeongeun Park,Juhan Park,Taekyung Kim,Sungjoon Choi,Dongyoon Han,Sangdoo Yun*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A retrieval-augmented vision-language-action policy is trained once on paired demonstrations and frozen; new tasks are added at deployment by indexing data into a retrieval pool instead of fine-tuning, with retrieval especially effective when combined with a video-generation-based world-action model (Cosmos Policy).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extending VLA policies to new tasks is costly, requiring task-specific teleoperated demonstrations and per-task fine-tuning for both data collection and compute.

Method: Train a retrieval-augmented policy once on paired demonstrations (target embodiment query + cheaper embodiment pool); freeze the policy; append new task demonstrations to retrieval pool at deployment; policy conditions on retrieved trajectories at each control step.

Result: On PushT, retrieval provides reusable high-level motion prior for cross-embodiment generalization to unseen goal angles; on RoboTwin 2.0, outperforms cross-embodiment baselines on unseen tasks; also demonstrated on a real robot.

Conclusion: Retrieval can replace per-task fine-tuning cost, especially effective with Cosmos Policy (video-generation-based WAM) where retrieval supplies coarse task progression and the future-image objective strengthens retrieval-conditioned actions.

Abstract: Extending a vision-language-action (VLA) policy to a new task typically requires task-specific teleoperated demonstrations and per-task fine-tuning, making adaptation costly in both data collection and compute. In this paper, we show that this target-side per-task adaptation cost can be replaced by retrieval. Our retrieval-augmented policy is trained once on paired demonstrations from the target embodiment (query) and a cheaper embodiment (pool, e.g., human-hand video), then frozen. New tasks are added at deployment by appending pool-side demonstrations to a retrieval pool. The frozen policy conditions on retrieved trajectories at every control step, so new tasks are absorbed by indexing data rather than updating parameters. Fine-tuning is needed only to take on a new, unseen embodiment, not for each new task. We show that retrieval improves policies beyond a specific backbone, including standard VLA policies, but its effect is especially pronounced in Cosmos Policy, a video-generation-based world-action model (WAM). In this setting, retrieval supplies coarse task progression, while the WAM's future-image objective provides an additional visual consistency signal that strengthens the retrieval-conditioned actions. On PushT, we study how retrieval provides a reusable high-level motion prior for cross-embodiment generalization to unseen goal angles, while on RoboTwin 2.0 our method outperforms cross-embodiment baselines on unseen tasks, and we additionally demonstrate the method on a real robot.

</details>


### [671] [TO-SoFiT: Topology Optimization of Hydraulic Soft Fish Tail Design for programmable undulating locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15645)
*A Padmaprabhan,Amal Shaji,Prabhat Kumar*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a topology optimization method for designing a hydraulic soft fish tail that achieves complex 3D swimming motions by balancing deformation efficiency, fluid-structure interaction, manufacturability, and stiffness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Soft robots lack systematic design frameworks for complex 3D motion (e.g., fish-like swimming), hindering development of hydraulic/pneumatic soft robots for underwater exploration.

Method: A topology optimization method using a Darcy law-based model with drainage term to simulate hydraulic pressure loads, translated into consistent nodal forces via FEM. A robust multi-criteria optimization balances deformation efficiency, fluid-structure interaction, manufacturability, and stiffness.

Result: The optimized 2D tail outperforms its rectangular counterpart. Cascaded tail segments enable programmable swimming patterns under different hydraulic loads, with tunable undulatory amplitudes and multiaxis bending for depth adjustment.

Conclusion: This work advances systematic codesign of hydraulic actuators and soft structures, enabling automated design of underwater robots with vertebrate-like agility. Code is publicly available.

Abstract: Soft robots leverage compliant materials to generate motion through controlled elastic deformation, making them ideal for delicate tasks such as underwater exploration and biomimetic marine systems. Although hydraulic/pneumatic actuation remains pivotal for such systems, the lack of systematic design frameworks has hindered the development of robots capable of complex 3D motion, such as fish-like swimming. This work introduces a topology optimization method to automate the design of a hydraulic soft fish tail, explicitly addressing the design-dependent coupling between fluidic actuation and structural deformation. We use a Darcy law-based model augmented with a drainage term to simulate spatially varying hydraulic pressure loads, translating these into consistent nodal forces via finite element analysis. The employed robust multi-criteria optimization formulation balances deformation efficiency, fluid-structure interaction, geometric manufacturability, and required stiffness for optimizing a bioinspired soft fish tail for 3D swimming kinematics. The optimized tail topology is incorporated into a pneumatic network actuator and computationally validated under various hydraulic loads, achieving tunable undulatory amplitudes and multiaxis bending for depth adjustment. The optimized 2D tail outperforms its rectangular counterpart. By cascading optimized tail segments, we demonstrate programmable swimming patterns in soft robotic fish tails at different hydraulic loads. This work advances the systematic codesign of hydraulic actuators and soft structures, offering a pathway to automate underwater robots with optimized design and vertebrate-like agility in confined aquatic environments. Our implementations and simulations are publicly available at 'https://github.com/PrabhatIn/TO-SoFiT'.

</details>


### [672] [PO-PDDL: Learning Symbolic POMDPs from Visual Demonstrations for Robot Planning Under Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15654)
*Wenjing Tang,Xuanjin Jin,Yuan Liu,Renming Huang,Cewu Lu,Panpan Cai*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces PO-PDDL, a symbolic POMDP formulation preserving PDDL's structure, and a demonstration-driven pipeline to learn these models from real-robot videos, enabling robust online belief-space planning under uncertainty. Experiments show it outperforms existing PDDL/POMDP approaches in long-horizon manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world robot task planning must handle both stochastic action execution and partial observability, but constructing POMDP models for real robotics domains remains difficult and labor-intensive.

Method: (1) Proposes PO-PDDL, a symbolic POMDP formulation with PDDL-like syntax. (2) Develops a demonstration-driven pipeline that reconstructs latent symbolic state trajectories from real-robot videos, identifies partial observability by detecting inconsistencies between inferred states and visual observations, and learns stochastic transition/observation models accordingly.

Result: On real-world long-horizon manipulation tasks, the method consistently outperforms existing PDDL and POMDP model-learning approaches, achieving robust task planning under uncertainty with significantly lower planning cost.

Conclusion: PO-PDDL enables reusable, belief-space planning under both perception and execution uncertainty, offering a practical bridge between symbolic PDDL planning and probabilistic POMDP models for real robotics.

Abstract: Real-world robot task planning must operate under both stochastic action execution and partial observability, yet constructing Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) models for real robotics domains remains difficult and labor-intensive. We introduce PO-PDDL, a symbolic formulation of POMDPs that preserves the relational structure and LLM-friendly syntax of the Planning Domain Definition Language (PDDL), while explicitly modeling partial observability, stochasticity, and beliefs. Building on this formulation, we propose a demonstration-driven pipeline for learning PO-PDDL models. The proposed method reconstructs latent symbolic state trajectories from real-robot execution videos, identifies partial observability via inconsistencies between inferred states and visual observations, and learns stochastic transition and observation models accordingly. The resulting PO-PDDL domains are reusable across tasks and enable online belief-space planning under both perception and execution uncertainty. Experiments on real-world long-horizon manipulation tasks show that our method consistently outperforms existing PDDL and POMDP model-learning approaches, achieving robust task planning under uncertainty with significantly lower planning cost.

</details>


### [673] [Learning New Tasks via Reusable Skills: Skill-Compositional Experts for Embodied Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15685)
*Shuaike Zhang,Shaokun Wang,Haoyu Tang,Jianlong Wu,Liqiang Nie*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SCE proposes a Skill-Compositional Experts framework for Embodied Continual Learning, using Compositional Skill Grounding to decompose demonstrations into reusable skills and Dual Execution-and-Transition Experts to enable new task learning via skill composition, improving retention and performance on LIBERO benchmarks and real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Embodied Continual Learning suffers from severe catastrophic forgetting because feature drift accumulates under closed-loop control and propagates through sequential decision-making. Additionally, existing methods lack explicit organization of skills for coherent task execution across continually evolving tasks.

Method: SCE framework with two key components: (1) Compositional Skill Grounding (CSG) that decomposes task demonstrations into reusable skills to build a skill base, and (2) Dual Execution-and-Transition Experts (DETE) where one branch ensures skill execution and the other supports transitions between skills for coherent behavior.

Result: Experiments on LIBERO benchmarks and real-world manipulation tasks show consistent improvement in retention and overall task performance. Feature drift analyses and ablation studies further verify the effectiveness of SCE.

Conclusion: SCE effectively addresses catastrophic forgetting in ECL by explicitly organizing reusable skills and enabling coherent skill composition for new task learning, outperforming existing methods.

Abstract: Embodied Continual Learning (ECL) aims to enable robots to continually acquire new manipulation tasks while retaining previously learned behaviors under closed-loop control. Compared with conventional continual learning, ECL suffers from more severe catastrophic forgetting. Feature drift accumulated under closed-loop control progressively propagates through sequential decision-making, leading to degradation of previously learned behaviors. A key challenge in ECL lies in structured skill reuse across continually evolving tasks, since existing methods primarily focus on skill learning without explicitly organizing them for coherent task execution. To address this issue, we propose SCE, a Skill-Compositional Experts framework for ECL. SCE builds a skill base via Compositional Skill Grounding (CSG), which decomposes task demonstrations into reusable skills. Based on this, Dual Execution-and-Transition Experts (DETE) enable new task learning through skill composition, where one branch ensures skill execution and the other supports transitions between skills for coherent behavior. Experiments on LIBERO benchmarks and real-world manipulation tasks demonstrate that SCE consistently improves retention and overall task performance. Further feature drift analyses and ablation studies verify the effectiveness of our method. Project website: https://eqcy.github.io/sce/.

</details>


### [674] [Can Causal Models Enhance Robot Navigation? Online Causal Adaptation for Real-Robot Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15691)
*Zhitao Liang,Alex Mitrevski,Emmanuel Dean,Karinne Ramirez-Amaro*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates that causal models can be integrated into real-robot navigation systems both for offline evaluation (predicting navigation competence) and online adaptation (intervening when competence is low), showing significant improvements in complex scenarios like cornering and obstacle avoidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Causality in robotics aims to produce more interpretable and flexible robot behaviours, but deploying causal models with existing real-world navigation systems remains understudied.

Method: The authors study causal model transfer in two ways: (i) offline evaluation module that predicts competence of recorded navigation trajectories, and (ii) online adaptation module that intervenes when predicted competence is low. Validated on a physical service robot patrolling corridors.

Result: Predicted competence correlates positively with path efficiency and negatively with path irregularities. Model predictions show strong agreement with human annotations (Cohen's kappa = 0.88). In online experiments, the method improves navigation in complex scenarios (cornering, obstacle avoidance) but provides limited benefit in simpler near-optimal scenarios.

Conclusion: Causal models developed for behavioural interpretation can be successfully integrated into real-robot navigation systems, and are particularly effective under increased task complexity.

Abstract: Causality in robotics aims to produce more interpretable and flexible robot behaviours by enabling robots to predict the consequences of their actions; however, deploying causal models with existing systems (e.g., navigation) operating in real environments remains understudied. This paper addresses the challenging problem of transferring causal models in real-robot experiments for a navigation scenario. We study this problem in two ways: (i) using the causal model as an offline evaluation module that predicts the competence of recorded real-robot navigation trajectories and relates it to quantitative navigation performance, and (ii) using the causal model as an online adaptation module that intervenes when the predicted competence of the default navigation is low. We validate our approach in a physical service robot that patrols around corridors. We show that the predicted competence correlates positively with path efficiency, and negatively with path irregularities (suboptimal behaviour). The model predictions also show strong agreement with human annotations (Cohen's kappa value of 0.88). In online experiments, the proposed method improves navigation performance in complex scenarios such as cornering and obstacle avoidance, yielding higher predicted competence and better navigation metrics than the default navigation baseline. In simpler scenarios, where the baseline already performs near-optimally, the causal adaptation provides limited benefit. These results indicate that causal models are particularly effective in enhancing navigation under increased task complexity. Overall, our results demonstrate that causal models developed for behavioural interpretation can be successfully integrated into real-robot navigation systems.

</details>


### [675] [LaWAM: Latent World Action Models for Efficient Dynamics-Aware Robot Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15768)
*Jialei Chen,Kai Wang,Kang Chen,Shuaihang Chen,Feng Gao,Wenhao Tang,Zhiyuan Li,Weilin Liu,Zhuyu Yao,Boxun Li,Yuanbo Xu,Chao Yu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LaWAM is a Latent World Action Model that improves robot control by predicting future visual states in latent space (instead of pixel-level video), achieving state-of-the-art success rates with much lower latency than prior world-action models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLAs lack explicit foresight into how actions change the scene, and prior World-Action Models rely on computationally expensive video generation with pixel-level redundancy, limiting real-time deployment.

Method: LaWAM trains a latent-action-conditioned Latent World Model (LaWM) in the latent space of a pretrained vision foundation model, repurposing its forward decoder to predict future observation features. Actions are conditioned on these latent visual subgoals for dynamics-aware control.

Result: LaWAM achieves 98.6% SR on LIBERO, 91.22% SR on RoboTwin, and competitive results on real-world tasks. It runs at 187 ms per action-chunk prediction, with up to 24x lower latency than pixel-space WAMs.

Conclusion: Latent-space future prediction is a viable and efficient alternative to pixel-space video generation for enabling foresight in robot policy learning, balancing performance with real-time inference.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) leverage large-scale vision-language pretraining for semantic robot control, but often lack explicit foresight into how robot actions change the scene. World-Action Models (WAMs) address this limitation by conditioning policies on predicted futures, yet existing approaches typically rely on computationally expensive video generation with substantial pixel-level redundancy. We present LaWAM, a Latent World Action Model that exposes predictive dynamics to robot policies through compact latent visual subgoals instead of reconstructed future video. At the core of LaWAM is a latent-action-conditioned Latent World Model (LaWM). We obtain LaWM by training a latent action model in the latent space of a pretrained vision foundation model and repurposing its forward decoder to predict future observation features for scene evolution. LaWAM then conditions action generation on these predicted latent visual subgoals to enable dynamics-aware robot control. LaWAM achieves state-of-the-art or competitive success rates (SRs) across LIBERO (98.6% SR), RoboTwin (91.22% SR), and real-world manipulation tasks while retaining low-latency inference. LaWAM runs in 187 ms per action-chunk prediction and achieves up to 24x lower wall-clock latency than pixel-space WAMs.

</details>


### [676] [FlashNav: Ultra-Fast Policy Training for Robot Navigation within 20 Seconds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15846)
*Shanze Wang,Yiwei Qian,Xinming Zhang,Jun Xue,Siwei Cheng,Xianghui Wang,Qingyuan Hu,Xiaoyu Shen,Wei Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FlashNav is a GPU-first framework for ultra-fast DRL-based robot navigation training, achieving seconds-level policy training (fastest deployable policy in less than 20 seconds) by aligning simulation with the navigation MDP and using a batched bitmap simulator with a fully GPU-resident training pipeline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep reinforcement learning for robot navigation suffers from long wall-clock training times, limiting practical deployment. The authors aim to drastically reduce training time to seconds-level while maintaining deployable obstacle-avoidance behavior.

Method: FlashNav removes unnecessary rendering and high-fidelity physics, preserving only essential MDP components (occupancy geometry, range sensing, goal-conditioned control, motion dynamics, collision, termination, reset). It uses a batched bitmap simulator and fully GPU-resident training pipeline with a FastDSAC learner to generate massive parallel navigation transitions entirely on GPU.

Result: Achieves 100% success-rate below 20 seconds on RTX 5090, tens of seconds across desktop GPUs. Policies transfer successfully to physical TurtleBot2 and Unitree Go2 robots in static and dynamic indoor scenes.

Conclusion: DRL-based robot navigation can be trained at seconds-level speed while preserving deployable obstacle-avoidance behavior, demonstrating that extreme simulation simplification aligned with the navigation MDP is sufficient for practical policy learning.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning has shown strong potential for robot navigation, but its practical deployment is still limited by the long wall-clock cost of policy training. This paper presents FlashNav, a GPU-first framework for ultra-fast range-based robot navigation training. To the best of our knowledge, FlashNav is the first DRL-based robot navigation framework that reaches seconds-level policy training, with the fastest deployable policy trained in less than 20 seconds. The key idea is to align simulation with the navigation MDP: FlashNav preserves the essential components for velocity-level navigation, including occupancy geometry, range sensing, goal-conditioned control, robot motion dynamics, collision handling, termination, and reset, while removing unnecessary rendering and high-fidelity physical details from the training loop. Built on a batched bitmap simulator and a fully GPU-resident training pipeline with our FastDSAC learner, FlashNav generates massive parallel navigation transitions entirely on GPU. Experiments on TurtleBot2 and Unitree Go2 show that FlashNav achieves a 100\% success-rate below 20 seconds on an RTX 5090 and remains within tens of seconds across desktop GPUs. The learned policies further transfer to physical wheeled and legged robots in static and dynamic indoor scenes, demonstrating that DRL-based navigation can be trained at seconds-level speed while preserving deployable obstacle-avoidance behavior.

</details>


### [677] [LoComposition: Terrain-Adaptive Energy-Efficient Quadruped Locomotion without Gait Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15896)
*Loukas Kordos,Leonard T. Franz,Simon Rappenecker,Oliver Hausdoerfer,Angela P. Schoellig,Pavel Kolev,Georg Martius*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A decomposed locomotion framework separates task rewards, operational constraints, energy minimization, and terrain perception, enabling emergent gaits without explicit priors, achieving 56% lower energy cost and 96% fewer constraint violations versus complex-reward baselines, with zero-shot transfer to a real Unitree Go2 robot.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Complex reward formulations entangle task specification, operational limits, gait preference, and terrain adaptation into a single objective. The authors propose separating these functions into distinct mechanisms for cleaner optimization and better performance.

Method: The method decomposes locomotion learning into: (1) rewards for task specification, (2) constraints for operational limits, (3) energy minimization for gait preference (emergent behavior, no explicit gait priors like air-time or foot-clearance), and (4) exteroceptive perception (LiDAR-based elevation mapping) for terrain-adaptive energy use.

Result: Compared to a complex-reward baseline, the method achieves comparable terrain traversal with 56% reduction in cost of transport and 96% fewer operational-limit violations. Policies transfer zero-shot to a physical Unitree Go2 robot.

Conclusion: Decoupling task rewards, constraints, energy minimization, and perception into separate mechanisms enables more efficient, terrain-adaptive quadruped locomotion without explicit gait priors, and each component's removal reveals distinct failure modes.

Abstract: Learning-based quadrupedal locomotion typically relies on complex reward formulations that entangle task specification, operational limits, gait preference, and terrain adaptation within a single optimization objective. We instead treat these functions through distinct mechanisms: rewards for task specification, constraints for operational limits, energy minimization for gait preference, and exteroceptive perception for adapting energy use to terrain difficulty. We show that these components jointly enable efficient, terrain-adaptive locomotion, and that removing each component exposes a distinct failure mode. Our formulation removes explicit gait priors (including air-time, contact-count, and foot-clearance targets) in favor of emergent behavior. Compared to a conventional complex-reward baseline, our formulation achieves comparable terrain traversal while reducing cost of transport by 56% and operational-limit violations by 96%. The resulting policies transfer zero-shot to a physical Unitree Go2 using LiDAR-based elevation mapping. Project website with videos: https://tinyurl.com/locomposition.

</details>


### [678] [VL2Spike: Spike-driven Distillation from VLMs for Low-Power Visual Perception in Embodied AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15898)
*Zinan Liu,Eric Zheng,Soumyaratna Debnath,Hao Shi,Ling Xiao,Lin Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VL2Spike is a knowledge distillation framework that transfers multi-modal knowledge from vision-language models (VLMs) into Spiking-Transformer models, boosting their performance by 6.81% on static datasets while using only 15.7% energy, and showing strong results on robotic visual place recognition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spiking neural networks (SNNs), despite being energy-efficient, suffer from limited learning capacity due to spike-based architecture and optimization challenges. The authors aim to leverage rich multi-modal knowledge from vision-language models (VLMs) to improve Spikformer training while preserving its energy efficiency.

Method: Two-stage knowledge distillation framework: (1) Spatial-temporal visual spike (SVS) distillation aligns VLM image features with spike tokens via shared manifold alignment and warm-started temporal consistency on membrane potentials/spike rates. (2) Spike prototype-guided linguistic (SPL) distillation aligns Spikformer's class prototypes and logits with promptable VLM text embeddings.

Result: 6.81% accuracy gain across three static datasets with only 15.7% energy consumption. 6.63% gain on robotic visual place recognition (VPR), demonstrating strong generalization for low-power embodied AI perception.

Conclusion: VL2Spike effectively bridges VLM knowledge with compact Spikformer models, enhancing learning capacity while preserving energy-efficiency, offering a practical pathway for low-power robotic perception in embodied AI.

Abstract: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) are brain-inspired, event-driven models that compute with sparse spikes, which enables highly efficient visual perception in resource-constrained embodied AI models. The emergence of Spiking-Transformer models with spike self-attention has substantially improved the learning capacity of pure SNNs. Although SNNs are energy efficient, their performance is still limited by the spike-based architecture and optimization challenges, as standard gradient descent rules cannot be directly applied. Recently, vision-language models (VLMs) have shown rich multi-modal knowledge representation capabilities for visual perception. Thus, it is promising to leverage VLMs for better Spikformer training. To this end, we present VL2Spike, a novel spike-based knowledge distillation (KD) framework that bridges multi-modal knowledge from VLMs with compact Spikformer models. This design enhances the learning capacity of Spikformer models while preserving their energy-efficiency merits, thereby offering a practical pathway toward low-power robotic perception. Our VL2Spike brings two key technical contributions. To align with spiking dynamics, we first propose spatial-temporal visual spike (SVS) distillation, which achieves (1) shared manifold alignment between VLM image features and spike tokens, and (2) warm-started temporal consistency on membrane potentials and spike rates. We then design a novel spike prototype-guided linguistic (SPL) distillation strategy that aligns Spikformer's class prototypes and logits with promptable VLM text embeddings. Extensive experiments show that VL2Spike achieves 6.81% gain across three static datasets with only 15.7% energy consumption. It also exhibits strong generalization capacity on robotic visual place recognition (VPR) with a gain of 6.63%, highlighting its potential for low-power perception in embodied AI.

</details>


### [679] [GeoTLM: Geometry-aware Tactile-Language Models for Contact Motion Orientation Reasoning of Dynamic Objects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15909)
*Qiutian Li,Zinan Liu,Lin Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GeoTLM is a geometric representation-guided tactile-language model that uses a lightweight Differentiable Geometric Representation (DGR) with antisymmetric pooling to improve contact orientation reasoning (rotation/sliding direction) by +14.6% and +16.2% over baselines, addressing the failure of existing TLMs on dynamic object physical properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing tactile-language models (e.g., Sparsh, AnyTouch2) struggle to understand physical properties of dynamic objects like rotation and sliding directions from tactile data, motivating the need for physically grounded geometric priors.

Method: GeoTLM introduces a Differentiable Geometric Representation (DGR, only 14k parameters) that preserves tactile shear-field geometry, uses contact-mask-guided representation learning, and aggregates features via antisymmetric seven-region pooling inspired by rotational contact's antisymmetric deformation patterns.

Result: GeoTLM improves novel-object rotation accuracy by +14.6% and real-sensor sliding accuracy by +16.2% over the same backbone without the geometric encoder.

Conclusion: Physically grounded geometric priors via DGR enable reliable tactile-language reasoning for dynamic contact events, paving a new way for contact-rich robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Modern tactile-language models (TLMs) have shown potential for robot learning tasks, such as material and texture recognition. However, for contact-rich scenarios, these TLMs struggle to understand the physical properties of dynamic objects, such as rotation and sliding directions. For instance, our preliminary experiments reveal that popular TLMs, such as Sparsh and AnyTouch2, exhibit weak performance on basic rotation direction reasoning from GelSight Mini tactile data. This surprising gap inspires us to explore a novel research question: Can we inject physically grounded geometric priors into TLMs to enable reliable contact orientation reasoning of dynamic object properties? To this end, we propose GeoTLM, a novel geometric representation-guided TLM for the perception of dynamic contact events. Our key idea is to preserve and structure tactile shear-field geometry before language-level reasoning, rather than forcing low-resolution tactile tokens into fragile closed-form physics operators. To achieve this, we propose a lightweight (only 14k parameters) yet novel Differentiable Geometric Representation (DGR). Specifically, DGR learns a contact-mask-guided representation in the shear field and aggregates it through an antisymmetric seven-region pooling design, motivated by the physical intuition that rotational contact produces antisymmetric deformation patterns. We conduct experiments on two representative tasks: rotation direction and sliding direction reasoning. Extensive experiments show that GeoTLM improves novel-object rotation accuracy by +14.6% and real-sensor sliding accuracy by +16.2% over the same backbone without the geometric encoder. Overall, our work paves a new way for physically grounded tactile-language reasoning, with strong potential for dynamic object understanding and contact-rich robotic manipulation.

</details>


### [680] [Identification of a Physics-Based Electrical Power Consumption Model for the Unitree G1 Humanoid Arm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15915)
*Nestor N. Deniz,Sebastian Vega,Simon Parsons,Fernando Auat Cheein*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A physics-based linear power consumption model for the Unitree G1 humanoid arm is developed, achieving R²=0.965 on unseen trajectories, revealing joint-specific loss types.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate power prediction is essential for energy-aware motion planning, battery management, and thermal monitoring in battery-powered humanoid robots.

Method: A physics-based, linear-in-parameters model combining actuator loss terms, baseline-torque correction for gravity compensation, and pairwise interaction terms for multi-joint coupling. Parameters identified via regression on 897 experimental trajectories from a Unitree G1 arm.

Result: R²=0.933 (RMSE 1.07W) on 897 identification trajectories; R²=0.965 on 46 unseen-speed validation trajectories.

Conclusion: The model generalizes well; identified parameters reveal distinct loss dominance: viscous friction (shoulder pitch, wrist joints), copper losses (shoulder yaw, elbow), Coulomb friction (shoulder roll).

Abstract: Accurate prediction of electrical power consumption is essential for energy-aware motion planning, battery management, and thermal monitoring in battery-powered humanoid robots. This letter presents a physics-based, linear-in-parameters model for the electrical power consumption of the seven-degree-of-freedom left arm of the Unitree~G1 humanoid robot. The proposed formulation combines actuator loss terms with a baseline-torque correction that captures changes in gravity-compensation load and enables accurate prediction of negative net power trajectories. Pairwise interaction terms are introduced to model power coupling during simultaneous multi-joint motion. Model parameters are identified from experimental data collected on a physical Unitree~G1 using onboard power measurements as the regression target. Across 897 trajectories covering single-joint and coordinated arm motions at multiple speed levels, the identified model achieves $R^2 = 0.933$ with an RMSE of 1.07 (W). Validation on 46 trajectories executed at previously unseen speeds yields $R^2 = 0.965$, demonstrating strong generalisation beyond the identification dataset. Analysis of the identified parameters reveals distinct power-consumption characteristics across the arm, with viscous friction dominating most joints (shoulder pitch and all three wrist joints), copper losses dominating shoulder yaw and the elbow, and shoulder roll uniquely dominated by Coulomb friction.

</details>


### [681] [Energy-Efficient Arm Reaching for a Humanoid Robot via Deep Reinforcement Learning with Identified Power Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15918)
*Nestor N. Deniz,Simon Parsons,Fernando Auat Cheein*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes an energy-aware reinforcement learning framework for humanoid robot arm reaching, combining a power model with SAC policy. Training on 5M steps achieves 69.9% success rate at ~98J mean energy in simulation, and physical validation on Unitree G1 shows 71.5J mean energy with ~2.6cm position error and ~6.9° orientation error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humanoid robots performing in-field manipulation tasks (e.g., apple harvesting) face severe energy constraints that limit the number of reaching motions per battery charge, motivating the need for energy-efficient motion planning.

Method: An end-to-end energy-aware RL framework using Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) on a 7-DOF arm, combining a physics-based experimentally identified electrical power model with a Pinocchio-based simulator. It uses incremental joint-position action space and a Hybrid Constellation Reward with end-effector constellation distance and torque-norm energy proxy.

Result: After 5×10⁶ training steps: 69.9% success rate over 1000 random targets in simulation at 98.16J mean energy. Physical validation on Unitree G1 over three 10-target batches: mean energy 71.5±48.3J, position error 2.64±1.04cm, orientation error 6.92±1.33°, within training tolerance of 4cm/8.6°.

Conclusion: This work constitutes a first step toward energy-aware reinforcement-learning-based arm reaching for humanoid robots, demonstrating feasibility of combining learned policies with energy optimization on physical hardware.

Abstract: Humanoid robots performing in-field manipulation tasks, such as robotic apple harvesting, face severe energy constraints that directly limit the number of reaching motions that can be executed per battery charge. This paper presents an end-to-end, energy-aware reinforcement learning framework for the 7-degree-of-freedom left arm of the Unitree~G1 humanoid robot, combining a physics-based, experimentally identified electrical power model with a Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) policy trained in a Pinocchio-based rigid-body dynamics simulator. The RL policy operates on an incremental joint-position action space and is trained with a Hybrid Constellation Reward that combines a four-point end-effector constellation distance with a torque-norm energy proxy; after % $5\times10^6$ training it reaches a $69.9\%$ success rate over $1\,000$ random targets in kinematic simulation, at a mean energy of \SI{98.16}{\joule} on successful episodes. Finally, on the physical Unitree~G1, the policy is validated over three independent 10-target batches, achieving a mean energy of $71.5 \pm 48.3$\,J, an end-effector position error of $2.64 \pm 1.04$\,cm, and an orientation error of $6.92 \pm 1.33^\circ$ -- within the \SI{4}{\centi\metre}/$8.6^\circ$ training tolerance. These results constitute a first step toward energy-aware reinforcement-learning-based arm reaching for humanoid robots.

</details>


### [682] [ControlMap: Controllable High-Definition Map Generation for Traffic Scenario Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15930)
*Marwan Farag,Steffen Wäldele,Yu Yao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a data-driven pipeline for controllable HD map generation using latent diffusion with ControlNet, enabling realistic map synthesis that follows specific road topologies while preserving city-specific details.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current autonomous driving simulation pipelines are limited by insufficient scenario diversity due to costly HD map creation, which requires expensive data collection and manual processing. Existing generative models also lack fine-grained control for targeting specific road topologies.

Method: The pipeline uses latent diffusion with ControlNet for spatial conditioning, being the first to inject spatial guidance signals into a diffusion model for HD map synthesis. It supports adjustable conditioning strength via classifier-free guidance and city-level style transfer through city label conditioning.

Result: The model generates realistic HD maps that faithfully follow input road topologies while accurately preserving city-specific details. Two novel metrics are introduced to evaluate control signal adherence and similarity to ground-truth maps.

Conclusion: The proposed controllable HD map generation pipeline addresses the scalability and diversity limitations of current simulation pipelines, offering a practical solution for autonomous driving validation.

Abstract: Simulation is central to validating autonomous driving systems, yet current pipelines are limited by insufficient scenario diversity due to costly High Definition (HD) map creation. Scaling HD maps requires expensive data collection and manual processing. Moreover, existing generative models lack the fine-grained control necessary to target specific road topologies during generation.
  This paper presents a data-driven pipeline for controllable HD map generation using latent diffusion and ControlNet for spatial conditioning. To our knowledge, we are the first to inject spatial guidance signals into a diffusion model for HD map synthesis. Furthermore, our model supports adjustable conditioning strength through classifier-free guidance and city-level style transfer via city label conditioning. To complement existing metrics, we introduce two novel metrics to evaluate adherence to the control signal and similarity to ground-truth maps. Experiments demonstrate that our model generates realistic HD maps that faithfully follow input road topologies while accurately preserving city-specific details.

</details>


### [683] [Friction Characterization of a Cable-Driven Differential Actuation System for Lower-Limb Exoskeletons](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15997)
*Alberto Maria Nobili,Fabio Salsedo,Alessandro Filippeschi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A novel differential actuation architecture for hip-knee exoskeletons enables sensorless torque estimation via model-based friction compensation, reducing system complexity and weight.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lower-limb exoskeletons need accurate joint torque control with low mass and encumbrance, but conventional designs rely on independent actuation and joint-level torque sensors, increasing complexity and weight.

Method: A differential actuation architecture with cooperative torque sharing between two motors via linear differential mapping, plus a model-based friction estimation strategy for sensorless torque estimation.

Result: Validated on a physical prototype, demonstrating feasibility of sensorless torque estimation in a differentially actuated hip-knee module.

Conclusion: The proposed differential actuation with model-based friction compensation enables accurate torque control without torque sensors, reducing system complexity for lower-limb exoskeletons.

Abstract: Lower-limb exoskeletons require actuation systems that can provide accurate joint torque control while preserving low mass and encumbrance. Conventional architectures often rely on independently actuated joints and joint-level torque sensors, increasing system complexity and weight. This paper presents a novel differential actuation architecture for hip-knee flexion/extension, enabling cooperative torque sharing between two motors via a linear differential mapping between motor and joint. To compensate for transmission losses, a model-based friction estimation strategy is developed and experimentally implemented, allowing accurate joint torque estimation without the need for torque sensors. The proposed solution is validated on a physical prototype, demonstrating the feasibility of sensorless torque estimation in a differentially actuated hip-knee module of a lower-limb exoskeleton.

</details>


### [684] [$λ$-Reachability: Geometric-Horizon Safety Bellman Equations for Humanoid Safety](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16022)
*Rui Chen,Shangtao Li,Yifan Sun,Changliu Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: λ-Reachability introduces a stochastic multi-step estimator for Hamilton-Jacobi safety analysis, interpolating between local self-consistency and long-horizon safety targets via an interpretable horizon-control parameter, achieving significant improvements in safe-set boundary classification and safety margin estimation for high-dimensional robotic systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior discounted formulations for Hamilton-Jacobi safety analysis rely on fixed one-step Bellman updates, which are insufficient for high-dimensional robotic systems where long-horizon safety reasoning is needed.

Method: λ-Reachability uses a geometrically distributed rollout horizon with randomly absorbed terminal, employing a stochastic multi-step estimator of the safety value controlled by parameters λ and δ. For δ<1, the update induces a contraction mapping enabling TD learning; as λ→1, it recovers the undiscounted reachability objective.

Result: Applied to high-dimensional safety learning with simulated and real humanoid robots under balance and collision avoidance constraints, λ-Reachability significantly improves safe-set boundary classification and safety margin estimation over single-step temporal-difference baselines.

Conclusion: λ-Reachability offers a scalable and interpretable approach to Hamilton-Jacobi safety analysis, effectively bridging local updates and long-horizon safety targets for high-dimensional robotic systems.

Abstract: We introduce $λ$-Reachability, a scalable approach to Hamilton--Jacobi safety analysis for high-dimensional robotic systems. Unlike prior discounted formulations that rely on fixed one-step Bellman updates, $λ$-Reachability employs a stochastic multi-step estimator of the safety value, using a geometrically distributed rollout horizon together with a randomly absorbed terminal. Conceptually analogous to TD($λ$), $λ$-Reachability interpolates between local self-consistency updates and long-horizon max-over-trajectory safety targets via an interpretable horizon-control parameter. Unlike TD($λ$), where the terminal value is always incorporated in learning targets, the terminal safety value in $λ$-Reachability is only used at a probability controlled by parameter $δ$. We formally show that for $δ<1$, the update induces a contraction mapping that allows temporal-difference learning; as $λ\to 1$, the estimator recovers the undiscounted reachability objective. We apply $λ$-Reachability to high-dimensional safety learning problems with both simulated and real humanoid robots under balance and collision avoidance constraints. Experimental results demonstrate that $λ$-Reachability significantly improves both safe-set boundary classification and safety margin estimation compared to single-step temporal-difference baselines.

</details>


### [685] [Leveraging Deep Learning for Object and Position Recognition of Load Carriers for Autonomous Logistics Vehicles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16042)
*Christoph Legat,Tobias Miller,Marco Riess*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a deep learning framework using CNNs on RGBD data to detect landmarks on load carriers and compute their pose for autonomous pickup by mobile robots in industrial logistics, with experimental validation of sufficient accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable autonomous mobile robots to detect and estimate the pose of load carriers for automated pickup in industrial intralogistics applications.

Method: A deep neural network (CNN) processes RGBD images to recognize predefined landmarks on the load carrier, then combines these inferred landmark positions with prior geometric knowledge to compute the carrier's 6-DOF pose.

Result: The approach achieves accuracy sufficient for reliable load carrier detection in industrial environments, validated through extensive experiments with both software and hardware implementations.

Conclusion: The method is suitable for autonomous intralogistics applications, confirming that deep learning-based landmark detection from RGBD data can enable reliable automated pickup of load carriers.

Abstract: This work explores the use of artificial intelligence in mobile robotics to achieve autonomous detection and pose estimation of load carriers for automated pickup. A deep neural network is designed to recognize predefined landmarks on the carrier from RGBD data; these landmarks are then used to compute the carrier's pose. The network operates directly on RGBD images to estimate landmark positions, which form the basis for determining the carrier's location. The approach is validated in extensive experiments and comprises both software and hardware implementations.
  A deep learning-based framework is presented to detect load carriers and estimate their pose for use with autonomous logistics vehicles. Our method uses a convolutional neural network to identify characteristic reference points on the carrier from RGBD input and computes its pose by combining these inferred landmarks with prior geometric knowledge. Experiments show that the resulting accuracy is sufficient for reliable load carrier detection in industrial environments, confirming the suitability of the method for autonomous intralogistics applications.

</details>


### [686] [A Deployment Case Study in Robotic Apparel Automation: Digital Twin Integration, Interoperability, and Workforce Enablement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16078)
*Gokul Narayanan,Abhiroop Ajith,Jonathan Zornow,Carlos Calle,Auralis Herrero Lugo,Jose Luis Susa Rincon,Chengtao Wen,Eugen Solowjow*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A case study of a robotic sewing system for denim manufacturing that integrates digital thread, digital twin, collaborative robots, runtime monitoring, and operator training tools, successfully deployed in factories for 2D pocket and 3D seam operations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Apparel automation remains challenging due to fabric deformability, unlike other manufacturing sectors. The paper aims to address the system-level integration needed for practical, scalable robotic sewing adoption.

Method: The system uses a digital thread module to parse DXF drawings into robot trajectories, a digital twin for pre-deployment validation (reach, clearance, layout, cycle time), a collaborative robot with conventional sewing equipment via an interoperability layer, and runtime monitoring (seam, collision, trajectory checks) plus operator training tools.

Result: Factory deployments on denim shorts (2D pocket operations and 3D garment-shaping seams) demonstrated that digital-twin validation, digital-thread task generation, interoperability, runtime verification, and operator training are critical for scaling robotic apparel automation.

Conclusion: System-level integration, including pre-deployment simulation, automated trajectory generation, runtime verification, and human-centered tools, is essential for practical robotic automation in apparel manufacturing, addressing the unique challenges of deformable fabric manipulation.

Abstract: Despite steady advances in flexible automation in sectors such as electronics and automotive manufacturing, apparel automation remains challenging because fabrics are deformable and difficult to manipulate with robots. This paper presents a deployment-oriented case study of a robotic sewing system for denim manufacturing, emphasizing the system-level integration required for practical adoption. At the engineering level, a digital thread module parses DXF production drawings into process parameters and executable robot trajectories, reducing manual programming effort and enabling rapid re-targeting across sewing operations. In parallel, a digital twin of the workcell is used during pre-deployment to validate reach and clearance, refine layout and sequencing, evaluate operator access, and assess cycle-time compatibility with upstream and downstream tasks, thereby reducing commissioning risk. At deployment, the system integrates a collaborative robot with conventional sewing equipment, welding, suction fixtures, and machine-level controllers through an interoperability layer. Runtime monitoring and verification, including seam monitoring, collision checking, and trajectory-level validation, improve robustness under environmental variability, while operator-facing training and guidance tools support setup, troubleshooting, and technology adoption. Two staged factory deployments on denim shorts, covering 2D pocket operations and 3D garment-shaping seams, show that digital-twin-based validation, digital-thread-driven task generation, interoperability, runtime verification, and operator training are important for scaling robotic apparel automation.

</details>


### [687] [Scaling Short-Term Memory of Visuomotor Policies for Long-Horizon Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16178)
*Rutav Shah,Rajat Kumar Jenamani,Xiaohan Zhang,Lingfeng Sun,Roberto Martín-Martín,Yuke Zhu,Deva Ramanan,Karl Schmeckpeper*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PRISM, a transformer-based visuomotor policy architecture with gated attention and hierarchical compression, achieves 5-12% improvement over baselines on short-term memory tasks up to two minutes, along with a new ReMemBench benchmark for evaluating memory in manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most visuomotor policies trained via imitation learning rely only on immediate sensory input without leveraging past experiences, limiting their ability to handle tasks requiring short-term memory (e.g., retrieving occluded objects, delayed actions).

Method: PRISM uses (1) gated attention to filter retrieved information and suppress irrelevant details, and (2) a hierarchical architecture that compresses local information into compact tokens then integrates them for temporally extended dependencies, improving memory and compute efficiency.

Result: PRISM achieves 5-12% absolute improvement over strongest baselines on ReMemBench, and 11-15% improvement over no-memory variants and large VLA baselines (GR00T-N1-3B, OpenVLA) on RoboCasa and LIBERO benchmarks, scaling memory up to two minutes.

Conclusion: PRISM and ReMemBench establish a foundation for developing and evaluating short-term memory-augmented visuomotor policies that scale to long-horizon manipulation tasks, without requiring large-scale pretraining.

Abstract: Many robotic tasks require short-term memory, whether it's retrieving an object that's no longer visible or turning off an appliance after a set period. Yet, most visuomotor policies trained via imitation learning rely only on immediate sensory input without using past experiences to guide decisions. We present PRISM, a transformer-based architecture for visuomotor policies to effectively use short-term memory via two key components: (i) gated attention, which filters retrieved information to suppress irrelevant details, improving performance by reducing the spurious correlations between the history and current action prediction, (ii) a hierarchical architecture that first compresses local information into compact tokens and then integrates them to capture temporally extended dependencies, improving its compute and memory footprint. Together, these mechanisms enable us to scale short-term memory in visuomotor policies for up to two minutes. To systematically evaluate memory in visuomotor control, we introduce ReMemBench -- a benchmark of eight diverse household manipulation tasks spanning four categories of short-term memory -- designed to foster general memory mechanisms rather than siloed, task-specific solutions. PRISM consistently outperforms prior works, including recurrent architectures, transformers, and their variants -- achieving an absolute improvement of 5%--12% over the strongest baseline. On the RoboCasa and LIBERO benchmarks, it achieves absolute improvements of 11%--15% over its no-memory variant and fine-tuned Vision-Language-Action baselines such as GR00T-N1-3B and OpenVLA, despite not leveraging any large-scale pretraining. Together, PRISM and ReMemBench establish a foundation for developing and evaluating short-term memory-augmented visuomotor policies that scale to long-horizon tasks. Additional materials are available at https://shahrutav.github.io/short-term-memory

</details>


### [688] [ATHENA: Accelerated Multi-Task Heterogeneous Influence Functions for Robot Data Curation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16208)
*Tao Xu,Jiaxin Wang,Runhao Zhang,Jiayi Guan,Xianchao Zeng,Weixi Song,Xinyu Zhou,Zhetao Chen,Guang Chen,Yong-Lu Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ATHENA is a scalable influence function framework for billion-parameter multitask Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models that achieves ~313x speedup in influence computation and matches full-data fine-tuning using only 50-66.7% of demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Influence functions are principled for quantifying demonstration effects in robot imitation learning, but scaling them to billion-parameter VLA models faces computational and multitask bottlenecks.

Method: Leverages Kronecker structure of linear-layer gradients to reduce projection cost, and approximates dense Hessian inversion with rank-r Random Truncated Approximation for ~313.4x speedup. Formulates global and local interactive influence to balance data curation across 50 jointly trained tasks.

Result: Matches or exceeds full-data joint fine-tuning using only 50% of demonstrations in simulation (RoboTwin 2.0) and 66.7% of data across six real-robot tasks, covering 9.34 and 6.90 hours of demonstrations respectively.

Conclusion: ATHENA demonstrates effectiveness for data curation in billion-parameter multitask VLA fine-tuning.

Abstract: In robot imitation learning, influence functions provide a principled approach to quantify each demonstration's effect on robot task outcomes, yet scaling them to billion-parameter Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models is limited by computational and multitask bottlenecks. To this end, we propose ATHENA, an influence function framework tailored for multitask VLA data curation at a billion-parameter scale. Concretely, it leverages the Kronecker structure of linear-layer gradients to reduce projection cost, and approximates dense Hessian inversion with a rank-r Random Truncated Approximation, achieving about a 313.4x speedup in influence computation. Furthermore, ATHENA formulates global and local interactive influence to balance data curation across 50 jointly trained tasks. Extensive evaluations on RoboTwin 2.0 and real-robot deployment, covering 9.34 and 6.90 hours of demonstrations, respectively, show that ATHENA matches or exceeds full-data joint fine-tuning using only 50% of demonstrations in simulation and 66.7% of data across six real-robot tasks. Overall, ATHENA demonstrates its effectiveness for data curation in billion-parameter multitask VLA fine-tuning.

</details>


### [689] [PolyMerge: Compressing 3D Gaussian Splats with Polytope Coverings for Provably Safe Resource-Constrained Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16232)
*Jihoon Hong,Chih-Yuan Chiu,Sara Fridovich-Keil,Glen Chou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PolyMerge converts dense 3D Gaussian Splatting models into lightweight convex polytope representations for real-time obstacle avoidance on resource-constrained drones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current radiance field methods (like 3DGS) provide high-fidelity obstacle detection but are too memory- and compute-intensive for on-board perception-based path planning on small drones.

Method: PolyMerge converts large 3DGS models into a union of convex polytopes that provably over-approximate obstacles, tunes polytope count to trade off conservativeness vs. compute cost, and integrates with Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) for collision-free path planning.

Result: Demonstrated on Crazyflie drones in both simulation and hardware; PolyMerge enables real-time safe trajectory computation under severe onboard compute constraints, outperforming baselines in speed while guaranteeing safety.

Conclusion: PolyMerge effectively bridges high-fidelity 3D reconstruction and real-time obstacle avoidance on resource-limited platforms, offering a practical path toward onboard safety-critical navigation.

Abstract: Obstacle avoidance is essential for safe navigation and motion planning. Recent radiance field reconstruction methods enable object detection and modeling with high fidelity, but remain too memory- and compute-intensive for on-board perception-based path planning. To address these limitations, we propose PolyMerge to convert a large, photorealistic 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) model of a scene into a lightweight representation of convex polytopes whose union provably over-approximates all obstacles in the original 3DGS model. PolyMerge tunes the polytope count to trade off conservativeness and compute cost, and integrates with control barrier functions (CBFs) to plan collision-free paths. We showcase PolyMerge in simulation and hardware experiments on a Crazyflie drone, which uses PolyMerge to compute and follow safe trajectories in real time under severe onboard compute constraints, outperforming baselines in speed while guaranteeing safety. For our code and videos, visit https://athlon76.github.io/PolyMerge-website/.

</details>


### [690] [TopoRetarget: Interaction-Preserving Retargeting for Dexterous Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16272)
*Jielin Wu,Shenzhe Yao,Guanqi He,Xiaohan Liu,Zhaoqing Zeng,Xiangrui Jiang,Han Yang,Wentao Zhang,Hang Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TopoRetarget preserves hand-object contact structure when retargeting human demonstrations to robot hands, improving dexterous manipulation RL policy learning with 40.6% better pen-spin success and zero-shot hardware transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human demonstrations for dexterous manipulation RL are degraded by retargeting artifacts that break contact structure. The authors aim to preserve task-relevant hand-object interaction during retargeting across diverse conditions.

Method: Constructs a sparse interaction graph over hand and object keypoints, then optimizes distance-weighted Laplacian deformation with directional consistency, kinematic constraints, and penetration handling.

Result: Best contact precision/alignment on ContactPose Dataset; 40.6 percentage point improvement in Pen-Spin training success over baselines; zero-shot transfer to Wuji Hand hardware on cube reorientation and pen spinning.

Conclusion: Preserving interaction structure during retargeting is critical for downstream RL policy performance, and TopoRetarget achieves this effectively with a unified parameter set.

Abstract: Human hand-object demonstrations provide dense reference motions for training dexterous manipulation reinforcement learning (RL) policies through reference tracking. However, to use such demonstrations for RL policy learning, retargeting must preserve hand pose and task-relevant hand-object contact structure. Otherwise, contact and feasibility artifacts can degrade downstream RL policy performance. We introduce TopoRetarget, an interaction-preserving retargeting framework that uses a single set of parameters across diverse retargeting conditions while maintaining task-relevant hand-object interaction and adapting human demonstrations to dexterous robot hands. The method constructs a sparse interaction graph over hand and object keypoints and optimizes distance-weighted Laplacian deformation with directional consistency, kinematic constraints, and penetration handling. Evaluations show that the generated references improve both interaction fidelity and policy learning: TopoRetarget achieves the best contact precision and alignment over all baselines on the ContactPose Dataset, improves Pen-Spin training success by 40.6 percentage points over the existing baseline methods, and enables zero-shot transfer to Wuji Hand hardware on cube reorientation and pen spinning.

</details>


### [691] [Is Your Trajectory Displacement Safe in Long-tail?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16313)
*Qiao Sun,Weicheng Zheng,Yixin Huang,Hang Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FluidTest is a new evaluation pipeline for autonomous driving planning that uses pairwise human annotation, a 32-category threat taxonomy, and a three-agent verification system to detect safety-relevant failures that standard metrics miss, finding threats in 65% of Poutine and 51% of RAP trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing autonomous driving evaluation pipelines fail to simultaneously achieve human alignment, safety awareness, verifiability, and explainability — closed-loop metrics saturate and human ratings are noisy without structured protocols.

Method: FluidTest frames planning evaluation as additional-threat detection comparing planner vs. expert trajectories, using: (1) a pairwise WebUI protocol for reliable human annotation; (2) a taxonomy of 32 semantic threats with evidence-grounded decision graphs; (3) a three-agent verification system with reflection for precision and auditability.

Result: On the WOD-E2E dataset, FluidTest produces consistent labels among trained annotators and identifies additional threats in 65% of Poutine trajectories and 51% of RAP trajectories, indicating state-of-the-art planners still exhibit substantial safety-relevant failures despite good RFS and ADE scores.

Conclusion: Standard metrics like RFS and ADE are insufficient to capture safety-relevant planner failures; FluidTest provides a more reliable, human-aligned, and explainable evaluation protocol for autonomous driving in long-tail scenarios.

Abstract: Long-tail scenarios remain a major bottleneck for autonomous driving evaluation, even as datasets grow by orders of magnitude. Existing evaluation pipelines are rarely human-aligned, safety-aware, verifiable, and explainable at the same time: closed-loop metrics often saturate among strong planners, while unstructured human ratings can be noisy without a carefully designed protocol. We formulate planning evaluation as additional-threat detection: given a planner trajectory and an expert reference, does the planner's displacement introduce new unsafe driving behavior? We propose FluidTest, an evaluation pipeline with three components: a pairwise WebUI protocol for reliable human annotation; a taxonomy of 32 semantic threats with evidence-grounded decision graphs; and a three-agent verification system with reflection for precision and auditability. Experiments on the WOD-E2E dataset show that FluidTest produces consistent labels among trained annotators and identifies additional threats in 65% of Poutine trajectories and 51% of RAP trajectories. These results show that state-of-the-art planners can still exhibit substantial safety-relevant failures despite high Rater Feedback Scores (RFS) and low Average Displacement Error (ADE). Additional details, guidance, and code are available at https://fluidtest.web.app.

</details>


### [692] [ART-Glove: Articulated Tactile Glove for Contact-Grounded Dexterous Interaction Capture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16370)
*Changyi Lin,Ding Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ART-Glove is an articulated tactile glove with 16 rigid surfaces, 22 joints, and 2048 tactile sensors that captures contact-grounded dexterous hand movements at 120 Hz for robot learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To capture contact-grounded dexterous demonstrations for robot learning while preserving natural human hand dexterity, addressing the need for explicit contact geometry in manipulation data.

Method: The glove uses 16 rigid functional surfaces (fingers, thumb, palm) connected by 22 anatomically aligned joints, with encoder-based motion tracking and 2048 piezoresistive tactile sensors, capturing synchronized 22-DoF joint and tactile data at 120 Hz.

Result: The system successfully captures synchronized 22-DoF joint measurements and 2048-taxel tactile data at 120 Hz, preserving human dexterity while providing contact-grounded information for downstream robot learning.

Conclusion: ART-Glove enables contact-grounded dexterous data collection without restricting human motion, supporting future dexterous robot learning applications.

Abstract: We present ART-Glove, an articulated tactile glove designed to capture contact-grounded dexterous demonstrations while preserving human dexterity. ART-Glove makes hand-side contact geometry explicit with 16 rigid functional surfaces covering the fingers, thumb, and palm. Twenty-two anatomically aligned joints connect these surfaces and allow them to follow human hand motion during dexterous manipulation. Encoder-based sensing tracks surface motion, while dense piezoresistive tactile sensing records contact over the same surfaces. The complete system captures synchronized 22-DoF joint measurements and 2048-taxel tactile measurements at 120 Hz. We evaluate ART-Glove across experiments on motion freedom, joint sensing, tactile sensing, and contact-rich interaction capture, demonstrating its ability to preserve human dexterity while recording contact-grounded information that can support downstream dexterous robot learning.

</details>


### [693] [SemGeoNav:A Safety-Guided Visual Navigation Approach with Semantic Reasoning and Geometric Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16400)
*Yu Liu,Zongyang Chen,Yan Guo,Chao Liu,Xianfei Pan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SemGeoNav is a hierarchical visual navigation framework combining high-level semantic reasoning of end-to-end models with geometry-based local planning for robust image-based navigation with improved obstacle avoidance, validated on a Unitree Go2 robot outperforming ViNT and NoMaD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Purely end-to-end learning models lack explicit geometric constraints, causing unpredictable obstacle avoidance in open environments. Traditional geometric planners are safe but struggle with high-dimensional visual targets.

Method: A hierarchical framework integrating semantic reasoning (high-level) with geometry-based local planning (low-level), plus a temporal trajectory smoothing mechanism for stable motion.

Result: Evaluated on Unitree Go2 quadruped robot in real-world environments; outperforms ViNT and NoMaD with higher success rates and shorter navigation times.

Conclusion: SemGeoNav effectively bridges semantic and geometric approaches for robust visual navigation with improved safety and efficiency.

Abstract: Learning-based visual navigation has enhanced semantic goal-reaching capabilities. However, due to their black-box nature, purely end-to-end models often lack explicit geometric constraints, leading to unpredictable and unreliable obstacle avoidance in open environments. Conversely, traditional geometric planners ensure safety but struggle with high-dimensional visual targets. To address these limitations, we propose SemGeoNav, a novel hierarchical visual navigation framework.It tightly integrates the high-level semantic reasoning of end-to-end models with the reliable local planning ability of geometry-based methods, achieving robust image-based navigation while significantly improving obstacle avoidance. Furthermore, we introduce a temporal trajectory smoothing mechanism to ensure continuous and stable robot motion. We evaluated SemGeoNav on a Unitree Go2 quadruped robot in real-world environments. The results demonstrate that SemGeoNav outperforms existing representative methods, including ViNT and NoMaD, achieving higher success rates and shorter navigation times.

</details>


### [694] [An Augmented Reality Brain-Robot Interface for Generalist Robot Arm Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16413)
*Shangkai Zhang,Rousslan Fernand Julien Dossa,Luca Nunziante,Marina Di Vincenzo,Kai Arulkumaran*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents an augmented reality brain-robot interface (AR BRI) that combines gaze-based object selection with motor imagery action control for generalist robot arm manipulation. Evaluated with 18 participants on daily living tasks, the system achieved good usability (SUS > 70) and enabled effective sequential task execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AR brain-robot interfaces are constrained to task-specific structures, limiting their utility in real-world environments. The authors aim to develop a more generalist system for robot arm manipulation.
