Method: UMA uses 3D object motion trajectories as a shared interface between robot actions and object motion, employs masked generative modeling where mask patterns determine supervision and inference modes, uses hindsight-relabeled motion contexts, and incorporates a contrastive objective to disentangle task intent from scene geometry.

Result: UMA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines specialized for each inference mode (motion-conditioned visuomotor control, motion-based dynamics modeling, and task adaptation from few-shot demonstrations).

Conclusion: Treating object motion and robot actions as co-evolving variables under a unified masked generative framework is an effective approach for multi-task pretraining across diverse data sources, achieving strong performance across multiple downstream tasks without task-specific architectures.

Abstract: We present Unified Motion-Action (UMA) Model, an approach that uses 3D object motion trajectories as a shared interface to bridge visuomotor control and dynamics modeling. UMA treats object motion and robot actions as co-evolving variables under a masked generative objective, in which the mask pattern determines both the supervision regime during pretraining and the inference mode at deployment. Using hindsight-relabeled motion contexts and a contrastive objective that disentangles task intent from scene geometry, UMA enables multi-task pretraining across heterogeneous data sources without requiring manually annotated task instructions. At deployment, the same pretrained parameters support motion-conditioned visuomotor control, motion-based dynamics modeling, and task adaptation from few-shot demonstrations. Pretrained on a mixture of robot demonstrations, human videos, and simulated data, UMA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines specialized for each inference mode.

</details>


### [722] [CrossMaps: Confidence-Aware Open-Vocabulary Semantic Mapping for Rover Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16935)
*Jan-Niklas Klein,Sona Ghahremani,Christian Medeiros Adriano,Holger Giese*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CrossMaps is a real-time, confidence-aware, open-vocabulary semantic mapping pipeline for rovers that uses RGB-D data, multi-scale CLIP embeddings, and a dual-memory architecture (STM/LTM) to build language-queryable maps for navigation under partial observability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rovers need spatial maps that encode both objects and sensor quality (range reliability, lighting, data density) to guide data fusion and navigation under partial observability, motivating a coupled perception-navigation mapping approach.

Method: Building on VLMaps-style approaches, CrossMaps integrates multi-scale CLIP embeddings with confidence-aware fusion and a dual-memory architecture: STM aggregates noisy observations using geometric, semantic, and temporal confidence cues; confident cells are promoted to LTM as persistent semantic landmarks. Deployed on Jetson Orin-powered UGV with SLAM.

Result: Runs in real time on a Jetson Orin-powered UGV and produces semantic heatmaps queryable with natural language to guide rover navigation.

Conclusion: CrossMaps enables real-time, confidence-aware open-vocabulary semantic mapping that couples perception and navigation for rovers under partial observability.

Abstract: Rovers rely on perception to maintain spatial maps that encode both objects and sensor quality (e.g., range reliability, lighting artifacts, data density), guiding data fusion, embedding updates, and navigation under partial observability. To study these coupled perception-navigation processes, we present CrossMaps, a real-time confidence-aware open-vocabulary semantic mapping pipeline that constructs language-queryable maps from RGB-D data. Building on VLMaps-style approaches, CrossMaps integrates multi-scale CLIP embeddings with confidence-aware fusion and a dual-memory architecture consisting of Short-Term Memory (STM) and Long-Term Memory (LTM). The STM aggregates noisy visual observations using geometric, semantic, and temporal confidence cues, while confident and coherent cells are promoted to the LTM as persistent semantic landmarks. Designed for deployment with a Jetson Orin-powered UGV alongside SLAM, CrossMaps runs in real time and produces semantic heatmaps that can be queried with natural language to guide rover navigation.

</details>


### [723] [SidewalkBench: Benchmarking Visual Navigation on Urban Sidewalks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16953)
*Zhizheng Liu,Honglin He,Vivek Alumootil,Akshat Pandya,Brad Squicciarini,Wayne Wu,Bolei Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SidewalkBench is a new benchmark for evaluating visual navigation models on urban sidewalks, featuring GPU-accelerated simulation with procedurally generated and real-scanned scenes, reactive pedestrian behaviors, and long-horizon scenarios. Evaluation of 9 models reveals pedestrian interaction and long-horizon robustness as key bottlenecks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Urban sidewalk navigation is challenging due to complex layouts, dynamic pedestrian behaviors, and long distances. Existing visual navigation models lack a unified benchmark for quantitative and reproducible evaluation.

Method: The authors built SidewalkBench on NVIDIA Isaac Sim, with GPU-accelerated simulation of diverse sidewalk environments (both procedurally generated and real-world scanned scenes), populated with reactive event-based pedestrian behaviors and flexible animation. They evaluated 9 visual navigation models across 330 unit-test, 800 pedestrian-reactive, and 105 long-horizon scenarios.

Result: Pedestrian interaction and long-horizon robustness remain critical bottlenecks for existing models. Scaling up sidewalk training with synthetic data is a promising solution.

Conclusion: SidewalkBench provides a unified benchmark for standardized evaluation of sidewalk visual navigation, highlighting key challenges that current models struggle with.

Abstract: Urban sidewalk navigation presents significant challenges due to complex structural layouts, dynamic pedestrian behaviors, and long distances. While recent visual navigation models offer a promising solution, the lack of a unified benchmark hinders quantitative and reproducible evaluation. To bridge this gap, we propose SidewalkBench, a comprehensive benchmark designed for visual navigation on urban sidewalks. Built upon NVIDIA Isaac Sim, SidewalkBench brings GPU-accelerated simulation of diverse, high-fidelity sidewalk environments, including both procedurally generated and real-world scanned scenes. We further populate the scenes with rich, reactive event-based pedestrian behaviors and flexible, efficient animation, enabling standardized model evaluation under realistic real-world settings. We conduct a comprehensive evaluation of 9 visual navigation models on 330 unit-test scenarios, 800 pedestrian-reactive scenarios, and 105 long-horizon scenarios. Our findings highlight that pedestrian interaction and long-horizon robustness remain critical bottlenecks for existing models, and scaling up sidewalk training with synthetic data emerges as a promising solution.

</details>


### [724] [When Should a Robot Replan? Regret-Guided Update Scheduling in Time-Varying MDPs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16972)
*Negin Musavi,Gokul Puthumanaillam,Ruben Hernandez,William Schafer,Melkior Ornik*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper formulates a skip-update scheme for robots operating in non-stationary environments, where limited budgets require deciding when to update policies. They propose an adaptive, regret-guided update rule that outperforms baselines in simulated and real-robot experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots in non-stationary environments face limited onboard energy and compute budgets, preventing continuous state estimation and re-planning. The key question is when to spend the limited budget along a horizon.

Method: Formulates the problem in TVMDPs with a known bound on transition drift rate. Proposes a skip-update scheme where at chosen times the robot estimates the transition kernel via MLE, computes a finite-horizon policy, and reuses it between updates under propagated state estimates. Derives a dynamic regret bound that yields an online, regret-guided update rule for adaptive budget allocation.

Result: The regret-guided update rule outperforms other budgeted baselines in a simulated Mars-rover navigation task with time-varying slip dynamics and on a Crazyflie quadrotor in indoor obstacle fields.

Conclusion: Adaptive allocation of limited update budgets, guided by dynamic regret analysis, is effective for robots operating in drifting environments.

Abstract: Robots operating in non-stationary environments must continually adapt their policies as the dynamics drift, but onboard energy and compute budgets cap how often a full state estimation and re-planning step can be performed. This raises a question: \emph{when}, along a horizon, should a robot spend its limited budget? We formulate this problem in time-varying Markov decision processes (TVMDPs) with a known bound on the rate of transition drift. We model execution as a \emph{skip-update} scheme in which, at chosen update times, the agent estimates the transition kernel by maximum likelihood and computes a finite-horizon policy, and between updates reuses this policy under a propagated state estimate. We analyze the dynamic regret of this scheme and show how it grows during skip intervals in terms of the properties of the TVMDP and the skip lengths; the resulting bound answers the opening question via an online, regret-guided update rule that allocates the budget adaptively. We evaluate the rule in a simulated Mars-rover navigation task with time-varying slip dynamics and on a Crazyflie quadrotor in indoor obstacle fields. Adaptive allocation outperforms other budgeted baselines.

</details>


### [725] [Task-Error Residual Learning for Real-Robot Five-Ball Juggling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16978)
*Kai Ploeger,Jan Peters*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates that residual learning with directional task-error feedback and a task error model for sample selection enables stable multi-ball juggling (3-5 balls) on anthropomorphic robotic arms, converging from the second attempt, by showing that information-rich supervision is more critical than the accuracy of the analytic prior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning suffers from poor sample efficiency in residual learning scenarios because scalar rewards carry limited information and random exploration wastes rollout data. The authors aim to achieve complex physical tasks (multi-ball juggling) efficiently by using richer supervision signals.

Method: The method uses residual learning with directional task-error supervision (instead of scalar reward) combined with a task error model for sample selection. The approach is a fixed-Jacobian Newton update, compared across three axes: directional information in learning feedback and commitment of the analytic prior, spanning Newton-style Jacobian updates, Composite Bayesian Optimization, and stochastic search.

Result: Achieved stable three-, four-, and five-ball juggling on anthropomorphic Barrett WAM arms. Convergence from the second attempt (first attempt drops, then task error decreases monotonically without further failures). Both directional feedback and informative prior are necessary; fixed-Jacobian Newton update is the most reliable. Learned residual tolerates prior misalignment and degraded joint tracking.

Conclusion: The bottleneck for residual learning on real robots is the information content of the supervision signal and how the learner uses it, not the accuracy of the surrounding analytic stack. Combining directional feedback with an informative prior is necessary for efficient learning.

Abstract: For residual learning that refines existing behavior, sample efficiency depends on two things: how much information each rollout returns, and how efficiently the learner uses that information. Reinforcement learning's standard scalar reward carries far less information than the directional task error that defines the task. Random exploration further discards whatever information each rollout returns. Through residual learning with directional task-error supervision and a task error model that drives sample selection, we achieve stable three-, four-, and five-ball juggling on anthropomorphic Barrett WAM arms. Despite planning and controlling through a simple, idealized stack, the system converges from the second attempt. The first attempt drops, after which task error decreases monotonically without further failures. In comparison, five-ball juggling typically takes humans years of practice. We compare residual learners across two ternary axes, the directional information in the learning feedback and the commitment of the analytic prior, spanning Newton-style Jacobian updates, Composite Bayesian Optimization, and stochastic search methods. Both axes prove necessary: neither directional feedback nor an informative prior suffices alone, and the simplest method that combines them, a fixed-Jacobian Newton update, is the most reliable. The learned residual tolerates substantial prior misalignment and degraded joint tracking, affecting mainly convergence speed. The bottleneck for residual learning on real robots is therefore the information content of the supervision signal and how the learner uses it, not the accuracy of the surrounding stack. Video documentation of all experiments is available at https://kai-ploeger.com/residual-juggling.

</details>


### [726] [ROVE: Unlocking Human Interventions for Humanoid Manipulation via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17011)
*Wei Xiao,Weiliang Tang,Yuying Ge,Hui Zhou,Yao Mu,Li Zhang,Yixiao Ge*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ROVE is a reinforcement learning framework that uses Optimistic Value Estimation and cross-embodiment human video supervision to enable humanoid VLA post-training despite imperfect human interventions, outperforming baselines on real-world manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human interventions for humanoid VLA post-training are often suboptimal due to complex whole-body kinematics and dexterous-hand control challenges, causing models to absorb hesitant or erroneous behaviors.

Method: ROVE combines a human-in-the-loop pipeline for data collection, Optimistic Value Estimation (OVE) to prioritize high-value behaviors from mixed-quality trajectories, and cross-embodiment human experience videos to robustify value estimation for long-tailed failure/recovery modes.

Result: ROVE outperforms experience-learning baselines on real-world contact-rich and fine-grained humanoid manipulation tasks, with consistent improvement across multiple rollout-intervention iterations.

Conclusion: ROVE effectively addresses both system and algorithmic challenges of humanoid VLA post-training with imperfect human interventions by steering the VLA actor toward high-value behaviors rather than indiscriminate imitation.

Abstract: Human interventions provide crucial corrective signals for post-training Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. However, enabling seamless humanoid interventions is a formidable systems challenge due to complex whole-body kinematics and dexterous-hand control. Consequently, the collected intervention trajectories are often suboptimal, and methods that rely on human interventions as expert supervision can absorb hesitant, inefficient, or even erroneous behaviors. To address both the system and algorithmic challenges, we propose ROVE, a reinforcement learning framework for humanoid VLA post-training with imperfect human interventions. First, ROVE introduces a human-in-the-loop pipeline capable of collecting deployment and intervention data for humanoid manipulation. Second, it utilizes Optimistic Value Estimation (OVE) to prioritize high-value behaviors from mixed-quality trajectories. To further robustify value estimation, we incorporate cross-embodiment human experience videos to provide rich supervision for long-tailed failure and recovery modes. The resulting critic yields informative advantage signals, steering the VLA actor to focus on high-value behaviors rather than indiscriminately imitating all actions. On challenging real-world contact-rich and fine-grained humanoid manipulation tasks, ROVE outperforms experience-learning baselines and consistently improves across multiple rollout-intervention iterations.

</details>


### [727] [R2RDreamer: 3D-aware Data Augmentation for Spatially-generalized 2D Manipulation Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17040)
*Xiuwei Xu,Haowen Sun,Angyuan Ma,Yiwei Zhang,Zhenyu Wu,Xiaofeng Wang,Bingyao Yu,Zheng Zhu,Jie Zhou,Jiwen Lu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: R2RDreamer is a real-to-real demonstration augmentation framework that improves spatial generalization for manipulation policies by combining lightweight 3D editing with 2D video completion, avoiding complex scene parsing while supporting RGB-based policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spatial generalization in imitation-learned manipulation requires diverse demonstrations which are costly to collect. Existing augmentation methods either suffer from sim-to-real gaps (simulation-based) or rely on strong 3D parsing and produce pointcloud-specific outputs (real-to-real).

Method: R2RDreamer first performs lightweight 3D augmentation by editing incomplete object pointclouds and end-effector trajectories in a shared 3D frame, then projects edited scenes into masked image-space control videos with occlusion-aware reasoning, and uses a dense-control image-to-video model to complete temporally coherent RGB observations.

Result: Experiments on spatially shifted manipulation tasks with both 2D diffusion-style and vision-language-action policies show improved spatial generalization from limited source demonstrations.

Conclusion: R2RDreamer effectively improves spatial generalization for diverse policy types by bridging 3D editing and 2D video completion, validated through analyses of its components: 3D editing, occlusion-aware projection, and video completion.

Abstract: Spatial generalization is critical for imitation-learned manipulation policies, but achieving it typically requires scaling demonstrations across diverse object poses, robot configurations, and camera viewpoints. Data augmentation from a few source demonstrations offers a practical alternative to costly real-world collection. Simulation-based augmentation can create controllable variation, but requires complex environment and object setup and may introduce a sim-to-real gap. Recent real-to-real methods avoid these issues by jointly editing 3D observations and action trajectories from real demonstrations, yet they still rely on strong 3D scene parsing and geometry completion, and often produce observations tailored to 3D pointcloud policies rather than RGB-based 2D policies. We propose R2RDreamer, a real-to-real demonstration augmentation framework that preserves the geometric consistency of 3D action-observation editing while moving visual completion to 2D video space. Specifically, R2RDreamer first performs lightweight 3D augmentation by editing incomplete object pointclouds and end-effector trajectories in a shared 3D frame; it then projects the edited scene into masked image-space control videos with occlusion-aware reasoning and uses a dense-control image-to-video model to complete temporally coherent RGB observations. Experiments on spatially shifted manipulation tasks with both 2D diffusion-style policies and vision-language-action policies show that R2RDreamer improves spatial generalization from limited source demonstrations, with analyses validating the contributions of 3D editing, occlusion-aware projection, and video completion.

</details>


### [728] [Hierarchical Advantage Weighting for Online RL Fine-Tuning of VLAs from Sparse Episode Outcomes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17043)
*Tongyan Fang,Siyuan Huang,Naiyu Fang,Ganlong Zhao,Zhongjin Luo,Jianbo Liu,Xiaogang Wang,Ying Dong,Hongsheng Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HABC proposes Hierarchical Advantage-Weighted Behavior Cloning to address sparse binary reward issues in VLA policy fine-tuning by using separate critic heads for viability and efficiency, state-adaptive gating, and intervention-aware credit assignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL fine-tuning of VLA policies uses a single binary outcome per episode, which conflates viability (task success) with efficiency (speed/quality). Once basic success is achieved, binary labels provide no gradient to distinguish good from slow completions. Also, real-world rollouts mix autonomous and human intervention segments, causing incorrect credit assignment when episode outcomes are naively applied across these boundaries.

Method: HABC trains two separate critic heads (viability and efficiency) on different data subsets. A state-adaptive gate g_t merges their one-step advantages, prioritizing viability when success is uncertain and shifting to efficiency when viability is high. The merged advantage is converted into per-transition weights for the actor loss. Intervention-aware credit assignment restricts outcome labels to segments executed by the current policy only.

Result: On three real-robot bimanual tasks, HABC improves success rates from SFT baselines of 36%, 44%, and 12% to 92%, 88%, and 38% respectively.

Conclusion: HABC effectively decouples viability and efficiency objectives and handles intervention segments, significantly outperforming supervised fine-tuning on contact-rich bimanual tasks.

Abstract: When pretrained VLA policies are fine-tuned through online RL, each rollout episode produces only a single binary outcome (success or failure), yet the actor update requires per-transition supervision. Existing approaches commonly reduce this sparse outcome to a single scalar reward or advantage signal, which conflates distinct forms of transition-level feedback and provides limited guidance once basic task success becomes achievable. First, a single scalar signal conflates the two objectives of viability and efficiency; once basic success is achieved, the binary label provides no gradient to distinguish efficient completions from slow ones. Second, real-world rollouts mix autonomous and intervention segments; naively assigning episode outcomes across these boundaries introduces incorrect credit assignment. To address these issues, we propose Hierarchical Advantage-Weighted Behavior Cloning (HABC), which trains separate critic heads for these two objectives on different data subsets and combines their outputs with a state-adaptive balance. A state-adaptive gate $g_t$ merges their one-step advantages, prioritizing viability when success is uncertain and shifting to efficiency only when viability is high, and converts the result into per-transition weights on the actor loss. Intervention-aware credit assignment further restricts outcome labels to segments executed by the current policy, preventing supervision from leaking across intervention boundaries. In real-robot experiments on three contact-rich bimanual tasks, HABC raises success from supervised fine-tuning (SFT) baselines of 36%, 44%, and 12% to 92%, 88%, and 38%.

</details>


### [729] [Geometric Action Model for Robot Policy Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17046)
*Jisang Han,Seonghu Jeon,Jaewoo Jung,René Zurbrügg,Honggyu An,Tifanny Portela,Marco Hutter,Marc Pollefeys,Seungryong Kim,Sunghwan Hong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GAM repurposes a geometric foundation model as a unified backbone for perception, temporal prediction, and action decoding in robot manipulation, outperforming larger baselines in accuracy, robustness, speed, and model size.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLAs and WAMs operate on 2D representations, lacking explicit 3D geometry crucial for contact-rich manipulation tasks.

Method: GAM splits a pretrained geometric foundation model at an intermediate layer: shallow layers encode observations, a causal future predictor inserted at the split forecasts future latent tokens conditioned on language/proprioception/action history, and remaining GFM blocks decode features into actions — all within a single backbone.

Result: Across simulation and real-robot benchmarks, GAM achieves higher accuracy, robustness, speed, and lower parameter count compared to foundation-model-scale baselines.

Conclusion: Minimal architectural modification to a geometric foundation model enables language-conditioned temporal world modeling while preserving strong 3D geometric priors for effective manipulation.

Abstract: Generalist robot policies must follow user instructions while reasoning about how objects, cameras, and robot actions interact in the 3D physical world. Recent vision-language-action models (VLAs) and video world-action models (WAMs) inherit strong semantic or temporal priors from large-scale foundation models, but they still operate primarily on 2D image frames or 2D-derived latent spaces, leaving implicit the 3D geometry required for contact-rich manipulation. We propose the Geometric Action Model (GAM), a language-conditioned manipulation policy that directly repurposes a pretrained geometric foundation model (GFM) as a shared substrate for perception, temporal prediction, and action decoding. GAM splits the GFM at an intermediate layer: the shallow layers serve as an observation encoder, and a causal future predictor inserted at the split layer forecasts future latent tokens conditioned on language, proprioception, and action history. The predicted future tokens are then routed through the remaining GFM blocks for feature propagation and decoding, allowing a single backbone to produce both future geometry and actions. This design equips the GFM with language-conditioned temporal world modeling through minimal architectural modification while preserving its rich geometric priors. Across a broad suite of simulation and real-robot manipulation benchmarks, GAM is more accurate, more robust, faster, and lighter than current foundation-model-scale baselines.

</details>


### [730] [Human Universal Grasping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17054)
*Kevin Yuanbo Wu,Tianxing Zhou,Isaac Tu,Billy Yan,Irmak Guzey,David Fouhey,Dandan Shan,Lerrel Pinto*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HUG is a flow-matching model that generates diverse human grasps for any object from a single RGB-D image, trained on 1M egocentric human grasp frames, and enables zero-shot robot grasping across various embodiments, outperforming baselines by 23-34%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-fingered robots struggle with generalizable grasping. The authors argue that human grasping data (from thousands of daily pickups) is the most natural source to learn from, motivating a large-scale egocentric dataset and generative model for human-like grasps.

Method: A flow-matching model that fuses RGB and depth from a single stereo-camera image to predict grasp parameters (wrist translation, rotation, MANO hand pose). Trained on 1M-HUGs, a new egocentric dataset (1M frames, 6,707 objects, 41 buildings) captured via smart glasses. Grasps are retargeted to various robot hands for zero-shot deployment.

Result: HUG outperforms state-of-the-art grasping baselines by +23% and +34% on HUG-Bench (90 unseen objects across 5 geometric categories). Real-world validation on 30 objects across multiple cameras, robot embodiments, and household environments.

Conclusion: HUG demonstrates that learning from human egocentric grasping data with a flow-matching model enables generalizable, zero-shot robot grasping across diverse objects and embodiments, significantly surpassing prior methods.

Abstract: Humans can grasp objects effortlessly, whereas multi-fingered robots are far from this level of generality. We argue that the most natural source of robot grasping data is from humans, who pick up thousands of objects every day. We present HUG, a flow-matching model that generates diverse human grasps for any user-specified object in a single RGB-D image captured from a stereo camera. Using smart glasses, we first collect 1M-HUGs, an egocentric dataset of human grasps spanning 1M frames (27.8 hrs) and 6,707 object instances across 41 buildings. Next, to model the distribution of natural human grasps, our novel flow-matching model fuses RGB and depth observations to output a grasp parameterized by wrist translation, wrist rotation, and MANO hand pose. Predicted grasps can be retargeted to various robot hands, enabling zero-shot grasping in everyday scenes. To standardize evaluation, we build a new simulated benchmark, HUG-Bench, of 90 unseen objects from five geometric categories and various sizes, with metric-scale 3D meshes. We evaluate HUG in the real world on the 30-object test set of HUG-Bench across multiple stereo cameras, robot embodiments, and household environments. HUG outperforms the state-of-the-art grasping baselines by +23% and +34% on our challenging object set. Code, data, benchmark, checkpoints, and an interactive demo are released on our website: https://grasping.io/

</details>


### [731] [T-Rex: Tactile-Reactive Dexterous Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17055)
*Dantong Niu,Zhuoyang Liu,Zekai Wang,Boning Shao,Zhao-Heng Yin,Anirudh Pai,Yuvan Sharma,Stefano Saravalle,Ruijie Zheng,Jing Wang,Ryan Punamiya,Mengda Xu,Yuqi Xie,Yunfan Jiang,Letian Fu,Konstantinos Kallidromitis,Matteo Gioia,Junyi Zhang,Jiaxin Ge,Haiwen Feng,Fabio Galasso,Wei Zhan,David M. Chan,Yutong Bai,Roei Herzig,Jiahui Lei,Fei-Fei Li,Ken Goldberg,Jitendra Malik,Pieter Abbeel,Yuke Zhu,Danfei Xu,Jim,Fan,Trevor Darrell*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a tactile-reactive robotic manipulation system using a 100-hour tactile dataset, a variable-rate Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture, and a temporal tactile VQ-VAE encoder, achieving over 30% higher success rate on 12 dexterous manipulation tasks compared to baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tactile sensing is crucial for dexterous manipulation, but existing VLA models lack tactile integration due to scarce data, architectural constraints, and limitations of static tactile encoders.

Method: (1) Large-scale 100-hour tactile dataset collected via a data-efficient recipe focusing on elementary motor primitives. (2) Variable-rate Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture. (3) Novel temporal tactile VQ-VAE encoder for high-frequency touch signals.

Result: Over 30% higher average success rate than the strongest baseline on 12 manipulation tasks requiring delicate force control and deformable object manipulation.

Conclusion: Tactile-reactive policies significantly outperform static-cue-based approaches, and the proposed dataset and architecture enable effective tactile integration in VLA models.

Abstract: The ability to react dynamically to tactile signals has long been considered crucial to agile human-level dexterity. Yet contemporary learning-based Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for robotic manipulation generally either overlook the tactile modality or are limited to encoders with static cues, due in part to the scarcity of diverse training data and standardized evaluation, architectural constraints in current VLA models, and limitations of static tactile encoders. In this paper, we push the frontier of tactile-reactive manipulation by addressing all of these limitations. We propose a large-scale, 100-hour tactile-rich dataset collected via a novel, data-efficient recipe that prioritizes elementary motor primitives. To effectively exploit naturally high-frequency touch signals without sacrificing the existing capabilities of existing VLAs, we introduce a variable-rate Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture equipped with a novel temporal tactile VQ-VAE encoder. We demonstrate the effectiveness of tactile-reactive policies on 12 manipulation tasks requiring delicate force control and deformable object manipulation, achieving over 30% higher average success rate than the strongest baseline.

</details>
